"Dead." The woman smiled, and there was sadness in it, deep and ancient. "I died giving birth to you. But that is not the whole truth, is it? The whole truth is that I was never truly alive. I was a ghost who loved a living man, and you are the child born from that love."

"The song," Bai Su whispered. "It's real."

"It is more than real." The woman stood and walked to the well, peering into its depths. "You are the child, Bai Su. You are neither living nor dead, but something in between. And the ghost who is stealing life qi from students—the one you seek—is your brother."

Bai Su's world tilted. "I don't have a brother."

"You do. He was born before you, to the same mother, the same father. But he was born wrong—too much ghost, not enough human. He has spent a hundred years trying to become fully alive, and he will not stop until he succeeds." The woman turned to face her, and her eyes were filled with tears. "He has already killed forty-seven students. You will be the forty-eighth."

"Why?"

"Because you are the only one who can give him what he needs." The woman reached out and touched Bai Su's face. "Your life qi. Your humanity. If he takes it, he will become fully human. And you will become fully ghost."

Bai Su pulled away. "No. I won't let him."

"You have no choice." The woman's voice hardened. "He has been waiting for you since the day you were born. He has been planning this, preparing this, for nineteen years. And now that you are here, now that you are standing in the

title: Chapter 9
content:
CHAPTER 9: The Weight of Forgotten Things

The headmaster's study smelled of ink and dried plums, a scent so thick it clung to the back of Bai Su's throat like cobwebs. She sat in the chair across from his desk, her hands folded in her lap, the phantom blood still itching against her palms.

Headmaster Wei was a living man—she was certain of that. His skin had warmth, his breath fogged the cold air, and his pulse beat visible in the thin skin of his temples. But his eyes belonged to something older. They watched her with the patience of a predator that had learned to wait.

"You've been busy, Miss Bai."

"I've been curious," she corrected.

He smiled. It didn't reach his eyes. "Curiosity killed the cat."

"Satisfaction brought it back."

The smile vanished. He reached into his desk drawer and produced a scroll, yellowed with age, the silk binding frayed and loose. He unrolled it across the desk, weighting the corners with inkstones.

"Tell me what you see."

Bai Su leaned forward. The scroll was an examination paper—eighty questions, densely written, the answers filled in with a practiced hand. The handwriting was elegant. Precise. Familiar.

She reached out to touch it.

"Don't." Headmaster Wei's hand shot out, stopping her wrist. His grip was stronger than she expected, his fingers cold despite his living flesh. "Look with your eyes. Not your spirit."

She pulled back. "Whose paper is this?"

"Yours."

The word hung between them like a blade.

"I've never taken the imperial exams," she said slowly. "I'm not even registered for this year's—"

"You've taken them seven times." He turned the scroll toward her, pointing to the name in the corner. The ink was faded, the characters smudged, but she could still read them. Bai Su. "You failed every time. The last attempt, you died during the third day of testing. Heart failure, the examiners wrote. But that wasn't true."

Bai Su's mouth went dry. "I would remember dying."

"Would you?" He rolled the scroll back up, his movements deliberate, measured. "Memory is a fragile thing, Miss Bai. Especially for those who cross the veil and come back."

"I didn't cross anything. I'm alive."

"Are you?"

The question struck her like a physical blow. She looked down at her hands—the same hands that had been covered in blood moments ago, the same hands that had left that footprint in the dust. She pressed them against her chest. Her heart was beating. She could feel it.

But when had she last felt hungry? When had she last slept? When had she last felt the sun warm her skin instead of just illuminating it?

"I'm alive," she repeated, but her voice cracked on the second word.

Headmaster Wei stood, walking to the window. The courtyard below was empty, the students confined to their quarters by the evening lockdown. A single lantern burned near the gate, its light casting long shadows across the stones.

"There are three kinds of students at Wutong Academy," he said, his back to her. "The living, who study for the exams. The dead, who study for redemption. And the returned—those who died and were given a second chance, their memories sealed to protect them from the truth."

"Which one am I?"

He turned. His face was unreadable. "That is what you need to discover."

---

Bai Su left the headmaster's study in a daze. The corridors were empty, the oil lamps flickering in their brackets, casting dancing shadows that seemed to move with purpose. She walked without direction, her feet carrying her through familiar halls that suddenly felt foreign.

She found herself at the library.

The building loomed before her, its windows dark, its doors sealed. But a single light burned in the upper floor—the reading room where the ghost scholars gathered at night. She pushed open the door. It creaked on its hinges, the sound too loud in the silence.

The stairs groaned beneath her weight. Or rather, the weight she thought she had. By the time she reached the top, she wasn't sure if the stairs had been protesting her presence or welcoming her.

The reading room was empty.

But a book lay open on the central table, its pages glowing faintly with residual qi. Bai Su approached it slowly, her eyes scanning the text. It was a registry—a list of names, dates, and causes of death. The Wutong Academy death records.

She ran her finger down the page.

Chen Feng. Died fifth year of the Dragon Emperor's reign. Cause: fall from height. Ruled suicide.

She turned another page.

Lin Wei. Died third year of the Serpent Empress's reign. Cause: drowning. Ruled accident.

Another page.

Wang Ming. Died first year of the current dynasty. Cause: examination corruption. Ruled murder.

The names went on and on. Hundreds of them. Thousands. Students who had entered the academy and never left, their spirits bound to the grounds by unfinished business and unfulfilled dreams.

And then she found it.

Bai Su. Died seventh year of the current dynasty. Cause: unknown. Ruled: pending.

Pending. For eighteen years, her death had been pending.

"You shouldn't be here."

Bai Su whirled around. The ghost scholar stood in the doorway, his form more solid than she had ever seen it, his eyes burning with an intensity that made her step back.

"I found the death records," she said, her voice shaking.

"I know." He walked toward her, each step deliberate, his bare feet making no sound on the wooden floor. "I was hoping you wouldn't."

"Who am I?"

He stopped in front of her, close enough that she could see the individual threads of his robes, the way they shifted between solid and transparent. He reached out, his hand hovering near her cheek, not quite touching.

"You're Bai Su," he said softly. "The girl who died in the examination hall, her heart stopped by a curse meant for someone else. The girl who was brought back by a scholar who loved her too much to let her go. The girl who has been living on borrowed time for eighteen years."

"That's not possible."

"You've seen ghosts. You've spoken to the dead. You've walked through walls of memory that no living person should be able to touch." He lowered his hand. "What part of this seems impossible to you?"

Bai Su's legs gave out. She collapsed into the nearest chair, her mind reeling, her heart—her borrowed heart—pounding against her ribs.

"The killer," she said, grasping for something concrete. "The ghost who's been stealing life qi. If I'm already dead, why would they target me?"

"Because you're not dead. Not entirely." He sat across from her, his form flickering like a candle in the wind. "You're in-between. A living ghost. Your qi is unique—part mortal, part spirit. For someone trying to break through the veil, your essence would be the perfect catalyst."

"That's why the headmaster wanted me here."

"That's why I warned you."

She looked up at him, meeting his eyes. "You knew. All this time, you knew what I was."

"I suspected." He looked away. "I didn't want to believe it."

"Why?"

"Because I was the one who brought you back."

The confession hit her like a wave of cold water. She stared at him, this ghost who had haunted the bell tower, who had spoken in riddles and warnings, who had watched her with eyes that held centuries of regret.

"Who are you?" she whispered.

"My name is Bai Chen." He smiled, a sad, broken thing. "And I am your brother."

---

The bell above them rang.

Once.

Twice.

Three times.

Bai Su stood, her body moving without her permission, her feet carrying her toward the door. The ghost scholar—her brother—reached for her, but his hand passed through her arm like smoke.

"It's too late," he said. "The seal is breaking."

"What seal?"

"The one that keeps your memories locked away." His voice was urgent now, desperate. "The killer knows who you are. They've been feeding you pieces of the truth, trying to force the seal open. If it breaks completely, you'll remember everything—including how to die."

The bell rang again. Four times. Five. Six.

Bai Su walked down the stairs, her mind a storm of fragmented images. A face she didn't recognize. A voice she knew too well. A hand reaching for her as she fell, fell, fell—

Seven. Eight. Nine.

She reached the bottom of the stairs. The front door of the library stood open, the night air rushing in, cold and sharp. Beyond the threshold, the courtyard was bathed in moonlight, and standing in the center was a figure she knew.

Lin Wei.

The drowned scholar smiled at her, water dripping from her hair, her robes, her skin. "Hello, Bai Su. I've been waiting for you."

Ten.

The bell fell silent.

And Bai Su remembered everything.

title: The Memory in the Ink
content:
The headmaster's office smelled of old paper and burning sage, a combination that made Bai Su's stomach turn. She stood before the massive rosewood desk, her hands still tingling from the phantom blood, and waited for the old man to speak.

He didn't.

Instead, Headmaster Wen sat motionless, his fingers steepled beneath his chin, his eyes fixed on something above Bai Su's left shoulder. She resisted the urge to turn around. Whatever he was looking at, she suspected she didn't want to see it.

"You've been busy, Miss Bai."

It wasn't a question. She answered anyway. "I've been investigating."

"Yes." He unfolded his hands and placed them flat on the desk. The movement was deliberate, almost ceremonial. "I've heard about your conversation with the library ghost. And your visit to the bell tower."

Bai Su's throat tightened. "How did you—"

"This academy has eyes everywhere. Some of them are even alive." He smiled, but there was no warmth in it. "Tell me, what did you find?"

She considered lying. The words formed on her tongue, ready to be spoken. But something in the headmaster's gaze stopped her. It wasn't authority she saw there. It was fear.

"The bell chamber," she said carefully. "There was a footprint. It matched mine."

"Did it now."

"I have no memory of being there before today."

The headmaster leaned back in his chair. The wood creaked beneath him, a sound like distant thunder. "Memory is a peculiar thing, Miss Bai. It shifts. It hides. It protects us from truths we are not ready to accept."

"You know something."

"I know many things. The question is whether you are prepared to know them as well."

Bai Su's hands clenched at her sides. "I came here to study. To pass the imperial exams. I didn't come here to solve murders or chase ghosts or—" She stopped, her breath catching. "I didn't come here to find blood on my hands."

"No one ever does."

The headmaster rose from his chair. He moved slowly, his joints protesting with each step, and made his way to a cabinet against the far wall. He opened it with a key that hung around his neck, a key that glowed faintly in the dim light.

Inside were scrolls. Hundreds of them, stacked and sorted by date.

"Do you know what these are?"

Bai Su shook her head.

"Examination records. Every test taken at Wutong Academy for the past fifty years." He pulled one out, its paper yellowed and brittle. "Including yours."

"I haven't taken any exams yet."

"No. But you will." He held the scroll out to her. "Open it."

She hesitated. Her fingers brushed the paper, and a shock ran through her—not electricity, but something older. Recognition.

She unrolled the scroll.

Her own handwriting stared back at her. Characters formed with the precise strokes she had practiced since childhood. A complete examination essay, written in her own hand, dated three months before she had ever set foot in Wutong Academy.

"This isn't possible."

"Read the first line."

Bai Su's eyes traced the characters. Her voice came out hollow. "'On the second day of the seventh month, I witnessed a death that should not have happened.'"

"Continue."

She didn't want to. Every instinct screamed at her to roll the scroll back up, to pretend she had never seen it. But her eyes kept moving, kept reading, kept uncovering words she had written and forgotten.

"'Chen Feng did not jump. He was pushed. By someone who had already died. Someone who should not have been there. Someone who—'"

The scroll ended.

Bai Su looked up, her hands trembling. "There's more."

"Of course there is." The headmaster took the scroll from her gently. "But you haven't written it yet."

The room spun. Bai Su grasped the edge of the desk to steady herself. "You're saying I already knew. Before I arrived. I knew what was going to happen."

"I'm saying you knew what had already happened. What is always happening." He placed the scroll back in the cabinet and locked it. "Wutong Academy exists in a loop, Miss Bai. Every year, the same students arrive. The same ghosts haunt the halls. The same deaths occur. And every year, someone tries to stop it."

"Someone like me."

"Someone exactly like you."

Bai Su's mind raced. The footprint. The blood on her hands. The voice that sang a song she should not have known. "How many times?"

"More than I can count."

"And I fail every time."

The headmaster's silence was answer enough.

"Why?" The word came out raw, desperate. "Why do I keep coming back? Why can't I stop it?"

"Because you are not meant to stop it." He walked back to his desk and sat down heavily. "You are meant to remember it. To carry the memory forward. Each time, you learn more. Each time, you get closer."

"Closer to what?"

"To the truth. The truth about this academy. About the ghosts. About yourself."

Bai Su stared at him. The old man's face was lined with exhaustion, his eyes clouded with a grief that had accumulated over decades. He knew. He had always known.

"Who pushed Chen Feng?"

"I cannot tell you."

"Why not?"

"Because you have to remember it yourself. If I tell you, the loop breaks differently. Not in the way we need."

"We?" Bai Su's voice sharpened. "Who is 'we'?"

The headmaster opened his mouth to answer, but a knock at the door interrupted him. The servant girl from before entered, her face pale as paper.

"Headmaster, there's been another incident."

"What kind of incident?"

The servant's eyes darted to Bai Su. "The ghost of the west wing. She's been seen walking through the dormitories. Touching the sleeping students."

"Touching them how?"

"On their foreheads. Leaving marks." The servant held up her hand. Her palm was stained black, the color of old ink. "The students who woke up... they can't remember their own names."

Bai Su's blood ran cold. "She's stealing their memories."

The headmaster's face hardened. "No. She's returning them."

"Returning them to whom?"

He didn't answer. He didn't have to. The answer was written in the way he looked at her, in the way the servant girl's eyes kept flicking to Bai Su's hands.

The hands that had been covered in blood.

The hands that had written a scroll she did not remember.

The hands that had pushed—

"Miss Bai." The headmaster's voice cut through her spiraling thoughts. "You need to go to the west wing. Now. Before she finishes."

"Finishes what?"

"Waking you up."

The servant girl stepped aside. Bai Su moved toward the door, her legs carrying her forward even as her mind screamed at her to stop. She paused at the threshold and turned back.

"Headmaster. What happens if I remember everything?"

The old man's face softened. For a moment, he looked almost kind.

"Then you will finally understand why you have to let go."

Bai Su ran.

The west wing was a maze of narrow corridors and locked doors. Bai Su counted her steps as she went, a habit she had developed in her first life—no, in a life she could not remember. Forty-three steps to the first intersection. Eighteen to the second. Twenty-seven to the third.

She had walked this path before. She had walked it so many times it had become instinct.

The door at the end of the hall was open.

Inside, a woman sat on a wooden stool, her back to the entrance. She wore the robes of a scholar, faded and frayed, and her hair hung in long, tangled strands down her back. In her hands, she held a brush. Before her, a scroll lay unrolled on a low table.

"Come in," the woman said without turning. "I've been waiting for you."

Bai Su stepped inside. The room was bare except for the table, the stool, and a single candle that burned with a blue flame. The walls were covered in writing—characters scratched into the plaster, layer upon layer of words that seemed to shift and writhe in the candlelight.

"You're the ghost of the west wing."

"I am many things." The woman set down her brush and turned. Her face was young, no older than Bai Su's, but her eyes held centuries. "I am a keeper. A witness. A mirror."

"A mirror for what?"

"For you." The woman stood and walked toward Bai Su, her feet making no sound on the floor. "Every time you come here, you leave a piece of yourself behind. Every time you fail, I collect it. I hold it until you return."

She stopped inches from Bai Su and raised her hand. Her palm was covered in the same black ink that had marked the servant girl.

"Let me show you what you've forgotten."

Bai Su wanted to step back. Wanted to run. But her body refused to move. The woman's hand came to rest on her forehead, and the world dissolved.

Images flooded her mind.

Chen Feng, standing at the bell tower window, his face twisted with terror. Not because he was about to fall. Because of what he saw behind her.

Herself, reaching out. Not to push. To pull him back.

Hands grabbing her from behind. Cold hands. Dead hands.

The ghost scholar from the library, his eyes burning with hunger.

"I'm sorry," he whispered. "But I need to live more than he needs to die."

She had not pushed Chen Feng.

She had tried to save him.

And the ghost scholar had killed them both.

Bai Su gasped and stumbled backward. The woman caught her, steadying her with hands that felt solid and real.

"Now you remember."

"I remember." Bai Su's voice cracked. "He killed me. The ghost scholar killed me. He took my qi and he threw me from the tower and he—" She stopped. "But I'm still here. I'm alive."

"You are alive because this academy does not let its students die. Not truly." The woman's eyes glistened. "Every time you die, the loop resets. You return, older and younger at the same time. You carry the scars of lives you cannot remember."

"Until now."

"Until now." The woman smiled, a sad and gentle thing. "This time, you remembered before he could take your qi. That changes everything."

"How?"

"He doesn't know." The woman leaned closer, her voice dropping to a whisper. "He thinks you are the same Bai Su who has always existed. He doesn't know that you remember. And that means you can finally beat him."

Bai Su's hands clenched into fists. "How do I stop him? How do I end this?"

The woman stepped back and gestured to the scroll on the table. "The answer is there. You wrote it. In the first loop. Before he killed you, before he took your memories, you wrote down everything."

Bai Su approached the table. The scroll was covered in handwriting—her handwriting—but the ink was ancient, dried and cracking. She read the first line.

"The ghost scholar is not a student. He is the academy itself."

She looked up. "What does that mean?"

The woman opened her mouth to answer, but a crash from outside interrupted her. The door slammed open. The ghost scholar stood in the doorway, his face a mask of cold fury.

Behind him, the corridor was filled with shadows that moved against the light.

"I wondered why this loop felt different." His voice was ice. "You remembered."

title: The Memory That Wasn't Mine
content:
The headmaster's office smelled of sandalwood and secrets.

Bai Su sat in the carved rosewood chair, her hands folded in her lap, trying not to think about the blood that had been on them minutes ago. The headmaster, a man named Wei Cheng whose age was impossible to guess, sat across from her, his fingers steepled beneath his chin. Behind him, a scroll painting of a mountain swallowed by clouds seemed to shift whenever she looked away.

"You've been asking questions about Chen Feng," he said. Not a question.

"I have."

"And have you found answers?"

Bai Su considered lying. But the headmaster's eyes were the color of old amber, and they seemed to see through her skin to the bones beneath. "I've found more questions."

A thin smile crossed his face. "That is often the way of things at Wutong Academy. The more you learn, the less you understand." He reached into his sleeve and withdrew a folded piece of paper, yellowed with age. "I found this among Chen Feng's belongings. I thought you should see it."

She took the paper carefully, unfolding it with fingers that still trembled. The characters were written in a woman's hand—delicate, precise, with a slight flourish on the final stroke of each word.

*To whoever finds this—*

*My name is Lin Yue. I was a student here six years ago. I am writing this because I am afraid I will not survive the night.*

*There is a ghost in the academy that does not know it is dead. It walks among us, attends classes, takes examinations. It believes it is still alive. And it is hungry.*

*I have discovered its identity. But discovery is not the same as survival.*

*If you are reading this, I am already gone. Please—*

The writing stopped mid-character, the brush trailing off into a jagged line, as if someone had pulled her hand away.

Bai Su looked up. "She never finished."

"No," the headmaster said. "She disappeared that same night. Her body was never found. But her ghost appears in the library sometimes, still writing. Still trying to finish the sentence."

"What was she trying to say?"

"I don't know. And neither does she." He leaned forward, his amber eyes catching the lamplight. "But I think you might find out, Miss Bai. Because the ghost she was writing about—the one that doesn't know it's dead—I believe it is still here. And I believe it has begun to kill again."

---

Bai Su left the headmaster's office with Lin Yue's letter folded carefully in her inner pocket. The corridors were empty now, the evening classes having ended. The oil lamps flickered in their sconces, casting dancing shadows that seemed to reach for her as she passed.

She needed to find the ghost scholar from the bell tower. He knew something. He had been trying to warn her.

But when she reached the bell tower, the door was locked.

Not barred from the inside—locked with a heavy iron padlock that she had not seen before. The chain was new, the metal gleaming in the torchlight. Someone had sealed it shut.

"Looking for something?"

Bai Su spun around. A student stood behind her, tall and lean, with sharp features and eyes that held no warmth. He wore the academy robes, but something about him seemed wrong—a slight stillness, a quality of being too perfectly still.

"I was just—"

"You were just what? Taking a walk?" He smiled, but it didn't reach his eyes. "The bell tower is off limits. Headmaster's orders. Haven't you heard about the accident?"

"I heard."

"Then you know why it's locked." He stepped closer, and Bai Su noticed that his feet made no sound on the stone floor. "People who go up there don't always come down the same way they went up."

"Is that a threat?"

"It's a fact, Miss Bai." He tilted his head, studying her. "You're new here. You don't understand how things work at Wutong Academy. Some doors are locked for good reason. Some questions should not be asked."

"And if I ask them anyway?"

"Then you might find yourself at the bottom of a tower, just like Chen Feng." He turned and walked away, his robes whispering against the floor, leaving no footprints in the dust.

Bai Su waited until he was gone, then pressed her hand against the lock. Cold. So cold it burned. She pulled her hand back, and frost clung to her fingertips.

The ghost scholar had been sealed inside.

---

She found the drowned scholar in the library, floating face-down in a pool of water that had no source. When she called his name, he lifted his head, and water poured from his mouth and nose like a fountain.

"Lin Yue," she said, holding up the letter. "Did you know her?"

The scholar's eyes widened. He sank beneath the water, then rose again, his expression twisted with something that might have been recognition, might have been fear.

"She was the first," he said, his voice bubbling through water. "The first to understand."

"The first to understand what?"

"That the ghost doesn't know it's dead. That it believes it is still taking the examinations, still studying, still living. Every year, it takes another student's life qi, and every year, it grows stronger. More real."

"Who is it?"

The drowned scholar's face contorted. "I cannot say its name. None of us can. The ghost hears when its name is spoken. It hears, and it comes."

"Then show me."

He hesitated. Then he reached into the water and pulled out a scroll, soaked and dripping. He held it out to her, and she took it, her fingers brushing against his cold, waterlogged skin.

"The examination records from six years ago," he said. "Lin Yue's class. Look at the names. Look for the one who never left."

Bai Su unrolled the scroll. The ink had bled, the characters smudged and running, but she could still make out the list of names. Forty students. All marked as graduated or deceased.

All except one.

The name was there, but the status column was blank. Neither graduated nor dead. Just... missing.

She read the name aloud.

The drowned scholar screamed.

The water around him turned to ice, cracking and shattering. He reached for her, his fingers elongating, his face splitting open, but Bai Su was already running, the scroll clutched to her chest, the name burning in her mind like a brand.

She knew who she was looking for.

---

The west wing was dark, the candles extinguished, the air thick with the smell of old incense and older sorrow. Bai Su moved through the corridors, her footsteps echoing in the silence, her heart pounding so loud she was sure it could be heard through stone.

The room was at the end of the hall. The door was open.

Inside, a girl sat at a desk, writing by candlelight. She was beautiful in the way that ghosts are beautiful—perfect and pale, with eyes that held no light and hair that moved as if underwater.

"You read the letter," the girl said without turning around.

"Lin Yue."

"Not anymore." The girl dipped her brush in ink, her movements precise, mechanical. "Lin Yue died six years ago. I am what remains. A sentence that never finished."

"You were trying to warn someone."

"I was trying to warn everyone." She set down her brush and turned. Her face was peaceful, almost serene. "But the ghost found me before I could finish. It always finds you before you can finish."

"Who is it?"

Lin Yue's smile was sad. "You already know. You read the name."

"Then tell me. Give me something I can use."

"The ghost doesn't know it's dead. That's the tragedy of it. It believes it is still studying, still preparing for the examinations. It doesn't understand why the sun never rises for it, why the other students don't speak to it, why the teachers never call its name." She paused. "It is lonely, Miss Bai. And loneliness in a ghost is a dangerous thing."

"How do I stop it?"

"You don't." Lin Yue stood, her robes rustling like dried leaves. "You can't stop something that doesn't know it needs to be stopped. You can only survive it."

"And Chen Feng? Did he try to stop it?"

"Chen Feng was trying to help it. He felt sorry for it. He thought he could share his life qi—just a little, just enough to let the ghost feel the sun again." Her voice dropped to a whisper. "But the ghost didn't want just a little. It wanted everything."

Bai Su's blood ran cold. "Then it's still here. Still hunting."

"Yes." Lin Yue stepped closer, and Bai Su saw that her feet did not touch the ground. "And it has already chosen its next victim."

"Who?"

The candle went out.

In the darkness, Bai Su heard Lin Yue's voice, soft and terrible.

"You."

---

She ran.

She ran through the west wing, through the courtyard, through the main hall, her lungs burning, her legs screaming. She didn't know where she was going, only that she had to get away, had to find light, had to find people—

She crashed into someone.

Strong hands caught her, steadied her. She looked up into the face of the headmaster.

"Miss Bai? What's wrong?"

"The ghost," she gasped. "It's—it's coming for me. Lin Yue said—"

"Lin Yue?" His expression hardened. "You spoke to Lin Yue?"

"She was in the west wing. She told me—"

"Miss Bai." His voice was calm, but his eyes were not. "Lin Yue's ghost has been sealed for three years. She cannot speak to anyone. She cannot leave her room."

Bai Su stared at him. "But I just—"

"You just spoke to something wearing Lin Yue's face." He grabbed her arm, his grip tight. "We need to leave. Now."

"Where are we going?"

"Anywhere it isn't."

He pulled her toward the main gate, but the gate was gone. Where it had stood was a wall of solid darkness, pulsing with a heartbeat that was not hers.

"The ghost is already here," the headmaster whispered. "It's been here the whole time."

Bai Su turned.

The headmaster's face was melting.

Not melting like wax—melting like a mask, sliding off to reveal something underneath. Something with eyes that had never seen the sun and a mouth that had never tasted food and a hunger that would never be satisfied.

"Thank you for saying my name," the ghost said with the headmaster's voice. "I had forgotten what it sounded like."

Bai Su tried to run, but her feet were frozen to the ground. The ghost reached for her, its fingers brushing her cheek, cold as the grave.

"Don't worry," it whispered. "You won't feel a thing."

The world went black.

And in the darkness, a bell began to toll.

title: The Song Before Drowning
content:
The headmaster's office smelled of ink and dried plums, a scent so ordinary it made Bai Su's skin crawl. She sat in the carved rosewood chair, her hands folded in her lap, still feeling the phantom residue of blood that had vanished from her palms.

Headmaster Wen was not at his desk. He stood by the window, his back to her, watching the courtyard below. The afternoon sun cast long shadows across the floor, and Bai Su noticed something she had missed before—the headmaster cast no shadow at all.

"You've been busy, Miss Bai." His voice was calm, almost amused. "Visiting the bell tower. Speaking with ghosts. Getting your hands dirty."

"I found a body," she said flatly. "Or rather, I found where a body used to be."

He turned, and his eyes were old in a way that had nothing to do with years. They held centuries, civilizations, the dust of dynasties that had risen and fallen while he watched from this very window.

"The ghost scholar you spoke with—he told you about the stolen life qi, I assume."

"Yes."

"And about the transfer."

"He said a ghost who remembers how to live remembers how to kill."

Headmaster Wen nodded slowly. He walked to his desk, opened a drawer, and withdrew a scroll tied with red silk. He placed it before her, his fingers lingering on the knot.

"This is a record of every student who has died at Wutong Academy. Every ghost who studies here, every soul bound to these halls. There are forty-seven names."

Bai Su did not reach for it. "You're telling me there are forty-seven suspects."

"I'm telling you there are forty-seven victims." He untied the silk and unrolled the scroll across the desk. The paper was yellowed, the ink faded to brown, but the characters were precise, each one written with the care of a master calligrapher. "And one of them has decided to stop being a victim."

She leaned forward, scanning the names. Some were familiar—students she had seen in the library, in the dining hall, walking the corridors with books pressed to their chests. Others were not.

Her finger stopped at an entry near the bottom. Chen Feng. Date of death: three years ago. Cause: fall from height.

"He's here," she murmured. "Chen Feng is listed as a ghost student."

"All students who die on academy grounds remain," Headmaster Wen said. "It is the price of admission. The academy gives them knowledge. It takes their freedom."

"But he was murdered."

The headmaster's expression did not change. "Allegedly."

"You know something."

"I know many things, Miss Bai. The question is whether you are ready to know them as well."

Bai Su looked up from the scroll, meeting his ancient gaze. "Why did you summon me here? To show me a list of dead students?"

"To warn you." He rolled the scroll back up, his movements deliberate, almost ceremonial. "The ghost who killed Chen Feng is not the one you should be hunting. The killer is merely a symptom. The disease is older. Much older."

"What disease?"

Headmaster Wen looked past her, toward the door. His eyes narrowed, and for a moment, Bai Su saw something flicker across his face—not fear, exactly, but recognition. The look of a man who has seen a storm coming and knows he cannot outrun it.

"Someone is listening," he said quietly. "We will continue this conversation when the walls have ears no longer."

He pressed the scroll into her hands. "Take this. Read it. Memorize it if you can. There is a pattern in the deaths, a rhythm that has repeated for a hundred years. Find it, and you will find the truth."

"And if I don't find it?"

"Then you will become the forty-eighth name."

He opened the door for her, a clear dismissal. Bai Su stepped into the hallway, the scroll clutched against her chest, and heard the door close behind her with a soft click.

The corridor was empty. The torches along the walls burned low, casting dancing shadows that seemed to move of their own accord. She walked quickly, her footsteps echoing in the silence, and did not stop until she reached the library.

The reading room was deserted. Even the ghost scholars who usually haunted the shelves were absent, as if they sensed something in the air that made them want to disappear.

Bai Su spread the scroll across an empty table and began to read.

The names were arranged by date of death, starting with the oldest—a scholar named Liu Wei, who had died in the academy's first year of operation. Cause of death: drowning. He had been found in the ornamental pond behind the east wing, his robes tangled in the lotus roots, his mouth open in a silent scream.

Next was a woman named Song Yulan. Death by fire. She had been studying in the west wing when a candle tipped over. The flames had consumed her before anyone could reach her.

Then a man named Zhao Jian. Poison. Then a woman named Mei Lin. Fall. Then a boy of twelve named Xiao Hu. Strangulation.

Bai Su read through the entire list, her eyes growing heavy with the weight of so much death. Forty-seven souls, each one extinguished before their time, each one bound to this academy for eternity.

She was about to roll the scroll back up when something caught her eye.

The dates.

She spread the scroll flat again, her heart beginning to race. She counted the years between each death. Five years. Every single one was exactly five years apart.

Liu Wei—Year 1
Song Yulan—Year 6
Zhao Jian—Year 11
Mei Lin—Year 16
Xiao Hu—Year 21

The pattern continued, unbroken, all the way to Chen Feng in Year 46. And then—

She stopped. The next entry after Chen Feng was blank. The ink had been scratched out, scraped away with something sharp, leaving only a gouge in the paper.

But underneath, faint, almost invisible, she could see the outline of characters that had been written and then erased.

Bai Su held the scroll up to the candlelight. The flame flickered through the thin paper, illuminating the ghost of the erased text.

Year 51. A name that began with the character "Bai."

She dropped the scroll as if it had burned her.

"No," she whispered. "No, that's not possible."

But the evidence was there, written in ink and then violently removed. Someone had tried to erase her from this record. Someone had known she would come looking.

The library door creaked open.

Bai Su spun around, her heart hammering against her ribs. A figure stood in the doorway, silhouetted against the torchlight from the hallway. Tall. Slender. Familiar.

"You shouldn't be here," said the ghost scholar from the bell tower. He stepped into the room, and the light revealed his face—pale, beautiful, marked by something that might have been sorrow or might have been hunger. "You've found the pattern."

"You knew," she said. "You knew about the five-year cycle."

"I knew." He walked toward her, his steps silent on the stone floor. "I also know that you're the only living student who has ever been admitted in Year 51. The cycle is broken because you are here."

"I'm not going to die."

"No one expects you to die." He stopped in front of her, close enough that she could see the faint translucence of his skin, the way the candlelight passed through him like water through gauze. "They expect you to kill."

The words hung in the air between them, cold and final.

"What are you talking about?"

"The erased name on that scroll—it's not a death date. It's a birth date." He reached out, and his fingers brushed against her cheek. They were cold, so cold they burned. "You are the forty-eighth ghost, Bai Su. You just haven't died yet."

She wanted to pull away. She wanted to scream, to run, to deny everything he was saying. But her body would not move. Her mind would not form the words.

"You remember the song," he said softly. "The one you heard in the bell tower. You know it, don't you?"

She did. She had known it her entire life, though she had never heard it before today. A lullaby her mother used to sing, her grandmother before that, her great-grandmother before that. A song about a scholar who drowned, a girl who burned, a child who was strangled in his sleep.

A song about the forty-seven souls who had died so that one might live.

"The cycle is almost complete," the ghost scholar whispered. "The killer is not trying to take your life qi, Bai Su. They're trying to protect it. Because you are the one who is supposed to die. And if you don't—"

The library door slammed open.

A servant girl stood in the doorway, her face white with terror. "Miss Bai! The headmaster—he's been attacked! Someone—" She choked on her words, tears streaming down her face. "Someone cut out his tongue."

Bai Su ran.

She did not look back at the ghost scholar. She did not think about the song, the scroll, the name erased from history. She ran through the corridors, past the startled faces of students and servants, up the stairs to the headmaster's office.

The door was open.

Headmaster Wen lay on the floor, his hands clutching his throat, blood pooling beneath his head. His eyes were open, staring at the ceiling, and his mouth was a dark red cavern where his tongue had been.

He was still alive.

Bai Su dropped to her knees beside him, pressing her hands against his wound, trying to stem the flow of blood. His eyes met hers, and in them she saw something that made her blood run colder than any ghost's touch.

He was trying to speak.

His lips moved, forming a single word, over and over again. She leaned closer, her ear almost touching his mouth, and heard the wet, gurgling whisper of his final message.

"Ru—n."

Then his eyes went still, and the blood stopped flowing, and Headmaster Wen joined the forty-seven names on the scroll.

Bai Su looked down at her hands.

They were covered in blood.

Not hers.

Not fresh.

Old blood, dried and cracked, staining her palms like rust.

The same blood that had been on her hands in the bell tower. The same blood that had vanished when the servant girl arrived.

But this time, when she looked up, there was no servant girl.

There was only the ghost scholar, standing in the doorway, watching her with eyes that held no pity.

"It's too late," he said. "The cycle has already begun."

And from somewhere deep in the academy, the bell began to ring.

title: Chapter 13
content:
CHAPTER 13: The Hidden Roster

The headmaster's office was a study in contradictions. Ancient scrolls lined the walls, their silk bindings frayed with age, while a modern oil lamp sat on the desk, its flame casting dancing shadows across a portrait of the Emperor. Headmaster Zhou Wei sat behind the desk, his fingers steepled, his face unreadable.

"Close the door," he said.

Bai Su obeyed, the click of the latch echoing in the silence. She kept her hands hidden in her sleeves, though the blood had vanished. The phantom warmth remained, a constant reminder of something she could not remember.

"Sit."

She sat. The chair was hard, the wood cold even through her robes.

"I received a report that you were seen entering the bell tower," Zhou Wei said, his voice flat. "You know that tower is off-limits to students."

"I was following a lead."

"A lead." He raised an eyebrow. "Concerning what, exactly?"

Bai Su considered her options. The headmaster was a living man—she could see the pulse beating in his throat, the rise and fall of his chest. But he presided over a school where half the students were dead. He had to know. He had to be complicit.

"I've noticed some unusual activity," she said carefully. "Students who seem... different. Distracted. Weak."

"Ah." Zhou Wei leaned back, his chair creaking. "You mean the life qi drain."

The words hung in the air between them, heavy with implication.

"You know about it," Bai Su said.

"I know about many things, Miss Bai. I know that you see ghosts. I know that you've been asking questions. I know that you found Chen Feng's body before anyone else." He paused. "I also know that you were the last person to see him alive."

Her blood ran cold. "That's not true. I found him in the courtyard. He was already—"

"Falling? Yes, that's what your testimony says. But testimony can be mistaken. Memories can be shaped." He opened a drawer and pulled out a scroll, unrolling it across the desk. "Do you recognize this?"

Bai Su leaned forward. The scroll was covered in names, hundreds of them, written in neat rows. Some were crossed out in red ink. Others had notes beside them: *Deceased. Transferred. Missing.*

"This is a roster," she said.

"It's the master list of every student who has ever attended Wutong Academy. Living and dead." He tapped a name near the bottom. Chen Feng. It was crossed out in red. "He was a promising scholar. Top of his class. But he started showing signs of weakness last month. Pale skin. Dark circles. Forgetting things that had just happened."

"The life qi drain."

"Indeed. I tried to protect him. Moved him to a different dormitory. Assigned him a curfew. But the killer was patient." Zhou Wei's eyes hardened. "And now he's dead. Which means the killer is getting desperate."

"Why are you telling me this?"

"Because I can't stop them myself." He rolled up the scroll and handed it to her. "The ghosts here follow different rules. They don't answer to living authorities. They don't fear expulsion or punishment. The only thing they respect is power." He looked at her, his gaze piercing. "And you have power, Miss Bai. You just don't know how to use it yet."

Bai Su took the scroll, her fingers trembling. "What am I supposed to do with this?"

"Study it. Learn the names. Figure out the pattern." He stood, signaling the meeting was over. "And be careful. The killer knows you're investigating. They've already tried to warn you off once."

"The blood on my hands."

"Was that a warning?" Zhou Wei shook his head. "No, Miss Bai. That was a message. Someone wants you to know that you've been here before. That you've touched this case in ways you don't remember."

He walked to the door and opened it, the hallway beyond dark and empty.

"Find the killer before they find you. That's your only option now."

---

Bai Su didn't go back to her dormitory. She couldn't sleep, couldn't rest, couldn't stop thinking about the blood on her hands and the footprint that matched her shoe. Instead, she found a quiet corner in the library, spread the roster across a reading table, and began to study.

The names told a story.

The first victim had died three months ago. A girl named Lin Yue, found in her room with no visible injuries. The official cause was heart failure, but the roster had her crossed out with a note: *Qi depleted, 30% remaining.*

The second victim came two weeks later. A boy named Zhang Wei, found in the bathing house. *Qi depleted, 25% remaining.*

Then a third. A fourth. A fifth. Each one drained of more life qi than the last, as if the killer was getting better at extraction.

But the notes also revealed something else. Every victim had been a top scholar. Every victim had been in the same study group. Every victim had attended a private tutoring session with—

Bai Su's breath caught.

*Tutor: Wei Chen, Ghost Scholar. Specializing in Classical Texts.*

The name was familiar. She had seen it before, on a plaque outside the west wing. The murdered prodigy who tutored for extra incense.

Wei Chen. The ghost who had left her the note. The ghost who had warned her in the bell tower. The ghost who had disappeared when the bell started ringing.

She looked closer at the roster. Next to Wei Chen's name, there was a note in tiny, cramped handwriting: *Subject of investigation. Possible suspect in life qi theft. Do not approach without authorization.*

"Looking for something?"

Bai Su jumped. The ghost scholar stood behind her, his translucent form glowing faintly in the dim light. He was reading over her shoulder, his expression unreadable.

"You," she said, her voice sharp. "You're on this list."

"I'm aware." He sat down across from her, his movements fluid, almost graceful. "I'm also aware that the headmaster has been watching me for weeks. He thinks I'm the killer."

"Are you?"

Wei Chen laughed, a hollow sound that echoed in the empty library. "If I were, would I have warned you? Would I have told you to find the killer before they break through the veil?"

"You could be playing games."

"I could be." He leaned forward, his eyes locking onto hers. "But I'm not. I've been dead for seven years, Miss Bai. I've had plenty of time to come to terms with my situation. I don't want to become human again. I don't want to steal life qi from living students." He paused. "I want to move on. And I can't do that as long as this killer is turning the academy into a hunting ground."

Bai Su studied him. His face was earnest, his voice sincere. But ghosts were masters of deception. They had nothing left but words.

"Prove it," she said. "Give me something I can use."

Wei Chen considered for a moment, then reached into his robe and pulled out a folded piece of paper. He slid it across the table.

"This is a list of every student who attended my tutoring sessions in the past month. Cross-reference it with the victims. You'll find a pattern."

Bai Su unfolded the paper. It was covered in names, twenty in total. She compared it to the roster, her finger tracing down the columns.

Five names matched. The victims.

But there was a sixth name that appeared on both lists. A name that wasn't crossed out.

*Bai Su.*

She looked up, her heart pounding. "Why am I on here?"

"Because you attended my session three days ago." Wei Chen's voice was gentle, almost pitying. "You don't remember, do you?"

The blood on her hands. The footprint in the dust. The bell that rang without a ringer.

"I don't remember," she whispered.

"You came to me asking about the life qi drain. You said you wanted to help. I told you the same thing I told the others—be careful, trust no one, and watch your back." He sighed. "But you didn't listen. You went to the bell tower alone. You found something there. Something that made you forget."

"What did I find?"

"I don't know. You never told me." He stood, his form flickering. "But I know who can tell you. The ghost bell ringer. He saw everything."

"The bell ringer? But the tower was empty. The rope was still."

"Was it?" Wei Chen smiled, a sad, knowing smile. "The bell ringer doesn't need to touch the rope. He's been dead for fifty years. He's learned other ways to make things move."

He began to fade, his outline dissolving into the air.

"Find him, Miss Bai. Before the killer finds you."

"Wait—" She reached out, but her hand passed through empty space. The ghost was gone.

The library fell silent. The oil lamp flickered, casting long shadows across the walls. Bai Su looked down at the paper in her hands, at her own name written in neat, careful script.

She had been there. She had been part of this from the beginning.

And she couldn't remember any of it.

---

The bell tower loomed before her, dark and silent against the night sky. Bai Su stood at its base, the roster clutched in one hand, the paper in the other. The wind had picked up, carrying the scent of rain and something else—something old and musty, like a grave that had been recently disturbed.

She entered the tower.

The stairs creaked beneath her feet, each step sending a shudder through the old wood. The air grew colder as she climbed, the temperature dropping with every floor. By the time she reached the top, her breath was coming in white clouds.

The bell chamber was empty.

But the rope was moving.

It swayed gently, back and forth, as if pulled by invisible hands. The bell above it remained silent, but Bai Su could feel the vibrations in the floor, a low hum that resonated in her bones.

"You're looking for me."

The voice came from everywhere and nowhere. Bai Su turned, searching the shadows, but saw nothing.

"The bell ringer," she said. "I need to talk to you."

"You need to remember."

The rope stopped moving. The hum faded. In the silence that followed, Bai Su heard something else—a whisper, soft and urgent, like a secret being passed from one ghost to another.

"You were here before," the voice said. "You saw what happened."

"Tell me."

"Chen Feng didn't fall. He was pushed."

The words hit her like a physical blow. "Who pushed him?"

"Someone you trusted. Someone you followed into this tower." The voice grew sad, almost mournful. "Someone who is still here."

Bai Su's blood ran cold. She turned, slowly, and saw a figure standing in the doorway.

Wei Chen.

The ghost scholar smiled, but it wasn't the gentle smile from before. This smile was sharp, predatory, hungry.

"I'm sorry, Miss Bai," he said. "But I can't let you remember."

He stepped forward, and the temperature plummeted. Frost spread across the floor, crawling up the walls, encasing the bell in a layer of ice.

"You were right to suspect me," he said. "But you were wrong about one thing."

He reached out, his hand passing through her chest, cold and empty and full of death.

"I'm not just the killer."

His fingers closed around something inside her—something warm and bright and fragile.

"I'm the one who killed you."

Bai

title: The Song She Never Knew
content:
The headmaster's office had changed since her last visit.

Bai Su noticed it the moment she stepped through the door—the way the shadows clung to the corners like living things, the way the candlelight flickered without wind, the way the air itself seemed thicker, heavier, as if the room was holding its breath.

Headmaster Zhou sat behind his desk, his face drawn and pale. He looked older than she remembered, the lines around his eyes carved deep by something more than time.

"Close the door," he said.

She did.

"Sit."

She didn't.

"I'd rather stand, sir."

He didn't argue. Instead, he slid a scroll across the desk—yellowed silk, frayed at the edges, tied with a red cord that had faded to the color of dried blood. "Do you know what this is?"

Bai Su looked at it but didn't touch. "An examination scroll."

"Not just any scroll. This one was recovered from the tower last night. From the bell chamber." He paused, letting the words hang in the air. "It has your name on it."

Her blood went cold.

"That's impossible. I've never—"

"Taken the imperial exams? No. You haven't." He untied the cord and unrolled the silk. "But this scroll was submitted fifteen years ago. By a candidate named Bai Su."

The room tilted.

"That's not my name," she said carefully. "I mean, it is. But there must be hundreds of women named Bai Su in the empire. It's a common name. Like Li Wei or Zhang Mei."

"There are." He turned the scroll toward her. "But none of them wrote this."

She leaned forward, her eyes scanning the characters. Beautiful calligraphy. Flawless structure. The kind of writing that came from years of disciplined practice. She recognized the hand. Not because she had seen it before, but because it mirrored her own exactly—the same curl at the end of each stroke, the same slight tilt to the right, the same way of pressing harder on the final character of each line.

It was her handwriting.

"I don't understand," she whispered.

"Neither do I." Headmaster Zhou rolled the scroll back up, his movements slow and deliberate. "But I know this: fifteen years ago, a candidate named Bai Su took the imperial exams in this city. She was the top scholar in her district. Brilliant. Promising. And on the night before the final examination, she disappeared."

"What happened to her?"

"No one knows. She was never found. Her family searched for months. The authorities investigated. But she had simply... vanished." He looked at her with eyes that seemed to see through skin and bone. "And now, fifteen years later, a girl with the same name arrives at Wutong Academy. A girl with the same handwriting. A girl who can see ghosts."

Bai Su's throat tightened. "You're saying I'm her."

"I'm saying nothing. I'm showing you evidence and letting you draw your own conclusions."

"That's not fair."

"Neither is murder." He stood, his chair scraping against the floor. "Chen Feng is dead. Wang Li is missing. And a ghost is walking the halls of this academy, stealing life qi from the living. The connection is clear to me, if not to you."

"What connection?"

"Your name is attached to every piece of evidence. The bloodstained scroll. The bell chamber. The old examination records that were stolen from my office last week. Someone is setting you up to take the fall for all of it."

Bai Su's mind raced. "Or someone is trying to send me a message."

"Who?"

She thought of the ghost scholar in the bell chamber. The way he had known her name. The way he had spoken to her like she was an old friend she couldn't remember. "I don't know yet. But I'm going to find out."

She turned to leave.

"Miss Bai."

She stopped.

"Be careful. The dead have long memories. And some of them remember things that should have stayed forgotten."

She didn't answer. She walked out of the office and into a hallway that seemed darker than it had been moments before.

---

The library was empty when she arrived.

Not unusual for this hour—most students were in the dining hall, eating breakfast before morning lessons. But the silence felt different. Heavy. Waiting.

Bai Su walked to the back corner, where the ghost scholar had shown her his desk. The books were still there, stacked in the same precarious piles. The dust was undisturbed.

She knelt down, running her fingers along the floorboards. Nothing. She checked between the books, behind the shelves, under the desk. Nothing.

She was about to give up when she noticed something—a loose brick in the wall behind the desk. She pulled it out. Behind it was a small compartment, barely large enough to hold a single object.

Inside was a jade pendant.

She picked it up, her fingers tracing the carved characters. A name. Her name. But not in the script she used now. This was an older form, a style that had fallen out of use decades ago.

And on the back, a date.

Fifteen years ago. The day of her disappearance.

She slipped the pendant into her pocket and stood, her heart pounding. The ghost scholar had known. He had known all along. And he had led her here, to this discovery, as surely as a shepherd leads a lamb to slaughter.

"Why?"

The word echoed in the empty library, unanswered.

She left, her footsteps quick and determined. She needed to find him. She needed to make him tell her the truth.

But when she stepped outside, she found something else.

A crowd had gathered in the courtyard. Students and teachers alike, their faces turned toward the bell tower. Someone was pointing. Someone else was screaming.

Bai Su pushed through the crowd, her eyes fixed on the tower's highest window.

A body hung from the bell rope.

Not a student. A servant. The same servant girl who had come to fetch her that morning. Her neck was bent at an impossible angle, her face frozen in an expression of pure terror.

And around her neck, a strip of yellowed silk.

An examination scroll.

Bai Su's blood turned to ice.

"Get her down!" someone shouted. "Someone get her down!"

But no one moved. No one could. They were all staring at the same thing—the characters written on the silk, visible even from the ground.

A name.

Bai Su.

She felt hands on her shoulders, pulling her backward. She didn't resist. She couldn't. Her eyes were locked on the servant's dead face, on the scroll that had become a noose, on the message that was clear to everyone now.

Someone was framing her for murder.

And they were doing it with her own name.

---

The headmaster's office was chaos.

Teachers shouted over each other, demanding answers, demanding action. Headmaster Zhou sat behind his desk, his face unreadable. And Bai Su stood in the center of it all, the jade pendant warm against her chest, the weight of a crime she didn't commit pressing down on her shoulders.

"This is absurd," one teacher said, a thin man with a mustache that quivered with indignation. "We cannot have a murderer walking among our students. We must expel her immediately."

"We must do more than that," another added. "We must hand her over to the authorities. Let them deal with this."

"Enough." Headmaster Zhou's voice cut through the noise like a blade. "Miss Bai is not leaving this academy until we have the truth."

"How can you be so certain she's innocent?"

"Because I know who the real killer is."

The room went silent.

Bai Su looked up, her heart in her throat. "You do?"

Headmaster Zhou nodded slowly. "I've known for three days. I've been waiting for the right moment to act."

"Then why didn't you—"

"Because I needed proof. And now I have it." He reached into his desk and pulled out a second scroll. This one was new, the ink still fresh. "This was found in the servant's quarters this morning. A confession."

"A confession from whom?"

He unrolled the scroll. The room leaned in.

The characters were beautiful. Flawless. The same hand as the scroll in the bell chamber. The same hand as Bai Su's own.

But the name at the bottom was different.

It read: *The Ghost Scholar of Wutong Academy.*

"Impossible," someone whispered.

"The ghost wrote a confession?"

"Ghosts can't write."

"This one can." Headmaster Zhou's voice was flat. "This one has been writing for fifteen years. Writing and waiting. Writing and planning. And now, it's finally ready to reveal itself."

Bai Su's mind reeled. The ghost scholar. The boy in the bell chamber. He had been writing all along. He had been framing her. He had been—

No.

She stopped herself.

Something was wrong. The timeline didn't match. The ghost scholar had warned her about the killer. He had shown her the bell chamber. He had been trying to help her, not hurt her.

Unless that was part of the plan.

Unless everything was part of the plan.

"The confession says the ghost will strike again tonight," Headmaster Zhou continued. "At midnight. In the main examination hall. It says it will take its final victim—the one it needs to complete its transformation."

"Who?" the thin teacher asked. "Who is the final victim?"

Headmaster Zhou's eyes met Bai Su's.

"You."

The word hit her like a physical blow.

"Me?"

"You are the one it has been waiting for. The one with the same name. The one with the same handwriting. The one who can see ghosts." He paused. "The one who was supposed to die fifteen years ago."

The room erupted into noise, but Bai Su didn't hear any of it. She was staring at the scroll, at the beautiful calligraphy, at the words that spelled her doom.

And somewhere, deep in the back of her mind, a voice began to sing.

An old folk song. Sweet and familiar.

Like something she had known before she was born.

She opened her mouth to speak, but the song grew louder, drowning out everything else. The room faded. The teachers disappeared. The headmaster's office dissolved into shadow.

And in the darkness, a figure emerged.

A girl. Young. Dressed in the robes of an imperial candidate.

Her face was Bai Su's face.

Her smile was Bai Su's smile.

And her eyes were empty, hollow sockets that stared into Bai Su's soul.

"Remember me?" the girl asked.

Bai Su tried to scream.

No sound came out.

The girl reached out, her fingers brushing Bai Su's cheek.

"Don't worry," she whispered. "You will."

And the darkness swallowed everything.

title: Chapter 15
content:
CHAPTER 15: The Memory in the Ink

The headmaster's study smelled of old paper and secrets.

Bai Su stood before the massive rosewood desk, her hands clasped behind her back to hide their trembling. The blood was gone from her palms—had it ever truly been there?—but she could still feel its ghostly warmth, like a memory her skin refused to forget.

Headmaster Wei sat across from her, his fingers steepled beneath his chin. He was a thin man with a scholar's stoop and eyes that missed nothing. Three other teachers flanked him: Old Chen from the library, whose right hand never stopped moving as if he were still filing scrolls; Madame Liang, who taught calligraphy with a brush that never touched paper; and a young man Bai Su didn't recognize, dressed in black robes that seemed to drink the candlelight.

"You've been busy, Miss Bai," Headmaster Wei said. His voice was calm, almost pleasant. "The bell chamber. Chen Feng's body. The ghost scholar in the west wing. You've covered more ground in three days than most students cover in three months."

"I was curious."

"Curiosity killed the scholar." The young man in black spoke without moving his lips. His voice seemed to come from everywhere and nowhere at once. "Though in your case, I suspect it might be the other way around."

Bai Su met his gaze. His eyes were wrong—not the color, which was a perfectly ordinary brown, but the way they reflected the candlelight. They should have glowed, caught the flame, done something. Instead, they absorbed light like ink absorbing water.

"And you are?"

"Someone who wishes you would stop poking your nose into matters you don't understand." He stood and walked around the desk, his movements too smooth, too fluid. "But since you've already started, I suppose we have no choice but to include you."

"Include me in what?"

"The investigation." Headmaster Wei pulled a scroll from his sleeve and unrolled it across the desk. It was covered in names, dates, and symbols Bai Su didn't recognize. "You've met the ghost scholar. You've seen the bell chamber. You've even touched the blood of a murdered boy, though I suspect you don't remember doing so."

Bai Su's blood ran cold. "How do you know about that?"

"Because I was there." The young man in black stopped in front of her, close enough that she could smell sandalwood and something else—something old and damp, like a grave freshly opened. "I've been watching you since the moment you arrived. I was in the courtyard when Chen Feng fell. I was in the bell chamber when you touched that window. And I was in your room last night, while you slept."

Bai Su's hand shot to her chest, where she kept a small jade pendant her mother had given her. It was warm. It should have been cold if a ghost had touched it.

"I'm not a ghost," the young man said, as if reading her thoughts. "I'm something worse. I'm the one who keeps the ghosts in line."

"The Ghost Warden," Madame Liang said, her brush-stroke hand finally stilling. "He oversees the boundary between the living and the dead within these walls. And he believes you've crossed it."

"Crossed it how?"

The Ghost Warden reached into his robes and pulled out a scroll. It was old, the paper yellowed and cracked, the ink faded to a pale brown. He unrolled it slowly, deliberately, as if revealing something sacred.

"This is the enrollment record from thirty years ago," he said. "The year of the Great Examination. The year the Wutong Academy had its first—and only—mass casualty event."

Bai Su leaned forward. The scroll was filled with names, each one marked with a date of enrollment and a date of... she squinted. The characters were strange, almost illegible.

"Date of death," the Ghost Warden said. "Twenty-three students died that year. Twenty-three souls trapped between worlds, unable to move on. They became the first ghosts of this academy."

"And you think I'm one of them?"

The Ghost Warden's lips curved into something that wasn't quite a smile. "No. I think you're something far more interesting." He pointed to a name near the bottom of the scroll. "Look."

Bai Su looked. The name was written in characters so small she had to squint to read them. But when she did, her heart stopped.

Bai Su.

Not Bai Su from this year. Bai Su from thirty years ago. The same characters, the same stroke order, the same name she had written a hundred times in her calligraphy practice.

"That's impossible," she whispered. "I wasn't born yet."

"Were you?" The Ghost Warden's eyes finally caught the light, and for just a moment, they glowed with an eerie amber fire. "Or have you simply forgotten?"

Headmaster Wei cleared his throat. "Miss Bai, we brought you here because we believe you may be the key to solving this mystery. The killings—both the living and the dead—they all trace back to that year. To that examination. To whatever happened in this academy thirty years ago."

"And you want me to investigate?"

"We want you to remember."

The Ghost Warden rolled up the scroll and tucked it back into his robes. "There's a memory locked in your blood, Miss Bai. A memory that's been waiting thirty years to surface. The bell chamber triggered it. Chen Feng's death accelerated it. And soon, very soon, it will break through completely."

"What happens when it does?"

"You'll either save this academy or destroy it." He turned away, his black robes swirling like smoke. "Personally, I'm hoping for the former. But I've learned not to hope."

Bai Su stood frozen, her mind racing. She wanted to deny everything—to scream that she was just a normal student from a normal village with a normal life. But the memory of the bell chamber clawed at her thoughts. The footprint that matched her shoe. The blood on her hands. The ghost scholar's knowing eyes.

"I need proof," she said finally. "Before I believe any of this."

The Ghost Warden paused at the door. "Proof." He laughed, a sound like dry leaves scraping stone. "Very well. Come with me."

He led her through the winding corridors of the academy, past classrooms where ghost students recited poetry they had memorized in life, past dormitories where living students slept fitfully, their dreams haunted by voices they couldn't quite hear. Finally, they stopped at a door Bai Su had never noticed before, hidden behind a tapestry of a phoenix rising from flames.

"The old archives," the Ghost Warden said. "Where the academy keeps its secrets."

He pushed open the door. The room beyond was vast, filled with shelves that stretched into darkness. Scrolls lined every surface, stacked in precarious towers, spilling across tables and chairs. The air was thick with dust and the smell of decay.

"In the back," he said, "you'll find a chest. Iron-bound, with a lock that has no key. Inside that chest is a journal. The journal of a student who died thirty years ago."

"Whose journal?"

"Yours."

Bai Su's breath caught. She wanted to argue, to demand explanations, but the Ghost Warden had already turned and was walking away, his voice echoing behind him.

"Find it. Read it. And then decide whether you still believe you're just a normal student from a normal village."

The door slammed shut, leaving Bai Su alone in the darkness.

She stood for a long moment, her heart pounding in her ears. Then, slowly, she began to walk. The shelves loomed around her, their contents whispering with the weight of centuries. She passed scrolls on medicine, on astronomy, on the art of war. She passed maps of lands that no longer existed, diagrams of buildings that had crumbled to dust.

And then she saw it.

A chest, iron-bound, sitting on a pedestal at the very back of the room. It was small, no larger than a shoe box, but it radiated a cold that made the air around it frost over. There was no lock, just as the Ghost Warden had said, but the lid was sealed with a strip of paper covered in characters that glowed with a faint, sickly light.

Bai Su reached out. Her fingers brushed the paper.

The glow flared, then died. The paper crumbled to ash.

The lid swung open.

Inside lay a journal, bound in leather so old it had turned black. The pages were yellowed, the ink faded, but the handwriting was unmistakable.

Her handwriting.

She picked it up. The leather was cold, almost frozen, but it warmed in her hands as if recognizing its owner. She opened it to the first page.

*Year of the Serpent, Seventh Month, First Day.*

*I have decided to keep a record. A record of what I see, what I hear, what I suspect. The academy is not what it seems. The teachers are not what they claim. And the students... the students are disappearing.*

*Not dying. Disappearing. As if they were never here at all.*

*I will find out why.*

*I will find out who.*

*And if I die trying, at least someone will know the truth.*

Bai Su's hands trembled. The handwriting was hers. Every stroke, every curve, every flourish. She had written this. But she had no memory of it. No memory of this room, this journal, this life.

She turned the page.

*Year of the Serpent, Seventh Month, Third Day.*

*I met a boy today. A ghost. He told me things I don't want to believe. He told me that the academy was built on a grave. Not one grave. Hundreds. Thousands. The ground beneath our feet is filled with the bones of scholars who never made it to the examination hall.*

*He told me that the ghosts aren't trapped here by accident.*

*They're trapped here on purpose.*

Bai Su's blood ran cold. She turned another page. And another. Each entry was longer than the last, the handwriting growing more frantic, more desperate.

*Year of the Serpent, Seventh Month, Seventh Day.*

*I know now. I know what the teachers are doing. I know why the students disappear. I know what happens to the ones who survive.*

*I have to warn the others.*

*But how do you warn someone when the truth sounds like madness?*

Bai Su flipped to the last entry. The ink was fresh, almost wet, as if it had been written moments ago.

*Year of the Serpent, Seventh Month, Tenth Day.*

*They know I've found out.*

*They're coming for me.*

*But I've prepared. I've hidden the truth where no one will find it. In the bell chamber. In the window. In the blood of the boy who will die thirty years from now.*

*If you're reading this, Bai Su, remember:*

*You are not the first person to discover the academy's secret.*

*You are not the first person to try to stop it.*

*And you will not be the last.*

*Because the dead do not stay dead.*

*And neither will you.*

The journal fell from her hands. Bai Su stumbled backward, her mind reeling, her heart screaming denials she could no longer believe.

The Ghost Warden's voice echoed from somewhere above her, soft and mocking.

"Remember now?"

Bai Su looked down at her hands. They were covered in blood again. Fresh blood, warm and wet, dripping onto the floor in a steady rhythm.

But this time, she knew whose blood it was.

Hers.

She looked up. The shelves around her had changed. The scrolls were gone. In their place were mirrors.

title: The Song Before the Fall
content:
The headmaster's study smelled of old paper and newer lies.

Bai Su recognized the scent now—the way fresh ink tried to cover something older, something that had been written and then erased, written and erased, until the page was too thin to hold anything at all. The headmaster sat behind his desk, his hands folded, his face arranged into an expression of paternal concern that didn't quite reach his eyes.

"Miss Bai." He gestured to the chair across from him. "Please. Sit."

She didn't sit. She stood with her back to the door, her hands still tingling with the memory of blood that wasn't there. "You said it was urgent."

"It is." He opened a drawer and withdrew a scroll, yellowed with age. "I found something in the archives. Something that concerns you."

"Concerns me how?"

"Concerns your presence here." He unrolled the scroll carefully, as if it might crumble at a touch. "Concerns the reason you were admitted to Wutong Academy in the first place."

Bai Su's blood went cold in a different way now—not fear, but recognition. She had known, somehow, that this moment would come. That the academy's perfect record, its 100% exam pass rate, its willingness to accept a girl from a village that had no scholars, no money, no connections—that all of it had been a trap waiting to snap shut.

"I don't understand," she said, though she understood perfectly.

"Don't you?" The headmaster's eyes were kind. They were also empty. "You see ghosts, Miss Bai. You always have. Did you think that was an accident?"

"I was born with it."

"No." He shook his head slowly. "You were made with it."

The words hung in the air between them, heavy as the bell that had rung nine times in the empty tower. Bai Su felt the floor shift beneath her feet, felt the world tilt sideways into something she didn't recognize.

"What do you mean?"

The headmaster didn't answer. Instead, he turned the scroll around so she could see it. The characters were old, written in a style that hadn't been used in centuries. But she could read them. Somehow, she could read them as easily as if they were written in her own hand.

*The Binding of the Living Veil. Ritual of the Ninth Scholar.*

Below the title was a drawing. A child. A circle of candles. A shadow that stretched in the wrong direction, reaching not away from the light but toward it.

"This ritual," the headmaster said, tapping the drawing with one finger, "was performed eighteen years ago. In a village called Luojia, three days' travel from here. The purpose was to create a vessel—a child who could see the dead, who could walk between worlds, who could be used as a key."

"A key to what?"

"The academy." He rolled the scroll back up. "Wutong Academy sits on a convergence of death energy. Every student who has died here, every ghost that haunts these halls, every soul that failed to pass on—they all feed into something. A barrier. A lock. And the lock needs a key."

Bai Su's throat was dry. "You're saying I'm that key."

"I'm saying you were made to be that key." He stood, and for the first time, she saw something like sorrow in his face. "The ritual was supposed to be completed on your eighteenth birthday. The barrier would open. The dead would flood into the living world, and the living would be consumed."

"But it didn't happen."

"No." He walked to the window, looked out at the courtyard where Chen Feng had fallen. "Because the scholar who performed the ritual died before he could finish it. The binding was left incomplete. You were left... incomplete."

Bai Su thought of the ghost girl in the dreams. Of the song that followed her through the halls. Of the voice that felt familiar, like something she had known before she was born.

"Who was he?" she asked. "The scholar who performed the ritual?"

The headmaster was silent for a long moment. When he spoke, his voice was barely a whisper.

"His name was Lin Chen. He was my brother."

---

The revelation hit Bai Su like a physical blow. She staggered back a step, her hand finding the doorframe for support.

"Your brother."

"My younger brother." The headmaster's voice was flat, emptied of emotion. "He was the most gifted scholar of his generation. He could see the dead, speak with them, command them. He believed that death was not an end but a transformation—that the living and the dead were meant to coexist, to merge, to become something greater."

"And the ritual was supposed to make that happen."

"Yes." He turned to face her. "But Lin Chen was not a patient man. He saw the barrier as an obstacle, not a protection. He believed that forcing the convergence would accelerate human evolution, that the dead would bring knowledge and power that the living had lost."

"Instead, he died."

"Instead, he died." The headmaster's jaw tightened. "The ritual required a sacrifice. A life for a life. Lin Chen offered himself—but the ritual was flawed. It took his body but left his spirit trapped, bound to the academy, unable to move on."

Bai Su's mind raced. "The ghost I saw in the bell chamber. The one who told me about the life qi. That was him."

"Yes."

"He's been here all along. Watching. Waiting."

"Yes."

"And now someone is killing students to transfer life qi. To complete the ritual that he started."

The headmaster's silence was answer enough.

"You knew." Bai Su's voice rose. "You knew all of this, and you let me come here. You let me walk into a trap."

"I had no choice." His voice cracked. "The barrier is weakening. It has been weakening for years. If the ritual is not completed—if a proper key is not used—the barrier will shatter on its own. And when it does, the flood will be uncontrolled. Millions will die."

"So you let me be the sacrifice instead."

"I let you have a choice." He reached into his drawer again, pulled out a second scroll. This one was newer, the ink still fresh. "This is a counter-ritual. It will sever the binding, release you from the spell that was placed on you as a child. You will lose your ability to see ghosts, but you will survive. You will be free."

"And the academy?"

"Will fall." He said it simply, without hesitation. "Without a key, the barrier will collapse. The dead will pour into the living world. But you will be far away, living a normal life, unable to see what you have escaped."
