Bai Su stared at the scroll. She thought of the ghost students she had met—the drowned scholar in the library, the suicide candidate in the west wing, the murdered prodigy who tutored for extra incense. She thought of their faces, their voices, their desperate hope that they might one day pass the exams and move on.

She thought of the killer. The ghost who was murdering living students, stealing their qi, trying to break through the veil.

"If I take the ritual," she said slowly, "the killer wins. They'll find another way. They'll keep killing until they have enough qi to break through on their own."

"That is likely."

"And if I don't take it?"

"Then you remain the key. And the killer will come for you." He met her eyes. "Because you are not just any key. You are the only key. If you die, the ritual dies with you. The barrier remains. The academy survives."

"So either I die, or I run, or I let the killer use me to open the door."

"Yes."

Bai Su took the scroll. She unrolled it, read the characters, felt the weight of the words settling into her bones.

The counter-ritual was simple. Too simple. A few words, a candle, a drop of blood. She could perform it tonight, in her room, alone. And by morning, she would be blind to the ghosts that haunted the academy. She would be normal. She would be safe.

She would also be a coward.

"The killer," she said. "Who is it?"

The headmaster's face twisted. "I don't know."

"You don't know, or you won't tell me?"

"I don't know." He slammed his hand on the desk. "Do you think I haven't tried to find out? Do you think I haven't searched every corner of this academy, interrogated every ghost, read every record? The killer is clever. They leave no trace. They take only enough qi to stay hidden, to grow stronger, to wait."

"Wait for what?"

"For the right moment." He sat down heavily. "For the barrier to weaken enough that a single push will break it. For you."

Bai Su rolled the scroll back up and tucked it into her sleeve.

"I need time to think."

"You don't have much time." The headmaster's voice was tired. "The killer has taken three students in the past week. They're getting bolder. They're getting stronger. And they know who you are."

"How do you know that?"

"Because they left this for you."

He slid a piece of paper across the desk. It was folded neatly, addressed to her in elegant calligraphy.

*Miss Bai Su,*

*I have been watching you. I know what you are. I know what you were made for.*

*Meet me in the library at midnight. Come alone.*

*If you don't, I will take another student. And another. And another.*

*Until you do.*

*Your fellow scholar,*
*The One Who Remembers*

Bai Su read the note twice. Then she folded it carefully and placed it next to the scroll in her sleeve.

"Midnight," she said. "The library."

"Don't go." The headmaster stood. "It's a trap. It's obviously a trap."

"Of course it's a trap." She smiled, and it felt strange on her face, like wearing a mask that didn't fit. "But if I'm going to be the key, I might as well find out who's trying to turn the lock."

She turned and walked out of the study, leaving the headmaster standing alone in the lamplight.

---

The library was dark when she arrived.

Bai Su had expected candles, shadows, the rustle of pages turning themselves. Instead, there was only silence. The shelves loomed around her like frozen waves, the books like stones in a river that had stopped flowing.

"Hello?"

Her voice echoed, bounced off the high ceiling, returned to her smaller than it had left.

"Hello, Bai Su."

The voice came from everywhere and nowhere. It was sweet, familiar, like a song she had heard in a dream.

"You came."

"Of course I came." She walked deeper into the library, her footsteps loud in the stillness. "You threatened to kill more students."

"And you believed me."

"Shouldn't I have?"

A pause. Then a laugh—soft, musical, genuinely amused.

"No. You shouldn't have. Because I'm not going to kill anyone else."

The candles on the nearest shelf flickered to life, one by one, illuminating a figure standing at the end of the aisle.

It was a girl. Young, perhaps sixteen, with long black hair and a face that was both beautiful and terrible. She wore the same academy robes as Bai Su, but they were stained with something dark, something that dripped onto the floor and evaporated before it touched the ground.

"You." Bai Su's breath caught. "You're the ghost from my dreams."

"I am." The girl smiled. "And you're the key I've been waiting for."

"I thought you

title: Chapter 17
content:
CHAPTER 17: The Song Before the Fall

The headmaster's office smelled of sandalwood and secrets.

Bai Su stood before the massive rosewood desk, her hands clasped behind her back to hide their trembling. The headmaster, a man so old his wrinkles had wrinkles, sat motionless as a statue, his fingers steepled beneath his chin.

"You visited the bell tower," he said. Not a question.

"I heard a bell ringing."

"There is no bell ringer at Wutong Academy." His voice carried no accusation, only a weary certainty. "The bell tower has been sealed for forty years. Ever since the incident."

Bai Su's throat tightened. "What incident?"

The headmaster rose, his joints cracking like dry twigs. He shuffled to the window, looking out at the courtyard where Chen Feng had fallen—where she had watched him fall from a tower she apparently had no memory of entering.

"A student died there," he said. "A girl. She was preparing for the imperial examinations. The most brilliant scholar the academy had ever seen." He paused. "She threw herself from the bell tower three days before the exams."

"Why?"

"Because she discovered something she should not have." The headmaster turned, his eyes like chips of obsidian. "She learned that the boundary between the living and the dead at this academy is not a wall, Miss Bai. It is a door. And someone had left it open."

Bai Su's blood ran cold. "The ghost scholar told me—"

"There is no ghost scholar."

"I saw him. I spoke to him. He knew about the qi transfer, about the killer—"

"You saw what you were meant to see." The headmaster returned to his desk, sliding open a drawer. He withdrew a scroll, yellowed with age, and unrolled it across the desk. "Look."

Bai Su stepped forward. The scroll was a student roster from forty years ago. Names, dates, examination results. Her eyes scanned the list, searching for something she didn't understand.

Then she saw it.

Halfway down the page, a name written in elegant calligraphy:

*Bai Su.*

Her name. Her exact name. With a date of death recorded beside it.

"That's impossible," she whispered.

"Is it?" The headmaster's voice was soft, almost kind. "You arrived at Wutong Academy with no family records, no previous school documentation, no one to vouch for your existence. You simply appeared one day, claiming to have been admitted."

"I received a letter—"

"We send no letters." He folded his hands. "The dead do not receive correspondence, Miss Bai. They simply... arrive."

Bai Su's mind reeled. She remembered the letter, perfectly composed, bearing the academy's seal. She remembered packing her bags, saying goodbye to a mother whose face she could no longer recall, traveling to a school she had never heard of until the letter arrived.

She remembered nothing before the letter.

*Nothing.*

"I'm not dead," she said, but her voice cracked on the word.

"Perhaps not entirely." The headmaster gestured to a chair, and Bai Su's legs gave way beneath her. "You have a body. You cast a shadow. You breathe. But you also see what others cannot. You hear the bell that rings for no living ear. You stood in a tower that no living student has entered in forty years."

He leaned forward, his ancient eyes boring into hers.

"You are caught between, Miss Bai. Neither fully alive nor fully dead. A bridge between worlds." He paused. "And someone has been using you."

"Using me for what?"

"To open the door permanently."

The words hit her like a physical blow. She thought of the ghost scholar—his translucent skin, his knowing smile, the way he had appeared exactly when she needed answers. She thought of the blood on her hands, the footprint that matched her shoe, the song she had heard on the stairs.

*Sweet and familiar. Like something she had known before she was born.*

"That song," she said suddenly. "The one I heard in the bell tower. It's a nursery rhyme, isn't it?"

The headmaster's face went pale. "Describe it."

Bai Su hummed the melody, the notes coming to her lips as if she had sung them a thousand times. The headmaster's hands began to shake.

"Where did you learn that?" he demanded.

"I don't know. It just... came to me."

"That song was written by the girl who died. She composed it the night before her death. Only she knew the melody."

The floor seemed to tilt beneath Bai Su's feet. "But I heard it. In the tower. A voice sang it to me."

"A voice," the headmaster repeated slowly, "or your own memory?"

The implication hung in the air like smoke.

*I am not Bai Su.*

The thought surfaced unbidden, and she realized with horror that she didn't know if it was true. She didn't know who she was. She didn't know what she was. She only knew that she was sitting in a headmaster's office, forty years after a girl with her name had thrown herself from a bell tower, and the song was still echoing in her skull.

"Who was she?" Bai Su asked. "The girl who died?"

The headmaster opened his mouth to answer, but a scream cut him off.

It came from outside, sharp and terrified, cutting through the evening air like a blade. Bai Su was on her feet before she knew she had moved, running through the door and down the corridor, her heart hammering against her ribs.

The courtyard was chaos.

Students clustered in a terrified circle, their faces pale in the lantern light. In the center lay a body—a living student, one of the seniors, his eyes wide and staring at nothing. His skin was gray, his lips blue, and his chest did not rise.

But there was no blood. No wound. No sign of violence.

He looked exactly like Chen Feng had looked before he fell.

"Stand back!" Professor Liang pushed through the crowd, his face ashen. "Everyone, get back to your dormitories. Now!"

The students scattered, leaving Bai Su alone with the body and the professor.

"This is the third one," Liang said, his voice barely a whisper. "Three living students in as many days."

"Three?" Bai Su's mind raced. "Chen Feng was the first. Who else?"

"The kitchen boy. Found this morning in the pantry. And now..." He gestured at the body. "This is escalating. The killer is growing bolder."

"It's not the killer." The words left Bai Su's mouth before she could stop them. "It's the ghost. The one who's taking their qi."

Liang's eyes widened. "You know about the qi transfer?"

"I know that someone—something—is trying to break through the veil. I know that every death brings them closer." She looked at the dead student, at his peaceful expression, as if he had simply fallen asleep. "And I know that I've been inside the bell tower."

"The bell tower is sealed."

"I was there. I saw the glass. I heard the bell." She paused. "And I heard a song. The song of a girl who died forty years ago."

Liang's face drained of color. "You couldn't have. That song is—"

"Known only to the dead girl. I know." Bai Su met his gaze. "Which means either I'm dead, or I'm her."

She expected denial. She expected horror. Instead, Liang did something she didn't expect.

He laughed.

A hollow, broken sound that echoed through the empty courtyard.

"You really don't know, do you?" He shook his head, his eyes glistening. "You're not her, Miss Bai. You're not a ghost, and you're not exactly alive." He stepped closer, his voice dropping to a conspiratorial whisper. "You're a remnant."

"A what?"

"A piece of someone's soul. A fragment that broke off when the girl died, imbued with enough life to walk and talk and think, but not enough to truly live." He gestured at her. "That's why you have no past. That's why you appeared out of nowhere. You were created, Miss Bai. Purpose-built for a specific task."

"What task?"

"You were meant to open the door."

The world tilted. Bai Su felt herself falling, but Liang caught her arm, his grip surprisingly strong.

"The ghost scholar," she managed. "The one who's been helping me—"

"Is the one who created you."

The words hit her like a physical blow. She thought of all their conversations, all his cryptic hints and careful guidance. He hadn't been helping her solve the mystery.

He had been making sure she followed the right path.

"Where is he now?" she demanded.

"I don't know. But I know where he'll be tonight." Liang's eyes were grim. "The bell tower. At midnight. That's when the veil is thinnest. That's when he'll try to complete the transfer."

"Then I'll be there."

"You'll die."

"I'm already not alive." Bai Su pulled her arm free. "What's the worst that could happen?"

She turned and walked away before he could answer, her footsteps echoing through the empty courtyard. Behind her, the body lay still, a silent witness to the beginning of the end.

The bell tower loomed ahead, dark and silent.

But as Bai Su approached, she heard it again.

The song.

Sweet and familiar, rising from the tower like smoke from a funeral pyre.

And this time, she knew the words.

*Little scholar, little scholar, why do you weep?*
*The moon is full, the night is deep.*
*Little scholar, little scholar, where do you go?*
*Down to the river, where the willows grow.*

Bai Su stopped at the base of the tower, her hand on the cold stone.

The song stopped.

And a voice, smooth as silk, spoke from the darkness above.

"You remembered."

Bai Su looked up. The ghost scholar stood at the broken window, his translucent form glowing in the moonlight.

"I know what you are," she said.

"Do you?" He smiled, a terrible, beautiful smile. "I was wondering when you would figure it out."

"You created me."

"I found you." He floated down, landing silently beside her. "You were a shattered piece of a broken soul, drifting in the void between worlds. I gave you form. Purpose."

"You gave me lies."

"I gave you a chance to exist." His eyes met hers, ancient and cold. "But all things have a price, Bai Su. Even borrowed life."

"What do you want from me?"

"You already know." He reached out, his fingers brushing her cheek. They were cold, so cold, like touching winter itself. "I want what was taken from me. A second chance. A heartbeat. A life."

"You're the girl," Bai Su whispered. "The one who jumped."

"I am the one who was pushed." His voice hardened. "I discovered the truth about this academy—that it feeds on the life of its students, that the dead are kept here to power a ritual that has been running for centuries. I tried to expose it, and they silenced me."

"Who?"

"The headmaster. The professors. Everyone who profits from the dead." He gestured at the tower. "They threw me from this window and called it suicide. They sealed the tower and pretended I never existed."

"But you survived."

"I am a ghost, Bai Su. I survived because I refused to die." His hand dropped. "And now I am going to live again. With your help."

"Never."

"Too late." He smiled. "You've already helped. Every step you've taken, every clue you've followed, every piece of the puzzle you've assembled—you've been drawing the ritual circle for me. You've been gathering the

title: Chapter 18
content:
CHAPTER 18: The Song Before Drowning

The headmaster's office smelled of burning paper and dried plums. Bai Su stood before the massive oak desk, her hands still trembling despite having scrubbed them raw in the courtyard well. The phantom blood was gone, but she could still feel it—a sticky memory that clung to her palms like second skin.

Headmaster Zhou sat motionless, his fingers steepled beneath his chin. Behind him, the window faced the eastern courtyard, where the cherry tree had begun to bloom out of season. White petals drifted past the glass like falling snow in spring.

"You visited the bell tower," he said. Not a question.

"Yes."

"Alone."

"I thought—"

"You thought incorrectly." He rose from his chair with a creak of old bones. For a moment, Bai Su saw him differently—not as the stern administrator who presided over examinations and discipline, but as a man who carried something heavy in his chest. Something that had been there a long time. "The bell tower has been sealed for thirty years. Do you know why?"

"I found a ghost. A scholar. He said his name was—"

"I know who he is." The headmaster's voice cracked. Just slightly. A hairline fracture in the mask of authority. "I knew him when he was alive."

Bai Su's breath caught. "He was your student?"

"He was my son."

The words hung in the air like smoke. Bai Su opened her mouth, closed it, opened it again. Nothing came out. She thought of the boy in the tower—his translucent skin, his hollow eyes, the way he had spoken of falling. Of the blood on her hands that had vanished like morning frost.

"His name was Zhou Liang," the headmaster continued, his gaze fixed somewhere beyond the window. "He was the brightest student I ever taught. He would have been the top scholar of his year, perhaps of the entire province. But three days before the examinations, he climbed the bell tower and jumped."

"Why?"

"He left a note. Said he couldn't bear the pressure. Said he was sorry." The headmaster's laugh was bitter, broken. "I didn't believe it then. I don't believe it now. My son was not a boy who broke under pressure. He was a boy who broke under *something else*. Something he never told me."

Bai Su stepped forward, her voice barely a whisper. "The shadow in the bell chamber. The song. Do you know what it means?"

The headmaster's face went pale. For a moment, Bai Su saw fear in his eyes—genuine, primal fear that had nothing to do with anger or authority.

"What song?"

"An old folk song. I heard it on the stairs. A woman's voice, singing about..." She paused, trying to remember. "About water. About sinking. About a bride who drowned on her wedding day."

The headmaster stumbled backward, knocking over his chair. He clutched the edge of his desk, his knuckles white.

"That song," he whispered, "has not been sung in this academy since the night my son died."

---

The library was empty when Bai Su arrived. The candles had burned low, their wicks drowning in pools of melted wax. She moved through the stacks with purpose, her fingers trailing across spines of books that had not been touched in decades.

She needed answers. The headmaster had refused to say more, had ordered her to return to her dormitory and forget what she had seen. But forgetting was no longer an option. The blood on her hands, the footprint that matched her shoe, the song that followed her like a ghost—these were threads she had to pull, no matter what unraveled.

*"You're looking in the wrong places."*

Bai Su spun around. The drowned scholar sat at a reading table, his wet hair dripping onto a stack of ancient scrolls. Water pooled around him, spreading across the floor in a slow, deliberate circle.

"I thought you only haunted the library," Bai Su said, her heart hammering.

"I follow interesting things." He smiled, and water dripped from the corners of his mouth. "You are the most interesting thing to happen to this academy in three decades."

"What do you know about the song?"

"More than you, less than I should." He gestured to the chair across from him. "Sit. I'll tell you a story."

Bai Su hesitated, then sat. The chair was cold, as if she had sat down on a block of ice.

"Thirty years ago," the drowned scholar began, "there was a girl. A servant girl, beautiful and clever, who worked in the headmaster's household. She fell in love with his son, the brilliant Zhou Liang. And he, against all reason and station, fell in love with her."

"They were lovers?"

"They were *everything*." The drowned scholar's eyes grew distant. "But the headmaster disapproved. He had plans for his son—a prestigious marriage, a political alliance, a future that did not include a servant girl. So he sent her away. Bought her passage on a boat to the southern provinces, where she would be married to a merchant twice her age."

Bai Su felt a chill that had nothing to do with the temperature. "The boat sank."

"Three days before Zhou Liang's examinations. The same day he jumped from the bell tower." The drowned scholar leaned forward, his wet breath misting against her face. "Do you see the pattern, Miss Bai? The song she sang was a wedding song. A drowning song. A song she sang as the water filled her lungs."

"But Zhou Liang killed himself. He left a note."

"He left a note that *someone else wrote*. He was pushed, Miss Bai. Not by hands, but by grief. By guilt. By a love that followed him even in death."

Bai Su's mind raced. "The shadow in the bell tower. The whisper on the stairs. Is it her?"

The drowned scholar's smile faded. "It is not her. She is gone, truly gone, as the dead should be. But something else has taken her song. Something that wears her voice like a mask."

"What?"

"I don't know. But I know this—it has been feeding. For thirty years, it has been feeding on the life qi of students who wander too close to the bell tower. Chen Feng was not the first. He was simply the first to die."

"There have been others?"

"Twelve. One every few years. Always written off as accidents, suicides, illnesses." He counted on his fingers, each one dripping water onto the table. "The academy's perfect record is a lie, Miss Bai. A beautiful, bloody lie."

Bai Su stood up, her chair scraping against the floor. "I need to see the records. The old incident reports."

"They're locked in the headmaster's office."

"Then that's where I'm going."

"Miss Bai." The drowned scholar's voice stopped her at the door. "Be careful. The thing that sings that song knows you now. It has marked you. I can see it—a thread of shadow wrapped around your heart."

Bai Su touched her chest. She felt nothing. But she remembered the blood on her hands, the footprint in the dust, the voice that had whispered her name in the dark.

"Thank you," she said, and left.

---

The headmaster's office was dark. Bai Su slipped through the door, her footsteps silent on the wooden floor. The moonlight streamed through the window, casting long shadows across the desk, the chairs, the shelves of books.

She found the records in a locked cabinet behind the headmaster's desk. The lock was old, rusted, and yielded easily to a hairpin. Inside, twelve scrolls bound with red string, each one marked with a date and a name.

She unrolled the first.

*Student Name: Wei Chen. Cause of Death: Drowning. Notes: Found in the eastern pond. Ruled accidental.*

The second.

*Student Name: Lin Mei. Cause of Death: Fall. Notes: Found at the base of the bell tower. Ruled suicide.*

The third.

*Student Name: Wang Zhi. Cause of Death: Unknown. Notes: Found in his dormitory, no visible injuries. Ruled natural causes.*

Bai Su read through all twelve. The patterns were subtle—different locations, different causes, different years. But each one had one thing in common.

Every victim had been found with a single white cherry blossom petal clutched in their hand.

Bai Su's blood turned to ice. She looked down at her own hands, remembering the cherry tree outside the headmaster's window. The one blooming out of season. The one she had passed every day without thinking.

The door creaked behind her.

"Miss Bai." The headmaster's voice was hard, cold. "I told you to forget."

"I can't." She turned to face him, the scrolls clutched in her arms. "Twelve students. Twelve deaths. And you covered them all up."

"I protected this academy."

"You protected yourself." She held up the scrolls. "What happened thirty years ago? The truth, not the story you told me."

The headmaster's face twisted. For a moment, Bai Su saw the grief beneath the anger, the guilt beneath the authority. But then it was gone, replaced by something darker.

"The truth," he said slowly, "is that my son's lover did not drown. She was pushed. By me."

Bai Su's breath caught.

"I paid the boat captain to throw her overboard. I thought it would end. I thought my son would forget her, take the examinations, marry the woman I chose for him." His voice cracked. "But he found out. He found out and he climbed that tower and he *jumped*."

"And the song?"

"The song followed me after she died. I heard it everywhere—in my dreams, in the wind, in the silence of empty rooms. I thought I was going mad. But then the deaths started. And I realized—the song was not a memory. It was a threat."

Bai Su stepped back, her mind reeling. "The shadow in the bell tower. It's her ghost. She's been killing students for thirty years to take their qi, to gain enough power to—"

"To kill me." The headmaster's laugh was hollow. "Yes. That is precisely what she intends. And now that you have found the records, now that you have heard her song, she will come for you too."

A cold breath brushed the back of Bai Su's neck. A woman's voice, sweet and familiar, began to sing.

*"The water is deep, the water is wide,*
*My love is waiting on the other side.*
*The boat is small, the night is long,*
*Sing me a wedding song."*

Bai Su whirled around. The shadow in the corner of the room was growing, stretching, taking shape. A woman's silhouette, dripping wet, her hair hanging like seaweed over a face that had no features.

The song grew louder.

*"The bride is dressed in white, white, white,*
*Her eyes are closed, her heart is tight.*
*The groom is waiting on the shore,*
*But she will never see him more."*

The shadow reached out a hand. Bai Su tried to move, but her feet were rooted to the floor. The cold crept up her legs, her spine, her chest.

The headmaster screamed. Something crashed. Bai Su couldn't turn to look. The shadow woman was standing right in front of her now, her featureless face inches away.

*"You found the scrolls. You read the names.*
*Now you must play my little games.*
*The bell will ring, the tower will fall,*
*And you will answer when I call."*

The shadow's hand touched Bai Su's cheek.

title: Chapter 19
content:
CHAPTER 19: The Name That Wasn't Written

The headmaster's study smelled of ink and sandalwood, a scent so thick it felt like drowning. Bai Su stood before the heavy rosewood desk, her hands clasped behind her back, still feeling the phantom residue of blood that no longer existed on her skin.

Headmaster Zhou was not alone.

Two figures stood in the shadows of the tall bookshelves—one she recognized, one she did not. The first was Instructor Liang, the calligraphy master whose students had an alarming tendency to develop permanent ink stains on their fingers. The second was a woman Bai Su had never seen before, dressed in robes of deep violet, her face half-hidden behind a veil of black gauze.

"You've been busy, Miss Bai." Headmaster Zhou's voice was calm, but his eyes were not. They moved over her like a merchant assessing damaged goods. "The bell tower. The library. The west wing archives. You've visited more restricted areas in three days than most students visit in three years."

"I got lost."

"Lost." He repeated the word as if tasting it for poison. "Lost enough to find the sealed chamber on the fourth floor of the bell tower. Lost enough to discover the hidden staircase behind the calligraphy hall. Lost enough to—" He paused, sliding a scroll across the desk. "To write this."

Bai Su looked at the scroll. The paper was yellowed, brittle with age. But the characters written on it were fresh, dark ink that still gleamed wet in the lamplight.

Her name. Bai Su. Written in her own handwriting.

"I've never seen that scroll before."

"No?" Instructor Liang stepped forward, his long fingers steepled. "Then explain how the ink matches the brush in your room. How the handwriting matches your admission exam. How the paper was found tucked inside your mattress."

"That's impossible."

"Is it?" The veiled woman spoke for the first time. Her voice was soft, almost musical, but it carried an edge that made Bai Su's skin prickle. "The ghost of Chen Feng was found clutching this scroll when his body was discovered. Tell me, Miss Bai—why would a murdered boy be holding a paper with your name on it?"

Bai Su's mind raced. Chen Feng. The student who had fallen from the bell tower. The boy whose death she had been investigating. The ghost who had spoken to her in the library, warning her away from something he couldn't name.

"I didn't kill him."

"No one said you did." Headmaster Zhou's voice was gentle now, which was somehow worse. "But you must understand how this appears. A new student arrives. A student dies. The student's ghost speaks only to you. And now this—" He tapped the scroll. "A message, written in your hand, found on a corpse."

"It's a setup."

"Of course it is." The veiled woman laughed, a sound like breaking porcelain. "Everything in this academy is a setup. The question is whether you're the one setting it up, or the one being set up."

Bai Su met her gaze through the gauze. "Who are you?"

"My name is Lady Wei. I am the Academy's Curator of Records." She inclined her head slightly. "I keep track of things that are forgotten. Names that are erased. Students who never were."

"Students who never were?"

Lady Wei reached into her sleeve and produced a second scroll. This one was even older, the silk backing frayed at the edges. She unrolled it slowly, revealing a list of names—hundreds of them, written in pale ink that seemed to shift and writhe in the lamplight.

"Every student who has ever attended Wutong Academy is recorded here. The living, the dead, and those who are... between." Her finger traced down the list, stopping at a name near the bottom. "Do you see this?"

Bai Su leaned closer. The name was partially obscured, as if someone had tried to erase it and failed. But the characters were still legible:

*Bai Su. Admitted: Autumn, Year of the Serpent. Status: Deceased.*

"That's not me."

"No?" Lady Wei's eyes glittered behind the veil. "Then explain why your name appears in our records, dated three years before your birth. Explain why the handwriting on this scroll matches your own, despite being written in a style that hasn't been used in decades. Explain why, when I search my memory for the day you arrived, I find nothing."

A cold knot tightened in Bai Su's stomach. "What do you mean, nothing?"

"I mean I remember your face. I remember your name. I remember processing your admission forms." Lady Wei's voice dropped to a whisper. "But I don't remember you walking through the gate. I don't remember your first day of classes. I don't remember you eating in the dining hall, sleeping in the dormitory, or speaking to a single soul before yesterday."

Instructor Liang cleared his throat. "The curator is suggesting that Miss Bai may have been... inserted into our memories."

"Inserted?"

"By the same force that is killing the students. The same force that left that scroll on Chen Feng's body." Headmaster Zhou stood, walking around the desk to stand before Bai Su. He was taller than she remembered, his shadow falling over her like a shroud. "Wutong Academy has stood for three hundred years. In that time, we have learned to recognize the patterns of the supernatural. The ghosts have their rules. The living have their roles. But occasionally, something slips through the cracks. Something that doesn't belong."

"You think I don't belong here."

"I think you may not belong *anywhere*." He reached out and touched her shoulder. His hand was warm, solid, real. "You feel real. You look real. Your qi is strong and bright. But the records say you died three years ago. And the blood on your hands says you've been busy."

Bai Su looked down. Her hands were clean. But when she flexed her fingers, she could feel it—the dried, cracked sensation of old blood, lingering just beneath the surface of her skin.

"I need to see the body."

"Excuse me?"

"Chen Feng's body. I need to see it." She pulled away from Headmaster Zhou's grip, stepping back toward the door. "If I wrote that scroll, if my name was on it, then there's something I'm not remembering. Something that happened before I arrived. Something that—"

"Something that you don't want to remember." The voice came from behind her.

Bai Su spun around.

The ghost scholar stood in the doorway, his translucent form flickering like a candle in the wind. He looked worse than before—more faded, more fractured, as if something was pulling him apart from the inside.

"Get away from her." Instructor Liang's hand went to his belt, where a small bronze mirror hung. "She's been contaminated."

"Contaminated is a strong word." The ghost scholar smiled, but there was no warmth in it. "I prefer 'awakened.'"

"Awakened to what?" Bai Su demanded.

"To the truth of what you are." He stepped closer, ignoring the way Instructor Liang raised the mirror, ignoring the way the light seemed to bend around him like water around a stone. "You asked me who I was. I never answered. Do you want to know now?"

"Tell me."

"My name was Lin Chen. I was a student here, thirty years ago. I died in the bell tower, just like Chen Feng." His eyes met hers, and for a moment, she saw something ancient and terrible looking out from behind them. "But unlike Chen Feng, I didn't stay dead. I was brought back. By someone who wanted to use me. Someone who wanted to tear a hole in the veil between life and death."

"Who?"

"You." He pointed at her, his finger passing through the air like smoke. "You brought me back, Bai Su. You don't remember it. You don't know it. But you did. Just like you brought back every other ghost in this academy. Just like you've been doing for three hundred years."

The room went silent.

Headmaster Zhou's face had gone pale. Instructor Liang's mirror clattered to the floor. Lady Wei was staring at Bai Su with an expression that could only be described as horror.

"That's not possible," Bai Su whispered.

"Isn't it?" Lin Chen tilted his head. "Then explain the bell tower. The scroll. The blood on your hands. Explain why the ghosts all know your name, even though you've never met them. Explain why the dead whisper to you in your sleep, begging you to remember."

"I don't—"

"Remember the fire." His voice dropped, becoming soft, almost tender. "Remember the night you died. Remember what you promised them."

"What I promised who?"

"The others." He reached out and touched her cheek. His fingers were cold, so cold they burned. "The ones who died with you. The ones who are still waiting. The ones who will keep killing until you finish what you started."

Bai Su's vision swam. The room tilted. She felt herself falling, but no one caught her.

The last thing she saw before the darkness took her was Lin Chen's face, twisted into a smile that was almost pity.

"Welcome back, Bai Su. We've been waiting for you."

When she woke, she was lying on a cold stone floor.

The ceiling above her was cracked and ancient, covered in faded murals of scholars and ghosts, living and dead, studying side by side under a sky that was neither day nor night. The air smelled of dust and incense and something else—something metallic, like blood that had dried long ago.

She sat up slowly, her head pounding.

She was in a chamber she didn't recognize. Round, with no windows and only one door, which was currently sealed by a massive stone slab. The walls were covered in writing—thousands of names, carved into the stone in a hundred different hands.

And in the center of the room, arranged in a perfect circle, were nine candles.

Nine candles, each one burning with a flame that was black as ink.

"Ah. You're awake."

Bai Su turned.

A figure sat in the shadows near the far wall, cross-legged, watching her with eyes that glowed faintly in the dark. It was a girl—young, maybe fourteen, with her hair cut short and a scar running from her temple to her jaw.

"Who are you?"

"My name doesn't matter." The girl smiled, and her teeth were too sharp, too many. "What matters is that you're finally here. Finally ready to remember."

"Remember what?"

"Everything." The girl stood, walking toward the circle of black-flamed candles. She knelt, blowing one out. The flame extinguished with a sound like a scream. "You built this academy, Bai Su. You filled it with ghosts. You made the rules that bind them."

"That's insane."

"Is it?" The girl blew out another candle. "Then why can you see them? Why can you touch them? Why can you hear their prayers, their pleas, their curses?" She blew out a third. "Why do they call you the Mother of the Dead?"

Bai Su's blood ran cold. "They don't call me that."

"They do." Fourth candle. "They just don't say it to your face. Because they're afraid of you. Afraid of what you'll do when you remember." Fifth candle. "Afraid of what you'll become."

"I'm just a student."

"You're just a student." Sixth candle. "And I'm just a ghost." Seventh. "And this is just an academy." Eighth. "None of it is real, Bai Su. None of it has ever been real."

She reached for the ninth candle.

"Wait." Bai Su

title: The Song She Shouldn't Know
content:
The headmaster's office smelled of burning paper and old secrets.

Bai Su stood before the massive rosewood desk, her hands clasped behind her back to hide their trembling. The dried blood was gone from her palms—had it ever truly been there?—but she could still feel its phantom weight, like gloves made of memory.

Headmaster Wen sat across from her, his fingers steepled beneath a chin that sagged with age and exhaustion. Behind him, the wall was covered in examination scrolls—hundreds of them, yellowed and brittle, each one bearing a single name crossed out in red ink.

"I've been watching you, Miss Bai," he said. His voice was dry as autumn leaves. "Since the day you arrived, you've been poking your nose where it doesn't belong. The bell chamber. The west wing. The library's restricted section."

"I was just trying to understand—"

"Understand what? That this academy is haunted? That half your classmates are dead?" He laughed, a hollow sound that echoed in the cramped office. "Every student figures that out by their second week. The real question is what you plan to do about it."

Bai Su's mouth went dry. "I don't understand."

"Yes, you do." The headmaster leaned forward, and for the first time, she noticed his eyes—pale yellow, like old parchment, with pupils that seemed to shift and writhe like smoke. "You've been talking to the Scholar. The ghost in the bell tower. He told you about the life qi transfers, didn't he?"

"Who's the Scholar?"

"Don't play dumb with me, girl." He slammed his palm on the desk, and the scrolls rattled. "That spirit has been haunting this academy for three hundred years. He was the first student to die here, back when this was still a monastery. They say he hanged himself the night before his examination, after a servant girl accused him of stealing her virtue."

"The servant girl," Bai Su repeated, her mind racing. "What happened to her?"

"She disappeared. They found her hair ribbon in the bell chamber, tangled in the ropes." The headmaster's yellow eyes bored into hers. "The Scholar swore he was innocent. Swore that someone else had killed her, and framed him for the crime. But no one believed him. The magistrate didn't even hold a trial. They just strung him up and left his body to rot."

A chill ran down Bai Su's spine. She remembered the blood on her hands. The footprint that matched her shoe. The folk song the shadow had whispered in her ear.

*The Scholar didn't kill that girl,* she realized. *He was framed. But by whom?*

"And now," the headmaster continued, "someone is using his old technique to steal life qi from living students. They're trying to break through the veil, just like he did. But the Scholar isn't the one doing it. He can't—he's bound to the bell chamber by his own death curse."

"Then who?"

"That's what you're going to find out." He reached into his desk drawer and pulled out a scroll, tied with a black ribbon. "This is the original file from the magistrate's investigation, three hundred years ago. The Scholar's trial records. The servant girl's autopsy. Everything."

Bai Su took the scroll with trembling hands. It was heavier than it should have been, as if it contained more than just paper.

"There are names in here that have been forgotten," the headmaster said. "Names of people who were at this academy when the girl died. Some of them are still here."

"Still here? But that was three hundred years ago—"

"Ghosts don't age, Miss Bai. They just wait." He sat back in his chair, his yellow eyes glinting in the lamplight. "The killer is among them. One of the undead scholars, wearing the face of a victim. And they've been planning this for centuries."

Bai Su's heart hammered against her ribs. "Why are you telling me this?"

"Because you can see them. Really see them, not just the masks they wear. And because—" He paused, his gaze flickering to something behind her. "Because you're the only one who can stop them."

She turned, following his gaze. The door was slightly ajar, and through the crack, she could see a figure standing in the hallway.

A girl. Young, no more than sixteen. Her face was pale and beautiful, her eyes dark and empty. She wore a servant's robes, faded and threadbare, and her hands were clasped in front of her as if in prayer.

But it was her hair that made Bai Su's blood stop cold.

A single ribbon, white silk, tied in a bow at the back of her head.

*The servant girl's hair ribbon. Found in the bell chamber.*

"Who is that?" Bai Su whispered.

The headmaster didn't answer. When she looked back at him, his chair was empty. The scrolls on the wall had vanished. The lamp had gone out.

She was alone.

No—not alone. The servant girl was still there, standing in the doorway, her dark eyes fixed on Bai Su with an intensity that made her skin crawl.

"Can you hear me?" Bai Su asked.

The girl tilted her head. Her lips parted, and a voice emerged—not from her mouth, but from everywhere at once, echoing off the walls like a song trapped in a well.

*"The bell tolls for the living, the bell tolls for the dead. One toll for the lover, two tolls for the friend. Three tolls for the killer, who wears a borrowed face. Four tolls for the victim, who never left this place."*

The song. The same song the shadow had whispered in the bell chamber.

Bai Su's hands tightened around the scroll. "You're the servant girl. The one who died three hundred years ago."

*"I never died. I was never born. I am the echo of a scream that never came."*

"Who killed you?"

The girl's lips curved into a smile. It was not a kind smile. It was the smile of someone who had been waiting for this question for a very long time.

*"No one killed me. I killed myself. But I didn't hang myself."*

"Then how—"

*"I drowned. In the well behind the south dormitory. They found my body three days later, bloated and blue, my hair tangled in the rope they used to pull me out."*

Bai Su's mind reeled. "But the records say—"

*"The records are wrong. The magistrate was paid to write a different story. The Scholar was paid to die for a crime he didn't commit."*

"Paid by whom?"

The girl's smile widened. Her eyes began to bleed—black tears that ran down her cheeks like ink.

*"Ask yourself, Miss Bai. Why are you the only one who can see me? Why are you the only one who remembers? Why do the ghosts whisper your name when they think no one is listening?"*

The words struck Bai Su like a physical blow.

*The ghosts whisper your name.*

She had heard them. In the library. In the west wing. In the bell chamber. Voices that faded when she turned to look, leaving only the echo of her own name hanging in the air.

"I don't know what you're talking about."

*"Yes, you do."* The girl stepped closer. Her bare feet made no sound on the wooden floor. *"You've been here before, Bai Su. Not in this life. But in another. You were here when I died. You were the one who found my body."*

"That's impossible. I'm seventeen years old."

*"And yet your hands remember."*

Bai Su looked down. The blood was back. Fresh this time, wet and warm, dripping from her fingers onto the scroll.

She screamed.

The world shattered.

When she opened her eyes again, she was lying on the floor of the bell chamber. The sun was setting, painting the walls in shades of amber and blood. The broken window had been repaired. The glass was whole again.

And standing over her was the Scholar.

He looked different. Younger. His face was unlined, his eyes bright with life. He wore the robes of a student, not the tattered shroud she had seen before.

"You're awake," he said. His voice was not the hollow echo of a ghost. It was warm, human, filled with relief.

Bai Su sat up, her head pounding. "What happened? Where am I?"

"The bell chamber. You fainted." He knelt beside her, his hand reaching out to touch her face—then stopping, as if he remembered something important. "You were asking questions. About the servant girl. About what really happened."

"I saw her. She said I found her body."

The Scholar's expression flickered. Pain. Guilt. Something darker.

"She told you the truth," he said quietly. "You were here. Three hundred years ago. You were a student at this academy, studying for the imperial examinations. You found her in the well. You tried to save her."

"But I don't remember—"

"Because you died, Bai Su. That night, someone killed you. Stabbed you in the heart and threw your body into the same well." His voice cracked. "I tried to stop them. I failed. I've been waiting for you to come back ever since."

Bai Su's world tilted. She looked down at her hands. The blood was gone again, but she could feel it—a memory beneath her skin, ancient and familiar.

"Who killed me?"

The Scholar's eyes met hers. They were dark. Empty. Filled with three hundred years of grief.

"I don't know. I never saw their face. But I know one thing with certainty."

"What?"

"The person who killed you, who killed the servant girl, who framed me for both murders—they're still here. And they know you've returned."

The bell above them began to ring.

Once. Twice. Three times.

Bai Su looked up. The rope was moving. Someone was pulling it.

But the chamber was empty.

"The killer is coming," the Scholar whispered. "They've been waiting for you to remember. Because the only way to finish what they started is to kill you again."

The bell rang four times. Five. Six.

And the door to the tower creaked open.

title: Chapter 6
content:
CHAPTER 6: The Headmaster's Confession

The headmaster's office was a place Bai Su had only seen once, during her entrance interview three weeks ago. She remembered it as warm, cluttered with scrolls and inkstones, the air thick with the smell of old paper and plum blossoms. Now, as she pushed open the door, she found it transformed.

Every surface was covered in talismans. They hung from the ceiling beams, plastered the walls, lay in overlapping layers across the floor. Red ink glowed faintly in the dim lamplight, the characters pulsing like heartbeats. The headmaster sat behind his desk, but he wasn't writing or reading. He was holding a bronze mirror, staring into it with an intensity that made Bai Su hesitate at the threshold.

"Come in," he said without looking up. "Close the door. Lock it."

She obeyed, the lock clicking into place with a sound that felt final. "Headmaster, what's happening? The bell—"

"I know." He set the mirror down and finally met her eyes. His face was drawn, years older than it had been three weeks ago. "The bell tower hasn't rung on its own in sixty years. Not since the last time." He gestured to the chair across from his desk. "Sit, Miss Bai. We have much to discuss, and very little time."

She sat, her hands clasped tightly in her lap. The blood was gone from her skin, but she could still feel it, a phantom warmth that wouldn't fade. "The last time what happened?"

"The last time a ghost tried to become human."

Bai Su's breath caught. "You know about the killings?"

"Killings?" His eyebrows rose. "What killings?"

"Chen Feng. He fell from the bell tower. The ghost scholar—" She stopped, realizing how insane she sounded. But the headmaster didn't laugh. He didn't dismiss her. Instead, he closed his eyes and let out a long, weary breath.

"Chen Feng is dead?"

"Yes. This morning. He was pushed."

"And you saw the one who pushed him?"

"I saw a ghost. A scholar, about seventeen, wearing robes from the old dynasty. He told me—" She hesitated, choosing her words carefully. "He told me that a ghost is stealing life qi from living students. That they're trying to become human again."

The headmaster was silent for a long moment. When he spoke, his voice was barely a whisper. "I was afraid of this. I've been afraid for sixty years."

He reached into his desk drawer and pulled out a scroll, yellowed with age, the edges frayed and crumbling. He unrolled it carefully, revealing a painting so detailed it seemed to breathe. It showed a young woman, beautiful and fierce, her hair flying wild, her eyes burning with an intensity that made Bai Su's skin prickle. She held a brush in one hand and a sword in the other, and behind her, the Wutong Academy burned.

"Her name was Lin Xiu," the headmaster said. "She was the most brilliant student this academy ever produced. Top of every examination. Praised by the emperor himself. And she died three days before the imperial exams."

"What happened to her?"

"She was murdered. Poisoned by a rival who feared her brilliance. But here's the thing about Lin Xiu, Miss Bai. She never accepted defeat. Not in life. Not in death." He traced the painting's edge with a finger. "She became a ghost, like so many others. But she didn't want to study. She didn't want to wait for reincarnation. She wanted revenge."

Bai Su leaned forward, her heart pounding. "What did she do?"

"She discovered something that no ghost should know. A method to transfer life qi from the living to the dead. She called it the Art of Returning. The process requires three things: the blood of a living student, the bone of a dead one, and a soul that remembers how to hate." He looked up, his eyes meeting hers. "She took seven victims before we stopped her. Seven living students, drained of their qi, left as empty shells that withered and died within days."

"How did you stop her?"

"We trapped her. The former headmaster, the senior professors, and a Daoist priest from the capital. They bound her spirit in the bell tower, using the bronze bell to anchor her soul. The bell was enchanted to ring whenever her power stirred, a warning to the living and a prison for the dead." He paused. "It hasn't rung in sixty years. Until today."

"Because someone freed her?"

"Because someone *became* her." The headmaster's voice was heavy with dread. "The Art of Returning isn't just a method. It's a curse. When Lin Xiu was trapped, her knowledge didn't disappear. It seeped into the academy itself, into the walls and the floors and the very air. Any ghost with enough willpower and resentment can find it. Can learn it. Can *become* her."

Bai Su's mind raced. "So the ghost who pushed Chen Feng—"

"Is Lin Xiu's successor. A new ghost who has learned the Art of Returning and is trying to complete what she started." He looked at her with something like pity. "And there's more, Miss Bai. Something you need to understand about yourself."

"What about me?"

"You can see ghosts." It wasn't a question. "You've always been able to, haven't you? Since you were a child."

She nodded slowly. "I thought I was going mad. My parents took me to doctors, to priests, to anyone who would listen. They said I had a 'sensitive constitution.' That I was prone to fantasies."

"They were wrong. You have a gift, Miss Bai. A rare one. The ability to see the dead means you can also touch them. Interact with them. And that means—" He stopped, as if the words were too heavy to speak.

"It means I'm a target," she finished. "The ghost needs living qi. And I'm the only living student who can see them coming."

"No." The headmaster shook his head. "It means you're the only one who can stop them."

The words hung in the air between them, heavy and impossible. Bai Su wanted to laugh, to tell him he was insane, to walk out of this office and never look back. But she had seen the blood on her hands. She had heard the bell ring without a ringer. She had felt the cold breath on her neck, heard the folk song that followed her down the stairs.

"Where do I start?" she asked.

The headmaster reached into his desk again, pulling out a second scroll. This one was newer, the ink still fresh. He unrolled it to reveal a list of names, twenty in total. Ten were crossed out.

"These are the ghosts currently haunting the academy," he said. "We keep a record, for... administrative purposes. The crossed-out ones have moved on to reincarnation. The remaining ten are still here."

Bai Su scanned the list. The names were faded, written in a careful hand. She recognized some from her time at the academy—the drowned scholar who haunted the library, the suicide candidate in the west wing, the murdered prodigy who tutored for extra incense.

And at the bottom of the list, a name that made her blood freeze.

*Bai Su.*

She looked up, her heart hammering. "My name is on this list."

"Yes."

"But I'm alive. I'm sitting here, talking to you. I'm *alive*."

The headmaster's face was unreadable. "The list isn't of the dead, Miss Bai. It's of those who can see them. Every person who has ever possessed your gift has their name written here. Some were alive when they were added. Some were dead." He paused. "The list doesn't distinguish."

"That doesn't make sense."

"It makes perfect sense." He leaned forward, his voice dropping to a whisper. "The ability to see the dead isn't a gift of the living. It's a gift of those who stand between life and death. Every person on this list has one foot in the world of the living and one in the world of the dead. Some will cross over completely. Some will return. And some—" He looked at her with an intensity that made her skin crawl. "Some will become the bridge through which the dead return."

Bai Su stood up so abruptly that her chair toppled backward. "I'm leaving. I'm going back to my dormitory, I'm packing my things, and I'm leaving this academy."

"You can't."

"Watch me."

"The gates are locked, Miss Bai. They've been locked since the bell rang. No one enters. No one leaves." He stood as well, his voice calm but firm. "The barrier that holds the dead in also holds the living in. It's a safety measure, designed to contain any outbreak of ghostly activity. It will remain in place until the crisis is resolved."

"Then resolve it. You're the headmaster. You have talismans, priests, *something*."

"I have a student who can see the dead." He stepped around his desk, approaching her slowly. "I have a student who walked into the bell tower and walked out alive. I have a student who can touch the dead without being drained, speak to them without being corrupted. Do you know how rare that is, Miss Bai? In six hundred years of this academy's history, there have been only three people with your gift. The first went mad. The second died. The third—" He stopped, his voice catching. "The third was Lin Xiu."

The world tilted around Bai Su. She grabbed the edge of the desk to steady herself. "That's impossible. Lin Xiu was a ghost. She was the *killer*."

"Lin Xiu was a student, like you. She could see the dead, like you. She was brilliant and fierce and determined to make her mark on the world." The headmaster's voice was heavy with grief. "And when she died, her gift didn't disappear. It transformed. It became something dark and hungry. She used her ability to see the dead to find the living. She used her knowledge of the veil to tear it open."

"I'm not her."

"No. You're not." He reached out, almost touching her shoulder, then stopped. "But you carry the same potential. The same power. The same ability to choose what you become."

Bai Su stared at him, her mind spinning. She thought of the ghost scholar in the bell tower, his warning, his fear. She thought of the blood on her hands, the footprint that matched her shoe, the folk song that followed her down the stairs.

She thought of Chen Feng, falling through the air, his life ending before it had truly begun.

"What do I need to do?" she asked, her voice barely a whisper.

"Find the ghost who pushed Chen Feng. Learn the Art of Returning. And destroy it, before it destroys the academy."

"How?"

The headmaster reached into his robes and pulled out a small jade pendant, carved in the shape of a bell. "This was Lin Xiu's. It was taken from her body after she was bound. It's attuned to her energy, to the knowledge she stole. If you wear it, you'll be able to sense the Art of Returning. To follow it back to its source."

He pressed it into her hand. The jade was warm, almost hot, pulsing with a rhythm that matched her heartbeat.

"Find the source, Miss Bai. Find the ghost who has become Lin Xiu's successor. And end this, before the tenth bell rings."

"What happens when the tenth bell rings?"

The headmaster looked past her, toward the window. The sun was setting

title: Chapter 7
content:
CHAPTER 7: The Song Before Drowning

The headmaster's office was a place of contradictions.

Ancient scrolls lined the walls, their silk yellowed with age, yet a modern oil lamp burned on the desk, its glass chimney polished to a mirror shine. The floor was worn stone, centuries of footsteps having carved shallow grooves into the surface, but the cushions on the chairs were new, embroidered with patterns that hadn't existed fifty years ago.

Bai Su stood before the headmaster's desk, her hands clasped behind her back to hide their trembling. The dried blood was gone from her palms, scrubbed raw against her robes until her skin stung, but she could still feel it. A phantom warmth that wouldn't fade.

"I heard you visited the bell tower," Headmaster Wei said.

He was an old man, his face a map of wrinkles and wisdom, but his eyes were sharp as shattered glass. They missed nothing. Bai Su had learned that in her first week.

"I was following a lead," she said.

"A lead." He didn't phrase it as a question. "And what did this lead show you?"

Bai Su considered lying. The words formed on her tongue, smooth and practiced. But something in the headmaster's gaze stopped her. He knew. Not everything, perhaps, but enough.

"The window where Chen Feng fell," she said. "There was broken glass on the outside of the sill. He didn't jump through it. He was pushed from behind, hard enough to shatter the pane on his way out."

Headmaster Wei nodded slowly. "The magistrate's investigation concluded suicide. The boy was despondent over his examination results."

"His examination results were excellent. Top of his class in classics, second in poetry." Bai Su pulled the folded paper from her sleeve, the one the ghost scholar had given her. "I found this in the library. It's Chen Feng's last examination scroll. Look at the grading."

She placed it on the desk. Headmaster Wei didn't touch it. His eyes moved across the red annotations, and something flickered in their depths. Recognition. And something else. Fear.

"This is not my handwriting," he said.

"I didn't think it was. But someone graded this scroll. Someone who gave him failing marks across every subject. Someone who wanted him to fail."

"And you believe this drove him to suicide?"

"No." Bai Su leaned forward, her palms flat on the desk. "I believe someone used this to drive him to despair. And then, when he was at his lowest, they pushed him through that window. Made it look like he jumped."

The headmaster was quiet for a long moment. The oil lamp flickered, casting dancing shadows across the walls. In the corner, a shadow that didn't match anything in the room stretched and contracted, breathing with a rhythm that wasn't human.

"Chen Feng came to me three days before he died," Headmaster Wei said finally. "He said he was being followed. That he heard singing at night, a woman's voice, coming from inside his own room. I told him it was examination stress. I gave him a sleeping draught and sent him back to his dormitory."

The breath caught in Bai Su's throat. "Singing?"

"An old folk song. Something about a scholar who drowned himself in the lake because his love rejected him. The ghost of the scholar is said to haunt the academy, looking for a replacement to drag into the water." Headmaster Wei smiled, but there was no humor in it. "Superstition. Ghost stories to frighten the younger students."

Bai Su thought of the voice she had heard in the bell tower. Sweet and familiar. A song she felt she had known before she was born.

"Where is this lake?" she asked.

"Behind the east wing. It's been filled in for decades. Nothing but mud and weeds now." The headmaster's eyes met hers, and for a moment, he looked old. Truly old, as if the weight of a hundred years pressed down on his shoulders. "Miss Bai, I must ask you to be careful. There are things in this academy that even I do not understand. Spirits that predate the buildings, memories that have soaked into the very stones. Some of them are harmless. Some are not."

"Are you warning me or threatening me?"

"I am warning you." He stood, and the shadows in the corner retreated, folding back into themselves. "The ghost you met in the library. The scholar who gave you Chen Feng's scroll. I know who he was. I knew him when he was alive."

Bai Su's heart stopped. "Who was he?"

"His name was Lin Wei. He was my student, thirty years ago. The most brilliant mind I had ever taught. He died three days before his final examination." Headmaster Wei's voice cracked, just slightly. "They said it was a heart attack. The stress of study, the pressure of expectation. But I never believed it."

"Why not?"

"Because I found him. In the bell tower, in the same room where Chen Feng fell. And there was a footprint in the dust beside him. A woman's footprint." He looked at Bai Su, and his eyes were wet. "I never told anyone. I thought I was protecting the academy. Protecting the other students. But I was only protecting myself. From the truth."

The oil lamp flickered again. The shadows pressed closer.

"What truth?" Bai Su whispered.

"That something is waking up. Something that has been sleeping in this academy for a very long time." Headmaster Wei reached into his desk and pulled out a small jade pendant, carved in the shape of a bell. "Take this. It belonged to Lin Wei. He was wearing it when he died. Perhaps it will help you understand."

Bai Su took the pendant. It was cold, colder than it should have been, and it seemed to pulse against her palm. A heartbeat made of stone.

"I don't understand," she said.

"You will." The headmaster sat back down, and his face became a mask again, distant and unreadable. "Now go. The night bell will ring soon. You should be in your room."

Bai Su wanted to argue. Wanted to demand more answers, more truth. But the headmaster's gaze was final. She bowed and left, the jade pendant clutched in her hand.

The hallway was empty. The torches on the walls burned low, their flames casting long shadows that seemed to move when she wasn't looking directly at them. Bai Su walked quickly, her footsteps echoing in the silence.

She had almost reached her dormitory when she heard it.

A voice. Soft at first, almost lost in the wind. But growing louder, clearer, as if the singer was walking toward her.

An old folk song. About a scholar who drowned himself for love.

Bai Su stopped. The voice came from ahead, from the direction of the filled-in lake. She turned to walk away, but her feet wouldn't move. Something held her in place, invisible hands gripping her ankles.

The song grew louder. Sweeter. And Bai Su recognized it.

It was the song her mother used to sing to her as a child. A lullaby, she had always thought. A gentle melody to soothe her to sleep.

But now she heard the words clearly for the first time.

*"Come to the water, my love, my dear,*

*The moon is bright and the night is clear.*

*The reeds will wrap you in their embrace,*

*And wash away the sorrow from your face."*

Bai Su's blood ran cold. This wasn't a lullaby. It was a siren's call. A promise of death wrapped in a mother's voice.

The shadows on the wall began to move. They coalesced, forming a shape. A woman. Tall and elegant, her features obscured by darkness, but her eyes visible. Red. Burning like coals in the night.

"You can see me," the shadow woman said. Her voice was the song, sweet and terrible. "You can hear me. You're special, Bai Su. Just like he was."

"Who?" Bai Su's voice was barely a whisper.

"Lin Wei. Chen Feng. All the others." The shadow woman stepped closer, and the temperature dropped. Frost formed on the walls, spreading like veins across the stone. "I've been waiting for someone like you. Someone who can see through the veil. Someone who can cross to the other side and come back."

"I don't want to cross anything."

"You don't have a choice." The shadow woman smiled, and her teeth were sharp, like a fish's. "You've already stepped into my world. The only question is whether you'll drown in it or learn to breathe."

The jade pendant in Bai Su's hand grew hot. Burning hot. She dropped it with a cry, and it clattered to the floor, cracking in two.

The shadow woman laughed.

"Lin Wei's protection won't save you. He couldn't save himself. He failed, just like all the others." She reached out a hand, and her fingers were long, impossibly long, stretching toward Bai Su's throat. "But you won't fail. You'll succeed. And when you do, I'll finally be free."

Bai Su's back hit the wall. There was nowhere to run. The shadow woman's fingers brushed her skin, and it felt like drowning. Cold water filling her lungs, darkness closing in from all sides.

Then a voice rang out, sharp and clear.

"Get away from her."

The shadow woman hissed and recoiled. The ghost scholar stood at the end of the hallway, his form flickering like a candle in the wind. He held a lantern in one hand, its light pushing back the darkness.

"You," the shadow woman snarled. "Always interfering. Always protecting the living. You should have stayed dead, Lin Wei."

"I should have," he agreed. "But I didn't. And neither will she."

He stepped forward, and the light from his lantern grew brighter. The shadow woman screamed, a sound that shattered the torches on the walls, plunging the hallway into darkness.

When the light returned, she was gone.

The ghost scholar stood over Bai Su, his hand extended. She took it, and his fingers were cold, but solid. Warm enough to pull her to her feet.

"You have to leave this academy," he said. "Tonight. Before she finds you again."

"Who is she?" Bai Su demanded. "What does she want?"

"She is the reason I died. The reason Chen Feng died. The reason every student who has ever died in this academy is dead." The ghost scholar's eyes met hers, and there was pain in them. Deep and ancient and endless. "She is the lake. The water that fills the empty spaces. And she wants you to join her."

"Why?"

"Because you're the only one who can open the door." He released her hand, and his form flickered again, growing fainter. "I have to go. The light is draining me. But remember, Bai Su. Trust no one. Not the living. Not the dead. Not even yourself."

"What does that mean?"

He didn't answer. He faded, like smoke dissolving in the wind, until only the darkness remained.

Bai Su stood alone in the hallway, the cracked jade pendant at her feet. She picked it up, and the pieces fit together in her palm, forming a bell that no longer rang.

From somewhere in the academy, she heard the song again. Fainter now, but still there. Still reaching for her.

*"Come to the water, my love, my dear..."*

Bai Su ran.

She didn't stop until she reached her room, locked the door, and pushed her desk against it. She sat on her bed, breathing hard, the jade pendant clutched in her hands.

Outside her window, the moon rose full and bright.

And in its light

title: Chapter 8
content:
CHAPTER 8: The Song She Shouldn't Know

The headmaster's office smelled of ink and dried plum blossoms, a scent so thick it coated the back of Bai Su's throat. She sat in the chair across from Old Xu's desk, her hands folded in her lap, her nails digging crescents into her palms.

Old Xu did not look up from the scroll he was reading. His brush moved in steady strokes, characters flowing like water from its tip. The silence stretched long enough that Bai Su began to count her heartbeats.

Twenty-seven.

He set down the brush.

"Miss Bai," he said, his voice carrying no warmth, "do you know why I called you here?"

"No, Headmaster."

He slid a piece of paper across the desk. It was her entrance examination. She recognized the handwriting, the looping curves of characters she had labored over for three days. But there was something wrong with it. The ink was smudged in places, blurring words she distinctly remembered writing clearly.

"Do you see anything unusual?"

Bai Su leaned closer. The smudges weren't random. They formed patterns, deliberate shapes that looked almost like—

Characters she had not written.

*I saw him fall.*

*I did not push him.*

Her blood turned to ice.

"I didn't write this," she said.

"No?" Old Xu's eyes were sharp, cutting. "Then who did?"

"Someone is trying to frame me."

"Frame you?" He laughed, a dry, brittle sound. "Miss Bai, have you considered that perhaps you wrote it and simply don't remember?"

"I would remember writing a confession."

"Would you?" He leaned back, steepling his fingers. "I've been teaching at Wutong Academy for forty years. Do you know how many students I've seen who didn't remember things? Ghosts who walked in their sleep, living students who woke up with blood on their hands and no memory of how it got there. The boundary between the living and the dead is thin here, Miss Bai. Thinner than you realize."

Bai Su's throat tightened. She could still feel the phantom warmth of dried blood on her palms, still hear the echo of that folk song in her ears.

"I'm not a ghost," she said.

"Are you certain?"

The question hit her like a physical blow. She opened her mouth to answer, to insist, to scream if she had to—but the words died in her throat.

She had no memory of her life before Wutong Academy.

She had assumed it was the stress of the entrance examination, the overwhelming nature of starting at a new school. But now, sitting in Old Xu's office, the realization pressed down on her like a tombstone.

"What is my family name?" she whispered.

Old Xu's expression flickered. Something that might have been concern. Something that might have been recognition.

"Your enrollment records say you are from the Bai family of Luoyang. But I have sent three letters of inquiry to the Bai family of Luoyang, and all three have been returned unopened."

He pushed another paper toward her. It was a letter, sealed with wax that had been cracked open. She recognized the family crest—a phoenix rising from flames, wings spread wide.

"The Bai family seal," she breathed.

"Yes. But look at the address."

She looked. The characters were wrong. The strokes were slightly off, the angles too sharp, the curves too flat. It was a forgery—a good one, but not perfect.

"Someone is impersonating the Bai family," Old Xu said. "Or the Bai family does not want to acknowledge you. Which is it, Miss Bai?"

She didn't know. She didn't know anything. Her past was a blank wall, and every time she tried to climb it, she found herself back on the ground, gasping for breath.

"I don't know," she said.

Old Xu studied her for a long moment. Then he sighed, and some of the hardness left his face.

"Chen Feng's body was found this morning," he said. "The coroner determined he died of a fall. But there were scratches on his wrists, Miss Bai. Scratches that suggest someone was holding him before he fell."

"I didn't—"

"I'm not accusing you." He held up a hand. "I'm telling you what the evidence says. And the evidence points at you. Your examination scroll is covered in a confession. Your shoe prints were found in the bell chamber. And you have no alibi for the time of his death."

Bai Su's hands were shaking. She pressed them flat against her thighs, willing them to still.

"What happens now?"

"Now?" Old Xu stood, walking to the window. The sun was setting, painting the courtyard in shades of amber and blood. "Now you find the real killer. Before the school board convenes in three days. If you don't have proof of your innocence by then, I cannot protect you."

"Protect me from what?"

He turned. His eyes were ancient, carrying the weight of secrets he would never share.

"From the consequences of being a living student accused of murder in a school half-filled with ghosts. The living will fear you. The dead will hunt you. And somewhere in between, the truth will bury you alive."

---

Bai Su left the headmaster's office in a daze. The hallways were empty, the other students having retreated to their dormitories for the evening meal. She walked without direction, her feet carrying her through corridors she had never seen before, past doors that creaked open to reveal rooms that hadn't existed moments ago.

The academy was changing around her.

She stopped in front of a door that was different from the others. It was made of black wood, polished to a mirror shine, with no handle or lock. Just a smooth surface that reflected her face back at her.

She raised her hand to touch it.

The reflection moved.

Bai Su jerked her hand back. The reflection—her reflection—was smiling. A slow, knowing smile that stretched too wide, showing teeth that were too sharp.

"Hello, Bai Su."

The voice came from inside the door. From behind the reflection. From everywhere and nowhere at once.

"Who are you?" Bai Su whispered.

"Don't you recognize me?" The reflection tilted its head, the motion fluid and wrong. "I'm you. The you that remembers. The you that knows what happened in the bell tower."

"I didn't do anything."

"Didn't you?" The reflection laughed, and the sound was the same folk song she had heard before, the sweet melody curling around her like smoke. "You don't remember, Bai Su. That's the problem. You don't remember the night you died."

"I'm not dead."

"Aren't you?" The reflection pressed against the door, its face flattening against the wood, distorting into something monstrous. "Then why can't you remember your past? Why does no one claim you? Why does the blood on your hands appear and disappear like a ghost?"

"Stop it."

"Why did you push him, Bai Su?"

"I didn't push anyone!"

"Liar."

The door burst open.

Bai Su stumbled backward, her heart slamming against her ribs. The room beyond was dark, filled with a cold that seeped into her bones. She could see shapes moving in the shadows—vague, formless things that whispered her name.

She turned to run.

The hallway was gone.

She stood in the bell chamber. The window was shattered. The rope hung still. And on the floor, arranged in neat rows, were examination scrolls. Hundreds of them. Each one stained with blood.

She walked forward, unable to stop herself. Her feet carried her to the nearest scroll, and she knelt, her hands reaching out to touch the bloodstained paper.

The characters were hers.

Every single one.

*I am sorry, Chen Feng. I did not mean to push you. But you saw what I am. You saw the truth I have been hiding. And the truth cannot be allowed to speak.*

Bai Su's vision swam. The words blurred, reformed, blurred again.

*I am not Bai Su. I am something else. Something that wears her face. Something that killed her first.*

"No."

She scrambled backward, away from the scrolls, away from the words that burned into her mind. She hit the wall and kept scrambling, her fingers scraping against the stone.

The door to the bell chamber swung open.

A figure stood in the doorway. Tall. Thin. Dressed in robes that seemed to absorb the light.

"I see you found my collection."

The voice was familiar. Bai Su looked up, her heart freezing in her chest.

It was the ghost scholar. The one who had warned her. The one who had told her to find the killer.

But his eyes were different now. They glowed with a light that was not of this world, a cold, hungry fire that devoured the shadows around him.

"You," she breathed.

"Me." He smiled, and it was the same smile she had seen in the reflection. "I've been waiting for you to catch up, Bai Su. Or should I call you by your real name?"

"I don't have a real name."

"No." He stepped closer, and she saw that his feet did not touch the ground. He floated above the stone, leaving no footprints. "You don't. Because you're not real, Bai Su. You never were."

"What are you talking about?"

He reached into his robes and pulled out a scroll. It was old, the paper yellowed and cracked, the ink faded to brown.

"This is the original enrollment record for the Bai family of Luoyang," he said. "It lists every child born to that house for the past three hundred years."

He unrolled it. The names stretched down in neat columns, each one followed by a date of birth and a date of death.

"There is no Bai Su."

He held the scroll up, letting her see the final entry. The line ended with a name she recognized.

*Bai Yan. Born 1582. Died 1601.*

"Bai Yan," she repeated. The name felt familiar on her tongue, like a song she had once known.

"Your sister," the ghost scholar said. "She died nineteen years ago. And you—" He paused, his eyes boring into hers. "You were never born."

"That's impossible."

"Is it?" He rolled the scroll back up, tucking it into his robes. "I've been dead for two hundred years, Bai Su. I've had time to learn the secrets of this academy. And the greatest secret of all is that some students are not students at all."

"What are they?"

He leaned in, his breath cold against her ear.

"They are memories. Ghosts of the living, so strong in their grief that they take form. They walk, they talk, they study. But they are not real. They are echoes of people who never existed, created by the desperate dreams of those who wish they had."

Bai Su's hands were shaking. She looked down at them, at the fingers that felt so solid, so real.

"I'm not a memory."

"You are the memory of a girl who should have been born. A sister who never existed. A daughter who was never mourned." He straightened, his voice softening. "Bai Yan's spirit created you. She was so lonely after her death, so desperate for a sibling to share her fate, that she willed you into existence. And for nineteen years, you have lived as her ghost."

"Where is she now?"

"I don't know." He gestured at the scrolls on the floor. "But I know that she is the one who wrote these. She is the one who killed Chen Feng. She is the one who has been killing the living students, taking their qi, trying to become real enough to bring you to life."

"To bring me to life?"

He nodded. "She wants you to be

title: Chapter 9
content:
CHAPTER 9: The Song Beneath the Skin

The headmaster's office smelled of ink and ash. Bai Su stood before the massive rosewood desk, her hands clasped behind her back, still feeling the phantom warmth of blood that wasn't there.
