Headmaster Zhao Wei was not alone. Three other teachers sat in the shadowed corners of the room—the calligraphy master, the arithmetic tutor, and a woman Bai Su had never seen before. She wore gray robes that seemed to absorb the lamplight, and her eyes were the color of old copper coins.

"You've been busy, Miss Bai." The headmaster's voice was calm, but his fingers drummed against the desk in a pattern that matched the bell's earlier toll. Nine beats. Silence. Nine beats. "Exploring areas of the academy that are strictly off-limits to first-year students."

"I got lost."

"The bell chamber is at the top of the East Observation Tower. Behind three locked doors. Each requiring a different key." He leaned forward. "How lost does one have to be to find themselves there?"

Bai Su said nothing. The gray-robed woman rose and circled her, moving with the strange, gliding gait of someone who had forgotten what it meant to walk on solid ground.

"She carries the stench of him," the woman said. Her voice was dry as old paper. "The boy who fell. Chen Feng."

"I don't know what you're talking about."

"Liar." The woman stopped in front of Bai Su, close enough that their breath mingled. Up close, Bai Su could see that the woman's eyes weren't copper-colored at all. They were gold. Real gold, inset into the sockets like coins placed on a corpse's eyelids. "You've been touched by a memory that isn't yours. A death that hasn't happened yet."

The headmaster cleared his throat. "Scholar Lin, perhaps we should—"

"No." Scholar Lin's hand shot out and gripped Bai Su's wrist. Her fingers were ice. "She needs to understand what she's carrying before it carries her."

The room dissolved.

Bai Su was no longer in the headmaster's office. She stood in the courtyard, but the courtyard was wrong. The trees were blackened, the stones cracked, the sky a bruised purple that bled into the horizon. Students lay scattered across the ground, their bodies twisted in impossible angles, their mouths open in screams that had long since faded.

She was holding something. A scroll. Unrolled, covered in characters that burned like hot iron against her palms.

*"The one who walks between worlds will break the seal. The one who sees what should not be seen will open the door."*

The words seared themselves into her skin, black and permanent, rising from the scroll to crawl up her arms like living things. She tried to drop the parchment, but her hands wouldn't open. The characters reached her shoulders, her neck, her face, filling her mouth and eyes and ears until she was nothing but writing, trapped in a body that was no longer hers.

*"And when the door opens, the dead will remember how to live. And the living will remember how to die."*

Then she was back in the office, gasping, her hands clean and whole. Scholar Lin had released her wrist and was now studying her with something approaching interest.

"The prophecy is incomplete," Scholar Lin said. "I've been trying to translate the rest for three hundred years. But you—" She tilted her head, studying Bai Su like a specimen. "You carry a fragment of it in your blood."

"I don't know what you're talking about." Bai Su's voice shook. She hated that it shook.

"Of course you don't. That's what makes you so useful." Scholar Lin turned to the headmaster. "She's the key. The ghost that's been killing students isn't doing it for life qi. They're doing it to complete the translation. Each death adds a character to the scroll."

The calligraphy master spoke up, his voice rough as gravel. "How many characters do they need?"

"One hundred and eight. The number of earthly desires." Scholar Lin's gold eyes glinted. "They've collected ninety-seven so far. Eleven more deaths, and the prophecy will be complete."

"Eleven more deaths?" Bai Su's stomach turned. "You mean eleven more students—"

"Will die, yes. Unless we find the ghost first." The headmaster stood, his face grim. "Which is why I've called you here, Miss Bai. Not to punish you, but to offer you a position."

"A... position?"

"Student liaison to the Ghost Affairs Committee." He slid a jade token across the desk. It was carved in the shape of an open eye, the pupil a single drop of blood-red carnelian. "You can see them. You can hear them. You've already proven you can enter places that are locked to the living. We need someone who can walk both paths."

"You want me to hunt a ghost."

"We want you to find the ghost. The hunting will be handled by others." He glanced at Scholar Lin. "Ghosts can only be truly destroyed by those who have already passed through the veil. Scholar Lin and her team will handle the confrontation. You will handle the investigation."

"And if I refuse?"

"Then you'll be expelled." The headmaster's voice was flat. "For trespassing, for damaging academy property—the broken window in the bell chamber—and for fraternizing with unauthorized spirits. The expulsion will be noted in your permanent record. No other academy will accept you. You'll never sit for the imperial exams."

Bai Su stared at the jade token. The carnelian pupil seemed to pulse, watching her with a hunger that made her skin crawl.

"What about the ghost who warned me? In the bell tower?"

Scholar Lin's expression flickered. "Describe him."

"Tall. Young. He wore scholar's robes, old style, like from a hundred years ago. He said he died before he could take the exams. He knew about the killings. He said the killer was trying to break through the veil."

The teachers exchanged glances. The calligraphy master's hand went to his throat, where a thin scar ran from collarbone to jaw.

"That sounds like Wei Chen," he said quietly. "The boy who jumped from the bell tower in the third year of the Jiajing reign."

"He's still here?"

"His ghost has never left. He's been a fixture of the academy for generations, warning students away from the tower, trying to protect them." The headmaster's voice softened. "Wei Chen was the first student to die at Wutong Academy. He's been trying to prevent others from suffering his fate ever since."

"Then he's on our side?"

"He's a ghost, Miss Bai. No ghost is entirely on the side of the living." Scholar Lin's gold eyes burned. "Especially not one who has been watching for four hundred years. Patience that long breeds desperation. And desperation breeds betrayal."

Bai Su picked up the jade token. It was cold against her skin, colder than it should have been, as if it had been stored in a tomb rather than a desk drawer.

"I'll do it."

"Good." The headmaster nodded to the door. "Your first assignment is in the archives. The ghost of a librarian has been refusing to let students access certain texts. Find out what she's hiding and report back."

"What texts?"

"Death records. Student death records, specifically, from the past fifty years." He paused. "We believe the killer may have been collecting more than just life qi. They may have been collecting names."

Bai Su tucked the token into her sleeve and turned to leave. At the door, she paused.

"Headmaster? That song. The one the ghost in the tower was singing. Do you know it?"

The room went silent. The calligraphy master's face drained of color. Scholar Lin's gold eyes widened, just slightly, the first genuine emotion Bai Su had seen from her.

"What song?" the headmaster asked carefully.

Bai Su hummed a few notes. The melody that had followed her down the stairs, sweet and familiar, like something she had known before she was born.

The headmaster's teacup shattered against the floor.

"Where did you hear that?" His voice was no longer calm. It was sharp, desperate, a man who had just seen a ghost that was not supposed to exist.

"The bell tower. The shadow that followed me—"

"That song hasn't been sung in four hundred years." Scholar Lin's hand went to her chest, where a jade pendant hung on a silver chain. "It was Wei Chen's song. The one he was singing when he jumped."

"But I heard—"

"Wei Chen didn't sing that song. He wrote it. He was the only one who ever knew the melody." Scholar Lin's voice dropped to a whisper. "He died before he could teach it to anyone else."

Bai Su's blood turned to ice.

"Then who was singing in the tower tonight?"

No one answered. The lamplight flickered, casting long shadows that danced across the walls. And in the silence, Bai Su heard it again—faint, distant, drifting through the cracks in the window frame.

The song.

But this time, it wasn't coming from the bell tower.

It was coming from the archives.

The room where she was supposed to go next.

She turned and ran, the jade token burning against her chest, the song growing louder with every step. Behind her, she heard the teachers shouting, the headmaster's voice rising in panic, but she didn't stop.

She burst through the archives door and froze.

The librarian's ghost stood in the center of the room, surrounded by floating scrolls, her translucent hands pressed against her ears. She was weeping, silent tears streaming down her spectral face.

"Make it stop," she whispered. "Please, make it stop."

The song filled the room, coming from everywhere and nowhere, sweet and terrible and familiar. It wrapped around Bai Su like a shroud, pulling her deeper into the stacks, past shelves of rotting paper and crumbling bindings, to a corner so dark that even the ghost's light couldn't penetrate.

And there, carved into the wall in characters that glowed with the same blood-red light as the jade token, was a message:

*"The eleventh death is already written. The one who sings will open the door. And the one who hears will become the door."*

Below it, in fresh ink that still dripped down the stone, a single name:

*Bai Su.*

title: Chapter 10
content:
CHAPTER 10: The Song Before Dying

The headmaster's study smelled of sandalwood and secrets. Bai Su stood before the massive rosewood desk, her hands clasped behind her back, still feeling the phantom warmth of blood that had long since vanished. Headmaster Zhou sat across from her, his fingers steepled, his eyes carrying the weight of centuries.

"We have a problem," he said.

Bai Su waited. The old man had a theatrical streak—he never delivered information without building suspense first.

"The Ministry of Rites has sent an inspector. He arrives tomorrow." Zhou pushed a scroll across the desk. The wax seal bore the Ministry's crest—a dragon coiled around a writing brush. "They've heard rumors. Whispers about our academy's 'unusual student body.'"

"Then we hide the ghosts."

"We've been hiding them for three hundred years." Zhou's voice carried no impatience, only exhaustion. "This inspector is different. He's a Seventh Rank scholar with a reputation for uncovering fraud. He exposed three fake academies last year alone. He knows what to look for."

Bai Su picked up the scroll. The writing was elegant, precise—the hand of a man who measured his words like coins.

*To Headmaster Zhou of Wutong Academy:*

*By decree of the Ministry of Rites, I shall conduct a full inspection of your institution on the fifteenth day of the eighth month. All students, living and... otherwise, shall present themselves for examination. Any irregularities will be reported to the capital.*

*Signed,*
*Inspector Lin Feng, Seventh Rank Scholar*

"He knows," Bai Su said.

"Suspects. There's a difference. But if he finds proof—" Zhou's hand trembled slightly. "They'll burn the academy to the ground. Every ghost, every record, every brick. They'll salt the earth so nothing can grow here again."

"Then we need to solve this before he arrives."

"Before that." Zhou opened a drawer and withdrew a folded piece of paper. It was yellowed, brittle, the ink faded to brown. "I found this in the library archives. It was hidden inside a copy of the *Analects*."

Bai Su unfolded it. The characters were written in a woman's hand—delicate, elegant, with flourishes that spoke of years of practice.

*To whoever finds this:*

*My name is Lin Xia. I was a student at Wutong Academy in the twenty-third year of the Kangxi Emperor's reign. I am writing this because I am afraid. I have discovered something terrible—a secret that the academy has kept for generations.*

*There is a ghost among us who is not content to simply study. They have found a way to steal life qi not just from the living, but from other ghosts. They have been doing it for centuries. Each time they take enough power, they grow stronger. Each time, they leave a trail of broken souls in their wake.*

*I know who it is. But if I write the name here, they will find this letter before anyone else does. So I will only say this: look to the tower. Look to the bell. Look to the student who never ages, who never changes, who has been studying here longer than any living memory can recall.*

*If you are reading this, I am already dead. Do not trust anyone. Not the headmaster. Not the teachers. Not the students who seem too kind, too helpful, too eager to earn your trust.*

*The killer is still here. They have always been here. And they will never leave.*

The letter ended abruptly, as if the writer had been interrupted. The bottom edge was torn, the final characters smudged by what might have been water—or tears.

"When was this written?" Bai Su asked.

"Three hundred years ago." Zhou's voice was barely a whisper. "Lin Xia was my student. She disappeared one night, three weeks before her graduation examination. We searched everywhere. The tower, the forest, the river. Nothing. No body, no trace."

"But she's still here."

"Her ghost? Perhaps." Zhou shook his head. "I've never seen her. Not once. But other students have reported seeing a woman in white robes walking the corridors at night, leaving flowers on windowsills, singing old songs."

Bai Su's blood went cold. The song. The folk song she had heard in the bell tower, sweet and familiar, like something she had known before she was born.

"What song?"

"I don't—"

"You know what song." Bai Su's voice came out harder than she intended. "The one she sang. Tell me."

Zhou's face went pale. He opened his mouth, closed it, opened it again. When he finally spoke, his voice cracked like old leather.

"*The River Lullaby.* It's an old folk song. Mothers sing it to their children in the southern provinces. It's about a girl who drowns herself because her lover betrayed her."

"Sing it."

"I can't."

"Sing it."

Zhou's hands shook as he began to hum. The melody was haunting, beautiful, carrying a weight that pressed against Bai Su's chest. Then he sang the words, his voice thin and reedy:

*"The river flows, the river knows*
*Where my true love's heartache goes*
*Underneath the silver moon*
*I will see my lover soon*

*The water's cold, the water's deep*
*Promises the soul can keep*
*Close your eyes and drift with me*
*Down to where the shadows be*

*My heart is stone, my blood is wine*
*Drink of me and make me thine*
*In the depths where no light shines*
*I will keep what once was mine."*

The last note hung in the air like smoke. Bai Su felt tears streaming down her face. She didn't know why. The song stirred something in her chest, something old and forgotten, a memory that wasn't hers.

"That's the song," she whispered.

"Lin Xia's song." Zhou's eyes were wet. "She sang it every night before bed. The other girls complained. Said it gave them nightmares."

"She's been singing it for three hundred years."

"Or someone has been singing it for her."

Bai Su looked down at the letter again. The words blurred through her tears. *Look to the student who never ages. Who never changes. Who has been studying here longer than any living memory can recall.*

"Who has been here the longest?" she asked.

"I don't—"

"The students. The ghosts. Who has been at this academy the longest?"

Zhou's face went through a series of emotions—confusion, realization, horror. "There's a student in the west wing. He's been here since before I became headmaster. Forty years ago, when I took this position, he was already old. Already a ghost. He said he died during the Qianlong era."

"Show me."

The west wing was older than the rest of the academy, built from stone that had weathered centuries of rain and war. The classrooms were empty, the desks covered in dust. Only one room showed signs of use—a small study at the end of the corridor, its door slightly ajar.

Bai Su pushed it open.

The ghost sat at a desk, copying texts by lamplight. He was old, impossibly old, his face lined with centuries of study. His robes were from a forgotten dynasty, his hair white as bone. He didn't look up when she entered.

"Student Bai," he said, his voice like dry leaves. "I wondered when you would find me."

"You knew I was coming."

"I knew someone would." He set down his brush and turned to face her. His eyes were black, completely black, with no whites at all. "I've been waiting for three hundred years."

"Lin Xia's letter."

"Ah." He smiled, a terrible expression on his ancient face. "So you found it. I wondered when someone would. I've been hiding it in different books for centuries, hoping the right student would discover it."

"*You* wrote it?"

"No." He gestured to the desk. "I've been copying it. Every ten years, I write a new copy and hide it somewhere in the library. The original crumbled to dust long ago."

"Why?"

"Because someone needs to know the truth." He leaned forward, his black eyes fixed on her face. "The killer isn't a student. It never was. It's the academy itself."

"What?"

"Wutong Academy was built on a mass grave. During the fall of the Ming dynasty, a general executed three hundred scholars here. Their bodies were piled in a pit and covered with lime. Then the Qing built this academy on top of their bones."

Bai Su felt the floor beneath her feet, solid and cold. Three hundred scholars, buried alive in the foundations.

"The ghosts we see—the drowned scholar, the suicide candidate, the murdered prodigy—they're not the original victims. They're newcomers, drawn here by the concentration of death. But the *thing* that lives beneath the academy, the amalgamation of those three hundred souls, *it* is what's been killing students for centuries."

"Then why didn't it kill you?"

"I'm not alive." He smiled again. "I died in the first year of the Qianlong era. I've been a ghost for over two hundred years. The thing beneath us feeds on life qi. I have none to offer."

"But it killed Chen Feng."

"Chen Feng was going to expose the truth. He had found the same evidence you're finding now. The thing beneath us can't allow that knowledge to escape these walls."

"Then how do I stop it?"

"You don't." The ghost's voice was flat, final. "You run. You leave this academy and never come back. You forget everything you've seen and heard. You live your life as if none of this ever happened."

"And let it keep killing?"

"It has been killing for three hundred years. One more century won't make a difference. But you—" He reached out, his hand passing through her arm like cold air. "You have a future. You have a life. Don't throw it away for a truth that will only destroy you."

The lamp flickered. The shadows in the room grew longer, darker, reaching toward her like grasping fingers.

"Do you hear that?" the ghost whispered.

Bai Su listened. From somewhere deep below, she heard it—a sound like stones grinding together, like bones shifting in the earth. And beneath that, barely audible, a voice singing.

*"The river flows, the river knows*
*Where my true love's heartache goes..."*

The floor cracked beneath her feet. Dust rose from the boards. The singing grew louder, more urgent, as if the singer was climbing toward them.

"She's coming," the ghost said. "She always comes when the truth is spoken."

"Lin Xia?"

"No." His black eyes widened with terror. "The thing that *wears* Lin Xia. The thing that stole her face and her voice and her song. The thing that has been walking these halls in her skin for three centuries."

The singing stopped.

Silence fell, heavy and absolute.

Then the door swung open, and Bai Su saw a figure standing in the corridor. A woman in white robes, her face beautiful and blank, her eyes empty as the sky. She smiled, and her mouth was full of earth and roots and bone.

"Bai Su," she said, and her voice was the sound of the river at midnight. "You've been looking for me."

Bai Su's hand went to her chest. Her heart was beating too fast, too loud, a drum that called the darkness closer.

"I'm not looking for you," she said.

"Then why have you come to my home?" The woman tilted her head, a gesture that was almost human. "Why have you been reading my letters, walking

title: The Song Before Drowning
content:
The headmaster's study smelled of ink and decay.

Bai Su stood before the massive rosewood desk, her hands still trembling despite having scrubbed them raw in the courtyard well. The old blood was gone—had it ever been there? She couldn't trust her eyes anymore. Couldn't trust anything.

Headmaster Zhou sat across from her, his face carved from stone. Behind him, a painting of the academy's founder watched with painted eyes that seemed to follow her every movement.

"You were in the bell chamber," he said. Not a question.

"I was following a ghost."

"Which ghost?"

"The scholar who died before the exams. He showed me the window where Chen Feng fell."

Zhou's left eye twitched. A muscle beneath his beard clenched. "What else did he show you?"

Bai Su hesitated. The blood. The footprint. The song she couldn't forget. "Nothing. He disappeared when the bell rang."

"The bell." Zhou stood abruptly, his chair scraping against the floor. He walked to the window, his back to her. "The bell hasn't rung in three hundred years, Miss Bai. Not since the last execution."

"Execution?"

The headmaster turned. For a moment, he looked old. Ancient. As if the years he had kept hidden were finally catching up with him.

"In the early days of the academy, a student was caught cheating on the imperial examinations. The punishment was death by public execution. They hanged him from the bell tower at dawn. The bell rang as he died—once for each year of his life. He was nineteen."

Bai Su's blood turned to ice. "Chen Feng was nineteen."

"Yes."

"And you're telling me the bell rang for him too."

"I'm telling you nothing." Zhou returned to his desk, his composure sliding back into place like a mask. "I'm telling you to stay away from the bell tower. Stay away from the ghosts. Focus on your studies, and you might survive until graduation."

"Survive? Or pass?"

The headmaster's eyes narrowed. "Is there a difference?"

Bai Su opened her mouth to answer, but a sound cut through the room. A song. Faint, distant, rising from somewhere below.

It was the same song she had heard in the bell tower. The same voice, sweet and familiar, singing an old folk tune about a girl who drowned in a well.

Zhou's face went white. "You hear that?"

"Yes."

"That's impossible."

The song grew louder. The floor beneath them vibrated. The painting on the wall rattled, the founder's eyes now weeping black tears that dripped down the canvas like blood.

"Who's singing?" Bai Su demanded.

Zhou didn't answer. He was staring at the door, his hands gripping the desk so hard his knuckles cracked.

The door burst open.

A servant stumbled in, her face twisted in terror. "Headmaster! The well in the east courtyard—there's a body!"

They ran.

The east courtyard was chaos when they arrived. Students crowded around the stone well, their faces a mix of horror and morbid curiosity. The ghost students stood apart from the living, their translucent forms flickering like candle flames in the wind.

Bai Su pushed through the crowd. The body lay face-down beside the well, dressed in student robes, water still dripping from their clothes. A girl. Her hair fanned out around her head like seaweed, tangled with leaves and mud.

The servant who had found her was weeping. "I was drawing water for the kitchen, and I saw her floating. She was floating, Headmaster, just beneath the surface, and her eyes were open, and she was—she was *singing*—"

"Silence." Zhou knelt beside the body. He turned it over.

Bai Su's breath caught.

The girl's face was blue-white, her lips peeled back from her teeth in a frozen scream. Her eyes were open, fixed on something no living person could see. But that wasn't what made Bai Su stumble backward, her hand flying to her mouth.

The girl's hands were bound behind her back with red silk cord. The same red silk cord that had tied the curtains in the bell chamber. The same red silk that had been used to hang the cheating student three hundred years ago.

And in her mouth, stuffed between her teeth, was an examination scroll.

Zhou pulled it out with steady hands, unrolling it despite the water damage. His face darkened as he read.

"What does it say?" Bai Su asked.

He handed it to her without a word.

The scroll was blank. Completely blank. But as Bai Su held it, the ink began to appear, bleeding through the paper like fresh wounds.

*"The drowned girl knows the truth. Ask her where she learned the song."*

Bai Su looked up. The ghost students were staring at her. All of them. Their hollow eyes fixed on her face, their mouths moving in unison, singing the same folk song under their breath.

*"She went to fetch water, a bucket in her hand. She fell into the well, and no one heard her land..."*

"What do you want?" Bai Su whispered.

One stepped forward. A boy with a pale face and dark circles under his eyes. He was young, no more than fifteen, his neck bent at an unnatural angle.

"You can hear us," he said, his voice like wind through dead leaves. "Really hear us. Not like the others."

"Who are you?"

"Nobody. A failed candidate. I hanged myself in the dormitory when I failed my exams. I've been here for sixty years." He smiled, and his teeth were black. "But that's not why I'm talking to you."

"Then why?"

He pointed at the dead girl. "I knew her. She arrived three days ago. A living student. She was always happy, always humming that song. I asked her where she learned it."

"What did she say?"

"She said she didn't know. She said she woke up one morning with it in her head, like someone had put it there while she was sleeping." He leaned closer, his breath cold against her cheek. "The same song is in my head now. And it's in yours too, isn't it?"

Bai Su wanted to deny it. But the song was there, humming beneath her thoughts, a constant presence she couldn't shake.

"Yes."

"Then you're already marked." He pulled back, his form flickering. "The song is a curse. It spreads from mind to mind. Once you hear it, you can't forget it. And once you can't forget it, you start to *remember*."

"Remember what?"

"Whatever the singer wants you to remember."

The dead girl on the ground twitched.

The crowd screamed and scrambled backward. The ghost students didn't move. They just watched as the drowned girl sat up, water streaming from her hair, her eyes still wide and staring.

She opened her mouth. Sang one note. High and pure and terrible.

Then she spoke.

"I fell into the well," she said, her voice hollow, echoing. "But I didn't fall. I was pushed. And the one who pushed me was wearing a red robe."

The ghost students erupted. Whispers flew through the crowd like wildfire. Red robes. The uniform of the academy's founders. The original teachers who had presided over the execution three hundred years ago.

"Who?" Bai Su demanded. "Who pushed you?"

The drowned girl's head turned, her neck cracking, her gaze locking onto something behind Bai Su.

"He's standing right there."

Bai Su spun around.

No one was there. Just empty space, a patch of shadow beneath the willow tree.

But the shadow moved.

It rose, taking shape, becoming a figure in a red robe, his face hidden beneath a hood. He was tall, impossibly tall, his hands long and thin, tipped with nails black as obsidian.

"The executioner," the ghost boy whispered. "He's come back."

The figure raised his hand. Pointed at Bai Su.

"You," he said, his voice the sound of grinding bones. "You have seen the bell chamber. You have heard the song. You have touched the blood."

"I don't know what you're talking about."

"You do." He stepped forward, and the ground froze beneath his feet. Frost crept across the stones, spreading like veins. "You were there, Bai Su. You were there when Chen Feng fell. You were there when the first student was hanged. You have always been there."

"That's impossible. I wasn't born yet."

"Birth is a door." He took another step. "Death is a door. And you, little scholar, have walked through both more times than you can count."

The dead girl screamed. The song exploded from her throat, filling the courtyard, shaking the walls, cracking the stones. The ghost students joined in, their voices rising, a chorus of the damned singing a song older than memory.

Bai Su clamped her hands over her ears, but the song was inside her head, inside her bones, inside her soul.

And in the chaos, the red-robed figure faded, leaving behind only a whisper.

"Find the well beneath the well, Bai Su. Find the truth beneath the truth. Or join the choir."

The song stopped.

The drowned girl collapsed, lifeless once more.

Bai Su stood alone in the courtyard, surrounded by ghosts and bodies and the weight of a memory that wasn't hers.

She looked at her hands. The blood was back.

And this time, it was fresh.

title: Chapter 12
content:
CHAPTER 12: The Song Before Death

The headmaster's study smelled of old paper and newer lies.

Bai Su stood before the massive rosewood desk, her hands clasped behind her back to hide their trembling. Headmaster Zhao sat across from her, his fingers steepled, his eyes never quite meeting hers. Behind him, a painting of Confucius stared down with judgment that felt far too personal.

"You understand the gravity of the situation, Miss Bai." His voice was honey over gravel, smooth and rough at once. "A living student, dead. On academy grounds. The magistrate will be here by nightfall."

"I understand."

"Do you?" He finally looked at her, and Bai Su saw something flicker in his gaze—not suspicion, but fear. "Because I find it rather curious that you were seen entering the bell tower moments before the body was discovered."

"I was following a ghost."

The headmaster's face went pale. "Miss Bai, I must warn you—"

"Everyone knows, Headmaster." She said it flatly, without accusation. "Half the students here are dead. The other half can see them. You don't need to protect the secret from me."

Silence stretched between them like a wire about to snap.

Then Headmaster Zhao did something unexpected. He laughed. It was a dry, brittle sound, like autumn leaves crumbling underfoot.

"You're sharper than you look, Miss Bai. Most new students take weeks to figure it out. Some never do." He leaned back in his chair, the wood groaning in protest. "Fine. You want the truth? Here it is: Chen Feng was murdered. By a ghost. And that ghost is getting stronger."

"Why Chen Feng?"

"He was a conduit." The headmaster opened a drawer and pulled out a scroll, yellowed with age. "Chen Feng's family line has a particular... affinity for spiritual energy. Every generation produces one child who can channel qi between worlds. He was this generation's bridge."

Bai Su's blood ran cold. "The killer wanted his qi."

"More than that." The headmaster unrolled the scroll, revealing a complex diagram of intersecting circles and characters. "They wanted his position. Chen Feng wasn't just any student—he was the anchor. The reason the living and dead can coexist here without tearing each other apart."

"And now he's gone."

"Now he's gone." The headmaster's voice dropped to a whisper. "Which means the boundary between our world and theirs is thinning. Every hour that passes, it weakens. By tomorrow night, it will be gone entirely."

"What happens then?"

"The ghosts won't be bound to the academy anymore. They'll be free to roam the living world. And the living world will be free to see them." He rolled the scroll back up with trembling hands. "Do you know what happens when ten thousand angry spirits descend on a city that doesn't believe in ghosts?"

Bai Su didn't answer. She didn't need to.

"Find the killer, Miss Bai. Find them before the magistrate arrives, before the boundary collapses, before we all become casualties of a war between the living and the dead." He handed her the scroll. "This maps every spirit node on the academy grounds. The killer will be drawn to them like a moth to flame. Follow the nodes. Find the killer."

"And if I don't?"

"Then pray the magistrate is merciful." He turned away, signaling the conversation was over. "Because I won't be."

---

Bai Su stepped out of the study into the courtyard, the scroll clutched against her chest like a shield. The sun was setting, painting the academy in shades of amber and rust. Students milled about, some solid, some translucent, all wearing the same expression of forced normalcy.

She unrolled the scroll and studied the diagram. Seventeen nodes, arranged in a pattern she didn't recognize. Three were marked in red—the bell tower, the library, and the west dormitory. The first two she already knew. The third was new.

The west dormitory.

She started walking before she could talk herself out of it.

The west dormitory was the oldest building on campus, a three-story structure of weathered brick and sagging eaves. It had been condemned for years, the windows boarded up, the doors chained shut. But as Bai Su approached, she noticed the chains were broken, hanging loose like dead snakes.

Someone had been here recently.

She pushed the door open, the hinges screaming in protest. Inside, the air was thick with dust and something else—something that smelled like wet earth and rotting flowers. The floorboards creaked beneath her feet, each step sending echoes through the empty halls.

The main staircase loomed ahead, its banister carved with faces that seemed to watch her as she passed. Bai Su climbed slowly, her hand trailing along the wall, feeling for the cold spots that marked spiritual activity.

Second floor. Empty.

Third floor. Empty.

But the roof access door was open.

She stepped out onto the flat rooftop, and the wind hit her like a wall. The sun was almost gone now, the sky bleeding into deep purple. Below, the academy spread out like a map, the buildings casting long shadows that seemed to move of their own accord.

And in the center of the roof, waiting for her, was a girl.

She was young—maybe sixteen—with hair that fell to her waist and a face that would have been pretty if not for the hollow emptiness in her eyes. She wore the academy uniform, but it was torn and stained, the fabric hanging loose on a frame that was too thin, too fragile.

"You're the new girl." Her voice was soft, almost gentle. "The one who can see."

"Who are you?"

"My name is Lin Wei. I died here, seven years ago." She smiled, but it didn't reach her eyes. "I was the first."

"First what?"

"The first to figure it out." Lin Wei walked to the edge of the roof and looked down. "The first to realize that the boundary between life and death is thinner than anyone wants to admit. The first to try and cross back."

Bai Su's hand tightened on the scroll. "You killed Chen Feng."

"I didn't." Lin Wei turned, and there was something like pity in her gaze. "I'm trying to stop the one who did."

"Then why are you here?"

"Because the killer is going to strike again. Tonight. At the third node." She pointed to the scroll in Bai Su's hands. "The library. They're going to drain another living student, take their qi, and use it to tear the boundary open completely."

Bai Su's heart stopped. "Who?"

"Someone you trust." Lin Wei stepped closer, close enough that Bai Su could see the cracks in her skin, the places where her spirit was fraying at the edges. "Someone who's been watching you since the moment you arrived. Someone who knows your name, your history, your fears."

"Tell me."

"I can't." Lin Wei's voice broke. "If I tell you, they'll know. They'll come for me, and I'll be gone, and then there will be no one left to guide you."

"Then what do I do?"

Lin Wei reached out and touched Bai Su's forehead. A jolt of cold electricity shot through her skull, and suddenly she was somewhere else.

---

She was standing in a classroom, but it wasn't the one she knew. The desks were arranged differently, the walls covered in writing she couldn't read. Students sat in neat rows, their faces blank, their eyes fixed on a figure at the front of the room.

A teacher. Tall. Thin. Wearing robes that seemed to absorb the light around them.

"You're late, Miss Bai."

The voice was familiar. Horrifyingly familiar.

"The murder has already begun. But you can still save the next victim." The teacher turned, and Bai Su saw their face.

It was her own.

"Find me before it's too late." The doppelgänger smiled, a cold, cruel thing. "Or join me in the grave."

---

Bai Su gasped, stumbling backward. She was back on the rooftop, the stars wheeling overhead. Lin Wei stood before her, her form flickering like a candle in the wind.

"You saw." It wasn't a question.

"A teacher. Someone who looks like me."

"Someone who wants to *be* you." Lin Wei's voice was barely a whisper now. "They've been collecting faces, collecting lives. Chen Feng was just the beginning. You're the endgame."

"Why me?"

"Because you're the only one who can stop them." Lin Wei's form began to dissolve, her edges bleeding into the night air. "Find the library. Save the next victim. And whatever you do, don't trust anyone who wears a smile."

"Wait—"

But Lin Wei was gone, her last words scattering on the wind like ash.

Bai Su stood alone on the rooftop, the scroll clutched to her chest, her mind reeling. Below, the academy lights flickered and died, plunging the grounds into darkness.

And in that darkness, she heard it again.

The song.

The old folk song, sweet and familiar, drifting up from the courtyard below. She looked down and saw a figure standing in the center of the empty square, their face hidden in shadow.

They were singing.

And they were looking right at her.

Bai Su backed away from the edge, her heart hammering. The song grew louder, more insistent, wrapping around her like a cold embrace. She turned to run—

And found herself face to face with the ghost scholar from the bell tower.

"You're out of time," he said.

"I know."

"He's already in the library. The next victim is waiting."

"Who is he?"

The ghost scholar's eyes flickered with something like regret. "Someone you've already met, Miss Bai. Someone you've already trusted." He stepped aside, revealing the door behind him. "The library is three minutes away. Run."

Bai Su ran.

She didn't look back.

She didn't see the ghost scholar's lips move as he whispered the song she had heard, the song that followed her down the stairs, through the halls, across the courtyard.

She didn't see him smile.

And she didn't see the figure in the square lower their hood, revealing a face that was eerily familiar.

A face that belonged to a student who had died three years ago.

A face that had been haunting the bell tower since the day she arrived.

The ghost scholar watched Bai Su disappear into the library, then turned to the figure in the square.

"It's done," he said.

The figure nodded. "Good. Now we wait."

"For what?"

"For her to realize the truth." The figure smiled, and in the darkness, their teeth glinted like knives. "That she's been dead all along."

High above, the bell tower rang.

Once.

Twice.

Three times.

And then, silence.

title: Chapter 13
content:
CHAPTER 13: The Blood That Binds

The headmaster's office smelled of ink and dried chrysanthemums. Bai Su stood before the massive rosewood desk, her hands clasped behind her back to hide their trembling. The blood had vanished from her skin, but she could still feel it—a sticky residue that memory refused to release.

Headmaster Wen sat motionless, his fingers steepled beneath his chin. He was old in the way mountains were old, his face a landscape of wrinkles that seemed to shift when you weren't looking directly at them. Behind him, the wall was covered in examination scrolls—thousands of them, stacked from floor to ceiling, their paper yellowed with centuries of waiting.

"You've been exploring places you shouldn't," he said. His voice carried no accusation, only a tired resignation.

"I was following a lead."

"A lead that took you to the bell chamber." He reached into his sleeve and produced a piece of glass—a shard from the broken window, its edges still sharp. "Where Chen Feng died."

Bai Su's throat tightened. "How did you know I was there?"

"The ghosts told me." Headmaster Wen set the glass on his desk. "They whisper, Miss Bai. They always whisper. It's one of the few pleasures they have left."

She wanted to ask if he could see them too, but the question died on her lips. Some truths were better left unspoken in an academy where half the students had already crossed the river Styx.

"I found a footprint," she said instead. "In the dust. It matched my shoe."

"Did it now."

"I don't remember being there before. I don't remember—" She stopped, her hands clenching. "I don't remember anything from that night. Not Chen Feng's death. Not climbing the tower. Nothing."

Headmaster Wen was quiet for a long moment. Then he stood, his joints cracking like old wood, and walked to the window. Below, the courtyard was empty—the living students were in class, the ghost students were wherever ghosts went during daylight hours.

"There's a story," he said, "that the old examiners used to tell. About a candidate who cheated on the imperial examination. He hid answers in his sleeve, copied from a scholar who had studied for ten years. The examiners caught him, of course. They always do."

He turned to face her, his eyes dark pools of memory.

"The punishment was death. But the candidate's family pleaded for mercy. They offered gold, land, their ancestral home—everything they had. The examiners accepted. They took the bribe and let the candidate live."

Bai Su waited. She knew there was more.

"The candidate went on to become a magistrate. A respected one, even. He judged cases, passed sentences, sent men to their deaths for crimes they may or may not have committed. And every night, he woke up screaming, because the ghost of the scholar he had cheated from was standing at the foot of his bed, holding the examination scroll that should have been his."

"The scholar died?"

"Of a broken heart. He had studied his entire life for that exam, and when he failed—not because of his own inadequacy, but because someone stole his work—he couldn't bear it. He hanged himself in the examination hall." Headmaster Wen returned to his desk. "The magistrate lived to be eighty years old. But he never slept a full night. Never smiled. Never knew peace."

"What does this have to do with me?"

"You asked about the memory loss. About the footprint that matches your shoe." He opened a drawer and pulled out a scroll, yellowed with age. "This is Chen Feng's examination scroll. The one he was writing when he died."

He unrolled it on the desk. The paper was covered in neat calligraphy—a scholar's hand, precise and confident. But halfway through, the writing changed. It became jagged, desperate, the strokes barely legible.

*I see her. She's always there. In the corner of my eye, in the mirror, in the space between heartbeats. She smiles and I can't breathe. She whispers and I can't hear anything else. I know what she wants. I know what she's doing. But I can't stop her because she's already inside me.*

*She's been inside me since the first day I came to this academy.*

Bai Su's blood turned to ice.

"Who is 'she'?"

"Read the last line."

She forced her eyes down the scroll, past the cramped, frantic handwriting, to the final sentence. It was written in a different hand entirely—elegant, flowing, almost beautiful.

*Bai Su. You know who I am. You've always known.*

The scroll slipped from her fingers and landed on the desk, face up. The words stared at her, black ink on yellowed paper, a message from beyond the grave.

"That's not possible," she whispered. "Chen Feng died before I even enrolled. He couldn't have known my name."

"Couldn't he?" Headmaster Wen's voice was soft, almost gentle. "Ghosts see things differently, Miss Bai. They exist outside of time, in the spaces between moments. Chen Feng may have died before you arrived, but that doesn't mean he didn't see you coming."

"This is insane."

"Is it?" He leaned forward. "You came to this academy because you see ghosts. You always have. But have you ever asked yourself why? Why you, and not someone else? Why this gift, this curse, was given to you?"

"Gifts aren't given," she said bitterly. "They're inflicted."

"Perhaps. But they're always inflicted for a reason."

A knock at the door made them both turn. The servant girl from before stood in the doorway, her face even paler than it had been in the bell chamber.

"Headmaster Wen, forgive the interruption, but there's—there's been an incident."

"What kind of incident?"

"A student. In the east dormitory." The servant's voice dropped to a whisper. "A living student. He's dead."

The words hit Bai Su like a physical blow. Another death. Another body. The killer was still active, still hunting, still stealing life qi from the living.

"Who?" Headmaster Wen demanded.

"Lin Zhao. The third-year from Jiangxi."

Bai Su's mind raced. Lin Zhao. She had seen him in the dining hall, laughing with friends, his face full of life and youth. He had been studying for the provincial exams, dreaming of becoming a scholar-official, of making his family proud.

Now he was nothing but a vessel for stolen qi.

"I need to see the body," she said.

Headmaster Wen shook his head. "The authorities will be here soon. You shouldn't involve yourself further."

"Involve myself?" She laughed, a hollow, bitter sound. "Headmaster, the killer wrote my name in a dead boy's examination scroll. I think I'm already involved."

She didn't wait for his permission. She turned and walked out of the office, her footsteps echoing in the empty hallway. Behind her, she heard Headmaster Wen say something to the servant, but the words didn't register.

All she could hear was the whisper from the bell chamber.

*Bai Su. You know who I am. You've always known.*

---

The east dormitory was chaos.

Students crowded the hallway, their faces a mixture of fear and morbid curiosity. Living and dead alike pressed against the barricade that the prefects had set up, trying to catch a glimpse of what lay beyond.

Bai Su pushed through the crowd, ignoring the protests and the grasping hands. She reached the door to Lin Zhao's room and stopped.

The ghost scholar was standing there, his translucent form blocking the entrance.

"You don't want to go in there," he said.

"Move."

"Bai Su, listen to me. This is different from Chen Feng. This is—"

"I said move."

He hesitated, then stepped aside. "Don't say I didn't warn you."

She pushed open the door.

The room was pristine. Neatly made bed, organized desk, books arranged in perfect rows on the shelf. It looked like the room of a scholar who took pride in his studies, who believed that order in one's surroundings led to order in one's mind.

Lin Zhao was sitting at his desk.

He was facing the wall, his back to the door, his brush still in hand. A half-finished essay lay before him, the ink still wet.

Bai Su walked around to see his face.

She wished she hadn't.

His eyes were open. That was the first thing she noticed. Wide open, staring at nothing, their usual brightness replaced by a dull, milky haze. His mouth was slightly parted, as if he had been about to speak.

But it was his hands that made her stomach turn.

Both palms were facing up, resting on the desk as if in supplication. And in the center of each palm, carved with surgical precision, was a single character.

Left hand: *Sheng*. Life.

Right hand: *Si*. Death.

"The killer took more than his qi this time," the ghost scholar said from behind her. "They took his memories. His skills. Everything that made him who he was."

"How do you know that?"

"Because I can see the marks." He pointed to Lin Zhao's forehead. "There. Faint, but there. The killer carved something into his soul before they left."

Bai Su leaned closer. In the dim light, she could barely make out the shape—a series of characters, written in reverse, as if meant to be read in a mirror.

She read them anyway.

*The first death was an accident. The second was practice. The third was necessity. The fourth will be revelation.*

*And you, Bai Su, will be the one to complete the circle.*

"The fourth," she breathed. "He's planning to kill again."

"He's planning to kill *you*."

She spun around. The ghost scholar was standing in the doorway, his face unreadable.

"How do you know that?"

"Because I've seen this before." His voice was hollow, ancient. "Long ago, when I was still alive, there was a ritual. A forbidden one, practiced by desperate scholars who had failed the exams one too many times. It required four deaths—three to gather the qi, and a fourth to transfer it."

"Transfer it to whom?"

"To the one who performed the ritual." He stepped closer, his translucent form passing through the doorway as if it wasn't there. "The fourth death is always the most important. It's the key that unlocks the door. The sacrifice that makes everything possible."

"And the person who dies fourth?"

"Becomes the vessel." He stopped in front of her, his eyes meeting hers. "The killer doesn't just want to become human again, Bai Su. They want to become *you*. Your identity, your memories, your place in the world of the living."

"That's impossible."

"Is it?" He gestured to Lin Zhao's body. "They've already learned how to steal qi. How to carve memories into flesh. How to write their will into a dead man's hand. Do you really think walking in your skin is beyond them?"

Bai Su's hands were shaking again. She looked down at her palms, half-expecting to see the characters carved into her own flesh.

They were clean.

For now.

"The bell tower," she said suddenly. "The footprint. The blood on my hands. You said I had been there before, but I don't remember. What if—" She stopped, the words catching in her throat. "What if I'm the killer? What if I've been doing this all along, and I just don't remember?"

The ghost scholar was quiet for a long moment.

"I don't think you're the killer," he said finally.

"Why not?"

title: Chapter 14
content:
CHAPTER 14: The Song Before the Fall

The headmaster's office looked nothing like Bai Su expected.

Instead of the stern, scholarly chamber she had imagined—scrolls lining the walls, a scholar's desk piled with examination papers—she found herself in what appeared to be a greenhouse. Glass panels slanted overhead, letting in the gray afternoon light. Strange plants grew from ceramic pots, their leaves silver and blue, pulsing with a faint luminescence that cast shifting shadows across the walls.

Headmaster Wen stood with his back to her, watering a vine that coiled around a bronze stand. The plant's flowers were black, each petal shaped like a human eye.

"Close the door, Miss Bai."

She obeyed. The latch clicked shut with a sound like a cage locking.

"You've been to the bell tower." It wasn't a question. He turned, setting down his watering can with deliberate care. "I felt the disturbance. Old magic, waking up. The kind that shouldn't be disturbed."

"I found a ghost."

"Yes." He moved to his desk—a simple wooden thing, completely bare except for a single scroll. "You found Chen Feng. Or rather, he found you. He's been waiting for someone who could see him for forty-three years."

"You knew?"

"I suspected." Headmaster Wen unrolled the scroll. It was blank. "The bell tower has been sealed since before I took this position. Official reason: structural instability. Unofficial reason: the last person who went in there came out with a ghost attached to their shadow."

Bai Su's skin crawled. She resisted the urge to look behind her.

"Chen Feng told me about the murder. About the ghost who's been killing students for their qi."

"He told you the truth." The headmaster's voice dropped. "But not all of it."

"What do you mean?"

He gestured for her to approach. When she stood before his desk, he reached into his sleeve and produced a small porcelain vial. The liquid inside was red. Not like blood—like crushed petals, suspended in some kind of oil.

"Put your hand out."

"Why?"

"Because you've been touched by something that doesn't want to be found. And if you want to survive the night, you need to see what's been hiding in your blind spot."

Bai Su hesitated. Every instinct screamed at her to run. But she had come here for answers. And running had never helped anyone at Wutong Academy.

She extended her hand.

The headmaster uncorked the vial and poured a single drop onto her palm. It burned cold, spreading across her skin like liquid ice. The red stain grew, tracing lines across her palm, her wrist, her fingers—forming characters she couldn't read.

"When the moon rises," he said, "look at your hands. The characters will show you what your eyes cannot see. The dead leave traces on the living. You just need the right eyes to read them."

"What is this? Some kind of ghost detection spell?"

"Something older. A scholar's trick from before the academy was built. When the land here was still wild, and ghosts walked openly among men." He capped the vial and returned it to his sleeve. "Now. Tell me everything Chen Feng said. Leave nothing out."

She did. The headmaster listened without interruption, his face unreadable. When she finished, he was silent for a long moment.

"Chen Feng is wrong about one thing," he finally said. "The killer isn't trying to become human again."

"Then what are they trying to do?"

"Humanity is a cage. It has limits. A body grows old. A mind forgets. A soul wears thin from being worn." He looked at her with eyes that seemed suddenly ancient. "What if you could become something else? Something that never dies, never forgets, never weakens?"

"A god."

"Close. A ghost that remembers how to live is dangerous. But a ghost that learns how to feed on the living—that becomes something else entirely. Something the old texts call a *shengshi*."

"Living corpse."

"Clever girl." He almost smiled. "A *shengshi* is a ghost that has consumed enough life qi to manifest a physical body. Not resurrected—remade. They can touch the living, walk in sunlight, even eat and drink. But inside, they are still dead. Hollow. Hungry."

"And this one has been killing students to become a *shengshi*?"

"Not just to become one. To *remain* one. The transformation requires constant feeding. If they stop, the body decays. The hunger grows unbearable." He paused. "There were three *shengshi* in the history of this academy. The first one killed seventeen students before a wandering exorcist put them down. The second one was a teacher. She fed on her own students for two years before anyone noticed."

"And the third?"

"The third one is still here."

The words hung in the air like smoke. Bai Su's blood turned to ice.

"Chen Feng was killed by a *shengshi*?"

"No. Chen Feng was killed by someone trying to *become* one." The headmaster leaned forward. "The question you should be asking is why Chen Feng's ghost has been waiting forty-three years. Revenge? Justice? Or something else entirely?"

"Something else?"

"Ghosts lie, Miss Bai. Even the ones who think they're telling the truth. They forget things. They twist things. They remember their own deaths a thousand different ways, each one more convenient than the last." He sat back. "Chen Feng told you he was pushed. But did he tell you why?"

"He said he found something. Evidence."

"Evidence of what?"

Bai Su opened her mouth—and realized she didn't know. Chen Feng had died before he could tell her.

"That's what I thought." The headmaster stood. "The bell tower is unsealed now. Whatever Chen Feng found is still there, waiting to be discovered. But you don't have much time."

"The bell ringing. What did it mean?"

"It means the veil between worlds is thinning. Ten rings—that's a countdown. Ten days until the barrier breaks completely. Ten days until the *shengshi* can walk freely among the living."

"Ten days to find the killer."

"Less than that." He moved to the door and opened it. "The killer knows you're looking. They've been watching you since you arrived. Every step you've taken, every person you've spoken to—they've seen it all."

"How do you know?"

"Because I've been watching too." He smiled, and for a moment, his face flickered. The warm light of the greenhouse turned cold, and Bai Su saw something move beneath his skin. "And I've seen the shadow following you. The one that leaves no footprints. The one that sings old songs in the dark."

Bai Su's hand went to her chest. The mark from the bell tower was warm again.

"What song?"

"I don't know the name. But the words are familiar." He hummed a few notes—a melody that twisted through the air like smoke. "A lullaby. About a child who drowned in a well, and the mother who followed them down."

"That's a ghost story."

"All ghost stories are true, Miss Bai. That's what makes them terrifying." He stepped aside. "You should go. The sun is setting, and the academy changes after dark. The ghosts grow bolder. The walls grow thinner. And the *shengshi*—" He paused. "Well. You'll find out soon enough."

Bai Su walked past him into the hallway. The door began to close behind her.

"Headmaster?"

"Yes?"

"Why did you help me? You could have let me keep stumbling in the dark."

A long pause. When he spoke, his voice was barely a whisper.

"Because forty-three years ago, I was the one who found Chen Feng's body. And I've been carrying his ghost ever since." The door clicked shut. "Find the truth, Miss Bai. For both of us."

---

The sun had set by the time Bai Su reached her dormitory.

The courtyard was empty. The lanterns that lined the paths had been lit, casting pools of yellow light that did nothing to dispel the darkness between them. The air was cold, carrying the scent of wet stone and dying flowers.

She stopped at the entrance to her building.

Someone was standing in the shadows of the archway.

"Miss Bai."

The voice was familiar. Female. Young.

Wei Ling stepped into the light. Her face was pale, her eyes red-rimmed, as if she had been crying. But her voice was steady.

"I need to tell you something. About the night Chen Feng died."

Bai Su's hand went to her pocket, where the vial of red liquid sat warm against her thigh.

"I'm listening."

"I was there." Wei Ling's voice cracked. "I saw everything. And I've been too afraid to speak."

"Afraid of what?"

"Afraid of *her*." Wei Ling's eyes darted to the side, checking the shadows. "The girl who killed him. She's still here. She's been pretending to be a student for four years."

"A living student? Or a ghost?"

"Both." Wei Ling stepped closer. Her breath fogged in the cold air. "She found a way to anchor herself to the living world. She kills one student every year, takes their qi, and uses it to maintain her body. But this year, something went wrong."

"What?"

"The headmaster sealed the bell tower. Her anchor was inside. She's been desperate to get back in, to retrieve it before the seal breaks and she loses her hold on this world entirely."

"Why are you telling me this now?"

"Because she knows you're close to discovering the truth. And tonight—" Wei Ling grabbed Bai Su's arm. Her grip was like iron. "Tonight, she's coming for you."

The lantern above them flickered. Went out.

Darkness swallowed the courtyard.

Bai Su heard footsteps. Slow. Deliberate. Coming from the direction of the bell tower.

"Run," Wei Ling whispered. "Run now."

But Bai Su couldn't move.

Because the footsteps were accompanied by a sound she recognized.

A song.

Sweet and familiar, like something she had known before she was born.

The lullaby of the drowned child.

The footsteps stopped behind her.

A cold hand touched her shoulder.

And a voice—gentle, almost loving—whispered in her ear:

"You shouldn't have gone to the tower, little scholar. Now you've seen things that weren't meant to be seen."

Bai Su turned.

Standing behind her was a girl she had never seen before.

Beautiful. Pale. With eyes like dead stars and a smile that stretched too wide.

And in her hands, she held a scroll.

Blood dripped from its edges.

"Let me show you what happens to students who ask too many questions."

The last thing Bai Su saw before the darkness swallowed her was Wei Ling's face—twisted in an expression she couldn't read.

Triumph.

Or grief.

title: Chapter 15
content:
CHAPTER 15: The Song Beneath the Skin

The headmaster's office smelled of ink and age, of secrets pressed between parchment like pressed flowers. Bai Su sat in the same chair she had occupied on her first day at Wutong Academy, but everything felt different now. The room seemed smaller, the shadows longer, the silence between heartbeats heavier.

Headmaster Zhou sat across from her, his hands folded on the desk. He was a man who had mastered the art of stillness, but tonight his fingers twitched, tapping an irregular rhythm against the wood.

"You've been busy, Miss Bai."

It wasn't a question.

"I found the bell chamber," she said. "The one where Chen Feng—"

"I know what you found." His voice cut through her words like a blade. "I know you spoke to the ghost in the library. I know you've been asking questions about the missing students. I know you visited the well at midnight, and I know what you saw in its reflection."

Bai Su's throat tightened. "How?"

"Because I am the headmaster of an academy where half the students are dead." He leaned back, his chair creaking. "I make it my business to know everything that happens within these walls. Including when a living student decides to play detective."

"Then you know someone is killing students. Both living and dead."

"I know that the veil between life and death has been thinning for months. I know that seven ghost students have vanished, their existence erased from the academy's records as if they never existed. I know that three living students have fallen into comas, their life qi drained, their bodies empty shells." He paused. "And I know that the killer is getting stronger."

Bai Su leaned forward. "Then why haven't you done anything?"

"Because I can't."

The admission hung in the air between them, raw and vulnerable. Headmaster Zhou looked older than she had ever seen him, the lines on his face deepening, the light in his eyes dimming.

"The killer," he said slowly, "is protected. By something older than this academy, older than the empire itself. Every time I try to investigate, I find my footsteps turning back. Every time I send someone to look, they come back with no memory of where they went. The academy itself is conspiring to hide the truth."

"The academy?"

"The building. The grounds. The very soil upon which we stand." He pressed his palm against the desk. "Wutong Academy was built on a graveyard, Miss Bai. Not ordinary graves. Mass graves. Thousands of bodies, piled layer upon layer, going back centuries. The imperial exam candidates who died of plague, of starvation, of broken hearts and broken minds. They were buried here because there was nowhere else to put them."

Bai Su felt the temperature in the room drop. "You're saying the entire academy is haunted."

"I'm saying the entire academy is *alive*." His eyes met hers, dark and serious. "And it has a will of its own. It chooses who to protect, who to hide, who to reveal. It has chosen to protect the killer."

"Then why am I able to find clues? Why did the ghost in the library speak to me? Why did the well show me the truth?"

Headmaster Zhou smiled, but there was no warmth in it. "Because the academy has also chosen you. As its witness."

A knock at the door interrupted them. The servant girl from earlier entered, her face even paler than before.

"Headmaster Zhou, there's been an incident. In the east dormitory. Room 47."

Bai Su was on her feet before the servant finished speaking. "That's my room."

She ran.

The east dormitory was chaos. Students crowded the hallway, their faces a mixture of fear and morbid curiosity. They parted as Bai Su pushed through, their whispers following her like smoke.

"Did you see her face?"

"The characters were written in blood—"

"—not blood, something worse—"

She reached Room 47 and stopped.

The door was open. Inside, her roommate Lin Mei stood frozen in the center of the room, her hands pressed against her mouth, her eyes wide with horror. On the wall above her bed, written in characters that seemed to crawl across the plaster, was a message:

*THE DEAD REMEMBER YOU, BAI SU*

*YOU WERE THERE*

*YOU SAW*

*YOU DID NOTHING*

Below the message, in smaller script:

*THE TENTH RING*

*THE FALLING BOY*

*YOUR HANDS*

*YOUR HANDS*

*YOUR HANDS*

Bai Su's own hands began to tremble. She looked down at them, half-expecting to see blood again. They were clean. But the memory of that dried, cracked stain on her palms burned like a brand.

"Miss Bai." A hand touched her shoulder. She flinched, spinning around to face a tall boy with sharp features and colder eyes. He wore the robes of a senior student, but something about him felt wrong. His skin was too smooth, his movements too precise. "I wouldn't touch anything if I were you."

"Who are you?"

"My name is Xu Yan. I'm the academy's record keeper." He pointed to the wall. "That message wasn't written with ink. Or blood. It was written with something else entirely."

"What?"

"I don't know. But I've seen it before. On the walls of the bell chamber, the day Chen Feng died."

Bai Su's blood ran cold. "You were there?"

"I was the one who found him." His voice was flat, emotionless. "I was the first one through the door. The walls were covered in the same writing. The same message. Over and over again, hundreds of times, as if someone had spent hours carving them into the stone."

"The academy covered it up," Headmaster Zhou said, stepping into the room. "We had to. The other students couldn't know."

"But you didn't cover it up well enough," Xu Yan said. His eyes never left Bai Su. "Because the writing is back. And this time, it's addressed to someone specific."

"You think I had something to do with Chen Feng's death?"

"I think you remember more than you're telling us." He stepped closer, and Bai Su noticed that he cast no shadow. "I think you were in the bell chamber that day. I think you saw what happened. And I think someone—or something—made you forget."

"That's impossible. I only arrived at the academy three days ago. Chen Feng died a month before I even—"

"Did you?" Xu Yan's smile was thin and cruel. "Or did you simply forget that too?"

Headmaster Zhou stepped between them. "That's enough, Xu Yan. You're frightening her."

"I'm trying to help her." Xu Yan didn't back down. "The academy has chosen her as its witness. Do you know what happens to witnesses? They remember. Eventually. And when they do, the truth destroys them."

He turned and walked out of the room, his footsteps making no sound on the wooden floor.

Lin Mei finally lowered her hands from her mouth. Her face was pale, her voice barely a whisper. "Bai Su, there's something else."

She pointed to the corner of the room. A small bundle lay on the floor, wrapped in cloth. Bai Su approached it slowly, her heart pounding in her chest. She knelt down and unwrapped it.

Inside was a bell.

Not a large one, like the one in the tower. This one was small, delicate, made of tarnished bronze. There was an inscription on its side, in characters so small she had to squint to read them:

*FOR THE ONE WHO RANG THE TENTH RING*

Bai Su picked up the bell. It was warm to the touch, as if it had been held recently. She shook it gently.

It made no sound.

But somewhere in the distance, she heard a bell ring. Once. Twice. Three times.

Then silence.

And then, from somewhere deep within the academy, she heard a voice. A woman's voice, singing an old folk song in a language Bai Su didn't recognize but somehow understood.

*"The bell tolls for the living, the bell tolls for the dead,*

*The bell tolls for the promise that was left unsaid.*

*Ten rings for the scholar, ten rings for the fall,*

*Ten rings for the one who remembers it all."*

The singing stopped.

The bell in Bai Su's hand began to vibrate.

And on the wall, the characters began to change, reforming themselves into a new message:

*FIND THE WELL*

*BEFORE THE MOON IS FULL*

*OR THE WITNESS BECOMES THE ACCUSED*

Bai Su looked out the window. The moon was nearly full. One more night.

One more night to find the truth.

One more night before she became the next victim.

Or worse.

The next killer.

She clutched the bell to her chest and ran.

title: Chapter 16
content:
CHAPTER 16: The Examination That Never Ends

The headmaster's office smelled of ink and sandalwood, a combination that usually calmed Bai Su's nerves. Today, it did nothing. She sat in the same chair she had occupied on her first day at Wutong Academy, but everything else had changed.

Headmaster Zhao was not alone.

Two men stood behind him, their faces hidden in the shadow of official caps. One held a scroll bound in red silk. The other had his hand resting on the hilt of a sword.

"Miss Bai," Headmaster Zhao said, his voice strained. "These gentlemen are from the Ministry of Rites. They have questions about the recent... incidents."

The man with the scroll stepped forward. He was young, perhaps twenty-five, with sharp eyes that missed nothing. When he unrolled the scroll, Bai Su saw her name written in elegant calligraphy, followed by a list of dates and times.

"You were present at the bell tower when Chen Feng died," he said. It was not a question.

"I found his body," Bai Su corrected. "I didn't see him fall."

"Yet your fingerprints are on the window frame. The one from which he fell."

Bai Su's mouth went dry. "I touched the frame when I looked down. To see what had happened."

"And the blood on your hands?"

She looked at her palms. Clean. But she remembered the rust-colored stain, the way it had cracked like old paint.

"There was no blood," she said carefully. "Not when I left the tower."

The official's eyes narrowed. "I didn't say when the blood appeared. Only that it was on your hands."

The room grew cold. Bai Su felt it before she understood it—a drop in temperature that had nothing to do with the weather. The candles flickered. The shadows in the corners stretched and twisted.

Headmaster Zhao cleared his throat. "Gentlemen, perhaps we should continue this discussion after Miss Bai has had time to—"

"There is no time." The second official spoke for the first time, his voice like stones grinding together. "Three students have died this week. Two living, one dead. The ghost population is growing restless. If we don't find answers soon, there will be chaos."

"Three?" Bai Su's head snapped up. "You said three. Chen Feng was one. Who were the others?"

The officials exchanged glances. The first one rolled the scroll back up with deliberate slowness.

"A girl named Li Hua was found in the library this morning. Hanging from the rafters. And a boy named Wang Jun was discovered in the east courtyard. Drowned." He paused. "The well in the east courtyard has been dry for three years."

Bai Su's mind raced. Li Hua. The shy girl who always sat in the corner of the library, surrounded by ghost stories. Wang Jun. The loud, obnoxious boy who had once tried to peek under her robe during the ghost festival.

Two deaths. Two impossible deaths.

"How long?" she asked.

"Excuse me?"

"How long were they dead before they were found?" She stood up, ignoring the officials' protests. "Li Hua was in the library every night. She was obsessed with a particular book—I saw her reading it just yesterday. A collection of Liaozhai tales. Wang Jun was supposed to be practicing for the archery competition. He was the best shot in the academy."

"You knew them well?"

"No. But I observed them. Everyone observes everyone in this place. That's what you do when half your classmates are already dead." She turned to Headmaster Zhao. "Where are the bodies now?"

"In the mortuary," he said. "Being prepared for... processing."

Processing. A polite word for a grim reality. The living students would be sent home, their deaths recorded as accidents or illnesses. The ghost students would be... what? Exorcised? Dispersed? She had never asked what happened to a ghost that died twice.

"I need to see them."

The first official stepped forward. "That is not permitted. The investigation is under the authority of the Ministry—"

"I don't care about your authority." Bai Su's voice was calm, but there was steel in it. "I care about the fact that someone—or something—is killing students right under your noses. And I care about the fact that my hands were covered in blood I didn't spill. If you want to find the killer, you need to understand how they're killing. And to do that, you need to see what they're leaving behind."

The room was silent. The candles had stopped flickering, frozen in place like amber caught in ice.

The second official laughed. It was not a pleasant sound.

"The girl has teeth," he said. "Fine. Take her to the mortuary. But I want a guard with her at all times."

"Agreed," Headmaster Zhao said quickly, before Bai Su could protest. "I will escort her myself."

---

The mortuary was a small stone building behind the main hall, its walls thick enough to keep out both the living and the dead. A single lantern hung from the ceiling, casting long shadows that danced across the three wooden slabs.

Li Hua lay on the first slab. She looked peaceful, her hands folded over her chest, her eyes closed. If not for the dark ring around her neck, she could have been sleeping.

Wang Jun was on the second slab. His skin was pale and wrinkled, his clothes still damp. His mouth was open, as if he had been trying to scream.

But it was the third slab that made Bai Su stop.

It was empty.

"There were only two bodies recovered," Headmaster Zhao said, following her gaze. "The slab is for Chen Feng, but his body was taken by the Ministry before we could—"

"No." Bai Su walked toward the third slab. "That's not for Chen Feng."

"How can you tell?"

She pointed at the cloth covering the slab. It was white, clean, perfectly pressed. A small tag was pinned to the corner, with a name written in neat characters.

Bai Su.

"That's my name," she whispered.

Headmaster Zhao paled. "That's impossible. I prepared the mortuary myself this morning. There were only two slabs set up."

Bai Su reached for the tag, her fingers trembling. The moment she touched it, the world shifted.

She was no longer in the mortuary.

She was standing in the bell tower, looking down at the courtyard. Chen Feng was falling, his arms flailing, his mouth open in a silent scream. But he wasn't falling toward the ground. He was falling upward, toward the sky, toward the moon, toward something vast and dark that waited with open arms.

She tried to look away, but she couldn't. Her eyes were fixed on the scene, drawn by an invisible force.

Then Chen Feng stopped falling.

He hung in the air, suspended by nothing, his face twisted in terror. Slowly, he turned his head to look at her.

"You're next," he said. His voice was the sound of breaking glass. "She's already chosen you. She's been choosing you since the beginning."

"Who?" Bai Su screamed. "Who chose me?"

But Chen Feng was already gone, pulled into the darkness, his body dissolving like smoke.

The courtyard was empty again.

Bai Su blinked, and she was back in the mortuary, her hand still touching the tag. Headmaster Zhao was shaking her shoulder, his face frantic.

"Miss Bai! Miss Bai, can you hear me?"

"I'm fine," she said, though she wasn't. Her voice was hollow, distant, like it belonged to someone else. "I'm fine."

"You went white as a sheet. What did you see?"

She didn't answer. Instead, she turned to the empty slab and pulled the cloth away.

Underneath was a mirror.

It was old, its surface cracked and tarnished, but Bai Su could still see her reflection. She looked tired. She looked scared. She looked like someone who had already died and didn't know it yet.

But there was something else in the mirror.

Behind her reflection, barely visible, a figure stood in the shadows. A girl, no older than fifteen, with long black hair and empty eyes. She was wearing a white burial dress, and her hands were covered in blood.

Bai Su's blood.

The girl smiled.

And then she reached out of the mirror, her cold fingers brushing against Bai Su's cheek.

---

"Miss Bai!"

The voice was distant, muffled, like it was coming through water. Bai Su opened her eyes to find herself on the floor, Headmaster Zhao kneeling beside her. The mirror was gone. The slab was empty.

"What happened?" she asked, her voice hoarse.

"You collapsed. One moment you were standing, the next you were on the ground. I think you had some kind of seizure."

Seizure. That was one word for it.

"I need to see the library," Bai Su said, struggling to her feet. "Li Hua's book. The one she was reading. I need to find it."

"Miss Bai, you need to rest—"

"There's no time." She grabbed his arm, her grip stronger than he expected. "The girl in the mirror. She's been here all along. She's the one who's been killing. But she's not just killing. She's preparing. For something. For someone."

"Preparing for what?"

Bai Su looked at her hands. Clean now. But she could still feel the blood, warm and wet, seeping into her skin.

"For me," she said. "She's preparing a place for me."

---

The library was empty when they arrived. The lanterns had been extinguished, the curtains drawn. In the dim light, the rows of books looked like tombstones.

Li Hua's corner was easy to find. A chair sat slightly askew, a candle stub still warm on the table. And there, lying open as if she had just stepped away for a moment, was the book.

Bai Su picked it up. It was old, the leather cover cracked and peeling. The title was written in faded gold leaf:

"The Eighteen Hells of the Wutong Academy."

She had never seen this book before. She had never heard of it. But as she opened it, she felt a pull, a recognition, like she was remembering something that had never happened.

The pages were filled with illustrations. Gruesome, detailed illustrations of students being tortured in ways that defied imagination. Hooks through their spines. Needles in their eyes. Their souls being pulled apart, piece by piece, while they screamed.

And at the end, on the last page, a name.

Bai Su.

Her name. Written in fresh ink, still wet.

She closed the book. Her hands were shaking.

"Miss Bai?" Headmaster Zhao's voice was worried. "What is it?"

She didn't answer. She couldn't. Because in the silence of the library, she heard a voice. Sweet and familiar, like a lullaby from her childhood.

"Found you."

The lanterns went out.

And when they came back on, Bai Su was alone.

The book was gone. Headmaster Zhao was gone. And in the corner, where Li Hua had always sat, a figure in white was waiting.

The girl from the mirror.

"Hello, sister," she said. "I've been waiting so long for you to come home."

Bai Su opened her mouth to scream. But no sound came out.

Because she recognized that voice. She had heard it every night in her dreams, for as long as she could remember.
