It was her own.

title: Chapter 17
content:
CHAPTER 17: The Scholar Who Never Left

The headmaster's study was smaller than Bai Su expected.

She had imagined something grand—towering shelves of ancient texts, silk scrolls unfurled across mahogany desks, the weight of centuries pressing down from carved wooden beams. Instead, she found a cramped room on the ground floor of the administrative building, its walls lined with mismatched bookshelves that bowed under the weight of papers and ledgers. A single oil lamp flickered on the desk, casting long shadows that danced like living things.

Headmaster Zhou sat behind the desk, his fingers steepled, his face unreadable. He was a man who looked like he had been carved from old wood—weathered, patient, and impossible to break.

"Close the door," he said.

Bai Su complied. The latch clicked into place with a sound too final for her liking.

"Sit."

She sat in the chair across from him. The wood was warm, as if someone had been sitting there moments before. She tried not to think about who that someone might have been.

"I received a report," the headmaster said, "that you were found in the bell tower."

"I was investigating."

"Investigating what, exactly?"

She met his gaze. "Chen Feng's death."

The headmaster's expression didn't change. "Chen Feng died of a heart condition. The physician confirmed it. The magistrate signed the death certificate. There is nothing to investigate."

"Then why did you send for me?"

"Because I received another report." He slid a piece of paper across the desk. "From the library."

Bai Su picked it up. The handwriting was delicate, almost feminine, but the message was anything but:

*The student who sees too much will soon see nothing at all.*

She set the paper down carefully, as if it might burn her.

"When did you receive this?"

"Two hours ago. It was tucked into a book on the third floor. A book on funeral rites, coincidentally." The headmaster leaned back. "I thought you should know that someone is watching you, Miss Bai. Someone who may not wish you well."

"Is that concern I hear, Headmaster?"

"It is pragmatism. You are a student at this academy, and I am responsible for your safety. If you die, I will have to fill out paperwork."

Despite everything, Bai Su almost smiled. Almost.

"Who else knows about my ability?" she asked.

"Besides the ghost who told you?" The headmaster's eyes flickered with something she couldn't name. "I would assume everyone who needs to know. And a few who don't."

"That's not reassuring."

"It wasn't meant to be."

She folded the threatening note and slipped it into her sleeve. "I'm keeping this."

"I expected you would."

The headmaster stood, signaling the meeting was over. But Bai Su didn't move.

"One more question," she said. "The bell tower. Who rang the bell?"

The headmaster's hand paused on a scroll. "No one rang the bell, Miss Bai. The bell tower has been locked for three years. Ever since the last student died in it."

"The last student?"

"A boy named Wei Cheng. He was found at the bottom of the tower stairs. Neck broken. The official report said he fell." The headmaster's voice was carefully neutral. "Unofficially, the students say he was pushed."

"By whom?"

"That's the question, isn't it?" The headmaster opened the door. "Good evening, Miss Bai. I suggest you get some rest. Tomorrow's examinations are not something you should face on an empty stomach."

She stepped into the hallway, and the door closed behind her with a soft click.

The corridor was empty. The oil lamps along the walls had been dimmed for the night, casting the passage in shadows that seemed to breathe. Bai Su walked quickly, her footsteps echoing off the stone floors.

She didn't make it ten steps before a hand grabbed her arm and pulled her into an alcove.

"Quiet," a voice whispered. "They're watching."

Bai Su froze. The figure beside her was familiar—the ghost scholar from the library, his face half-illuminated by the distant lamplight.

"You," she breathed. "Where did you go?"

"I had to leave. The headmaster's study is warded. I couldn't get within ten feet of it."

"Warded? Against what?"

"Against me." He released her arm, his expression grim. "The headmaster knows more than he's telling you, Bai Su. He always has."

"How do you know him?"

"He was my teacher. Before I died."

The words hit her like a physical blow. She stared at the ghost scholar, seeing him anew—the fine robes, the scholarly demeanor, the way he carried himself like someone who had once belonged to these halls.

"You were a student here?"

"Forty years ago." He smiled, but there was no warmth in it. "I was the top scholar of my year. I was going to sit the imperial examinations. I was going to make my family proud."

"What happened?"

"I found something I wasn't supposed to find." He glanced down the hallway, his eyes tracking something Bai Su couldn't see. "There's a room beneath the academy. A chamber that isn't on any map, that doesn't exist in any record. The headmaster knows about it. So did Chen Feng."

"Chen Feng knew?"

"He died for it." The ghost scholar turned back to her, his gaze intense. "The same way I did. The same way Wei Cheng did. The same way every student who has ever tried to uncover the truth has died."

Bai Su's blood ran cold. "How many?"

"Seven." His voice was barely a whisper. "Seven students in forty years. All of them found in accidents. All of them ruled natural causes. All of them killed by the same hand."

"The ghost. The one who's been taking qi."

"No." He shook his head. "The ghost is a symptom, not the cause. The thing beneath this academy—the thing that's been here since before the academy was built—that's the real danger. The ghost who's been killing students is just its servant. Its tool."

"Then what is it?"

He opened his mouth to answer—

And the lamps went out.

Darkness swallowed the corridor, absolute and suffocating. Bai Su felt the ghost scholar's hand find hers, cold as grave dirt.

"Don't move," he whispered. "Don't breathe. It's here."

She heard nothing. Saw nothing. But she felt something—a presence, vast and ancient, pressing against her consciousness like a hand against a window pane. It was aware of her. It was curious.

And it was hungry.

The moment stretched into eternity. Bai Su's lungs burned with the effort of holding her breath, her heart pounding so loud she was sure it would give her away.

Then, as suddenly as it had come, the presence receded.

The lamps flickered back to life.

The ghost scholar was gone.

In his place stood a girl.

She was young—no more than sixteen—with hair the color of midnight and eyes like black stones. She wore the uniform of the academy, but it was stained with something dark and wet. Water dripped from her sleeves, pooling on the stone floor.

"You shouldn't be here," she said. Her voice was soft, almost kind. "This is not your story, Bai Su."

"Who are you?"

"I'm the one who died first." The girl smiled, and water poured from her mouth, cascading down her chin in rivulets. "I'm the one who started all of this. I'm the one who opened the door."

"What door?"

"The one beneath the academy. The one that should never have been opened." The girl's smile widened, and her eyes began to bleed—black ichor that streaked down her cheeks like tears. "I wanted to see what was on the other side. I wanted to understand. But some things aren't meant to be understood. Some doors should stay closed forever."

Bai Su's hand went to the talisman at her belt. "Stay back."

"I'm not here to hurt you." The girl raised her hands, palms open. "I'm here to warn you. The ghost you're hunting—the one who killed Chen Feng—is not your enemy. It's a victim, just like the rest of us. Just like you will be, if you keep digging."

"I don't believe you."

"You should." The girl's form began to waver, becoming translucent, her edges bleeding into the air like ink in water. "Because I was just like you once. Curious. Brave. Certain I could change things." Her voice grew fainter, like an echo fading into nothing. "And now I'm a warning. A story told to frighten children. A footnote in someone else's tragedy."

She dissolved into mist, leaving only a puddle of water on the floor.

Bai Su stood alone in the corridor, her breath coming in ragged gasps. The lamps flickered steadily, casting their warm glow over the stone walls.

She looked down.

The puddle of water had formed words.

*The examination scroll. Look at the blood.*

Bai Su's heart stopped.

She knew those words. They were the same words that had been written on the threatening note. The same handwriting. The same delicate, almost feminine strokes.

The girl had written that note.

But the girl had been dead for decades.

Which meant the threat hadn't come from a living student at all.

It had come from a ghost.

And Bai Su had just been given a clue by the one who had written it.

She turned and ran, her footsteps echoing through the empty corridors, her mind racing faster than her feet.

The examination scroll. The blood.

She had to get to the archive room.

She had to see what was written in Chen Feng's blood.

She rounded the corner and slammed into a solid wall of flesh.

Strong hands caught her before she could fall. She looked up into a familiar face—sharp features, calculating eyes, a smile that didn't reach the gaze.

Wei Ming.

"Miss Bai," he said, his voice smooth as silk. "Running rather late, aren't you? The curfew bell rang ten minutes ago."

"I know. I was—"

"Investigating?" He released her, stepping back. "I heard. You've been quite busy since you arrived. Making friends. Asking questions. Poking your nose where it doesn't belong."

"Is that a warning?"

"It's an observation." He tilted his head, studying her like a scholar studying a particularly interesting specimen. "The last student who was as curious as you is dead. The one before that is dead. The one before that is also dead. Do you see a pattern, Miss Bai?"

"I see a coincidence."

"There is no such thing as coincidence at Wutong Academy." He leaned in, his voice dropping to a whisper. "There is only design. And you, Miss Bai, are dancing to a tune you cannot hear."

He walked past her, disappearing into the shadows of the east corridor.

Bai Su stood frozen, her heart hammering against her ribs.

She had come to Wutong Academy to learn.

She was beginning to realize that the only lesson this place taught was how to die.

And she was determined not to fail that examination.

She turned and continued toward the archive room, the ghost girl's words echoing in her mind:

*The examination scroll. Look at the blood.*

Whatever was written in Chen Feng's blood, she was going to find it.

Even if it killed her.

Especially if it killed her.

Because if she was going to die at this academy, she was going to die knowing the truth.

She was going to die with her eyes wide open.

And she was going to take the secret of Wutong Academy with her into the grave.

title: Chapter 18
content:
CHAPTER 18: The Song of Forgotten Things

The headmaster's office smelled of old books and older secrets. Bai Su sat in the same chair she had occupied on her first day at Wutong Academy, but everything felt different now. The walls seemed closer. The shadows in the corners moved when she wasn't looking directly at them.

Headmaster Wen paced behind his desk, his normally composed face drawn tight with worry. He had aged since she last saw him—or perhaps she was simply better at seeing the truth now. The gray in his hair, the slight tremor in his hands, the way he refused to meet her eyes.

"I received a letter this morning," he said, stopping mid-stride. "From the Ministry of Rites."

Bai Su waited. The silence stretched uncomfortably.

"They're sending an inspector," he continued. "Someone to evaluate the academy. To ensure..." He trailed off, pressing his fingers to his temples.

"To ensure what?"

He sighed heavily. "There have been rumors. Whispers in the capital about students who vanish from Wutong Academy. About strange lights in the bell tower at midnight. About a teacher who speaks to empty chairs."

"That's not entirely inaccurate," Bai Su said quietly.

Headmaster Wen laughed, but there was no humor in it. "No. It's not. Which is precisely why we have a problem. The inspector arrives in three days."

"Three days? But the qi transfers—"

"Will stop." His voice was sharp, cutting. "They must stop. If the inspector witnesses anything unusual, anything that cannot be explained away, the academy will be investigated. And if they investigate deeply enough..."

"They'll find the ghosts."

"They'll find the ghosts," he confirmed. "And they'll seal this place shut. Every spirit bound, every student expelled, every memory of Wutong Academy erased from existence."

Bai Su's mind raced. Three days. The killer needed more qi to complete their transformation. The ghost scholar in the bell tower had said they would not stop until every living student was dead. And now an inspector was coming to poke through their secrets.

"What do you want me to do?" she asked.

"Find the killer." He said it simply, as if asking her to fetch tea. "Before the inspector arrives. Before the qi transfer is complete. Before more students die."

"And if I can't?"

Headmaster Wen's face softened. For a moment, he looked less like a stern administrator and more like a tired old man. "Then we close the academy. Permanently."

The words hung in the air like a death sentence.

---

Bai Su left the headmaster's office with her head spinning. Three days. She had three days to solve a murder mystery where the victims were already dead, the killer might be invisible, and the evidence included her own unexplained presence at a window where a boy had fallen to his death.

She walked through the courtyard, watching the living students laugh and study and argue about poetry. They had no idea. Not a single one of them knew that half their classmates were dead, that a killer walked among them, that the academy itself was built on bones and buried secrets.

"Miss Bai!"

She turned. A girl was running toward her, breathless, her face flushed with exertion. Her robes were disheveled, her hair escaping its pins.

"Miss Bai, you have to come quickly. It's the library. There's something—" She stopped, gasping for air. "There's something terrible."

Bai Su followed her without question. They raced through the corridors, past startled students and confused teachers, until they reached the library's main hall.

The door was open. Books lay scattered across the floor, pages torn and scattered like fallen leaves. Shelves had been pulled away from the walls, revealing hidden passages that twisted into darkness. And in the center of the room, surrounded by chaos, stood a group of students staring at something on the floor.

Bai Su pushed through the crowd.

A body lay face-down on the marble tiles. A living student—she could tell by the faint warmth that still radiated from the skin. A boy, perhaps sixteen, his robes neat and untouched. No blood. No wounds. No visible cause of death.

But his mouth was open, frozen in a silent scream, and his eyes—

His eyes were gone. Not removed, not gouged. Simply... absent, replaced by smooth, unbroken skin where they should have been.

"Who found him?" Bai Su asked, her voice steady despite the cold knot forming in her stomach.

"I did." A trembling girl stepped forward. "Zhang Wei was studying here last night. He said he was going to translate an old text. When he didn't come back to the dormitory, I came looking for him."

"Did you see anyone else? Hear anything unusual?"

The girl shook her head, tears streaming down her face. "Nothing. The library was empty. The doors were locked from the inside."

Bai Su knelt beside the body. She reached out, hesitating, then touched the boy's shoulder. His skin was cold, but not yet stiff. He had been dead only a few hours.

"What happened to his eyes?" someone whispered.

Bai Su didn't answer. She was too busy examining the boy's hands. They were clenched into fists, so tight that his nails had drawn blood from his palms. She pried his fingers open carefully.

In his right hand was a piece of paper. Crumpled, yellowed, covered in characters so small they were barely legible. She smoothed it out.

One line of text. Written in ink that seemed to shift and move as she watched.

*The sky forgets, the earth remembers. The dead do not stay buried.*

The characters faded as she read them, dissolving into nothing, leaving only blank paper behind.

"What does it mean?" someone asked.

Bai Su looked up at the gathered students. Living and dead, they stood together, united by fear. The spirits among them were easier to spot now—the slight transparency, the way light bent around them, the faint chill that emanated from their forms.

"It means," she said slowly, "that the killer is sending a message."

"But who?" the trembling girl demanded. "Who would do this? And how?"

Bai Su didn't have an answer. But she had a lead.

She remembered the ghost scholar's words. *They won't just become human again. They'll become something worse.* Something worse than a ghost that could kill. Something that could leave a body with no eyes and a message that dissolved into air.

Something that was learning to move between worlds.

---

The afternoon passed in a blur of questions and accusations. The teachers locked down the academy, forbidding anyone from leaving their dorms after sunset. The headmaster held emergency meetings with his senior staff. Students whispered and wept and pointed fingers at each other.

Bai Su slipped away during the commotion.

She returned to the bell tower, climbing the spiral stairs with careful precision. The broken window had been boarded up, the glass swept away. The rope still hung motionless from the bell.

But something had changed.

A circle had been drawn on the floor. Not in chalk or paint, but in something darker. Something that smelled of copper and old stone. She knelt to examine it, her fingers hovering just above the surface.

Blood. Dried and cracked, but unmistakably blood.

The circle was intricate, covered in symbols she didn't recognize. Some were Chinese characters, twisted into unfamiliar shapes. Others were patterns she had never seen before, spirals and angles that hurt to look at directly.

"Don't touch it."

She spun around. The ghost scholar stood in the doorway, his face pale, his eyes wide with alarm.

"It's a binding circle," he said, stepping closer but keeping his distance from the symbol. "Someone is trying to trap a spirit. A very powerful spirit."

"Who?"

"I don't know. But look—" He pointed at the center of the circle, where a small object lay. A jade pendant, carved in the shape of a lotus flower. Bai Su recognized it immediately.

It belonged to Headmaster Wen.

She had seen him wearing it every day since she arrived. He never took it off. He had told her it was a family heirloom, passed down through generations, a symbol of his position at the academy.

"Why would the headmaster's pendant be here?" she asked.

The ghost scholar shook his head slowly. "That's not the right question."

"Then what is?"

He looked at her, and in his eyes she saw something she hadn't expected. Fear. The ghost who had faced death and walked through the veil was afraid.

"The right question," he said, "is why the pendant is covered in fresh blood."

Bai Su picked up the jade. It was warm to the touch, almost hot, and sticky with crimson that hadn't yet dried. Fresh blood meant the headmaster had been here recently. Tonight. Within the last hour.

"Where is Headmaster Wen now?" she asked.

"Meeting with the teachers. He's been in the main hall since dinner."

"Are you sure?"

The ghost scholar hesitated. "I saw him there. All of them. They were arguing about the inspector."

Bai Su turned the pendant over in her hands. It was warm. Still warm. As if it had just been removed from someone's neck.

"He could have left," she said. "Slipped away during the argument. Come here, drawn the circle, placed the pendant—"

"For what purpose?"

She didn't have an answer. But she had a terrible feeling growing in her chest, a certainty that something was very, very wrong.

"We need to find him," she said.

They left the bell tower together, the ghost scholar gliding silently beside her. The academy was quiet now, the students confined to their dorms, the teachers still meeting in the main hall. Bai Su moved through the empty corridors, her footsteps echoing off the stone walls.

The headmaster's quarters were on the third floor of the main building, a suite of rooms separate from the administrative offices. Bai Su had never been inside. She had never had a reason.

Until now.

The door was unlocked. She pushed it open slowly, wincing as the hinges creaked.

The room inside was immaculate. Neatly arranged furniture, scrolls organized on shelves, a single painting of a mountain landscape on the wall. Nothing out of place. Nothing suspicious.

But the ghost scholar had stopped at the threshold. His face was pale, paler than usual, his form flickering like a candle in the wind.

"There's something here," he whispered. "Something old. Something that doesn't want to be found."

Bai Su stepped inside. The air was cold, colder than it should have been, and it carried a faint smell of incense. Sandalwood. The same scent that lingered in the bell tower.

She searched the room methodically, opening drawers, checking under the bed, examining the scrolls on the shelves. Nothing seemed out of the ordinary. Nothing gave her any answers.

But then she noticed the painting.

The mountain landscape was beautiful, executed with skill and precision. But something was wrong with it. The brushstrokes were too clean, the ink too dark. She stepped closer, studying the details.

The mountain had a cave. And inside the cave, barely visible, was a figure. A person, rendered in strokes so fine they were almost invisible. Kneeling. Head bowed. Hands raised in supplication.

And behind the figure, another shape. A shadow that seemed to move as she watched, shifting and growing, spreading across the mountain like a stain.

"Look," she said, pointing.

The ghost scholar leaned in. His breath caught. "That's not part of the original painting."

"What do you mean?"

"The artist died fifty years ago. I know his work. This—" He traced the shadow with his finger. "This was added later. Recently. By someone who wanted to hide a secret."

Bai

title: Chapter 19
content:
CHAPTER 19: The Song Before Drowning

Bai Su's feet carried her through the winding corridors of Wutong Academy on instinct alone. Her mind remained trapped in the bell chamber, replaying the phantom blood on her hands, the matching footprint, the song that followed her like a shadow stitched to her soul.

*"A scholar went a-walking, by riverside so deep..."*

She knew that song.

The realization struck her mid-step, nearly causing her to stumble. She knew it the way she knew her own reflection, the way she knew the scent of rain before a storm. It was buried somewhere deep, beneath layers of memory she had never excavated, a fossil pressed into the sediment of her childhood.

But whose childhood? Hers? Or someone else's?

"Miss Bai!" The servant girl waited at the end of the corridor, wringing her hands. "The headmaster is in the east study. Please hurry—he said it couldn't wait."

Bai Su nodded, pushing the song to the back of her mind. She would deal with it later. She had to. Because if she stopped to think about what the song meant, about why her hands had been covered in blood she couldn't remember spilling, about why her footprints were already in a room she had never entered—

She would break.

And breaking was not an option. Not when there were ghosts who remembered how to kill.

The east study was a modest room compared to the headmaster's main office, furnished with simple rosewood furniture and scrolls stacked in neat piles. Headmaster Zhou sat behind a desk that was too large for the space, his face drawn and pale in the lamplight.

He was not alone.

A woman stood by the window, her back to the door. She wore mourning white, the fabric stark against the darkness outside. Her hair was pinned up in the style of a widow, but when she turned, Bai Su saw she was young—barely older than the senior students, her face smooth and unlined save for the dark circles under her eyes.

"Come in, Miss Bai," Headmaster Zhou said. "Close the door."

Bai Su obeyed, her eyes fixed on the woman. There was something familiar about her, something that tugged at the edges of Bai Su's consciousness like a half-remembered dream.

"This is Madam Liu," the headmaster said. "She arrived this evening. She has... information about the incidents at the academy."

Madam Liu's gaze swept over Bai Su with clinical precision. "You're the one who can see them."

It wasn't a question.

"Yes," Bai Su said. "And you are?"

"I am a messenger." Madam Liu reached into her sleeve and withdrew a folded piece of paper, yellowed with age. "I was instructed to deliver this to the one who walks between worlds. That would be you."

Bai Su took the paper. Her fingers tingled as she touched it, the same cold sensation she felt when brushing against a ghost. She unfolded it carefully.

The characters were written in a hand she recognized—bold, precise strokes that pressed deep into the paper, as if the writer had been in a great hurry or great pain.

*The drowned scholar was not the first. He was the seventh.*

*The veil weakens on the fifteenth night of each month. Three days from now.*

*There is a ghost within these walls who does not know they are dead.*

*Find them before they find you, or join us in the river.*

There was no signature.

"Where did you get this?" Bai Su asked, her voice steady despite the trembling in her hands.

"I was paid to deliver it. The sender did not reveal themselves." Madam Liu's eyes flickered to the headmaster, then back to Bai Su. "But they told me to tell you one more thing. Something I was not to write down."

"What?"

"The song you heard in the bell tower." Madam Liu's voice dropped to a whisper. "Your mother used to sing it to you. Before she died."

The world tilted.

Bai Su grabbed the edge of the desk to steady herself. "That's impossible. I don't—my mother died when I was three. I don't remember her voice."

"You don't remember it consciously." Madam Liu stepped closer, close enough that Bai Su could smell the river water clinging to her clothes, could see the way her skin glistened with moisture even though she was perfectly dry. "But she sang it to you every night. Until she didn't."

"You speak as if you knew her."

"I did."

The words hung in the air, heavy with unspoken history. Headmaster Zhou shifted in his seat, his face betraying nothing, but Bai Su could see the tension in his shoulders, the way his hands gripped the armrests of his chair.

"Madam Liu," he said, "perhaps you should explain the full context of your visit."

"The full context." Madam Liu's laugh was brittle, sharp as broken glass. "The full context is that I died in the river twenty years ago. I was the one who found your mother, Miss Bai. I was the one who pulled her body from the water."

Bai Su's blood turned to ice.

"My mother drowned?"

"Did you think she died in her sleep? Of illness? That's what they told you, wasn't it?" Madam Liu's eyes were wet now, though no tears fell. "She drowned in the same river that took the seven scholars before her. The same river that will take you, if you don't find the ghost who doesn't know they're dead."

"How do you know all this?"

"Because I am that ghost."

The room went silent. Bai Su heard the blood rushing in her ears, felt the cold creeping up her spine like fingers tracing her vertebrae.

"You're dead," she said slowly. "But you're standing here. Talking to me."

"I'm dead. But I didn't know it. Not until three days ago, when I found my own body preserved in a cave beneath the river." Madam Liu's voice cracked. "I have been walking among the living for twenty years, believing I was one of them. I married. I bore a child. I watched that child grow, and I never once questioned why I did not age, why the river called to me in my dreams, why I could feel the cold of the water even on the hottest summer day."

Headmaster Zhou stood abruptly. "This is—"

"There is a ghost in this academy who does not know they are dead," Madam Liu interrupted. "They are killing the living to steal their qi, but they don't understand what they're doing. They think they're just... tired. That the world has become foggy and strange. That everyone else is the one who has changed."

"How do you know this?"

"Because I did the same thing." Madam Liu's voice was barely a whisper now. "Twenty years ago, before I knew I was dead, I killed a man. I drained his life qi and felt it warm my cold bones. I thought I was saving myself from some unnamed illness. But I was only prolonging my death, delaying the inevitable."

She looked at Bai Su with an expression that was equal parts pity and terror.

"The ghost in this academy is the same. They don't know what they are. And if they don't find out before the fifteenth night, they will break through the veil entirely. They will become something that should not exist—a dead thing that feeds on the living, forever hungry, forever alone."

"Can you help me find them?"

"I can give you the means to identify them." Madam Liu reached into her sleeve again and produced a small mirror, its surface black as obsidian. "This mirror shows the true nature of anyone it reflects. Living or dead. Ghost or human. Look into it, and you will see what they truly are."

Bai Su took the mirror. It was cold, colder than ice, and it seemed to absorb the light around it, leaving a patch of darkness in her palm.

"But there's a price," Madam Liu said.

"There's always a price."

"You must look into it yourself, first. You must see what you truly are."

Bai Su's heart stopped.

"What do you mean?"

"You can see ghosts, Miss Bai. You can touch the veil between worlds. You hear songs from your dead mother's lips." Madam Liu's eyes bore into hers, unblinking. "Have you never wondered why? Have you never questioned what makes you different?"

"I'm alive. I'm human."

"Are you?"

The question hit Bai Su like a physical blow. She stared at the mirror in her hands, at the darkness that seemed to pulse and breathe, waiting for her to peer into its depths.

"If I look," she said slowly, "what will I see?"

"I don't know. No one does. The mirror reveals truth, but truth is different for everyone." Madam Liu stepped back, toward the door. "But if you want to find the ghost before it finds you, you must know yourself first. That is the rule. That is the price."

Headmaster Zhou moved to block the door. "You can't just leave her with this—"

"I have delivered my message." Madam Liu's form began to flicker, growing translucent around the edges. "The river is rising, Headmaster. The veil is thinning. And there are things in the water that have been waiting for a very long time."

She turned to Bai Su one last time.

"Your mother's song. Do you remember the second verse?"

Bai Su's throat tightened. "I don't—"

"She sang it to you the night she died. You were three years old. You were the last person to see her alive." Madam Liu's voice broke. "You were in the river with her, Bai Su. You both drowned that night. But only one of you came back."

The door slammed shut.

Bai Su stood frozen, the mirror cold against her palm, the headmaster's voice distant and muffled as he shouted for guards.

She looked down at the black surface of the mirror.

She saw her own reflection, terrified and pale.

She saw the blood on her hands again, old and dried and cracked.

She saw the river behind her eyes, dark and deep and calling her name.

And she remembered.

The water. The cold. The way her mother's hands pushed her upward, toward the surface, toward the light.

The way she had opened her mouth to scream and tasted nothing but river water.

The way everything went dark.

And then—the way she opened her eyes again, lying on the riverbank, gasping for air that burned her lungs, while her mother floated face-down in the current.

She had been dead.

For how long?

The mirror trembled in her hand. The darkness at its center began to swirl, forming shapes, forming images, forming a face—

Her mother's face, pale and bloated, eyes wide and staring.

"Find me," the face whispered. "Come home to the river, my darling. Find me before the veil breaks."

"Miss Bai?" Headmaster Zhou's hand on her shoulder. "Miss Bai, are you alright?"

Bai Su looked up.

Her reflection in the window showed a girl with wild eyes and wet hair.

She had not been near water all day.

The wetness dripped down her neck, cold and endless, pooling at her feet in a puddle that grew larger with every passing second.

She looked down.

The mirror was gone.

In its place was a river stone, smooth and black, engraved with a single character: *Drown*.

And somewhere, far away, a bell began to toll.

One. Two. Three.

The sound came from beneath her feet.

title: The Song She Sang Before
content:
The headmaster's office smelled of burning incense and something else—something Bai Su had learned to recognize over the past weeks. The smell of ghosts who had been crying. She stopped in the doorway, her hand resting on the frame.

The headmaster sat behind his desk, his face drawn and gray. He looked older than she remembered, the lines around his eyes carved deep like riverbeds in drought. Across from him, three figures stood in a row, their backs to her.

She recognized them by their stillness. The way they barely breathed. The way the light seemed to bend around them, reluctant to touch their skin.

Ghosts.

"Come in, Miss Bai," the headmaster said. His voice was flat, drained of its usual authority. "We have a problem."

Bai Su stepped inside. The door swung shut behind her, sealing with a click that echoed like a coffin lid. The three ghosts turned.

The first was a woman in her twenties, her hair still wet, water dripping from her robes to pool at her feet. She wore the expression of someone who had been drowning for a very long time.

The second was a young man with his neck bent at an impossible angle, his head lolling to one side like a broken flower. He smiled at her, and the smile did not reach his eyes.

The third was a girl. No older than fourteen. Her face was blank, her eyes empty, her hands clasped in front of her as if she were waiting for a lesson to begin. But there was something wrong with her mouth. It moved slightly, forming words without sound. Singing.

The song from the bell tower.

Bai Su's blood turned to ice.

"Sit down," the headmaster said. "They have something to tell you."

Bai Su did not sit. She stood in the center of the room, her back straight, her hands clenched at her sides. "Who are they?"

"The drowned woman is Lin Yue. She died in the academy pond seven years ago. The broken-necked boy is Zhao Wen. He fell from the east dormitory five years ago." The headmaster paused. "The girl is Xiao Mei. She died in the bell tower. Three months before you arrived."

Three months.

The timeline pressed against Bai Su's skull like a vice. She had arrived at Wutong Academy two months ago. Which meant Xiao Mei had been dead for only one month before Bai Su had stepped through the gates.

"Tell her what you told me," the headmaster said.

The drowned woman spoke first. Her voice was wet, gurgling, as if she were still swallowing pond water. "The one who is taking the life qi. We know who it is."

Bai Su's heart lurched. "Who?"

"A ghost. But not one of us." Lin Yue's eyes were dark, bottomless. "He is older. Much older. He has been here since the academy was built. He died in the first year of its founding, and he has been waiting ever since."

"Waiting for what?"

"For someone to wake him."

The broken-necked boy's head lolled as he spoke, his words coming out in a wheeze. "The bell tower was his prison. The bell was forged with iron from a temple that had been blessed by a hundred monks. Its ringing kept him bound. Kept him asleep."

"But the bell rang," Bai Su whispered. "Tonight. I heard it."

"Someone rang it," Zhao Wen said. "Someone who knew what they were doing. Someone who wanted to wake him."

Xiao Mei stopped singing. Her empty eyes fixed on Bai Su, and for the first time, something flickered in them. Recognition. Fear.

"You," she said.

Her voice was high and clear, like a child's. But there was nothing childlike in the way she looked at Bai Su.

"You rang the bell."

The room went silent. The headmaster stared at her. The ghosts stared at her. Bai Su felt the accusation land on her chest like a stone dropped into deep water.

"No," she said. "I didn't."

"I saw you." Xiao Mei took a step forward. Water dripped from her robes, but Xiao Mei was not wet. The water came from somewhere else, from the memory of drowning, from the echo of a death that had not been natural. "I was in the bell chamber when you came. I was hiding in the shadows. I saw you pull the rope."

"You're lying."

"I was a student here. I never lied to my teachers." Xiao Mei's voice cracked. "Why would I lie to you now?"

Bai Su's mind raced, trying to piece together the fragments of the evening. She had gone to the bell tower because the ghost scholar had led her there. She had climbed the stairs. She had seen the broken window. She had found the footprint that matched her shoe.

But she did not remember ringing the bell.

"Someone else was there," Bai Su said. "A ghost. A scholar. He told me to come to the tower."

The headmaster's face went pale. "Describe him."

"Tall. Thin. He wore old robes, like from a different era. His skin was translucent, like he had been dead for a long time. He spoke to me in the library first, then in the courtyard."

The headmaster closed his eyes. When he opened them, they were filled with a dread that made Bai Su's stomach clench.

"His name was Chen Wei," he said. "Chen Feng's older brother."

The name hit Bai Su like a physical blow. Chen Feng. The boy who had fallen from the bell tower. The boy who had been murdered.

"Chen Wei died eight years ago," the headmaster continued. "He was a brilliant student. Top of his class. Everyone expected him to pass the imperial exams with honors. But he grew obsessed. He began studying day and night, refusing to sleep, refusing to eat. He wanted to be perfect. He wanted to be the best."

"What happened to him?"

"He hanged himself in the bell tower three days before the exams. He left a note. It said: 'I have studied everything. There is nothing left to learn. There is nothing left to live for.'"

Bai Su felt the pieces clicking together, each one a knife twisting in her gut.

"Chen Wei's ghost has been here all along," she said. "He's the one taking life qi. He's the killer."

"Yes."

"But he's been dead for eight years. Why now? Why start killing now?"

The headmaster looked at Xiao Mei. The girl's face was blank again, but her lips were moving, forming the words of the old song.

"Because of the new moon," the headmaster said. "Because of the girl who can see ghosts. Because of the blood on your hands."

Bai Su looked down at her palms. They were clean now. But she could still feel the warmth of it, the phantom stickiness that refused to fade.

"I didn't ring the bell," she said again, but her voice was weaker now, less certain.

"Then who did?"

She had no answer.

The ghosts dissolved into the shadows, leaving Bai Su alone with the headmaster and the weight of an accusation she could not disprove. He poured her a cup of tea with trembling hands. The liquid was dark and bitter, and she drank it without tasting anything.

"There's more," he said. "Something I haven't told you."

"Tell me."

"Chen Wei was not the only one who died in the bell tower." He set down the teapot, his fingers leaving smudges on the ceramic. "Xiao Mei died there too. But she didn't fall. She didn't hang herself. She was pushed."

"By who?"

"By Chen Wei. He killed her because she saw what he was doing. She saw the ritual he was performing. She saw the first transfer of life qi."

Bai Su's mind flashed back to the bell chamber. The broken glass. The blood on her hands. The footprint that matched her shoe.

"If Chen Wei pushed Xiao Mei," she said slowly, "then he's been able to touch physical objects this whole time. He's not just a ghost. He's something more."

"Exactly." The headmaster's voice was barely a whisper. "He has been gathering power for eight years. Slowly. Patiently. He only needed two things to complete his transformation: the sound of the bell to break the seal, and a living vessel to anchor his spirit."

He looked at her. His eyes were wet.

"Bai Su," he said, "you are the vessel."

The words hung in the air like smoke, thick and suffocating. Bai Su wanted to deny them, but she could not. The evidence was there, written in the blood she could still feel on her skin, in the song that echoed in her memory, in the ghostly scholar who had appeared to her at every turn.

He had chosen her. From the very beginning, he had chosen her.

"Why me?" she asked. "Why not anyone else?"

"Because you can see him. Because you can hear him. Because your soul is open to the other side in a way that most living people's are not." The headmaster's voice broke. "He has been preparing you. Training you. Making you strong enough to hold him."

The implications crashed down on her like a wave.

The ghost scholar who had appeared in the library. The one who had warned her about the killer. The one who had led her to the bell tower.

He had been feeding her information. Gaining her trust. Shaping her into the perfect vessel.

And she had walked right into his trap.

"What do I do?" she asked.

The headmaster shook his head. "I don't know. I've never encountered anything like this. The academy's founders made the bell to contain him, but now that it has been rung, the seal is broken. He will be able to move freely. To take what he wants."

"Then I have to stop him."

"Stop him how? You're a student, Bai Su. You're barely seventeen. You've been at this academy for two months. You don't have the training, the knowledge, or the power to fight a ghost that has been gathering strength for eight years."

"I don't have a choice."

She stood up. Her legs were shaking, but she forced them to hold her weight.

"If I'm the vessel," she said, "then I'm the only one who can stop him. He needs me alive. That means he won't kill me. And that means I have time to figure out how to destroy him."

The headmaster stared at her, his face a mask of grief and fear.

"You're going to get yourself killed."

"Maybe." Bai Su walked to the door. She paused with her hand on the handle. "But if I don't try, he'll kill everyone else. And I can't live with that."

She opened the door and stepped out into the hallway.

The academy was silent. The torches had gone out, leaving only the faint glow of moonlight filtering through the windows. The shadows were deeper than they should have been, stretching and shifting like living things.

Bai Su walked down the corridor, her footsteps echoing in the emptiness. She could feel him watching her. She could feel his presence pressing against her skin, cold and hungry and patient.

She reached the end of the hallway and turned the corner.

He was waiting for her.

Chen Wei stood in the center of the courtyard, his translucent form glowing faintly in the moonlight. He was smiling. Not the friendly smile he had worn in the library, but something sharper. Something triumphant.

"You finally understand," he said.

"Understand what?"

"That you were never the one hunting the killer." He stepped closer, and the air around him grew cold, frost forming on the stones at his feet. "You were the one being prepared for the kill."

Bai

title: The Song Before Drowning
content:
The folk song followed Bai Su down the spiral staircase, curling around her ears like smoke. She knew this melody. Every child in Jiangnan knew it—a lullaby about a scholar who drowned himself in the West Lake because his love married another man. Mothers sang it to their children before bed. It was supposed to be soothing.

It was not soothing now.

Bai Su's footsteps echoed in the narrow stairwell, each step taking her deeper into the tower's belly. The walls were damp here, slick with something that gleamed in the lamplight. Water. But the tower had been dry above. She touched the wall. Her fingers came away wet, smelling of algae and silt, like the bottom of a pond that hadn't been disturbed in years.

*Splash.*

She froze. The sound came from below. Not footsteps. Something heavier. Something dragging itself across wet stone.

"S-stop that," she called out, her voice steadier than she felt. "I know you're following me. Show yourself."

The dragging stopped.

The song continued.

*"The scholar's brush wrote poems of love, the scholar's heart sank like a dove..."*

Bai Su forced herself to keep walking. She had read about ghosts that fed on fear. In the academy library, tucked between treatises on Confucian ethics and agricultural reform, there was a slim volume titled *Records of Spectral Manifestations in Imperial Examination Halls*. The author, a disgraced censor who had spent his final years investigating hauntings, had written at length about "echo spirits"—ghosts that mimicked the voices of the dead to lure the living into danger.

The trick, the censor wrote, was to ignore them. To refuse to acknowledge their presence until they ran out of the energy required to maintain the manifestation.

Easier said than done when the song was getting louder.

Bai Su rounded the final corner and emerged into the tower's ground floor. The servant girl was waiting for her, hands clasped, face composed.

"This way, Miss Bai. The headmaster is in his office."

The girl turned and walked through the main hall, past the reception desk where an old clerk was sorting papers by candlelight. Bai Su followed, her eyes scanning the shadows. The song had stopped. In its place was an oppressive silence that pressed against her ears like cotton.

"Miss Bai?"

She blinked. The servant had stopped at a door at the end of the hall, holding it open with an expectant look.

"The headmaster is waiting."

Bai Su nodded and stepped through the doorway.

The room was empty.

She turned around. The door was closed. The servant was gone.

"Headmaster?" she called, her voice swallowed by the heavy curtains that lined the walls. The office was smaller than she expected, cluttered with books and scrolls and half-finished calligraphy. A desk sat in the center, covered in papers. A lamp burned low, casting long shadows that danced across the ceiling.

On the desk, a single examination scroll lay open.

Bai Su approached it slowly. The scroll was old, the paper yellowed and brittle at the edges. The ink had faded to a pale brown, but the characters were still readable. It was an examination essay—a response to a prompt about governance and virtue. The handwriting was elegant, precise, the work of a scholar who had spent years perfecting his craft.

At the bottom of the scroll, in the space reserved for the examiner's seal, was a bloodstain.

Not a splash or a smear. A deliberate mark, shaped like a fingerprint pressed into the paper. Dried. Cracked. Old.

Bai Su reached out to touch it.

The lamp went out.

Darkness swallowed the room, thick and absolute. Bai Su held her breath, listening. The silence was complete—no wind, no creaking wood, not even the sound of her own heartbeat.

Then, from somewhere behind her, a voice spoke.

"Don't touch that."

She spun around. A figure stood in the corner, barely visible in the darkness. Tall. Slender. Wearing the robes of a scholar, soaked through with water that dripped onto the floor in steady, measured drops.

"You're the ghost," Bai Su said. "The one who's been following me."

The figure didn't move. "I'm not the one you should be afraid of."

"Then who should I be afraid of?"

"The one who killed Chen Feng." The voice was soft, almost gentle, like a teacher explaining a difficult concept to a slow student. "The one who killed him and made it look like an accident. The one who's been killing for a very, very long time."

Bai Su's hand went to her pocket, where she kept the jade pendant her mother had given her. It was warm to the touch—a sign, the old women in her village said, that evil was near.

"Why should I believe you?"

"Because I'm the one he killed first."

The figure stepped forward, into a sliver of moonlight that cut through the curtains. Bai Su saw his face—young, handsome, with eyes that held no malice. Just sadness. A deep, ancient sadness that seemed to have no bottom.

"I was the academy's top scholar," he said. "Twenty years ago. I was supposed to sit for the imperial examinations that autumn. I had been preparing my whole life. My family had sacrificed everything for my education. My father sold his land. My mother sold her jewelry. My sister..." His voice cracked. "My sister sold herself."

Bai Su's throat tightened. "What happened?"

"I died." He smiled, a bitter twist of his lips. "Three days before the examinations. The official cause was drowning. They said I fell into the lake while studying by the shore. They said I was drunk. They said I was careless." He shook his head. "But I didn't fall. I was pushed."

"By who?"

"I never saw his face. He came up behind me, put his hand on my head, and held me under. I fought. I scratched. I bit. But he was stronger. He held me until my lungs filled with water, until the light faded, until I became..." He gestured at himself. "This."

Bai Su's mind raced. "You've been here for twenty years. You've seen every death since then. You know who's behind it."

"Yes."

"Tell me."

"I can't."

"*Why not?*"

The ghost scholar's face twisted. "Because he's standing behind you."

Bai Su didn't turn. She didn't breathe. She felt the presence before she heard it—a cold that spread across her back like frost, a weight that pressed against her spine like a hand.

"Clever boy," a voice said. It was smooth, cultured, the voice of a man who had spent his life giving orders. "I wondered when you'd find the courage to speak to one of the living."

The ghost scholar's eyes widened. "Run," he whispered. "Miss Bai, run."

"Now why would she do that?" The voice behind her chuckled. "We've only just met. I've been watching you, Bai Su. Since the day you arrived. Do you know why?"

She forced herself to speak. "Because I can see ghosts."

"Because you can see ghosts." A pause. "And because you look just like your mother."

The words hit her like a physical blow. She turned.

The headmaster stood before her, but he was not the same man she had met on her first day. His face was the same—kind eyes, gentle smile, the bearing of a scholar who had dedicated his life to education. But his shadow was wrong. It stretched behind him, too long, too wide, and within it, Bai Su could see faces. Dozens of faces. All young. All male. All screaming.

"You knew my mother?" she whispered.

"I knew her very well." The headmaster's smile didn't change, but his eyes did. They grew cold, flat, like the surface of a frozen lake. "She was a student here, you know. A brilliant one. The best I ever taught. But she made a mistake."

"What mistake?"

"She fell in love with a ghost."

The room tilted. Bai Su grabbed the desk to steady herself. "My father—"

"Was a specter. A wisp. A remnant of a scholar who died before he could take his examinations." The headmaster's voice was conversational, almost pleasant. "She met him in the library. They studied together. They fell in love. And when she discovered she was pregnant with you, she ran. She left the academy, abandoned her studies, and disappeared into the countryside to raise you alone."

"*You're lying.*"

"I wish I were." The headmaster sighed. "Your mother was my greatest disappointment. I had such hopes for her. She could have been the first woman to pass the imperial examinations. She could have changed history. Instead, she threw it all away for a ghost."

Bai Su's hands were shaking. The jade pendant was burning against her chest. "If my father was a ghost, then I'm..."

"Half-dead," the headmaster finished. "From the moment you were born. That's why you can see spirits. That's why you can walk through the veil without tearing it. That's why you're the only one who can stop what's coming."

"What's coming?"

"The transfer." The headmaster stepped closer, and Bai Su saw that his feet didn't quite touch the ground. "You see, Bai Su, I've been collecting life qi for twenty years. Taking it from the living students, one by one, adding it to my own. I've been patient. I've been careful. But I'm almost there. One more harvest, and I'll have enough to become human again."

"You're a ghost."

"*I was a ghost.*" His voice hardened. "I died in this office, on the night before the examinations. A heart attack. I was forty-seven years old, at the peak of my career, and my heart simply gave out. I watched my own funeral from the ceiling. I watched them bury my body. I watched them replace me with a younger man who didn't know the first thing about teaching." His eyes burned. "I refused to leave. I refused to accept it. I found a way to stay."

"You found a way to kill."

"I found a way to *survive*." He spread his arms. "These twenty years, I've been building something. A new body. A new life. And you, Bai Su, are going to help me finish it."

"Why would I help you?"

"Because if you don't, I'll kill every living student in this academy. One by one. Starting with your friend." He smiled. "The girl you share a room with. What's her name? Mei Ling?"

Bai Su's blood turned to ice.

"You have until midnight," the headmaster said. "Then I start the harvest. You can either help me, or you can watch them die. Your choice."

He vanished.

The lamp flickered back to life.

Bai Su stood alone in the headmaster's office, staring at the bloodstained examination scroll on the desk. She picked it up, her fingers tracing the dried stain, and noticed something she hadn't seen before.

At the very bottom of the scroll, written in tiny characters that had faded nearly to invisibility, was a name.

*Bai Wei.*

Her mother's name.

The scroll trembled in her hands. She looked closer, and realized the bloodstain wasn't just a fingerprint. It was a seal. A personal seal, pressed into the paper with enough force to leave an impression on the wood beneath.

The seal of the headmaster.

But the headmaster had been dead for twenty years. The scroll was dated twenty-three years ago, three years before his

title: Chapter 7
content:
CHAPTER 7: The Blood That Binds

The headmaster's study smelled of sandalwood and secrets.

Bai Su stood before the massive oak desk, her hands clasped behind her back to hide their trembling. Headmaster Wen sat across from her, his fingers steepled, his ancient face unreadable in the flickering lamplight. Behind him, the wall was covered in scrolls—hundreds of them, each one bearing the name of a student who had passed through Wutong Academy.

Living and dead.

"The bell tower," he said, his voice carrying the weight of decades. "You were seen entering it this morning."

"I was following a lead."

"A lead." He unfolded his hands and placed them flat on the desk. "Miss Bai, do you know what happened in that tower three years ago?"

She shook her head.

"A student named Lin Wei hanged himself from the bell rope. He was found at dawn, his neck broken, his examination scroll stuffed into his mouth." The headmaster's eyes never left hers. "They say he failed the provincial exams. That the shame drove him to it."

"But that's not what happened."

It wasn't a question. Bai Su didn't know how she knew—only that the words felt true, settled in her bones like old grief.

"No," the headmaster agreed. "Lin Wei came in first place. He was the top scholar of his year. But when the results were posted, someone else's name appeared on the list. Someone with connections in the capital." He paused. "Lin Wei didn't kill himself. He was murdered."

The air in the room grew cold.

"The ghost in the bell chamber," Bai Su said slowly. "That was him?"

"One of him." The headmaster reached into his desk drawer and pulled out a scroll, yellowed with age. He unrolled it carefully, revealing rows of elegant calligraphy. "Lin Wei was the first. Since then, six more students have died in that tower. Each one a top scholar. Each one found with their examination scroll stuffed down their throat."

"Someone is killing the best students."

"Someone is collecting them." He slid the scroll across the desk. "Look at the names."

Bai Su leaned forward. The list was written in crimson ink, the characters sharp and deliberate:

Lin Wei - 1st Place, Provincial Exams, Year of the Tiger
Chen Feng - 1st Place, Provincial Exams, Year of the Rabbit
Wang Xue - 1st Place, Provincial Exams, Year of the Dragon
Zhang Lei - 1st Place, Provincial Exams, Year of the Snake
Liu Hua - 1st Place, Provincial Exams, Year of the Horse
Zhao Ming - 1st Place, Provincial Exams, Year of the Goat
Sun Yi - 1st Place, Provincial Exams, Year of the Monkey

Seven names. Seven dead scholars.

And at the bottom, written in fresher ink, still wet:

Bai Su - 1st Place, Provincial Exams, Year of the Rooster

She jerked her hands back as if burned.

"I've never taken the provincial exams."

"No. But you will, in three months." The headmaster's voice was barely a whisper. "And if the pattern holds, you will be found in that bell tower the morning after, your scroll in your mouth and your soul torn from your body."

The lamp flickered. Shadows danced across the walls like grasping hands.

"Why are you telling me this?"

"Because I think you already know." He folded his hands again. "The blood on your hands—you saw it, didn't you? In the bell chamber. Old blood. Dried blood. Blood that should have been washed away years ago."

Bai Su's throat tightened. "How do you know about that?"

"Because I put it there." The headmaster's face was stone. "Every student who enters that tower leaves with a mark. A stain that only they can see. It's a curse, Miss Bai. A curse that passes from victim to victim, binding them together in death."

"A curse?"

"An oath. A promise." He leaned forward, his eyes burning. "The ghost in that tower isn't just Lin Wei. It's all of them. Seven souls, twisted together, hungry for the eighth. They've been waiting for you since the day you enrolled."

Bai Su's blood turned to ice.

"The ghost scholar who warned me—"

"Is one of them. The most dangerous one." The headmaster's voice hardened. "He appears as a friend. A guide. He tells you just enough truth to make you trust him. And then, when you least expect it, he takes everything."

She thought of the boy in the library. His translucent skin. His sad, knowing eyes. The way he had touched her hand, and how warmth had bloomed in her chest like a flower opening to the sun.

"He's the killer."

"He's the instrument." The headmaster stood, his robes pooling around him like shadows given form. "The true killer is older. Stronger. A ghost who has been feeding on life qi for centuries, growing more powerful with each death. The scholars are just fuel. You are the final ingredient."

"For what?"

"For resurrection." He walked to the window and gazed out at the courtyard below. "There is a ritual, Miss Bai, that can bring a ghost back to life. But it requires a price. Seven souls to build the vessel, and one more to fill it. A living soul, taken at the moment of greatest achievement."

"The examination results."

"Exactly." He turned to face her. "You will take the provincial exams in three months. You will come in first place. And on that night, while you sleep, they will come for you."

Bai Su's hands were steady now. The fear had burned away, replaced by something cold and sharp.

"Then I'll stop taking the exams."

"You can't." The headmaster's voice was soft, almost gentle. "The curse has already marked you. If you don't take the exams, the ghost will come anyway. He'll find you in your sleep, in your dreams, in the spaces between heartbeats. And he will tear your soul apart, piece by piece, until there's nothing left."

"Then what do I do?"

"Find the source." He walked back to his desk and pulled out a second scroll, older than the first, its edges crumbling. "This is the original record of Wutong Academy. It was founded three hundred years ago by a scholar named Li Feng. He was brilliant, ambitious, and utterly ruthless. He built this academy on a grave."

"A grave?"

"His own." The headmaster unrolled the scroll. "Li Feng died the night before the imperial exams. Poisoned by a rival. But he refused to accept death. He performed a ritual—the same ritual that now threatens you—and bound his soul to this place. He became the academy's first ghost."

"The headmaster before you."

"Exactly." The headmaster's eyes were hollow. "Every headmaster since has been a ghost. Including me."

Bai Su took a step back.

"I died forty years ago, Miss Bai. Strangled in this very room by a student who had discovered the truth." He touched his throat, where faint discoloration marked the skin. "But Li Feng's curse keeps me here. Bound to this desk, these walls, this endless cycle of death."

"Then why are you helping me?"

"Because I am tired." His voice cracked. "Three hundred years of blood and secrets. Three hundred years of watching brilliant young minds be snuffed out before their time. I want it to end."

"How?"

"Destroy the source." The headmaster's hands trembled. "Li Feng's body is buried beneath the bell tower. His heart was removed and preserved in a jade jar, kept beating by the life qi he's stolen over the centuries. If you destroy the heart, the curse breaks. All the trapped souls will be freed."

"All of them?"

"Every ghost in this academy." He paused. "Including the one you trust."

The ghost scholar. The boy with the sad eyes and the gentle voice.

"He doesn't know," Bai Su said. "He doesn't know he's part of the curse."

"He knows." The headmaster's voice was bitter. "He just doesn't care. The promise of resurrection is too sweet. He'll betray you, Miss Bai. They all will."

A knock at the door made them both turn.

The servant girl from earlier stood in the doorway, her face pale. "Headmaster, there's been an incident."

"What kind of incident?"

"It's the library." She swallowed hard. "The east wing has collapsed. They're pulling bodies from the rubble."

Bai Su's heart stopped.

"Which bodies?"

The servant girl's eyes filled with tears. "Three students. All living. All top scholars." She held out a scrap of paper, crumpled and bloodstained. "This was found on the first body."

The headmaster took it and read aloud:

*"Eight is the number of completion. Eight is the number of the grave. Come, eighth scholar, join your brothers. The bell tolls for you."*

The writing was in Bai Su's own hand.

She stared at the words, her mind reeling. She had never written that. She had never seen that paper before in her life. But the characters were unmistakable—her unique stroke patterns, her distinctive brush technique, her signature at the bottom.

"Miss Bai," the headmaster said slowly, "when was the last time you slept?"

She couldn't remember.

She tried to think back, to count the nights, but the memories blurred together like watercolors in rain. The library. The bell tower. The blood on her hands. The footprint that matched her shoe.

She looked down at her palms.

They were clean.

But beneath her fingernails, she saw it.

Dried blood. Fresh this time.

"I don't understand," she whispered.

"Neither do I." The headmaster's face was pale. "But I think you need to tell me everything. From the beginning."

Bai Su opened her mouth to speak.

And the bell began to toll.

One. Two. Three.

Each ring shook the walls, rattled the scrolls, sent dust cascading from the ceiling.

"Who's ringing the bell?" the servant girl cried.

No one answered.

Because they all knew.

The bell tower was empty. The rope hung still. And yet the bell continued to ring, louder and louder, until the air itself seemed to scream.

Four. Five. Six.

Bai Su's hands flew to her ears, but the sound was inside her head, pounding against her skull, a rhythm that matched her heartbeat.

Seven. Eight.

The ninth ring never came.

Instead, a voice filled the room, soft and sweet, singing an old folk song:

*"Eight scholars went to the tower,*
*Eight scholars climbed the stair.*
*Seven came down with hearts a-flower,*
*One remained to rot up there."*

The lamp went out.

In the darkness, Bai Su felt a hand touch her shoulder. Cold. Familiar.

"Miss Bai," the ghost scholar whispered, his breath like frost on her neck, "I told you not to trust him."

She spun around.

He was standing in the moonlight that streamed through the window, his face half in shadow, his eyes glowing faintly.

"You're the killer," she said.

"I'm a victim." He stepped closer. "Just like you."

"I saw the list. I saw my name."

"And whose handwriting was it?" He held up the bloodstained paper. "Yours. You wrote that note, Bai Su. You just don't remember doing it."

"I would never—"

"Wouldn't you?" He tilted his head, and for a moment, his face flickered, showing something beneath—something ancient and hungry and terrible. "How do you know

title: Chapter 8
content:
CHAPTER 8: The Song Before Death

The headmaster's study smelled of ink and old paper, but beneath that lay something else. Something metallic. Bai Su paused at the threshold, her hand resting on the doorframe.

"Come in, Miss Bai." Headmaster Zhang sat behind his desk, his fingers steepled. His robes were immaculate, not a single thread out of place. But his eyes—his eyes were wrong. Too bright. Too alert. Like a man who had seen something he could not unsee.

She stepped inside. The door swung shut behind her, though no one had pushed it.

"Close," the headmaster said, gesturing to the chair across from him. "I have questions."

"So do I." Bai Su did not sit. "The bell tower—"

"Is irrelevant at the moment." He slid a scroll across the desk. It was old, yellowed at the edges, sealed with wax the color of dried blood. "This arrived for you this morning."

"For me?" She took it, turning it over in her hands. No return address. No name. Just her own, written in elegant calligraphy that seemed to shift as she watched. "Who delivered it?"

"The night watchman found it pinned to the academy gates at dawn. There was blood on the pin." The headmaster's voice was flat, clinical. "I have already had the gates inspected. No sign of forced entry. No sign of struggle."

Bai Su broke the seal.

The scroll unrolled with a whisper, revealing a single line of text written in the same shifting calligraphy:

*The living die. The dead remember. But who writes the song before the first note is sung?*

Below it, a date. Three days from now.

Below that, a name.

Chen Feng.

Her hands began to tremble. "This is a death threat."

"It is a warning." The headmaster stood, walked to the window. His reflection in the glass was hazy, as if seen through water. "Chen Feng died three months ago, Miss Bai. You know this. I know this. Everyone in the academy knows this."

"Then why—"

"Because someone is trying to tell you something." He turned to face her, and for the first time, she saw cracks in his composure. A slight twitch at the corner of his mouth. A tremor in his hands. "The question is: what?"

Bai Su read the line again. *But who writes the song before the first note is sung?*

The ghost scholar's words echoed in her mind. *A ghost that remembers how to live is a ghost that remembers how to kill.*

"What if it's not a warning?" she said slowly. "What if it's a confession?"

The headmaster's eyebrow rose. "Explain."

"The bell tower. The footprint. The blood on my hands." She looked down at her palms, still feeling the phantom warmth. "What if I was there? What if I saw something I wasn't supposed to see?"

"What if you did something you weren't supposed to do?"

The question hung in the air between them, heavy as a stone.

Bai Su's throat tightened. "I don't remember."

"No." The headmaster's voice softened, almost pitying. "You don't. And that, perhaps, is the most troubling part of all."

He returned to his desk, opened a drawer, and withdrew another scroll. This one was newer, the ink still fresh. "I received this yesterday from the imperial examiners. They have decided to advance the examination date. It will now be held in one month instead of three."

"A month?" Bai Su's mind raced. "But that's not enough time to prepare—"

"It is enough time for someone to complete what they have started." He unrolled the scroll, revealing a list of names. "These are the registered examinees. Living and dead, side by side. The examiners do not care about the distinction. They only care about results."

Bai Su scanned the list. Her name was there, near the bottom. Above it, names she recognized from the classes she attended. Names of students she had seen in the hallways, in the library, in the dining hall.

And at the very top, a name that made her blood run cold.

Li Wei.

The ghost scholar from the bell tower.

"He's registered?" she asked, her voice barely a whisper.

"He is a student of this academy." The headmaster's tone was careful, measured. "He has as much right to sit the examination as anyone else."

"He's a ghost!"

"So are half the students here. As I said, the examiners do not care." He rolled the scroll back up, tucked it into his sleeve. "But I would advise you to be careful, Miss Bai. The closer we get to the examination, the more desperate people become. Living and dead alike."

The door opened behind her. Bai Su turned to see a servant standing there, her face pale, her hands clasped in front of her.

"Headmaster," the servant said, her voice trembling, "there's been an incident."

"What kind of incident?"

"The library. The students found something. You need to come quickly."

Bai Su followed the headmaster through the winding hallways of the academy, her heart pounding in her chest. The servant led them to the main library, a cavernous room filled with towering shelves and the musty smell of ancient paper.

A crowd had gathered near the back wall. Students—living and dead alike—stood in a loose circle, their faces a mixture of horror and fascination.

"Make way," the headmaster commanded. The crowd parted, revealing what they had been gathered around.

A body lay on the floor. A living student, a boy Bai Su recognized from her morning classes. His eyes were open, his mouth frozen in a silent scream. His chest was still, his skin already turning cold.

But that wasn't what made Bai Su's stomach lurch.

It was the scroll clutched in his hands. Unrolled. Written on it, in the same shifting calligraphy as the one she had received, was a single line:

*The song begins.*

And below it, a name:

Chen Feng.

"Chen Feng's name again," Bai Su breathed. "But Chen Feng is already dead."

"Chen Feng is dead," the headmaster repeated, his voice hollow. "But his name keeps appearing. As if someone is trying to bring him back."

"Or as if someone is trying to finish what he started."

The voice came from behind her. Bai Su spun around.

Li Wei stood at the edge of the crowd, his translucent form flickering in the dim light. His eyes were fixed on the body, his expression unreadable.

"What do you know?" Bai Su demanded.

"Enough." He stepped forward, the crowd parting around him like water. "Chen Feng was not the first. He will not be the last."

"The first what?"

Li Wei's gaze met hers. There was something in his eyes—fear, perhaps, or sorrow. "The first to try. The first to fail."

"Try what?"

"To cross the veil." He knelt beside the body, his hand hovering over the dead boy's face without touching it. "To become something more than a ghost. To become something that should not exist."

The headmaster's face went pale. "The forbidden art."

"Yes." Li Wei looked up. "Chen Feng discovered it. A ritual that allows a ghost to take the life qi of the living and use it to reconstruct their own body. A body that can touch, can feel, can live again."

"But Chen Feng is dead," Bai Su said. "He died before he could complete it."

"His body died. But his knowledge did not." Li Wei stood, his form shimmering. "Someone else has found his notes. Someone else is trying to finish what he started."

The room fell silent. The students looked at each other, their faces a mixture of confusion and dread.

"The deaths," Bai Su said slowly. "The students who have been found drained of qi. That's what's happening. Someone is collecting life qi to fuel the ritual."

"Yes."

"But you said Chen Feng's notes were destroyed."

"I said I believed they were destroyed." Li Wei's voice was barely a whisper. "I was wrong."

"Where are they now?"

"I don't know." He turned to face her fully, and for a moment, she saw something raw and vulnerable in his eyes. "But I know who has them."

"Who?"

He opened his mouth to answer—

And the lights went out.

Bai Su heard screams, the sound of bodies colliding, the crash of books falling from shelves. She reached out, trying to find something solid, but her hand grasped only air.

Then she heard it.

A voice, soft and sweet, singing an old folk song. The same song she had heard in the bell tower. The same song that had followed her down the stairs.

*The moon hangs low. The river flows. The dead come home where the wild rose grows.*

She turned, following the sound. Her foot hit something soft. She looked down, and in the faint light filtering through the windows, she saw the body of the dead boy.

But his eyes were now closed. His hands, which had been clutching the scroll, were now folded neatly across his chest.

And his mouth was moving.

Singing.

*"The candle burns. The wheel still turns. The living learn what the dead have earned."*

Bai Su stumbled backward, her heart hammering against her ribs. She wanted to scream, but her voice had deserted her.

The singing stopped.

The lights came back on.

Li Wei was gone. The body was still. The scroll had vanished.

And in its place, on the floor where the boy had lain, was a single white flower.

A wild rose.

The headmaster stared at it, his face ashen. "This cannot be."

"What?" Bai Su demanded. "What is it?"

He looked up, and for the first time since she had met him, he looked genuinely afraid.

"The ghost of Chen Feng," he said slowly, "was buried with a wild rose in his hands. It was his final request. A symbol of his unfinished work."

Bai Su's blood ran cold.

"Someone is not just continuing his work," the headmaster continued, his voice shaking. "Someone is becoming him."

"Becoming him? How is that possible?"

The headmaster opened his mouth to answer—

The bell rang.

Once. Twice. Three times.

Then stopped.

And from somewhere far away, Bai Su heard the singing again. Fainter this time, almost drowned out by the pounding of her heart.

But clear enough to understand.

*"The song ends here. The path is clear. The ghost who lives will soon appear."*

The doors of the library slammed shut.

The windows darkened.

And Bai Su realized, with a cold certainty that settled into her bones like ice:

The killer was in the room with them.

And they had nowhere to run.

title: Chapter 9
content:
CHAPTER 9: The Face in the Ink

The headmaster's study smelled of old paper and dried plums, a scent so thick it coated the back of Bai Su's throat like dust. She stood before the massive rosewood desk, her hands clasped behind her back to hide the trembling she couldn't quite control.

Headmaster Zhou was not alone.

Three other teachers sat in the shadowed corners of the room—Old Chen, the calligraphy master whose brush strokes could cut paper; Madame Liang, the mathematics instructor whose abacus beads clicked like counting bones; and a woman Bai Su had never seen before, draped in gray silk that seemed to drink the lamplight.

"The bell tower," Headmaster Zhou said, his voice flat. "You were seen entering. You were seen leaving. And now Chen Feng's ghost has disappeared from the registry."

"I didn't—"

"The registry does not lie." He slid a scroll across the desk. It unrolled with a hiss, revealing row after row of names written in pale ink. Near the bottom, a single entry had faded to nothing, leaving only a water stain where Chen Feng's name had been.

Bai Su forced herself to breathe. "I found him in the bell chamber. He was trying to tell me something, but he vanished before he could finish."

"Vanished." The gray-clad woman spoke for the first time. Her voice was silk over steel. "How convenient."

"And you are?"

"Lin Daiyu. Spiritual Affairs Liaison." She did not offer her hand. "I've been assigned to investigate the recent... irregularities in student life force distribution."

Madame Liang's abacus clicked. "Thirty-seven students have reported night terrors in the past three days. Twenty-two have fainted during class. Four have coughed blood during morning exercises." Click click click. "The pattern suggests active qi draining, not passive haunting."

"The killer is accelerating," Old Chen rasped. "They're getting desperate. Or strong."

"Or both." Headmaster Zhou's eyes fixed on Bai Su. "You've been at this academy for less than a month. In that time, a ghost boy fell from the bell tower, a student nearly died during a summoning ritual, and now a registered spirit has been erased from existence. Forgive me if I find the coincidence... troubling."

"Someone is framing me."

"Are they?" Lin Daiyu rose from her chair. She moved like smoke, flowing around the desk until she stood directly in front of Bai Su. Her eyes were dark, almost black, with no discernible pupil. "Because I checked the bell chamber. There were two sets of footprints in the dust. One wearing academy-issued shoes. One wearing nothing at all."

"That was me and Chen Feng."

"No." She held up a piece of paper—a rubbing of the floor. "The barefoot prints were smaller. Delicate. A woman's foot, I'd say. Or a very small man." Her lips curved. "And they were on top of your prints. You walked into that room before Chen Feng arrived. You stood at the window. You watched him come up the stairs."

Bai Su's stomach dropped. "I don't remember—"

"Memory loss. How tragic." Lin Daiyu turned away. "Headmaster, I recommend placing Miss Bai under observation. She is either the killer, or she is being used as a vessel by the killer. Either way, she is not safe to be around living students."

"Agreed." Headmaster Zhou nodded. "Miss Bai, you will be confined to the east dormitory until further notice. Your classes will be suspended. Meals will be brought to you."

"You can't—"

"I can. I am the headmaster." He waved his hand. "Madame Liang, please escort her."

The mathematics teacher stood, her abacus clicking a final time. "This way, child."

Bai Su wanted to argue, to fight, to run. But the ghost scholar's words echoed in her mind: *They won't just become human again. They'll become something worse.*

She needed answers. She needed time. And she was about to get neither.

---

The east dormitory was reserved for female students, a three-story building of weathered brick and creeping ivy. Madame Liang led Bai Su to a room on the second floor—small, sparse, with a single window that overlooked the courtyard.
