It was a student roster. The names of every living and dead scholar at Wutong Academy, neatly written in columns of black and red. Black for the living. Red for the dead.

Her name was written in red.

Bai Su's breath caught in her chest. "That's impossible. I'm alive. I breathe. I eat. I—"

"All things that ghosts can do, given enough life qi." The headmaster's voice was gentle now, almost kind. "Tell me, Miss Bai. Do you remember anything before you arrived at this academy?"

She opened her mouth to answer. Closed it.

Nothing. She remembered nothing before the moment she had stepped through the academy gates. The carriage ride. The letter of acceptance. The face of her mother, waving goodbye—

No. She did not remember her mother's face. She remembered the idea of a mother waving goodbye, but when she tried to picture it, the image dissolved into a blur of colors and shapes, like a painting left out in the rain.

"I remember—" She stopped. What did she remember? The ghost scholar in the library. The drowned boy. The bell tower. The blood on her hands. The song.

*The song.*

"The song," she whispered. "The ghost was singing a song. An old folk song. I knew it. I shouldn't have known it, but I did."

"What song?"

She hummed a few notes. The melody curled through the air like smoke, ancient and melancholy. The headmaster's face went pale.

"Where did you hear that song?"

"The bell tower. After I found the footprint. A voice sang it to me."

"That's impossible." He stood abruptly, his chair scraping against the floor. "That song hasn't been sung in thirty years. Not since—" He stopped, his jaw tightening.

"Not since what?"

Headmaster Zhao walked to the window, his back to her. When he spoke again, his voice was hollow. "Not since the last time a student of this academy went mad and killed seven of her classmates before taking her own life. Her name was Bai Yu. She was your sister."

Bai Su felt the world tilt beneath her. "I don't have a sister."

"So you say. And yet the records show that Bai Yu, daughter of the House of Lingnan, was accepted into Wutong Academy seven years ago. She was a brilliant scholar. A prodigy. But something changed in her during her second year. She became withdrawn. Paranoid. She claimed she could see things that others could not." He turned to face her. "She claimed she could see ghosts."

"That's not—" Bai Su's hands were shaking. "That's just a coincidence. Many people claim to see ghosts."

"Do they? In this academy, perhaps. But Bai Yu was not at this academy when she made those claims. She was at home, in Lingnan, three hundred miles away. She came here to escape the visions. And three months after her arrival, she killed seven people and threw herself from the bell tower."

The bell tower. The window. The footprint.

"You think I'm her." The words tasted like ash. "You think I'm Bai Yu, come back to finish what she started."

"I think," the headmaster said slowly, "that the dead do not always stay dead. Especially not in this place. Especially not when they have unfinished business."

"Then why did you accept me into the academy? If you knew who I was, why let me in?"

"Because I wanted to see what you would do." He smiled, but there was no warmth in it. "And because the seal that held your sister's spirit required a living vessel to contain it. We needed someone to carry her. Someone who shared her blood, her face, her name."

Bai Su stared at him. "You used me as a cage."

"A cage with a key." He reached into his desk drawer and pulled out a jade pendant, carved in the shape of a lotus. "This was hers. She wore it the day she died. It contains the last vestiges of her consciousness. As long as you wear it, she cannot fully possess you."

He held it out to her. Bai Su did not take it.

"Why are you giving this to me?"

"Because the killer is not a ghost." The headmaster's eyes locked onto hers. "The killer is Bai Yu. She has been draining life qi from the living students, gathering strength, preparing for the moment when she can break through the seal entirely. When that happens, she will not just take over your body. She will erase you completely."

"And you want me to stop her."

"I want you to wear the pendant. It will suppress her influence, but it will also let you see through her eyes. You will know what she knows. You will remember what she remembers." He paused. "Including how she died."

Bai Su took the pendant. It was warm against her palm, pulsing with a faint rhythm that matched her heartbeat.

"What happened to her? The records say suicide, but you don't believe that."

"I believe that Bai Yu was murdered." The headmaster's voice dropped to a whisper. "I believe that someone in this academy killed her and made it look like a suicide. I believe that same someone is still here, still teaching, still walking these halls. And I believe that Bai Yu's ghost has been trying to tell us who it is for seven years."

"Then why hasn't she?"

"Because every time she gets close, something silences her. A death. A disappearance. A student who suddenly withdraws from the academy and is never heard from again." He leaned forward. "The killer knows she's awake. They have been keeping her quiet. And now that you are here, carrying her spirit, they will try to silence you too."

The pendant pulsed again, stronger this time. Bai Su felt a warmth spread through her chest, followed by a cold so deep it stole her breath.

*Little sister.*

The voice was soft, feminine, achingly familiar.

*Don't trust him. He was there. He saw everything. And he did nothing to stop it.*

Bai Su's head snapped up. The headmaster was watching her, his expression unreadable.

"She's speaking to you, isn't she?"

"Yes."

"What does she say?"

Bai Su looked at the jade pendant in her hand. She thought about the blood on her palms in the bell tower. The footprint that matched her shoe. The song she should not have known.

*He lies,* Bai Yu's voice whispered. *He wears the same pendant. He has always worn it. He was the one who killed me.*

Bai Su's fingers tightened around the lotus.

"She says you're a liar."

The headmaster's face did not change. "Of course she does. She has been telling everyone that for seven years. It is the last desperate attempt of a dying spirit to avoid the truth."

*He was my teacher,* Bai Yu's voice said, and now there was sorrow in it, deep and old and bleeding. *I trusted him. I told him everything. And when I learned what he was doing, he threw me from the tower with his own hands.*

"He lies," the headmaster said.

*She lies,* Bai Yu said.

Bai Su stood. The chair scraped against the floor. "I need time to think."

"Take all the time you need." The headmaster's eyes followed her like a hawk tracking prey. "But remember, Miss Bai. Every hour you wait, another student dies. And the closer Bai Yu gets to freedom, the more dangerous she becomes."

Bai Su walked to the door. Her hand was on the handle when he spoke again.

"One more thing. The song you heard. The one your sister used to sing." His voice was soft, almost tender. "She sang it to her victims. Every single one. The last thing they heard before she killed them was that melody."

Bai Su turned. "Why are you telling me this?"

"Because I want you to understand." He smiled, and it was the smile of a man who had already won. "You are not Bai Su. You never were. You are Bai Yu, wearing her sister's skin like a coat, and the song you heard in the bell tower—" He paused. "—was your own."

The pendant in Bai Su's hand turned to ice.

And somewhere deep inside her mind, Bai Yu began to laugh.

title: Chapter 18
content:
CHAPTER 18: The Weight of Forgotten Things

Bai Su's feet carried her through the winding corridors of Wutong Academy, but her mind remained trapped in the bell chamber. That footprint. Her footprint. The dried blood that appeared and vanished like a taunt from her own memory.

She had been there before Chen Feng died.

She had stood at that window.

The realization settled into her bones like frost, cold and creeping.

The headmaster's office sat at the end of a long, dim hallway lined with portraits of former scholars. Their painted eyes followed her as she walked. One of them—a stern-faced man with a scholar's cap and a white beard—seemed to frown deeper as she passed.

*You know something,* she thought at him. *You all know something, and no one will tell me.*

She raised her hand to knock, but the door swung open before her knuckles touched wood.

Headmaster Zhou stood in the doorway, his usual composed expression cracked by something she hadn't seen before: fear.

"Come in. Quickly."

She stepped inside. The office looked the same as it had during her first visit—scrolls stacked on every surface, the smell of old paper and ink, the bronze incense burner shaped like a qilin that never seemed to stop smoking. But there was something new on his desk.

A scroll.

Unrolled. Bloodstained.

"I found this in Chen Feng's room," Headmaster Zhou said, his voice tight. "Hidden beneath a floorboard. He must have written it before he died."

Bai Su approached the desk. The scroll was covered in small, precise characters, but they weren't in any language she recognized. Symbols coiled and twisted like living things, black ink on yellowed paper, punctuated by dark brown splatters that could only be dried blood.

"What is it?"

"I was hoping you could tell me." He looked at her with an intensity that made her skin prickle. "You're the one who can see them. The ghosts. The truth behind the veil."

"I can see them, but I can't read dead languages."

"Neither can I. But I know someone who can." He reached into his sleeve and pulled out a small jade pendant, shaped like a lotus flower. "Do you know what this is?"

Bai Su shook her head.

"This is a Soul Anchor. It binds a ghost to a specific location, prevents them from moving on to the afterlife. They're forbidden at Wutong Academy. Possession of one is grounds for immediate expulsion." He set the pendant on the desk beside the scroll. "I found this in Chen Feng's pocket."

"Chen Feng had a Soul Anchor?"

"No." Headmaster Zhou's voice dropped to a whisper. "Chen Feng never carried this. I found it in his pocket yesterday. But when I checked this morning, it was gone. I searched everywhere. Then I came back to my office, and it was sitting on my desk."

Bai Su's blood ran cold. "Someone's sending you a message."

"Someone's mocking me." He picked up the pendant, turning it over in his fingers. "Soul Anchors are powerful objects. They require a tremendous amount of qi to create. Only a master of necromantic arts could forge one."

"And you're telling me this because...?"

"Because the only person at this academy with that level of skill is me." He met her eyes. "And I did not create this."

The incense burner hissed. Smoke curled upward, forming shapes that dissolved before they could become anything recognizable.

"Someone is trying to frame you," Bai Su said slowly.

"Or someone is trying to tell me that there's a necromancer among the students." He set the pendant down. "Either way, I need your help. The ghost scholar you've been speaking to—the one in the bell chamber. He's been here longer than anyone. He knows things that are not written in any book."

"How do you know about him?"

"I know many things, Miss Bai. I know that you saw Chen Feng fall. I know that you found his examination scroll in the abandoned classroom. I know that you've been dreaming of a woman who sings an old folk song, a song that hasn't been sung in this region for three hundred years."

Bai Su's breath caught in her throat. "How—"

"Because that woman was my grandmother."

The room seemed to tilt. Bai Su gripped the edge of the desk, her knuckles white.

"Your grandmother?"

"She was a student here, a hundred and twenty years ago. She died in the bell chamber. Fell from the same window that Chen Feng fell from." He paused. "She was the first."

"The first what?"

"The first to die at Wutong Academy. The first of a pattern that has repeated itself every generation since." He opened a drawer and pulled out a stack of old records, bound with red string. "Six students. All fallen from that window. All on the night of the full moon. All with examination scrolls covered in the same symbols."

Bai Su stared at the records. Six deaths. Six students. And now Chen Feng made seven.

"Why didn't you tell me this before?"

"Because I didn't know if I could trust you." He untied the red string, letting the papers scatter across the desk. "But I've watched you, Miss Bai. I've seen the way you question things. The way you refuse to accept easy answers. You're not like the other students who come here, desperate to pass their exams and leave. You're looking for something."

"I'm looking for the truth."

"So was my grandmother." He handed her a yellowed photograph. A young woman with kind eyes and a serious face, dressed in the robes of a century past. She stood outside the bell tower, one hand raised as if waving at the photographer. "Her name was Lin Mei. She was the most brilliant student this academy had ever seen. She could read the language of the dead before she turned seventeen."

"The language of the dead?"

"The symbols on that scroll. It's a dead language, one that predates the empire itself. Only those who have touched the veil between life and death can read it." He looked at her. "And you've touched the veil, Miss Bai. More than once."

Bai Su thought of the ghost scholar, of the way his hand had felt solid against her wrist. She thought of the blood on her palms, the footprint that matched her shoe.

"I don't remember," she whispered.

"No. You wouldn't." Headmaster Zhou's voice softened. "The veil takes things from you. Memories. Senses. Pieces of yourself that you don't realize are missing until you need them."

"Then how do I get them back?"

He was quiet for a long moment. Then he reached into his collar and pulled out a second pendant, identical to the one on the desk.

"You find the one who's been taking them."

Bai Su's mind raced. "The killer. The ghost student who's been stealing life qi."

"Precisely. Every time they take qi, the veil weakens. Every time the veil weakens, the dead grow stronger." He pressed the second pendant into her hand. "Take this. It will protect you from the worst of their influence. But it comes with a price."

"What price?"

"It binds you to this academy. As long as you wear it, you cannot leave. The moment you step beyond the gates, the pendant will drain your qi until you're nothing but a husk."

Bai Su looked at the pendant in her palm. A Soul Anchor. A prison.

"If I wear this, I'm trapped here forever."

"If you don't, you'll be dead before the week is out." He turned away, walking to the window that overlooked the courtyard. The moon was rising, fat and white, casting silver light across the stones where Chen Feng had fallen. "The killer knows you're close. They've been watching you since the day you arrived. The blood on your hands, the footprint in the dust—those weren't coincidences. They were warnings."

"Or memories."

He turned, surprised.

"Maybe I really was there," Bai Su said slowly. "Maybe I saw Chen Feng fall. Maybe I was the one who found his scroll, who hid it in that classroom." She looked at her palms, clean now but still tingling with phantom warmth. "Maybe I wrote those symbols."

"You don't believe that."

"I don't know what I believe anymore." She closed her fingers around the pendant. It was warm, almost alive. "But I know one thing: whoever's doing this, they want me to doubt myself. They want me to question my own mind. And that's the one thing I refuse to do."

Headmaster Zhou smiled, a sad, weary expression that made him look older than his years.

"You remind me of her. My grandmother." He turned back to the window. "She said the same thing, the night before she died."

The pendant pulsed in Bai Su's hand. A warning. A promise.

The bells began to ring.

Not the bell chamber—these were smaller, closer. The bells on the headmaster's desk, the ones he used to summon the faculty. They rang without anyone touching them, a frantic, jangling alarm that set Bai Su's teeth on edge.

"What's happening?"

Headmaster Zhou's face went pale. "Someone's broken the seal on the East Wing."

"The East Wing? That's where they keep the—"

"The bodies. The students who died before they could be buried." He grabbed his coat. "If a ghost gets inside, if they possess one of those bodies—"

"Then they become real. Flesh and bone. A body that can live." Bai Su remembered the ghost scholar's warning. *A ghost that remembers how to live is a ghost that remembers how to kill.*

They ran.

The East Wing was a squat, windowless building at the edge of the academy grounds, surrounded by iron gates that had been bent and twisted like wire. The doors stood open, a black maw that swallowed the moonlight.

Inside, the air was cold and still. Rows of wooden tables stretched into the darkness, each one bearing a covered form. The smell of formaldehyde and decay hung thick as fog.

A figure stood at the far end of the room.

Tall. Slender. Wearing the robes of a student.

"Hello, Bai Su," the figure said, and the voice was familiar. Sweet. Like a lullaby she had heard in dreams. "I've been waiting for you."

The figure turned.

It was the woman from her dream. The singer of the old folk song. But her face was wrong—too smooth, too pale, like a mask stretched over something that did not belong.

"You're the one," Bai Su whispered. "The one who killed Chen Feng."

"I'm the one who set him free." The woman smiled, and her teeth were too sharp, too many. "He was trapped here, just like the rest of us. Just like you."

"I'm not trapped."

"Not yet." She stepped forward, and the bodies on the tables began to stir. Fingers twitched. Lids fluttered. "But you will be. We all will be. That's the curse of Wutong Academy."

"Curse?"

"The bell tower. The window. The fall." She stopped in front of Bai Su, close enough to touch. "Every generation, a student dies. Every generation, the cycle repeats. And every generation, someone tries to stop it."

She reached out and touched Bai Su's cheek. Her fingers were cold as stone.

"You're the one who ends it, Bai Su. Or the one who becomes it."

She leaned in, her lips brushing Bai Su's ear.

"Find me in your dreams tonight. I'll show you what really happened to Chen Feng."

She stepped back. Her body dissolved into mist, swirling upward, vanishing through the cracks in the ceiling.

The bodies stopped moving.

The bells stopped ringing.

Bai

title: Chapter 19
content:
CHAPTER 19: The Song Before Dying

The headmaster's study smelled of ink and burning paper, a combination that made Bai Su's stomach turn. She had been here only once before, on her first day at Wutong Academy, and the memory of that interview—the headmaster's piercing gaze, his questions about her ability to see ghosts—had not faded.

Now she sat across from him, her hands folded in her lap, still feeling the phantom warmth of blood that had long since vanished.

"You've been busy, Miss Bai." Headmaster Zhou was a thin man with a scholar's beard and a corpse's pallor. He did not blink when he spoke, which made every conversation feel like an interrogation. "I hear you've been asking questions. Disturbing the peace. Accusing dead boys of murder."

"I asked one question to one person."

"And that person came straight to me." He slid a scroll across the desk. "Do you know what this is?"

Bai Su unrolled it. The characters were elegant, precise, written in the hand of someone who had spent years perfecting their calligraphy. It was a death record. Chen Feng's death record.

"Written by the magistrate himself," Headmaster Zhou continued. "Confirmed by three witnesses. Chen Feng fell from the bell tower. He was alone. He had been drinking. The verdict was accident."

"I saw a footprint."

"You saw dust and shadow and made it into a conspiracy." He leaned forward, his eyes narrowing. "Tell me, Miss Bai. Why is a girl from the countryside, barely two weeks into her studies, so determined to prove that a dead man was murdered?"

Bai Su met his gaze. "Because someone is killing students. And I think Chen Feng was the first."

The headmaster's face did not change. But the candle on his desk flickered, casting long shadows across the room. For a moment, Bai Su could have sworn she saw something move in the darkness behind him.

"You're accusing a ghost."

"I'm accusing someone. Or something. The ghost students are scared, Headmaster. They're disappearing. And the living students are starting to notice that their classmates are acting strange."

"Strange how?"

Bai Su hesitated. She had not told anyone about what she had seen in the dormitory—the way Li Wei's eyes had glazed over, the way his skin had felt cold to the touch, the way he had spoken in a voice that was not his own.

"Possession," she said finally. "I think the killer is taking over living bodies."

Headmaster Zhou was silent for a long moment. Then he laughed. It was not a pleasant sound—dry and brittle, like leaves crumbling underfoot.

"You've been reading too many ghost stories, Miss Bai. This is an academy, not a haunted temple. The dead study here. They do not possess the living."

"Then explain Li Wei."

"What about him?"

"Yesterday, he didn't recognize his own name. He stared at his hands like he had never seen them before. He asked me what year it was." She leaned forward. "I think someone is inside him, Headmaster. I think the same someone who killed Chen Feng is wearing Li Wei's skin."

The headmaster's smile faded. He reached into his sleeve and pulled out a folded piece of paper, yellowed with age. He did not open it. He simply held it, his fingers tracing the edges.

"How much do you know about the ghost who haunts this academy, Miss Bai?"

"I know there are dozens. Maybe hundreds."

"I'm not talking about the students." He unfolded the paper. On it was a charcoal sketch of a woman, her hair long and wild, her eyes hollow and dark. She was beautiful in the way that corpses are beautiful—perfect and terrible and utterly empty. "I'm talking about the one who came before them. The one who built this place."

Bai Su stared at the sketch. There was something familiar about the woman's face, something that tugged at the edges of her memory like a half-remembered dream.

"Her name was Lin Xiu," the headmaster said. "She was the daughter of a wealthy merchant. She was also a scholar—one of the most brilliant minds of her generation. She took the imperial exams disguised as a man and placed first in the province."

"Then she was discovered."

"Then she was discovered." He nodded slowly. "They stripped her of her title. They burned her essays. They locked her in her family's compound and forced her to marry a man she had never met." He paused. "On the night before her wedding, she hanged herself from the beam of her study."

The candle flickered again. The shadows seemed to grow longer, reaching toward Bai Su like grasping hands.

"They say she cursed the exams before she died. Cursed the scholars who had taken everything from her. Cursed the academy that had refused her entry." He folded the paper and tucked it back into his sleeve. "And they say her ghost still walks these halls, looking for students who succeeded where she failed."

"You think she's the killer."

"I think she has been waiting a long time for someone like you." He looked at her with something that might have been pity. "Someone who can see the dead. Someone who can hear their whispers. Someone who can open doors that should remain closed."

Bai Su's blood ran cold. "What doors?"

"The doors between the living and the dead. The veil that separates our world from theirs." He stood, walking to the window. Outside, the courtyard was empty, the students all in their classrooms. "Lin Xiu was powerful when she was alive. She is more powerful now. And if she finds a way to break through the veil completely—"

"She'll kill everyone."

"She'll do worse than that." He turned to face her. "She'll make them join her. Every living student in this academy will become a vessel for the dead. And the dead will walk the earth again, wearing the faces of the living."

Bai Su thought of Li Wei's empty eyes. Thought of the coldness of his skin. Thought of the voice that had spoken through him—a woman's voice, sweet and melodic, singing an old folk song.

"What song is it?" she asked suddenly.

"I beg your pardon?"

"The song. The one she sings. It's familiar, but I can't place it."

Headmaster Zhou's face went pale. "You've heard her sing?"

"On the stairs. In the bell tower. Everywhere I go, I hear it." She hummed a few bars. "It sounds like a lullaby."

"It is a lullaby." He sat down heavily, his hands trembling. "She wrote it for her son. The one she was forced to give up before her wedding. The one she never got to hold."

Bai Su felt the world tilt beneath her. "She had a child?"

"She was pregnant when she hanged herself. They cut the baby from her womb, but it was too late. The cord was wrapped around its neck." He closed his eyes. "They say the child's ghost haunts the bell tower, waiting for its mother to come back. Waiting for someone to hold it."

The bell above them rang. Once. Twice. Three times.

Bai Su looked up. The bell was ringing on its own again, but this time, she noticed something she had not noticed before. The sound was not random. It was a rhythm. A pattern.

It was the lullaby.

"It's not Lin Xiu," she whispered.

"What?"

"The killer. It's not Lin Xiu." She stood, her heart pounding. "It's her child. The one in the bell tower. The one who has been waiting all these years for someone to notice."

Headmaster Zhou's face went slack with horror. "That's impossible. The child was stillborn. It never drew breath."

"It didn't need to." Bai Su was already moving toward the door. "It's been feeding on life qi since the day it was born. Taking just enough to stay visible. Just enough to grow."

"Grow into what?"

She stopped at the threshold and looked back at him. "Into something that remembers what it feels like to be alive. And that will do anything to feel it again."

She ran.

The bell tower was empty when she reached it, but the rope was still swinging, creaking back and forth like a pendulum counting down to something terrible. Bai Su climbed the stairs, her legs burning, her lungs screaming for air.

At the top, she found Li Wei.

He was standing at the window, his back to her, his shoulders shaking with silent sobs. The moonlight streamed through the broken glass, casting his shadow across the floor.

"Li Wei?"

He turned.

It was not Li Wei.

The thing that looked at her had his eyes, his mouth, his face. But the expression was wrong. Too old. Too knowing. Too hungry.

"You can see me," it said. But the voice was not Li Wei's. It was the voice of a child, high and sweet and terrifying. "No one has seen me in a hundred years."

"You're the one who killed Chen Feng."

"I didn't kill him." The thing in Li Wei's body smiled. "I just helped him fall. He was so scared, you see. So scared of failing the exams. I told him I could make it stop. I told him I could make all the pain go away."

"And he believed you."

"They all believe me." It stepped closer. "They hear me singing, and they think it's a lullaby. They think I'm trying to comfort them." It laughed, a sound like breaking glass. "I'm not comforting them. I'm calling them home."

"Home?"

"To the place where the dead sleep. To the place where the veil is thinnest." It reached out and touched Bai Su's face. Its fingers were cold. "You're special, Bai Su. You can see me. You can hear me. You could help me."

"Help you do what?"

"Help me wake up." The thing smiled wider, and for a moment, Bai Su saw something flicker behind Li Wei's eyes—a child, small and pale, wrapped in shadows. "Help me become real."

The bell rang again. The thing in Li Wei's body began to sing.

And Bai Su realized, with a horror that froze her blood, that she knew the words.

title: Chapter 20
content:
CHAPTER 20: The Song Beneath the Skin

The headmaster's office smelled of ink and ash, a combination that made Bai Su's stomach turn. She stood before the massive oak desk, her hands still damp from scrubbing them raw in the courtyard well. The blood wouldn't come off. Not the real blood—that had vanished the moment the servant appeared—but the memory of it. The weight. The warmth.

Headmaster Zhou sat across from her, his fingers steepled beneath his chin. He was a living man, but barely. His skin had the color of old parchment, and his eyes were the pale gray of winter clouds. He had been at Wutong Academy for forty years, or so the rumors said. No one knew for certain. No one asked.

"We have a problem," he said.

Bai Su said nothing. She had learned that silence was safer than speech in this place.

"The ghost student you encountered in the bell tower. Chen Feng." The headmaster's voice was flat, clinical. "He was murdered three years ago. Thrown from that very window. The case was never solved."

"I know."

"Then you also know that he has been haunting that chamber ever since. Waiting. Watching. And now, someone has been feeding him life qi."

Bai Su's breath caught. "Feeding him?"

"Not all qi transfers are violent, Miss Bai. Some are consensual. A student who feels sympathy for a ghost might offer a strand of their own life force. A touch here. A whisper there. Nothing that would kill them outright, but enough to sustain a spirit past its natural decay."

"Who would do that?"

"We were hoping you could tell us." The headmaster slid a scroll across the desk. It was yellowed with age, the edges frayed and crumbling. "This was found in Chen Feng's cell late last night. It contains a list of names. Living students who have been marked for death."

Bai Su unrolled the scroll. Her own name was at the top.

She stared at it for a long moment, her mind racing through possibilities. A warning? A threat? A confession? The characters were written in a careful, elegant hand, each stroke deliberate and precise. She recognized the style.

"This is calligraphy," she said. "Not just writing. This is the work of someone who trained in the classical tradition. A scholar."

"Indeed."

"And the ink." She brought the scroll closer to her nose. "It's mixed with cinnabar. The kind used in talismans."

The headmaster's eyes narrowed. "You know your arts."

"My grandmother was a medium. She taught me to recognize the tools of the trade." Bai Su set the scroll down. "Cinnabar ink is used to bind spirits. This isn't a list of victims. It's a list of anchors. Someone is using living students to hold Chen Feng's soul in place."

The headmaster's face went pale. "That's impossible. Such rituals require the consent of the living—"

"Or their ignorance." Bai Su's voice was sharp. "If a student unknowingly wears a talisman, carries a charm, accepts a gift that has been blessed with their own name written in cinnabar—they become an anchor without ever knowing it."

The silence between them stretched like a wire about to snap.

"We have three days," the headmaster finally said. "At the end of this week, the moon will be dark. The veil between worlds will thin to nothing. If Chen Feng receives enough qi by then—"

"He'll break through," Bai Su finished. "I know."

"How do you know?"

She didn't answer. She couldn't explain the dream she had been having every night for the past week: a bell tower, a window, a fall that never ended. She couldn't tell him about the voice that sang to her in her sleep, the same song she had heard in the bell chamber. Sweet. Familiar. Like something she had known before she was born.

"Miss Bai." The headmaster leaned forward. "There is something else you should know."

She looked up.

"Chen Feng did not die alone. There were three other students who fell from that tower that night. All of them were marked with the same symbol." He drew a character on the desk with his finger: a circle with a line through it, like a broken moon. "Do you recognize this?"

Bai Su's blood turned to ice.

It was the same symbol carved into the back of her grandmother's headstone.

She stood up so quickly her chair scraped against the floor. "I need to see the other bodies."

"Miss Bai—"

"Where are they buried?"

The headmaster hesitated. Then he opened a drawer in his desk and pulled out a key made of bone. "The academy keeps a record of all deaths on its grounds. There is a vault beneath the east courtyard. But I must warn you, Miss Bai—what you find there may change everything you think you know about this place."

Bai Su took the key. It was cold in her hand, colder than bone had any right to be.

She left the office without another word.

---

The east courtyard was empty when she arrived. The sun hung low in the sky, casting long shadows across the cobblestones. Bai Su found the entrance to the vault hidden beneath a rusted grate, half-concealed by fallen leaves and neglect.

She fitted the bone key into the lock. It turned with a sound like a scream.

The stairs descended into darkness. Bai Su lit a lantern she had borrowed from the library and made her way down, each step echoing in the narrow corridor. The air grew colder as she descended, thick with the smell of earth and decay.

The vault was a single room, circular, with shelves carved into the stone walls. On each shelf rested a clay jar, sealed with wax and marked with a name. Bai Su counted them. Twenty-seven jars. Twenty-seven dead.

She found Chen Feng's jar near the back. It was smaller than the others, the wax seal cracked and crumbling. She picked it up, careful not to break it further.

A piece of paper was tucked beneath the jar. She unfolded it.

It was a letter. Written in the same elegant hand as the scroll.

*Chen Feng,*

*I told you to wait. I told you to be patient. But you never listened, did you? You always had to see, always had to know. And now you know too much, and I cannot let you live with that knowledge.*

*Forgive me.*

*I will see you on the other side.*

It was signed with the same symbol: the broken moon.

Bai Su's hands trembled as she read the letter again. The handwriting was achingly familiar. She had seen it thousands of times before, on birthday cards and grocery lists and letters sent across the sea.

It was her grandmother's handwriting.

But her grandmother had died ten years ago. She had been buried in the family plot, three provinces away. She had never set foot in Wutong Academy.

Had she?

The lantern flickered. The shadows in the vault seemed to grow longer, darker, as if they were reaching for her.

Bai Su turned to leave.

A figure stood at the top of the stairs.

It was a woman, her face obscured by shadow, her robes trailing across the stone floor. She was holding a lantern of her own, its light pale and cold.

"Bai Su," the woman said. Her voice was sweet, familiar, like a song remembered from childhood. "I've been waiting for you."

Bai Su's breath caught in her throat. "Grandmother?"

The woman stepped forward, and the light fell across her face.

It was her grandmother. The same kind eyes, the same gentle smile, the same silver hair braided down her back. But her skin was translucent, glowing faintly in the dark, and her feet did not touch the ground.

"I know you have questions," the ghost said. "But we don't have much time. He knows you're here. He's coming."

"Who?"

"The killer. The one who's been feeding Chen Feng. The one who started all of this." Her grandmother's smile faded. "And the one who killed me."

Bai Su's world tilted. "What?"

"I didn't die of old age, Su. I was murdered. Here, in this academy, forty years ago. My body was buried beneath this very vault." She gestured to the jars around them. "I am one of them. I have always been one of them."

The lantern in Bai Su's hand went out.

Darkness swallowed the vault.

And in the darkness, her grandmother's voice whispered, "The killer is not a ghost, Su. The killer is a living student. Someone you know. Someone you trust."

The sound of footsteps echoed from above.

"Heavy footsteps. Deliberate. A living man's tread.

"Run," her grandmother said. "Run, and do not stop until you find the truth."

Bai Su ran.

She burst out of the vault, into the courtyard, into the fading light of dusk. She did not look back. She did not stop until she reached the library, her chest heaving, her mind reeling.

She collapsed against the door, gasping for breath.

And then she saw it.

A talisman, pinned to the inside of her sleeve. Small, delicate, written in cinnabar ink.

Her own name, in the same hand as the scroll.

She had been an anchor all along.

The bell above the academy began to toll.

Once. Twice. Three times.

And somewhere in the darkness, a voice began to sing.

title: The Song Before Drowning
content:
The headmaster's office was a study in contradictions. Ancient scrolls lined walls that hummed with electric lights. A bronze incense burner sat beside a modern fountain pen. The headmaster himself was a thin man with a scholar's robe and a bureaucrat's spectacles, his face a mask of professional concern that did not quite reach his eyes.

"Miss Bai." He gestured to a chair. "Please sit."

Bai Su did not sit. She stood with her back to the door, her hands clasped behind her to hide their trembling. "You said it was urgent."

"Your classmate, Lin Wei. He has been reported missing since this morning's meditation period."

Lin Wei. The boy who had sat beside her in calligraphy class. The one who had whispered that the ghost students always sat in the back row because the living blocked their view of the teacher. Bai Su remembered his laugh, easy and warm, like sunlight through paper screens.

"What does that have to do with me?"

"I'm told you were the last person to see him." The headmaster adjusted his spectacles. "He was heading toward the east library after breakfast. You were seen following him."

"I wasn't following him. We were both going to the same place."

"At the same time? On the same path?" The headmaster's voice remained calm, but his eyes sharpened. "Miss Bai, I am not accusing you of anything. But we have a missing student, a dead student, and a ghost problem that seems to be getting worse by the hour. You are the only living student who can see the spirits directly. I need to know everything you know."

Bai Su opened her mouth to answer, but the words died in her throat.

The song.

It came from somewhere deep in the building, muffled by walls and distance, but unmistakable. A woman's voice, sweet and high, singing a lullaby that Bai Su had never heard before. And yet, somehow, she knew every word. She knew the next note before it came. She knew the way the melody would twist and fall at the end of each verse, like water cascading over stones.

"Do you hear that?" she whispered.

The headmaster frowned. "Hear what?"

"The singing. It's coming from—" She stopped. The voice was not coming from anywhere. It was coming from inside her head. From inside her chest. From somewhere so deep and old that it felt like memory.

"Miss Bai, you look pale. Perhaps you should sit down after all."

"I need to go to the east library."

"The library is closed until we find Lin Wei."

"Then I need to find Lin Wei."

The headmaster's frown deepened. He studied her with the careful attention of a man who had spent decades navigating the impossible. "You know something."

"I know that the song won't stop until I find the singer."

"The song—" He paused, his professional mask cracking just enough to show a flicker of genuine alarm. "You hear a song?"

"Don't you?"

The silence that followed was answer enough.

---

The east library was a three-story building that leaned slightly to the left, as if exhausted by the weight of its own knowledge. The door was locked, but Bai Su found it unlocked, the chain hanging loose like a snapped necklace.

She pushed it open.

The smell hit her first. Wet stone and rotting paper, the particular mustiness of a place that had been flooded and never properly dried. The floor was damp, dark spots spreading across the wooden planks like bruises. Books lay scattered everywhere, their pages bloated and illegible.

And there, in the center of the room, sat Lin Wei.

He was cross-legged on the floor, surrounded by a circle of open texts. His eyes were open but unseeing, his lips moving silently. Water dripped from his hair, his clothes, his fingertips, pooling around him in a spreading stain.

"Lin Wei?"

He did not respond. His lips continued to move, forming words that Bai Su could not hear but somehow understood. The song. He was mouthing the song.

She stepped closer. The water around him was cold, far colder than it should have been. Her shoes squelched with each step. The books in the circle were all open to the same page—a woodcut illustration of a woman drowning, her hair spreading through the water like ink, her mouth open in a silent scream.

"The scholar who killed himself," Bai Su whispered. "He told me there was another story. A girl who drowned herself in the academy well."

Lin Wei's eyes snapped into focus.

"She didn't drown," he said, his voice hollow and distant. "She was pushed."

Bai Su's blood went cold. "Who pushed her?"

"The same person who pushed Chen Feng." Lin Wei's gaze met hers, and for a moment, she saw something ancient looking out from behind his eyes. "The same person who has been killing students since the academy was founded. The same person who will keep killing until every living student is dead."

"That's impossible. Someone would have noticed."

"Someone did." Lin Wei smiled, but it was not his smile. It was too wide, too sharp, too knowing. "The headmaster. The teachers. The ghost scholars who came before you. They all noticed. And they all chose to look away."

"Look away from what?"

"The truth about this academy." Lin Wei's voice began to shift, growing higher, sweeter, taking on the cadence of the song that still echoed in Bai Su's skull. "Wutong Academy was not built on a graveyard. It was built on a well. And in that well, there is a girl who has been waiting for a very long time."

"Waiting for what?"

"For someone to hear her song."

The water around Lin Wei began to rise. It crept up Bai Su's ankles, cold and grasping, pulling at her robes like eager hands. She tried to step back, but her feet were rooted to the floor. The water was climbing higher, faster, reaching for her knees, her waist, her chest.

"Lin Wei, snap out of it!"

"I'm not Lin Wei," the boy said, and his voice was no longer his. It was a girl's voice, young and sweet and terrible. "I'm the one he was looking for. I'm the one everyone has been looking for. I'm the one who has been singing since before you were born."

Bai Su's hands were shaking. Her breath came in short, sharp gasps. The water was at her throat now, cold and suffocating, and she could feel something under the surface, something that brushed against her legs like long, wet hair.

"What do you want?"

"I want to be heard." The girl's voice was everywhere now, filling the room, filling Bai Su's mind, filling the spaces between her thoughts. "I want to be seen. I want to be remembered. I've been alone in the dark for so long, and I am so tired of being forgotten."

"I don't even know your name."

There was a pause. The water stopped rising.

"You don't know my name?"

"No. No one told me. The ghost scholars, they only told me about the boys who died. They never mentioned you."

The water began to recede. Slowly at first, then faster, draining into the floorboards like it had never been there at all. Lin Wei slumped forward, unconscious, his breath evening out into the steady rhythm of sleep.

And in the space where the water had been, a girl stood.

She was young, no more than fifteen, with long black hair that hung in wet ropes around her face. Her skin was pale as paper, translucent as silk, and her eyes were the color of deep water. She wore a simple dress, soaked through and clinging to her thin frame, and her feet were bare and blue with cold.

She was the most beautiful thing Bai Su had ever seen.

And the most terrifying.

"You want to know my name?" The girl smiled, and her teeth were too sharp, too white, too many. "My name is Mei. And I died in this well one hundred and thirty-seven years ago."

Bai Su's heart hammered against her ribs. "Who pushed you?"

"The same person who will push you, if you're not careful." Mei stepped closer, her bare feet leaving no prints on the wet floor. "The ghost scholar who killed Chen Feng. The ghost scholar who has been killing for centuries. The ghost scholar who wears the face of the living and hides in plain sight."

"Who is it?"

Mei reached out and touched Bai Su's cheek. Her fingers were ice, and they left trails of cold that did not fade.

"Look at the blood on your hands, Bai Su. Look at the footprint that matches your shoe. Look at the song that only you can hear." Her smile widened, cracked and terrible. "Ask yourself: why you? Why now? Why can you see the ghosts when no one else can?"

"I don't know."

"Don't you?" Mei leaned in, her lips brushing Bai Su's ear. "You've been here before, Bai Su. In another life. In another body. You were the one who pushed me into the well."

Bai Su's world shattered.

"No. That's impossible. I'm seventeen years old. I've never even been to this city before—"

"You have been reincarnated a hundred times since that day. And every time, you find your way back here. Every time, you walk through these gates. Every time, you hear my song." Mei's voice was soft now, almost tender. "And every time, you forget. Until now."

The bell above them began to ring.

One. Two. Three.

Bai Su stumbled backward, her mind reeling, her heart screaming, her hands reaching for something—anything—to hold onto.

Four. Five. Six.

Mei began to fade, her form dissolving into mist, her voice echoing through the empty library.

"Find me, Bai Su. Find me in the well. Remember what you did. Remember what you are."

Seven. Eight. Nine.

"I'll be waiting."

The bell rang ten times.

And Bai Su woke up screaming.

title: Chapter 7
content:
CHAPTER 7: The Scholar Who Forgot Her Own Death

The headmaster's study smelled of old paper and burnt incense, a combination that made Bai Su's stomach turn. She stood before the massive rosewood desk, her hands still tingling from the phantom blood, while Headmaster Zhou shuffled through a pile of examination scrolls with the careful deliberation of a man who had already lived too long.

"You've been busy, Miss Bai." He didn't look up. "Visiting the bell tower. Speaking with the dead. Getting yourself covered in blood that isn't yours."

"How do you—"

"I know everything that happens in this academy." He finally raised his eyes, and Bai Su felt the weight of them like stones pressing on her chest. "Including the fact that you've been having dreams. Dreams about falling."

Bai Su's breath caught. She hadn't told anyone about the dreams. Every night for the past week, she had woken up mid-scream, her body jerking as if she had just hit the ground, the sensation of wind rushing past her ears still fresh in her mind.

"I don't—"

"Sit down, Miss Bai. Before you collapse."

She sat. Her legs gave out more than she intended, and she landed hard on the wooden chair opposite the desk. Headmaster Zhou pushed a cup of tea toward her. Steam rose in delicate spirals, carrying the scent of jasmine and something else, something metallic that she couldn't place.

"You're wondering why I called you here."

"The servant said it was urgent."

"It is." He folded his hands on the desk. "But not for the reasons you think. I need to tell you a story. A story about a student who came to Wutong Academy five years ago. A student who died before she ever took the examination."

Bai Su's fingers tightened around the teacup. "Why are you telling me this?"

"Because that student was you."

The words hung in the air, sharp and cold as winter frost. Bai Su stared at the headmaster, waiting for him to laugh, to take it back, to reveal it as some cruel joke. But his face remained stone still, his eyes holding a sadness that looked ancient.

"That's impossible." Her voice came out steady, which surprised her. "I'm alive. I breathe. I have a heartbeat. I—"

"You have all the memories of being alive. But memories are just stories we tell ourselves. They're not proof."

"I remember my mother's face. The village I grew up in. The day I left for the academy—"

"Do you remember the examination?" He leaned forward. "Do you remember sitting in the hall, brush in hand, writing your answers? Do you remember the exact questions? The other students around you?"

Bai Su opened her mouth. Closed it. The memories were there, but fuzzy, like looking through frosted glass. She remembered studying. She remembered the long walk to the city. She remembered—

Nothing. The day of the examination was blank.

"I died." She said it flatly, testing the words on her tongue. They felt foreign, like a language she had once known but forgotten.

"Five years ago. You fell from the bell tower. The exact same tower you visited today. The exact same window that Chen Feng fell from."

"The window was already broken when I got there."

"No, Miss Bai. You broke it. Five years ago. When you jumped."

Bai Su's teacup shattered. She hadn't realized she had been squeezing it, but the ceramic pieces lay scattered across the desk, the tea spreading in a dark stain that looked like blood. She stared at her hands. The same hands that had been covered in blood in the bell tower. The same hands that had broken the glass.

"I didn't jump." Her voice cracked. "I wouldn't—"

"You don't remember. That's the problem." Headmaster Zhou pulled a scroll from his drawer and unrolled it across the desk. It was old, yellowed, the ink faded to brown. A list of names. Student records. And there, at the bottom, crossed out with a single red line: Bai Su, Age 16, Deceased.

"I was a ghost. All this time."

"Not exactly." He tapped the scroll. "You were a ghost who forgot she was dead. It happens sometimes, especially with students who die before the examination. The desire to complete what you started is so strong that you keep going, keep studying, keep pretending you're alive. Your qi anchors you to this world, and your mind fills in the gaps."

"But I can touch things. I can eat. I—"

"Strong ghosts can. Especially here, where the qi is thick and the boundary between life and death is thin. You've been living on borrowed time, Miss Bai. Borrowed qi."

The words hit her like a physical blow. Borrowed qi. The same thing the killer was stealing. The same thing that was making the ghost students fade.

"Is that why I can see them? The other ghosts?"

"You see them because you are them." He stood up, walked around the desk, and knelt beside her chair. For the first time, Bai Su noticed how old he looked. How tired. "I'm sorry I didn't tell you sooner. I was hoping you would remember on your own. The memories usually return after five years."

"Five years." She laughed, but it came out hollow. "There's a timer on forgetting you're dead?"

"Something like that." He hesitated. "There's more. The ghost who's been killing the living students—the one who's been stealing qi—you know them."

"I don't—"

"You do. You've known them since before you died. They were your friend. Your study partner. The one who was with you in the bell tower the night you fell."

The room spun. Bai Su gripped the armrests of her chair, her vision swimming with colors that didn't exist. A face floated up from her memory. A girl with sharp eyes and a sharper smile, who always smelled like ink and sandalwood. Who stayed up with her until dawn, drilling examination questions. Who—

"Liang Yue." The name escaped her lips like a curse.

"Liang Yue died three days after you. They said she couldn't bear the guilt. They said she threw herself from the same window."

"No." Bai Su shook her head. "She wouldn't. She was going to pass the examination. She was the smartest person I knew. She—"

"She found your body. She was the one who identified you. And then she was the one who started seeing things. Seeing you."

Bai Su's blood ran cold—or would have, if she still had blood. "She saw me?"

"Everywhere. In the library. In the examination hall. Standing at the window where you fell. She couldn't escape you. And then, one night, she climbed the bell tower and jumped."

"She killed herself because of me."

"She killed herself because she loved you, Miss Bai. And because she couldn't save you."

The study door slammed open. A servant stumbled in, his face white as rice paper. "Headmaster! Headmaster, come quick! The west wing—there's been another one!"

They ran. Through the corridors, past the startled students, into the west wing where a crowd had gathered. The body lay in the center of the study hall, sprawled across a desk covered in examination scrolls. A living student. His eyes were open, his mouth frozen in a scream that never came out.

And written on the wall above him, in what looked like fresh blood:

*"Three down. Six to go. Come find me, Bai Su. I've been waiting."*

The handwriting was elegant. Precise. Painfully familiar.

Bai Su's hands trembled as she read the message. The ink—the blood—dripped down the wall in thin rivulets, pooling on the floor at her feet. She looked at her hands again. Clean. No blood. But she could feel it. Old blood, dried and cracked, staining her palms like rust.

Just like in the bell tower.

"Miss Bai?" The headmaster's voice came from far away. "Miss Bai, are you alright?"

She turned to face him, and the room fell silent.

Her eyes were bleeding.

Not crying blood. Bleeding. Thin streams of crimson running down her cheeks, dripping off her chin, staining her robes. She touched her face, and her fingers came away red.

"I remember," she whispered. "I remember everything."

The memory hit her like a wave of cold water.

She was standing in the bell tower. Night. The stars were out, cold and distant. Liang Yue was beside her, her face wet with tears.

"I can't do this anymore," Bai Su said. "I can't keep pretending I'm going to pass. I've been failing every practice examination. My calligraphy is worse than a child's. I'm not smart enough. I'm not good enough. I'm never going to make it."

"You're being too hard on yourself." Liang Yue reached for her hand. "You're brilliant. You just need more time."

"There is no more time. The examination is in three days."

"Then we'll study harder. I'll help you. I'll—"

"Stop." Bai Su pulled away. "Stop pretending this is going to work. It's not. I'm going to fail. I'm going to go home in disgrace. My family will never forgive me. I'll never forgive myself."

She climbed onto the windowsill. The wind was cold against her skin, pulling at her robes like eager hands.

"Bai Su, don't." Liang Yue's voice was desperate. "Please. Don't do this."

"Tell my mother I'm sorry."

She jumped.

But she didn't fall alone.

Liang Yue grabbed her hand. Tried to pull her back. Lost her balance. And together, they plummeted through the night air, their screams merging into one.

Bai Su hit the ground first. Liang Yue landed on top of her, the impact breaking her neck instantly.

They died together.

But Bai Su didn't stay dead.

She woke up in the infirmary three days later, with no memory of the fall. She walked back to her dormitory. She picked up her brush. She studied for the examination she had already missed.

And Liang Yue—Liang Yue stayed dead. But not gone.

She became the ghost in the shadows. The whisper in the wind. The hand that pushed when no one was looking.

And now she was killing.

"Miss Bai!" The headmaster's voice cut through her memories. "Your eyes—"

Bai Su touched her face. The blood had stopped. But the knowledge remained, heavy and terrible.

Liang Yue wasn't just killing to steal qi.

She was killing to bring Bai Su back to life.

The blood on the wall spelled it out in screaming red:

*"I died for you. Now it's your turn to live for me."*

Bai Su looked at her hands.

For the first time, she could see through them.

title: Chapter 8
content:
CHAPTER 8: The Song Beneath the Skin

The headmaster's office was a mausoleum of bureaucratic ambition. Scrolls stacked like coffins against the walls. A desk carved from a single slab of black stone, polished to a mirror shine that reflected nothing clearly. And behind it, Headmaster Zhou waited with the patience of a spider who had long since stopped counting the flies in its web.

Bai Su entered and immediately felt the temperature drop. Not the cold of autumn seeping through cracks, but something deeper. Something that settled into her bones like damp rot.

"Miss Bai." Headmaster Zhou did not rise. He gestured to the chair across from him—a simple wooden thing that looked older than the academy itself. "Please. Sit."

She sat. The wood groaned beneath her, a sound like a dying breath.

"I understand you've been exploring the bell tower." His voice was smooth, practiced. The voice of a man who spent decades learning exactly how much to say and precisely when to say nothing at all. "An interesting choice for a new student. Most prefer the library."

"The library was crowded."

"Was it?" He smiled, but it didn't reach his eyes. Those eyes were black stones, flat and ancient. "Tell me, what did you find in the tower?"

Bai Su considered her options. Lie, and risk being caught in a web she couldn't see. Tell the truth, and reveal how much she already knew. She chose a third path.

"I found a window that shouldn't have been broken. A rope that rang a bell by itself. And a footprint that matched my shoe."

The headmaster's smile flickered. Just for a moment. Then it returned, wider than before. "That's quite a collection for one afternoon. Did you also find the student who fell from that window?"

"Chen Feng."

"Ah." He leaned back, the chair creaking beneath him. "So you've heard the story. Let me tell you the version I know. Chen Feng was a promising scholar. Top of his class. Destined for greatness, they said." He paused, his fingers drumming on the desk. "Then one night, he walked to the bell tower and jumped. No note. No warning. No reason anyone could find."

"And you believe that?"

"I believe in evidence, Miss Bai. The evidence said suicide. The case was closed."

"By who?"

The headmaster's eyes narrowed. "By Magistrate Lin, a week after Chen Feng's death. He was thorough. Spoke to every student, every teacher, every servant who might have seen something. He found nothing."

Bai Su's mind raced. Magistrate Lin. The name meant nothing to her, but the timing did. A week after the death. Long enough for evidence to be planted. Long enough for witnesses to be paid off or silenced.

"And his investigation is still on file?"

"Of course. Everything in Wutong Academy is documented. We believe in transparency." He pushed a scroll across the desk. "This is a copy. Read it if you like. But I warn you—it won't give you peace."

Bai Su took the scroll. It was heavy, sealed with red wax stamped with the academy's emblem—a phoenix rising from flames. She didn't open it. Not yet.

"Is there anything else, Headmaster?"

"One more thing." He reached into his desk and pulled out a small wooden box. It was unadorned, the wood dark with age. "This was found among Chen Feng's belongings. The servants thought it was trash, but I had a feeling it might interest you."

He slid it across the desk. Bai Su caught it before it could fall.

"Open it."

She did.

Inside was a lock of hair. Long, black, braided with a single red thread. And beneath it, a slip of paper with characters written in a hand so small and cramped she had to squint to read them.

*The third bell tolls for the living dead.*
*The scholar who remembers what should be forgot.*
*The song beneath the skin.*

Bai Su's blood went cold. The song beneath the skin. She had heard that phrase before. Somewhere. The memory was there, just out of reach, like a word on the tip of her tongue.

"Do you know what it means?" the headmaster asked.

"No."

"Neither do I. But I have a feeling you'll find out." He stood, signaling the end of the audience. "Take it. Study it. And Miss Bai?"

She looked up.

"Be careful what you remember. Some memories are better left buried."

---

Bai Su left the headmaster's office with the box tucked under her arm and the scroll in her hand. She didn't go to her dormitory. She went to the library.

The librarian, a gaunt woman named Mrs. Chen who looked like she hadn't seen sunlight in decades, barely glanced up as Bai Su entered. "Back so soon?"

"I need to see the old records. Anything from three years ago."

"Third shelf, east wing. Don't touch the red scrolls."

Bai Su found the shelf easily enough. It was crammed with dusty tomes and brittle papers, the kind of records that no one had touched in years. She pulled out a stack and began to read.

Three years ago. The year Chen Feng died.

The records were meticulous. Class rosters, examination scores, disciplinary reports. She found Chen Feng's name easily enough—he was at the top of every list, his scores near-perfect, his conduct record spotless.

Too spotless.

She searched for any mention of conflict, of arguments, of enemies. There were none. It was as if Chen Feng had been a ghost long before he died, a perfect student who left no mark on the world except his grades.

But everyone leaves marks.

She dug deeper. Found the servant records, the kitchen logs, the maintenance reports. And there, buried in a list of broken furniture from the east wing, she found something.

*Room 47. Desk destroyed. Cause unknown. No disciplinary action taken.*

Room 47. Chen Feng's room.

She found the key in the librarian's office and made her way to the east wing. The building was older than the rest of the academy, its walls stained with damp and its floorboards warped from centuries of footsteps. Room 47 was at the end of a long, dark hallway, its door sealed with a padlock that looked new.

Bai Su unlocked it and stepped inside.

The room was exactly as it had been left. A bed, stripped of sheets. A desk, its surface scarred with knife marks. A window that looked out onto the courtyard where Chen Feng had died.

She walked to the desk and ran her fingers over the cuts. They were deep, deliberate. Some formed characters, others were just lines, as if someone had been trying to carve something out of the wood.

And then she saw it.

*You are not the first to see.*

The words were carved into the underside of the desk, hidden from anyone who didn't think to look. The strokes were uneven, desperate, like a man writing in the dark.

Bai Su knelt down and read the rest.

*They are watching. Always watching. The song comes at night, when the bells are silent. It calls to you. It knows your name. It knows the name you had before you were born.*

*Do not answer.*

She felt a cold hand on her shoulder and spun around.

No one was there.

But the air had changed. It was heavier now, thick with something that pressed against her skin like damp cloth. And she heard it.

A song.

Faint at first, barely audible over the beating of her heart. But it grew louder, sweeter, a melody that curled around her thoughts like smoke.

*The moon is round, the night is long,*
*Come with me, I'll sing you a song.*
*Of scholars dead and scholars born,*
*Of souls that wait until the morn.*

Bai Su clamped her hands over her ears. The song didn't stop. It seeped through her fingers, through her skin, through her skull, until it was inside her, a voice that was not her own but felt like it had always been there.

She stumbled out of the room and slammed the door shut. The song stopped.

But the echo remained.

---

That night, Bai Su sat in her dormitory, staring at the lock of hair and the slip of paper. The headmaster's words echoed in her mind. *Be careful what you remember.*

She didn't know what she was supposed to remember. She didn't know why the song felt familiar. She didn't know why the footprint in the bell tower had matched her shoe.

But she knew one thing.

She had been to Wutong Academy before.

The memory surfaced like a corpse rising from water. A girl, no older than twelve, standing in the courtyard. Her mother beside her, squeezing her hand too tightly. The headmaster—the same headmaster, though younger then—smiling down at her.

*"Welcome to Wutong Academy, Miss Bai. I hope you will be happy here."*

She had been a student here. Years ago. Before she left. Before she forgot.

But why had she left? And why couldn't she remember?

The lock of hair glinted in the candlelight. The red thread seemed to pulse, alive with a warmth that had nothing to do with fire.

Bai Su picked it up and held it to her nose.

It smelled of incense. And blood.

A knock at the door made her jump.

"Miss Bai?" It was the servant girl from earlier. "Miss Bai, there's someone here to see you. A visitor."

"A visitor? At this hour?"

"She says it's urgent. She says—" The servant paused, her voice dropping to a whisper. "She says she knew Chen Feng."

Bai Su's heart stopped.

"Where is she?"

"The east gate. She wouldn't come inside. She said she'd wait."

Bai Su grabbed her cloak and ran.

---

The east gate was a relic, rusted and half-swallowed by ivy. No one used it anymore. No one had used it in years.

But someone was waiting there.

A woman, young, her face hidden beneath a hood. She stood in the shadows, her hands clasped in front of her, her posture rigid with fear.

"You're Bai Su?" Her voice was a whisper, barely audible over the wind.

"I am. Who are you?"

The woman pulled back her hood.

Bai Su's blood froze.

The face was young, pretty, with delicate features and dark eyes that held a depth of sorrow that seemed ancient. But it was the scar that drew Bai Su's attention—a thin, white line that ran from the woman's temple to her jaw, as if someone had tried to carve her face off.

"My name is Lin Yue. I was Chen Feng's betrothed."

Bai Su stared at her. "Chen Feng was engaged?"

"Secretly. His family didn't approve. Mine didn't either. But we loved each other." She took a shaky breath. "And I know he didn't jump."

"How?"

"Because I was there."

The words hung in the air, heavy as stones.

"I was in the bell tower that night," Lin Yue continued. "We had arranged to meet. He had something to tell me. Something important. But when I arrived, he was already—" Her voice broke. "He was already falling."

"Who pushed him?"

"I don't know. I didn't see. But I heard something."

"What?"

Lin Yue's eyes met hers. "A song. Sweet and familiar. Like something I had heard before I was born."

Bai Su's blood ran cold.

*The song beneath the skin.*

"Do you remember the words?"

Lin Yue nodded. She opened her mouth to sing—

And stopped.

Her eyes went wide. Her face went pale. She

title: The Song Before Drowning
content:
The headmaster's study was a tomb of paper. Scrolls piled in precarious towers, ink stones crusted with centuries of dried pigment, and a single lantern that cast more shadows than light. Headmaster Lu sat behind his desk like a judge awaiting a condemned prisoner, his fingers steepled, his eyes empty of warmth.

"You've been busy, Bai Su."

It wasn't a compliment.

Bai Su's hands still tingled with the memory of blood that wasn't there. She kept them pressed to her sides, knuckles white against fabric. "I was investigating Chen Feng's death."

"I know what you were doing." Headmaster Lu slid a scroll across the desk. Unrolled, it revealed a charcoal rubbing of a footprint—delicate, precise, perfectly preserved. "This was found in the bell chamber this morning. Care to explain?"

"It's mine."

The admission came out before she could stop it. Headmaster Lu's eyebrows rose a fraction of an inch. "You admit it?"

"I was there last night." Bai Su forced her voice steady. "I followed a ghost scholar into the tower. He told me about the life qi transfer. About the ghost who's trying to break through the veil."

"And you believed him?"

"Why would he lie?"

"Why would any ghost tell the truth?" Headmaster Lu stood, his robe rustling like dry leaves. He moved to the window, hands clasped behind his back. Outside, the courtyard was empty. The students had been confined to their dormitories since the bell's midnight toll. "You've been at this academy for three days, Bai Su. Three days, and already you're sneaking into restricted areas, consorting with spirits, and leaving your footprints at crime scenes."

"I didn't kill Chen Feng."

"I didn't say you did." He turned, and his eyes caught the lantern light, flickering yellow. "But someone did. And that someone is still here, still hunting. The ghost you spoke to—did he tell you how to identify the killer?"

Bai Su hesitated. The ghost scholar hadn't told her much at all. He'd warned her, cryptically, and then vanished. Left her with blood on her hands and a footprint that matched her shoe.

"He said the killer was taking life qi to break through the veil."

"Obvious." Headmaster Lu's voice dripped with contempt. "Any ghost with a grudge could tell you that. What else?"

"He said if the killer succeeded, they'd become something worse than a ghost."

"Also obvious. A ghost that remembers how to live is a ghost that remembers how to kill. Every child in the spirit realm knows this." He stepped closer, his presence pressing against her like a physical weight. "What did he tell you that I don't already know?"

Bai Su's mind raced. The ghost scholar had said something else, hadn't he? Something about the bell, about the ringing, about—

"He said it was starting."

"Starting what?"

"I don't know. He vanished before he could explain."

Headmaster Lu's expression flickered—something between anger and fear—before settling back into stone. "You're useless to me, girl. Get out. Stay in your dormitory. Do not investigate. Do not speak to any more ghosts. The next time you break the rules, I will expel you. And expulsion from Wutong Academy means you never take the imperial exams. Do you understand?"

"Yes, Headmaster."

She bowed and left, her heart hammering against her ribs. But she didn't go to her dormitory.

---

The library was empty when she arrived. Not empty of books—it was always full of those—but empty of students. The living were confined, and the dead... the dead were watching her from the corners.

Bai Su could feel their eyes. Cold, curious, hungry. The drowned scholar was there, his skin blue-white, water dripping from his robes onto the floorboards. The suicide candidate hung from the rafters, her neck twisted at an impossible angle, her eyes fixed on Bai Su like a hawk watching prey. The murdered prodigy sat at a desk, his fingers tracing patterns in the dust, his expression unreadable.

"You shouldn't be here."

The voice came from behind her. Bai Su turned to find a girl she didn't recognize—pale, pretty, with hair that floated around her face as if underwater. Her robes were old-fashioned, from a dynasty long past, and her feet didn't quite touch the ground.

"I'm looking for information," Bai Su said. "About the ghost who's been taking life qi."

The girl smiled, and it was not a kind smile. "You mean the one who killed Chen Feng?"

"Yes."

"They're not the only one." The girl drifted closer, her movements fluid, unnatural. "There have been others. Before Chen Feng. Before you came to this academy."

Bai Su's blood ran cold. "How many?"

"Three. All living students. All found dead in their beds, their bodies drained of qi, their faces frozen in terror." The girl's smile widened. "The headmaster didn't tell you, did he? He's been covering it up. Fearing a panic. But we know. We ghosts always know."

"Why are you telling me this?"

"Because I want you to stop them." The girl's expression shifted, the smile fading into something desperate. "The killer isn't just after the living. They're after us too. Ghosts who refuse to join them. Ghosts who remember what it means to be human." She reached out, her fingers passing through Bai Su's arm like smoke. "I don't want to die again. I don't want to be erased."

"How do I find them?"

The girl's eyes darted to the left, toward a shelf of ancient scrolls. "The examination records. Look for the ones who died during the test. The ones whose names were struck from the roll." She leaned in, her breath cold against Bai Su's ear. "The killer is among them. A ghost who never accepted their death. A ghost who has been waiting for a chance to come back."

"And if I find them?"

"Kill them. Again." The girl pulled back, her expression hardening. "Before they kill you."

She dissolved into mist, leaving Bai Su alone with the watching dead.

---

The examination records were kept in a locked cabinet behind the librarian's desk. Bai Su picked the lock with a hairpin—a skill she'd learned from a street urchin in her village, never thinking she'd use it in a place like this—and pulled out the oldest scrolls.

They were brittle, yellowed, the ink fading to brown. She unrolled them carefully, scanning the names. Most were unfamiliar. Students who had died in the centuries since Wutong Academy was founded, their names preserved in ink and memory.

But one name caught her eye.

Lin Wei.

The same name as the ghost scholar who had led her to the bell chamber. The same name that appeared in the records three times—once as a living student, once as a deceased candidate, and once, strangely, as a name that had been crossed out and rewritten.

She traced the final entry with her finger. The ink was fresher. Recent. As if someone had added it within the last few days.

*Lin Wei. Deceased. Year of the Serpent. Cause of death: murder.*

Murder.

The ghost scholar had been murdered.

Bai Su's mind raced. If Lin Wei was a murder victim, then he had as much reason to seek revenge as any other ghost. But he had warned her about the killer. He had told her to find them before they broke through the veil.

Unless he was the killer.

Unless everything he had told her was a lie.

She rolled up the scroll and tucked it into her robes. The librarian would notice it missing eventually, but by then, she'd either have answers or be dead.

Neither option was appealing.

---

The west wing was silent. The ghost students had retreated to their haunts, and the living were locked in their dormitories. Bai Su walked alone through the empty corridors, her footsteps echoing against the stone walls.

She found Lin Wei in the same classroom where she had first seen him. He was sitting at a desk, staring at a blank examination scroll, his fingers tracing the empty lines.

"You lied to me."

He looked up, and his eyes were tired. "I didn't lie. I just didn't tell you everything."

"You're the killer."

"No." He stood, his movements slow, deliberate. "I'm the one who stopped them. The first time."

"Then why is your name in the records? Why did you die?"

Lin Wei's expression flickered with pain. "Because I tried to save her. The girl who became the killer. She was my fiancée. We were supposed to take the exams together, marry, start a life. But she died during the test. A fever. The proctors didn't notice until it was too late."

"Her name?"

"Xiao Yue." He whispered it, as if the name itself was sacred. "She died in this classroom, hunched over her scroll, her hand still gripping her brush. They buried her in an unmarked grave outside the academy walls. No ceremony. No mourning. Just another dead student, forgotten."

"And she became a ghost."

"A vengeful one." Lin Wei's voice hardened. "She's been gathering life qi for decades. Taking it from the living, draining them dry. She almost broke through the veil ten years ago, but I stopped her. I gave my life to seal her away."

"How?"

"I bound her spirit to the bell chamber. The bell's toll keeps her trapped, prevents her from crossing over." He looked at his hands, translucent, fading. "But the seal is weakening. Every time the bell rings, it cracks a little more. And last night, when I showed you the chamber, I broke it completely."

Bai Su's blood ran cold. "You broke the seal?"

"I had to. I needed you to see the truth. To understand what we're dealing with." He stepped closer, his eyes pleading. "Xiao Yue is free now. She's been gathering qi faster than ever. Chen Feng was just the beginning. She'll kill every living student in this academy if she has to."

"How do I stop her?"

"You can't." The voice came from behind her, sweet and familiar, like a lullaby she had heard before she was born. "You're already too late."

Bai Su turned.

A girl stood in the doorway. Beautiful, ethereal, her hair flowing like water, her robes pristine white. Her eyes were black pools, depthless and cold, and her smile was the smile of a predator.

Xiao Yue.

"I've been waiting for you, Bai Su." She stepped forward, and the air around her grew heavy, suffocating. "I've been waiting for all of you."

Lin Wei moved in front of Bai Su, his body a shield of fading light. "Run."

"Where would she go?" Xiao Yue laughed, and the sound was like breaking glass. "The academy is mine now. The bell chamber is empty. The seal is broken. And I..." She raised her hand, and the lanterns flickered, died. "I am finally whole."

The darkness swallowed them whole.

Bai Su ran.

She didn't know where she was going. The corridors twisted and turned, the walls shifting, the doors opening onto rooms that shouldn't exist. Behind her, she heard Xiao Yue's laughter, soft and terrible, echoing through the stone.

And then she heard the song.

An old folk song, the one the shadow had sung in the bell chamber. Sweet and familiar, like something she had known before she was born.

*"The moon is high, the river wide,*
*The girl who drowns has nothing to hide.*
*She waits beneath the silver light,*

title: Chapter 10
content:
CHAPTER TEN: The Song of Drowning Girls

The headmaster's study smelled of old paper and something floral—osmanthus, perhaps, or jasmine. Bai Su sat in the carved rosewood chair, her hands clasped so tightly in her lap that her knuckles had gone white. Across from her, Headmaster Zhou poured tea with the practiced grace of a man who had done it ten thousand times before.

"I trust you found the bell tower enlightening," he said, not looking up from the stream of amber liquid.

Bai Su said nothing. Her palms still tingled with the memory of blood that wasn't there.

"Ah." He set the teapot down with a soft click. "I see you've already encountered the echo. Most students don't trigger it until their third month here."

"The echo?"

"A memory stain. When something violent happens in a place, the building remembers. Students who are sensitive enough—or unlucky enough—can sometimes... experience it." He finally looked at her, his eyes dark and unreadable. "Chen Feng's death left a deep mark on that tower."

"I saw him fall," Bai Su whispered. "I felt it."

Headmaster Zhou nodded slowly, as if she had confirmed something he already suspected. "The echo shows you what happened. But it can also show you what you fear happened. Or what you think you might have done."

"I don't understand."

He leaned forward, his voice dropping. "The echo is not objective, Miss Bai. It adapts. It finds your deepest anxieties and feeds them back to you. The blood on your hands, the footprint that matched your shoe—none of it was real. But your fear of it was."

Bai Su's stomach twisted. "How do you know what I saw?"
