"Because every sensitive student who enters that tower sees something different. But they all see something that makes them doubt themselves." He sat back, his face softening almost imperceptibly. "It's the academy's first test. A reminder that what we see is not always truth, and what is true is not always visible."

She wanted to believe him. She wanted to believe that the blood had been a trick of the light, a phantom of her own making. But the ghost scholar's words echoed in her mind: *You need to find the killer. Before they take enough qi to break through the veil entirely.*

"What about the bell?" she asked. "It rang ten times. But no one was pulling the rope."

Headmaster Zhou's expression flickered—the first crack in his composure she had seen since entering the room. "The bell rings of its own accord sometimes. It is very old. The wood expands and contracts with the weather."

"Nine times is the warning for a ghost student," Bai Su pressed. "Everyone knows that. But ten times? I've never heard of ten."

He was silent for a long moment. Then he reached into his sleeve and pulled out a scroll, yellowed with age. He unrolled it on the desk between them.

It was a list of names. Dozens of them, written in fading ink, each with a date beside it. The dates spanned over a hundred years.

"The tenth bell," Headmaster Zhou said slowly, "has not rung since I was a student here. It rings when a living student dies within the academy walls."

Bai Su's blood went cold. "Chen Feng—"

"His was the ninth. Students who die before they can take the imperial exams linger as ghosts. That is the ninth bell's purpose—to mark their transition." He traced his finger down the list, stopping at the very bottom. "But the tenth bell rings for a different kind of death. A death that was not meant to be."

She read the names. Each one was followed by a brief notation: *Fell from the east wall. Drowned in the garden pond. Poisoned in the dining hall. Found in the library with a blade through the heart.* The most recent entry was from forty years ago.

"These are murders," she breathed.

"Yes." Headmaster Zhou rolled the scroll back up with deliberate care. "And every single one remains unsolved."

"Why are you telling me this?"

"Because you saw the tenth bell ring. Because you triggered the echo on your first day. Because there is a ghost student stealing life qi from the living, and the last time that happened, the tenth bell tolled for three nights straight." He looked at her with an intensity that made her want to shrink into her chair. "I am telling you this because I believe you are the only one who can stop it from happening again."

"I'm just a student," Bai Su protested. "I've been here for two days. I don't know anything."

"You see ghosts. You survived the echo. And you have already attracted the attention of something that has been dormant for forty years." He paused. "The ghost scholar. The one who warned you. Did he tell you his name?"

Bai Su shook her head.

"He was the last living student to die in this academy. His name was Lin Wei, and he was murdered on the night of his graduation, three days before he was to sit for the imperial exams." Headmaster Zhou's voice grew heavy. "He has haunted these halls ever since, searching for his killer. And he has never spoken to anyone until you."

"Why me?"

"Perhaps because you can see him clearly. Perhaps because you remind him of someone. Perhaps because he knows something that the rest of us do not." He stood, signaling that their conversation was over. "Whatever the reason, Miss Bai, I urge you to trust him. But I also urge you to be careful. Ghosts remember their deaths differently than they lived their lives. And Lin Wei's death was particularly... cruel."

Bai Su rose on unsteady legs. "What happened to him?"

"He was drowned. In a barrel of water no deeper than your waist. The examiners found him the next morning, still wearing his scholar's robes, his hands bound behind his back with silk cord." Headmaster Zhou's eyes were distant, lost in a memory he had never witnessed. "They say he was alive when they put him in. They say he fought so hard that he broke his own fingers trying to free himself."

The image was so vivid that Bai Su could almost see it—the dark water, the desperate struggle, the slow surrender. She pressed a hand to her mouth.

"I will assign you a room in the west wing," Headmaster Zhou continued, his tone businesslike once more. "Near the library. You will have unrestricted access to the archives, and I will instruct the gatekeepers to allow you to come and go as you please. If you need anything, you may send word through the servant girl, Lian."

"You're making me an investigator?"

"I am making you a student with certain privileges." He handed her a small bronze key, worn smooth by years of use. "This opens the restricted section of the library. I suggest you start there."

Bai Su took the key, its weight heavier than it should have been. "And if I find something?"

"Then you bring it to me. Directly. No one else." He held her gaze. "Trust no one, Miss Bai. Not the living, and not the dead. The killer is still here, and they have had forty years to perfect their secrets."

She nodded and turned to leave. Her hand was on the door handle when he spoke again.

"One more thing. The song you heard—the one following you down the stairs?"

Bai Su froze.

"It is an old folk song from the southern provinces. A lament for a daughter who drowned herself rather than marry a man she did not love." His voice dropped to barely a whisper. "It was Lin Wei's favorite song. He used to sing it in the courtyard, every evening, for the entire year before he died."

"Why would a boy sing a song about a drowned girl?"

"No one knows. He never explained." Headmaster Zhou's face was unreadable. "But perhaps you can ask him yourself."

---

The west wing was quieter than the main dormitory, its corridors lined with faded tapestries and portraits of scholars long dead. Bai Su's room was at the very end, a small chamber with a single window overlooking the graveyard. The bed was narrow but clean, and a writing desk sat beneath the window, its surface scarred with ink stains from a hundred years of use.

She set down her bag and sat on the edge of the bed, the bronze key cold in her palm. Her mind was a storm of questions, each one more tangled than the last.

A knock at the door made her jump.

"Miss Bai?" It was Lian, the servant girl from earlier. "I've brought you some tea and a copy of the academy rules."

Bai Su opened the door. Lian stood there with a tray, her face pleasant but her eyes sharp. She was young—maybe sixteen—with the kind of beauty that was easy to overlook until you looked twice.

"Thank you," Bai Su said, taking the tray.

"Headmaster Zhou asked me to remind you that the library closes at midnight. The restricted section has its own rules, but he said you would understand them when you saw them." Lian paused. "There's something else."

"Yes?"

"I was here forty years ago. When the last murder happened." Lian's voice dropped, her pleasant demeanor fading. "I was a servant girl then, too. I saw things. Things I've never told anyone."

Bai Su's heart rate quickened. "What did you see?"

"The night Lin Wei died, I was walking past the bell tower. It was late, past midnight, and I shouldn't have been out. But I heard singing." She shivered, her face pale. "It was Lin Wei. He was standing at the top of the tower, singing that song about the drowning girl. I thought nothing of it—he sang it every night. But this time, when he finished, he didn't come down."

"What do you mean?"

"I waited. I waited for half an hour, but he never came out. So I went to the headmaster and told him. By the time we returned, Lin Wei was gone." She met Bai Su's eyes. "They found his body the next morning, in the exam hall. In the barrel."

"You think someone killed him while he was in the tower."

"I know someone did. Because I saw a shadow leave the tower that night, after the singing stopped." Her voice was barely a whisper now. "A shadow with a woman's shape."

Bai Su's blood ran cold. "A woman?"

"Yes." Lian looked down at her hands. "I've never told anyone because I was afraid. The headmaster at the time was very clear—no one was to speak of what happened. But you're different. You can see things. You can find the truth."

"Lian, who was she?"

The servant girl shook her head. "I don't know. I never saw her face. But I heard her voice." She looked up, and her eyes were filled with a terror so old it had calcified into certainty. "She was singing, Miss Bai. The same song Lin Wei always sang. She was singing it as she walked away."

The wind outside picked up, rattling the window in its frame. Somewhere in the distance, a bell began to toll.

One.

Two.

Three.

Bai Su counted, her breath catching in her throat.

Four.

Five.

Six.

"It's starting again," Lian whispered.

Seven.

Eight.

Nine.

The ninth bell faded into silence. Bai Su waited, her heart pounding, for the tenth.

The silence stretched on.

And then, from somewhere deep in the academy, a woman's voice rose in song. Sweet. Familiar. A lament for a daughter who drowned herself rather than marry a man she did not love.

The tenth bell never rang.

But the singing grew louder, closer, until it

title: Chapter 11
content:
CHAPTER 11: The Song Beneath the Skin

Headmaster Zhou's office had never looked more ordinary. Scrolls lined the walls in neat rows. A pot of tea steamed on the low table. The afternoon sun filtered through paper screens, casting everything in warm amber light.

Bai Su sat on the visitor's cushion, her hands folded in her lap. She had wiped them clean three times since leaving the bell tower, but she still felt the dried blood cracking against her palms. A phantom sensation. A memory her skin refused to forget.

"You're pale," Headmaster Zhou observed, pouring her a cup of tea. "The ghost scholar's bell chamber isn't exactly a welcoming place for the living."

"I needed answers."

"Did you find any?"

Bai Su hesitated. The footprint. The blood on her hands. The song that followed her down the stairs. None of it made sense. None of it could be true.

"I found more questions," she admitted.

The headmaster nodded as if he had expected this answer. He pushed the tea toward her, and she took it gratefully, letting the warmth seep into her cold fingers.

"Chen Feng's body has been moved to the mortuary," he said. "The coroner will examine him tomorrow. But I suspect his death will be ruled a suicide."

"He didn't jump."

"I know." Headmaster Zhou's voice was quiet, heavy. "But proving it is another matter. The ghost students won't testify. The living students didn't see anything. And the only witness who might have spoken is now dead."

"What about the bell ringer? The one who rang the bell this morning?"

The headmaster's expression didn't change, but something flickered in his eyes. A warning. A hesitation.

"There is no bell ringer," he said slowly. "The bell chamber has been locked for three years. Since the last suicide."

Bai Su's blood turned to ice.

"Then who—"

"Rang the bell this morning?" Headmaster Zhou finished her question. "I was hoping you could tell me."

She thought of the empty chamber. The still rope. The bell that rang nine times without a hand to pull it.

"I don't know," she whispered.

"You should rest." The headmaster stood, signaling the audience was over. "Tomorrow, we will begin the investigation in earnest. But tonight, you need to eat, sleep, and forget what you saw in that tower."

"Forgive me, Headmaster, but I don't think I can forget."

"Then learn to live with it." His voice was not unkind, but it carried an edge of finality. "That's what we all do here."

---

Bai Su did not go to the dining hall. She did not go to her dormitory. She walked instead to the west wing, where the suicide candidate's desk still sat in the corner of the empty study room.

She had questions. And there was only one person—one ghost—who might be willing to answer them.

The study room was dark when she arrived. The candles had burned down to stubs, and the only light came from the moon filtering through dusty windows. She lit a fresh candle with trembling hands and set it on the desk.

"I know you're here," she said to the empty room. "I know you've been watching me since I arrived. I know you followed me to the bell tower."

Silence.

"I need to understand what happened to Chen Feng. I need to understand why I saw his death before it happened. I need to understand why there was blood on my hands when I woke up in that tower."

Still nothing. But the candle flickered, and Bai Su felt the temperature drop.

"Please," she said, and her voice cracked on the word. "I'm scared. I don't know what's happening to me. And I think—I think I'm the only one who can stop whatever is coming."

A shadow moved in the corner of her eye. She turned, and there he was.

The suicide candidate. Young. Seventeen, maybe eighteen. His neck was bent at an unnatural angle, and a rope mark circled his throat like a collar. He wore the same scholar's robes he had died in, stained with ink and age.

"You shouldn't be here," he said. His voice was soft, like wind through dry leaves.

"I know."

"Everyone who comes to this room leaves with questions they can't answer. Everyone who lights a candle for me ends up burning themselves."

"I know that too."

He studied her for a long moment. His eyes were dark, depthless, like wells that had gone dry.

"What do you want to know?"

"Everything."

He laughed, a hollow sound that didn't reach his eyes. "That's a dangerous word. Everything includes things you don't want to hear."

"Start with why Chen Feng died."

The ghost scholar was silent for a moment. Then he sat down on the desk beside the candle, his feet dangling above the floor. He didn't touch the ground. He never did.

"Chen Feng came to me three weeks ago," he said. "He had questions about the bell tower. About the student who died there three years ago."

"Li Wei."

"You know the name."

"I know he was accused of stealing examination answers. I know he jumped from the bell tower before he could be expelled."

"That's what the official record says." The ghost scholar's voice dropped. "But Li Wei didn't steal those answers. He was given them. By someone who wanted him dead."

Bai Su's heart hammered against her ribs. "Who?"

"I don't know. I never knew. But Chen Feng was trying to find out. He said he had found something in Li Wei's old belongings. A journal. A record of everything Li Wei saw in his final days."

"Where is this journal now?"

The ghost scholar's expression flickered. Grief, maybe. Or fear.

"Chen Feng had it with him when he died. The headmaster's office confiscated all his belongings. If the journal exists, it's in Headmaster Zhou's private collection now."

Bai Su stood up, her mind racing. "I need to see it."

"You need to be careful." The ghost scholar's hand shot out, grabbing her wrist. His grip was cold, impossibly strong. "You're not the first student to come asking these questions. You're not the first to see the blood on their hands."

"What do you mean?"

"There have been others. Students who saw things they shouldn't. Students who heard the song."

"The song?"

"The one that followed you down the stairs." His eyes met hers, and for a moment, she saw something ancient in them. Something that had been waiting a very long time. "It's called the 'Drowning Maiden.' An old folk song. About a girl who was thrown into a well by her lover and swore she would return to drag him down with her."

"I've never heard it before."

"Are you sure?" His voice was gentle now. Almost pitying. "Think carefully, Bai Su. Haven't you heard it in your dreams? Haven't you hummed it without knowing why?"

She wanted to say no. She wanted to deny it with all her being.

But as he spoke, she realized she could remember the melody. The way it rose and fell. The way it curled around her thoughts like smoke.

She had heard it before. In her sleep. In the space between waking and dreaming.

"I don't understand," she whispered.

"You will." He released her wrist, and she saw faint finger-shaped bruises forming on her skin. "But you need to hurry. The killer is getting stronger. Every death feeds them. Every life they take brings them closer to breaking through."

"Through the veil?"

"Yes." The ghost scholar looked toward the window. Outside, the moon was rising, full and silver and wrong. "And when they do, this academy will become a graveyard. Not for the dead. For the living."

---

Bai Su left the west wing with the melody of the Drowning Maiden echoing in her skull. She tried to hum something else, anything else, but the song kept returning. A loop. A curse.

She was halfway across the courtyard when she saw the figure standing beneath the ancient ginkgo tree.

A girl. Young. Dressed in white robes that seemed to glow in the moonlight. Her hair was long and wet, plastered to her face. Water dripped from her fingertips, pooling at her bare feet.

Bai Su stopped breathing.

The girl turned.

Her face was pale. Her eyes were black, entirely black, without iris or pupil. She smiled, and her mouth was too wide, too full of teeth.

"You heard my song," she said. Her voice was sweet, like honey over poison. "I've been waiting for someone to hear it."

Bai Su's legs wouldn't move. Her voice wouldn't work.

"Don't be afraid." The girl stepped closer. Water followed her, spreading across the stone path like living darkness. "I've been looking for you, Bai Su. For a very long time."

"How do you know my name?"

"I know everything about you." The girl stopped an arm's length away. She was taller than Bai Su, and when she leaned down, her wet hair brushed against Bai Su's cheek. "I know the song your mother sang to you when you were a child. I know the dream you had the night before you came here. I know the name of the boy you left behind."

Bai Su's blood ran cold. "Leave him out of this."

"Too late." The girl's smile widened. "He's already dreaming of me. They all are. The living boys with their warm hearts and their beating blood." She reached out, and her fingers were cold, so cold, as they touched Bai Su's face. "But I don't want them. I want you."

"Why?"

"Because you're the only one who can see me." The girl's black eyes bore into hers. "You're the only one who can remember me. You're the only one who can bring me back."

"Back from where?"

"From the well." The girl's fingers traced down Bai Su's cheek, her jaw, her throat. "From the place where I drowned. From the darkness where I've been waiting for a hundred years."

Bai Su's heart was pounding so hard she thought it might burst.

"I don't understand."

"You will." The girl leaned in, her lips brushing against Bai Su's ear. "Tomorrow night, when the moon is highest, come to the old well behind the library. Bring the journal. Bring the truth. And I will show you everything."

"I don't have the journal."

"Then find it." The girl pulled back, and her smile was sharp, hungry, terrible. "Before someone else does. Before they use it to bury the truth forever."

She stepped back, and the water receded with her, flowing back into the shadows like a living thing.

"See you soon, Bai Su."

And then she was gone.

Bai Su stood alone in the courtyard, her breath fogging in the cold night air. The ginkgo tree rustled above her, shedding golden leaves like tears.

She looked down at her hands.

They were wet.

Not with blood this time.

With water.

Cold, dark water that smelled of mud and stone and something older than the academy itself.

She wiped them on her robes, but the wetness remained. A stain that wouldn't fade. A mark that wouldn't leave.

And in the distance, she heard the bell ring again.

Once.

Twice.

Three times.

She counted, her heart keeping pace with each toll.

Four. Five. Six.

On the seventh ring, she started running.

Not toward the bell tower.

Toward the headmaster's office.

She needed that journal.

Tonight.

title: Chapter 12
content:
CHAPTER 12: The Shadow in the Scroll

The headmaster's office was smaller than Bai Su expected.

She had imagined something grand—towering shelves of rare books, calligraphy scrolls worth more than a scholar's lifetime salary, perhaps a desk carved from the wood of a thousand-year-old tree. Instead, she found a cramped room in the eastern administration building, its walls lined with examination scrolls stacked so high they threatened to topple. A single oil lamp flickered on a plain wooden desk, casting long shadows that danced like living things.

Headmaster Lu sat behind the desk, his face half-hidden in darkness. He was an old man, older than Bai Su had realized—his skin papery thin, his eyes sunken but sharp, like a hawk watching from a great height. He did not rise when she entered.

"Miss Bai." His voice cracked like dry leaves. "Please, sit."

She sat. The chair was uncomfortable, deliberately so. She wondered if he had chosen it that way to keep his visitors from lingering.

"I understand you've been investigating the incident in the bell chamber," he said.

Not a question. An accusation wrapped in silk.

"I was curious about the broken window," Bai Su said carefully. "It seemed strange that no one had mentioned it."

"Strange." Headmaster Lu smiled, but there was no warmth in it. "Strange is a kind word for what happens in this academy. I prefer the word 'dangerous.'" He leaned forward, the lamp light catching his eyes. "You saw something in that tower, didn't you? Something you couldn't explain."

Bai Su's hands were still clean, but she could feel the phantom blood on her palms. She kept them flat on her knees.

"I saw a ghost," she said.

The headmaster did not flinch. "Of course you did. This academy is full of them. Half our students are dead, Miss Bai. Did you think that was a metaphor?"

"I saw the ghost of a boy who jumped from that window. He said his name was Chen Feng."

The headmaster's expression flickered—just for a moment, just enough for Bai Su to catch it. Recognition. And something else. Fear.

"Chen Feng," he repeated, the name falling from his lips like a stone. "You saw him?"

"He spoke to me. He said he was pushed."

The silence that followed was thick enough to choke on. The headmaster stared at her, his ancient eyes unreadable. Then he reached into his desk drawer and pulled out a scroll, yellowed with age, tied with a red ribbon that had faded to brown.

"I was going to burn this," he said. "I should have burned it years ago. But I couldn't." He untied the ribbon with trembling fingers. "I couldn't destroy the last proof that I failed him."

He unrolled the scroll and turned it toward Bai Su.

It was an examination paper. The ink had bled in places, smeared by what looked like water stains. But the handwriting was still legible—precise, elegant, the work of a scholar who had practiced for years.

At the top, in bold characters: Chen Feng.

And below, a single sentence repeated over and over, filling the entire scroll, line after line, until the characters blurred together:

*"I did not jump. I did not jump. I did not jump. I did not jump—"*

Bai Su's breath caught in her throat. The scroll was covered in the desperate plea of a dead boy, written in his own blood.

"When did you find this?" she asked.

"Three days after he died. In his dormitory, hidden beneath his bed. The servants were going to throw it away, but I recognized his handwriting." The headmaster's voice cracked. "I knew he hadn't jumped. I knew it the moment I saw his body. But I did nothing."

"Why?"

"Because the alternative was worse." He looked up at her, and for the first time, Bai Su saw the weight of years pressing down on him. "Chen Feng was not the first student to die in this academy. He was not the second. He was not even the tenth. Every year, a student dies—falls from a window, drowns in the pond, chokes on their own food. Accidents, we called them. But they were not accidents."

"What were they?"

"Murders." The word hung in the air like smoke. "Murders committed by something that has been feeding on this academy for longer than I have been alive. A ghost that does not just haunt this place—it owns it."

Bai Su's mind raced. The ghost scholar in the bell chamber had warned her. *They'll become something worse.* But he hadn't told her the full truth.

"Who is it?" she demanded. "Which ghost is killing the students?"

The headmaster shook his head. "I don't know. I've spent forty years trying to find out, and I have failed. Every time I got close, the trail ended in death." He reached across the desk and grabbed her wrist, his grip surprisingly strong. "You must leave this academy, Miss Bai. Tonight. Before the killer finds you."

"I can't leave. The midterms are in three weeks—"

"To hell with the midterms!" He slammed his fist on the desk, making the lamp jump. "Do you think I care about examination scores when children are dying? I have watched seventeen students die in my time as headmaster. Seventeen. And I have done nothing to stop it because I was too afraid of what I would find."

Bai Su pulled her wrist free. "Then why are you telling me this now?"

"Because you saw Chen Feng." The headmaster's voice dropped to a whisper. "No one has seen him in ten years. No one has heard his voice. But you did. That means something. That means the veil is thinning, and the killer is getting stronger." He looked at her with desperate eyes. "You are the only person in this academy who might be able to stop what is coming."

"And what is coming?"

The headmaster opened his mouth to answer—

The lamp went out.

Darkness flooded the room, absolute and complete. Bai Su heard the headmaster gasp, heard the scrape of his chair as he stood. Then she heard something else. A wet, tearing sound. A gurgle.

"Headmaster Lu?"

No answer.

She fumbled for the lamp, her fingers finding the glass, the wick. She lit it with shaking hands.

The headmaster was still standing. But his throat was open, a dark slit from ear to ear, and blood was pouring down his robes in rhythmic pulses. His eyes were wide, his mouth moving soundlessly, forming words that would never be spoken.

Behind him, a figure stood in the shadows.

Bai Su saw only glimpses—a flash of pale skin, a strip of dark cloth, a hand that held a blade still dripping red. And then the figure stepped back, melting into the darkness of the corner, and was gone.

"Headmaster!"

She caught him as he fell, his body heavy and warm against hers. Blood soaked through her robes, hot and wet, and she pressed her hands against his throat, trying to stop the flow, knowing it was useless.

His lips moved again, and this time, sound came out—a whisper so faint she had to lean in to hear it.

"The scroll," he breathed. "The names. All the names..."

"Where? Where are the names?"

But his eyes had already gone glassy, staring at something she could not see. His chest rose once, twice, and then stopped.

Bai Su laid him gently on the floor. Her hands were covered in blood—real blood this time, warm and terrifying. She looked at the scroll still lying on the desk, Chen Feng's desperate message smeared across its surface.

She grabbed it and ran.

The halls of the academy were dark, the lanterns unlit, the windows showing only the black of night. Bai Su's footsteps echoed as she sprinted, her breath ragged, her mind a storm of images: the headmaster's throat opening, the figure in the shadows, the whisper of an old folk song that followed her down the stairs.

She burst through the main doors into the courtyard.

The bell tower loomed above her, its windows dark, its rope hanging still. But the bell was moving—swaying gently, as if someone had just rung it.

She turned to run toward the dormitory—

And stopped.

The courtyard was full of students.

They stood in perfect silence, faces pale, eyes fixed on her. They were not moving. They were not breathing. They were simply *watching*, rows upon rows of them, filling the space between the buildings like statues in a garden of the dead.

Among them, Bai Su recognized faces. The servant girl from the bell chamber. The librarian who had warned her about the west wing. The ghost scholar who had told her the truth.

But their eyes were different now. Empty. Hungry.

One of them stepped forward.

It was the girl from the library, the one who had drowned. Her skin was blue-white, her hair dripping water that pooled at her feet. She opened her mouth, and water poured out, but she spoke anyway, her voice echoing as if from a great distance.

"The master has seen you," she said. "The master knows your name."

The other students began to move, shuffling toward Bai Su in a slow, inexorable tide.

"The master wants to meet you," the drowned girl said. "He has been waiting for a very long time."

Bai Su backed away, her hand clutching Chen Feng's scroll, her other hand reaching for the gate behind her.

"What master?" she demanded.

The drowned girl smiled, and her smile was full of teeth that should not have been there, rows upon rows of them, like a creature from the deep.

"The one who wrote the scroll," she said.

Then the students surged forward, and Bai Su turned and ran.

She did not look back.

She did not stop running until she reached the boundary wall, her lungs burning, her legs screaming. She scrambled over the bricks and fell into the darkness beyond, landing hard on the grass outside the academy grounds.

She lay there for a long moment, staring up at the stars, her breath coming in ragged gasps.

Then she unrolled the scroll again.

In the moonlight, she saw what she had not noticed before. At the very bottom of the scroll, beneath Chen Feng's blood-soaked plea, there was a seal.

A red stamp, faded with age.

A name.

*Wei Zhen.*

The name of the academy's founder.

The man who had built the school three hundred years ago.

The man who had died in the bell tower, under circumstances that had never been explained.

Bai Su looked up at the academy walls, at the bell tower rising above them, at the shadows moving in the windows.

And she understood.

The killer was not a student.

The killer was not even a ghost.

The killer was the academy itself.

And it had been waiting for her all along.

title: Chapter 13
content:
CHAPTER 13: The Song She Shouldn't Know

The headmaster's office smelled of sandalwood and secrets.

Bai Su stood before the mahogany desk, her hands clasped behind her back so the headmaster wouldn't see them tremble. The old man—Scholar Wu, they called him, though he was neither scholar nor Wu anymore—sat with his fingers steepled, studying her with eyes that had seen too many funerals.

"You were in the bell tower," he said. Not a question.

"Yes, Headmaster."

"And you touched nothing?"

A lie sat on Bai Su's tongue, heavy and bitter. She swallowed it. "I touched the window frame. The glass." She paused. "The bell rope."

Scholar Wu's expression didn't change, but something flickered in his eyes. A shadow. A recognition. "The rope was cut. Did you know that?"

"I saw it."

"It was cut from the inside." He leaned forward, his voice dropping to a whisper that carried through the silent room like wind through bamboo. "Whoever killed Chen Feng did not enter the tower. They did not leave through the door. They were already there, waiting for him to arrive."

Bai Su's blood turned to ice water. "That's impossible. The bell chamber was locked from the outside. The servants confirmed it."

"The servants confirmed what they saw." Scholar Wu reached into his sleeve and pulled out a scroll, yellowed with age, its edges frayed like old wounds. "But there are things in this academy that the living cannot see. Doors that open only for the dead. Passages that exist between one breath and the next."

He unrolled the scroll across the desk. It was a map of Wutong Academy, but not the one Bai Su had seen in the orientation hall. This map was different. The buildings were drawn in ink so dark it seemed to drink the light, and between them, thin red lines connected points that should not connect—the library to the bell tower, the west wing to the headmaster's residence, the kitchen to the grave garden.

"What is this?" Bai Su asked, though she already knew.

"The ghost paths." Scholar Wu traced one red line with a gnarled finger. "The academy was built on a convergence of ley lines. The living see only the surface—classrooms, dormitories, courtyards. But beneath, woven through the walls like veins through a body, there is a second academy. A shadow academy. And the dead walk its halls freely."

Bai Su thought of the ghost scholar in the bell chamber. The way he had appeared and disappeared without a sound. The way the dust had settled around him as if he had never been there at all.

"Chen Feng was killed by a ghost," she said.

"No." Scholar Wu shook his head slowly. "Chen Feng was killed by a student. A living student who used the ghost paths to move unseen, to enter locked rooms, to stand behind their victim before the victim even knew they were there."

A living student. Bai Su's mind raced through the faces she had seen in the dining hall, in the classrooms, in the corridors. Any one of them could have done it. All of them had access to the ghost paths, if they knew where to look.

"The life qi," she said. "The killer is stealing life qi to become more human. But Chen Feng was alive. Why kill a living student when there are ghosts everywhere?"

"Because the living have more qi." Scholar Wu's voice hardened. "One living death equals the life force of ten ghosts. And the killer needs a great deal of qi to complete their transformation."

"How do you know all this?"

The headmaster was silent for a long moment. When he spoke, his voice was barely audible. "Because I have seen it before. Thirty years ago, another student discovered the ghost paths. Another student learned to walk between worlds. And another student killed seven of their classmates before we stopped them."

Bai Su's breath caught in her throat. "You caught the killer?"

"We buried them." Scholar Wu's eyes met hers, and in them she saw something she had never expected to see in the headmaster of Wutong Academy. Fear. "But the dead do not always stay buried."

The bell in the tower began to ring again.

One. Two. Three.

Bai Su counted each toll, her heart beating in perfect synchrony with the sound. Four. Five. Six. The headmaster's face went pale, his hands gripping the edges of his desk until his knuckles turned white.

Seven. Eight. Nine.

"It can't be," he whispered. "We sealed her. We buried her with iron and salt and the bones of a saint. She cannot rise."

"Who?" Bai Su demanded. "Who was the killer?"

The tenth ring never came.

Instead, the sound stopped mid-toll, cut off as if someone had grabbed the bell and silenced it with their bare hands. The silence that followed was worse than any noise. It pressed against Bai Su's ears, against her skin, against her soul.

Scholar Wu stood up so fast his chair toppled backward. "The seal is broken."

"How do you know?"

"Because the bell only rings nine times when she wakes." He was already moving, grabbing a lantern from his desk and lighting it with trembling hands. "On the tenth ring, she steps out of her grave."

"Who is 'she'?"

But the headmaster was already out the door, his robes billowing behind him like the wings of a dying bird. Bai Su followed, her feet carrying her through corridors she had never seen before—twisting passages that shouldn't exist, staircases that led down when they should lead up, doors that opened onto walls and walls that opened onto doors.

The ghost paths.

She was walking the ghost paths.

Somehow, she knew the way. Her feet moved without her direction, turning left where she should have turned right, ducking through low archways that appeared just in time for her to pass through. The map in her head—a map she had never seen before—unfolded with every step, showing her the hidden architecture of the academy, the bones beneath the skin.

She came to a door.

It was made of iron, black and rusted, set into a wall of rough stone that should not exist beneath a building from the Qing dynasty. The door was covered in inscriptions—seals and prayers and names written in blood so old it had turned to brown stains.

Scholar Wu stood before it, his lantern held high, his face a mask of horror.

"The seal is broken," he said again, as if saying it enough times would make it untrue. "She broke it from the inside."

Bai Su looked at the door more closely. The inscriptions were intact. The seals were unbroken. But in the center of the door, where the largest seal should have been, there was a hole. A perfect circle, as if something had been removed. And through that hole, she could see darkness. Darkness that moved.

"What was in the seal?" she asked.

"Her heart." Scholar Wu's voice cracked. "We removed her heart and sealed it in the door. Without it, she could not rise. Without it, she could not walk. Without it, she could not kill."

"But someone took it."

The headmaster turned to look at her, and in his eyes she saw the answer before he spoke the words.

"Someone took it," he confirmed. "And that someone is still in this academy. Walking the ghost paths. Stealing life qi. Preparing for her return."

A sound came from beyond the iron door. Soft at first, barely audible. Then louder, clearer, rising like a song from the depths of the earth.

Bai Su recognized it immediately.

It was the same song she had heard in the bell tower. The same melody that had followed her down the stairs, whispered into her ear by a voice that should not exist. An old folk song, sweet and familiar, like something she had known before she was born.

She opened her mouth to speak.

The words came out before she could stop them.

"*The moon rises over the western hill, my love, my love. The river carries your shadow still, my love, my love. I will wait for you beneath the stone, where the living walk and the dead are alone.*"

Scholar Wu stumbled backward, his lantern crashing to the ground. The flame guttered and died, plunging the corridor into darkness.

"How do you know that song?" His voice was a razor's edge, sharp with terror. "That was her song. That was the song she sang to her victims before she killed them."

Bai Su tried to answer, tried to say she didn't know, tried to explain that the words had come from somewhere deep inside her, a place she had never known existed until this moment.

But the darkness around her was growing thicker, colder, and the song was still rising from beyond the iron door, and her lips were still moving, still singing, as if she had no control over them at all.

"*I will wait for you beneath the stone, where the living walk and the dead are alone.*"

The iron door groaned.

Something on the other side was pushing against it.

And Bai Su realized, with a horror that froze the blood in her veins, that the song she was singing was not a warning.

It was an answer.

The door swung open.

The darkness poured out.

And the last thing Bai Su saw before the world went black was her own reflection in the rusted iron—except her reflection was smiling, and she was not.

title: Chapter 14
content:
CHAPTER 14: The Song She Shouldn't Know

Bai Su's footsteps echoed through the empty corridors of Wutong Academy, each step carrying the weight of questions she couldn't answer. The blood on her hands had vanished, but the memory of it remained, a phantom stain she couldn't scrub away. She flexed her fingers as she walked, half-expecting to see them turn red again.

The headmaster's office sat at the end of the east wing, a heavy oak door that had stood unchanged for three centuries. Bai Su had passed it a dozen times since arriving, but she had never been summoned inside. The thought made her stomach tighten.

She raised her hand to knock, but the door swung open before her knuckles touched wood.

"Come in, Miss Bai. And close the door behind you. The draft is terrible this time of year."

Headmaster Zhou sat behind a desk piled high with examination scrolls, his spectacles perched precariously on the bridge of his nose. He was a small man, round and soft, with the kind of face that looked perpetually worried. Bai Su had heard rumors that he had been at the academy for forty years, that he had seen more ghost students than living ones, that he knew things about the school that no one else did.

She believed all of it.

"You wanted to see me, Headmaster?"

"Sit, please." He gestured to a wooden chair that creaked under her weight. "I've been told you visited the bell tower this afternoon."

Bai Su's heart stuttered. "I was just exploring. I didn't mean to cause any trouble."

"No trouble at all." Zhou pushed his spectacles up his nose, studying her with eyes that were far sharper than his soft appearance suggested. "I'm more curious about what you found there."

She hesitated. The ghost scholar had warned her not to speak of him. But the headmaster was supposed to know about the spirits in the academy. He was supposed to be the one who kept the balance between living and dead.

"I found a broken window," she said carefully. "And a footprint that shouldn't have been there."

Zhou's expression didn't change. "Whose footprint?"

"Mine."

The word hung in the air between them. Bai Su watched the headmaster's face, looking for any sign of surprise, of recognition, of something. But he simply nodded, as if he had expected this answer.

"I see." He reached into his desk and pulled out a scroll, yellowed with age. "Tell me, Miss Bai. Have you ever had dreams that felt more like memories?"

The question hit her like a physical blow. She thought of the song, the one that had followed her down the stairs, the one that felt familiar in a way that made her bones ache. She thought of the bell chamber, the feeling that she had stood there before, watching something terrible unfold.

"Sometimes," she admitted.

Zhou unrolled the scroll. It was covered in elegant calligraphy, the characters flowing like water across the parchment. But what caught Bai Su's attention was the name written at the top.

Bai Su. Her name. In her own handwriting.

"What is this?"

"Your admission exam." Zhou turned the scroll so she could see it more clearly. "You don't remember taking it, do you?"

She didn't. She remembered arriving at the academy gates, remembered being greeted by the servant girl, remembered being shown to her room. But the exam itself was a blank space in her mind, a gap she had never thought to question.

"How is that possible?"

"Because you didn't take it as a living student." Zhou's voice was gentle, almost apologetic. "You took it three years ago, Miss Bai. The day you died."

The world tilted. Bai Su gripped the arms of her chair, her knuckles white. "That's not possible. I'm alive. I breathe. I eat. I can feel the sun on my skin."

"So can half the students in this academy." Zhou sighed, a heavy sound that seemed to age him ten years. "Wutong Academy has a unique property. The boundary between life and death is thin here. Sometimes, if the conditions are right, a spirit can become so attached to the living world that they forget they've left it. They walk, talk, study, sleep—all while being as dead as the students they sit next to in class."

Bai Su shook her head, her vision swimming. "No. I remember my village. I remember my parents. I remember walking here through the forest, the leaves crunching under my feet—"

"You remember what the academy showed you." Zhou rolled up the scroll, his movements slow and deliberate. "When you arrived, you were a confused spirit with no memory of your death. The academy gave you a story to hold onto, a thread to follow. It's what it does. It's how it keeps the ghost students from going mad."

"Then what really happened to me?"

Zhou was silent for a long moment. When he spoke, his voice was barely a whisper.

"I don't know. And that's what frightens me."

He opened a drawer and pulled out a second scroll, this one covered in red seals and official stamps. "This is the incident report from the night you died. Three years ago, on the eve of the imperial examinations, a fire broke out in the east dormitory. Seven students died. You were one of them."

Bai Su's mind reeled. A fire. She had no memory of fire, no memory of smoke or flame or the terrible heat of burning wood. But as she stared at the report, something stirred in the depths of her consciousness. A flicker of orange light. The crackle of flames. A scream that might have been her own.

"I don't remember," she said, her voice shaking.

"I know." Zhou's eyes were kind, but there was something else there too. Fear. "The problem is, neither does anyone else. The fire was ruled an accident, but the investigation was... incomplete. The other six victims were identified and buried. But you—" He paused, choosing his words carefully. "You were never found."

"What do you mean, never found?"

"I mean there was no body. Just a scorched patch of floor and a name on the roster. Everyone assumed you had burned beyond recognition, that your remains were mixed with the ashes. But now, I'm not so sure."

Bai Su's blood ran cold. She thought of the ghost scholar, of the way he had looked at her with something like recognition. She thought of the blood on her hands, the footprint that matched her shoe, the song that haunted her steps.

"The fire wasn't an accident, was it?"

Zhou shook his head slowly. "I've suspected for three years that someone started it. But I could never prove it. The other ghost students don't remember anything. They just woke up one day dead, with no memory of how they got here."

"And you think I remember?"

"I think you're the key to everything." Zhou leaned forward, his voice dropping to an urgent whisper. "You were in the bell tower today, standing at the same window where Chen Feng fell. You left a footprint in the dust that shouldn't exist. You're not just a ghost, Miss Bai. You're a ghost who's starting to remember."

The door behind her creaked open. Bai Su turned to see the servant girl standing in the doorway, her face pale and drawn.

"Headmaster, there's been another incident."

Zhou's expression hardened. "What kind of incident?"

"The library. One of the living students found a ghost student unconscious on the floor. She's... fading. Her qi is almost gone."

Bai Su was on her feet before she realized she had moved. "Where in the library?"

"The east reading room. The one with the poetry collections."

She knew that room. She had passed it on her first day, had felt a strange pull toward the shelves of classical verse. She had told herself it was just curiosity, the natural attraction of a scholar to knowledge.

Now she wasn't so sure.

"Take me there."

The servant girl hesitated, glancing at Zhou. The headmaster nodded, a grim acceptance in his eyes. "Go. But be careful, Miss Bai. The killer is still out there. And if you're right about your own memories, then you might be closer to them than you think."

Bai Su didn't wait for him to finish. She ran through the corridors, her footsteps echoing off the ancient stone walls. The academy felt different now, the shadows deeper, the silence heavier. Every corner she turned held the threat of something waiting.

She reached the east reading room to find a crowd of students gathered around the door. Living and dead alike pressed forward, trying to catch a glimpse of what lay inside. A young girl lay motionless on the floor, her skin pale and translucent, her eyes open but unseeing.

Bai Su pushed through the crowd, her heart pounding. She knelt beside the girl, checking for a pulse she knew she wouldn't find.

"What happened?" she asked, her voice sharp.

No one answered. The students around her exchanged glances, their faces a mixture of fear and confusion.

"I saw something," a small voice said. Bai Su turned to find a boy no older than twelve, his eyes wide and trembling. "I saw a shadow. It was standing over her, singing."

"Singing what?"

The boy's face went pale. "I don't know. I don't remember. But it sounded familiar. Like something I heard a long time ago."

Bai Su's blood froze. She opened her mouth to ask another question, but the words died in her throat.

The song came again. Soft at first, barely a whisper on the wind. Then louder, clearer, a melody that wrapped around her like a shroud.

She knew this song.

She had heard it before.

In her dreams. In her nightmares. In the moments between waking and sleeping, when the world blurred and memories became something else entirely.

And suddenly, she remembered.

The fire. The screams. The running footsteps in the dark.

A figure standing in the doorway, silhouetted against the flames.

A voice singing this exact song.

Bai Su's vision went white. When it cleared, she was standing alone in the reading room. The students were gone. The body was gone. Even the dust motes had stopped moving, frozen in the air like tiny stars.

The song stopped.

"Hello, Bai Su."

She turned slowly, her heart pounding so hard she could feel it in her throat.

A girl stood in the doorway. Young, beautiful, with hair as black as ink and eyes that held no light. She was wearing the same uniform as the other students, but it was stained with soot and torn at the edges.

"Do you remember me now?"

Bai Su's breath caught in her chest. She didn't know this girl. She had never seen her face before in her life.

But she knew her voice.

It was the voice from the bell tower. The voice that had followed her down the stairs. The voice that had sung the song she shouldn't know.

"Who are you?"

The girl smiled, a terrible, beautiful smile that showed too many teeth.

"I'm your sister," she said. "And you're the reason I'm dead."

title: Chapter 15
content:
CHAPTER 15: The Ninth Victim

The headmaster's study smelled of ink and ashes, a combination that had become synonymous with death in Bai Su's mind. She stood before the massive oak desk, her hands still tingling from the phantom blood that had coated them minutes ago.

Headmaster Zhou Zhao sat motionless, his fingers steepled beneath his chin. His eyes, normally sharp with scholarly intensity, seemed hollowed out, shadowed by something deeper than exhaustion.

"We have another body," he said.

Bai Su's stomach clenched. "Who?"

"Lin Wei. Third year. Found in the eastern library wing an hour ago."

The name hit her like a physical blow. Lin Wei was one of the few living students who had been kind to her since her arrival—a quiet girl from the calligraphy society who always saved her a seat during morning lectures.

"How?"

"Same as the others." The headmaster pushed a scroll across his desk. "Drained of life qi. But this time, there's a difference."

Bai Su unrolled the parchment. It was an examination scroll, the kind used for the monthly assessments. The answers were written in elegant brushstrokes, each character perfectly formed. But the ink was wrong. It wasn't black.

It was red.

"What am I looking at?"

"The victim's final exam. She wrote it yesterday, before she died." The headmaster's voice cracked. "We only found it today, tucked inside her sleeve. The ink appears to be her own blood."

Bai Su's eyes scanned the answers. They were flawless. Every question answered with a precision that spoke of hours of study, of memorization, of desperation.

"Look at the last question," the headmaster said.

She turned to the final section. The prompt asked for a poem on the theme of autumn's passing. Below it, in that same crimson ink, was written:

*The ninth leaf falls before the first frost,*
*The ninth candle burns before the last,*
*The ninth student dies before the ghost,*
*The ninth bell tolls before the past.*

"Nine," Bai Su whispered. "Chen Feng was the eighth victim. Lin Wei is the ninth."

"And look at the signature."

She did. Where Lin Wei's name should have been, there was only a single character, written larger than the rest, bleeding through the paper:

*Bai.*

The room grew cold. Bai Su's fingers went numb, and the scroll slipped from her grasp, landing on the desk with a soft thud.

"That's not my handwriting," she said.

"I know."

"Then how—"

"I don't know." The headmaster stood, his joints popping audibly in the silence. "But someone is trying to frame you, Miss Bai. And given that you were seen entering the bell tower alone last night, I must ask: where were you between the hours of midnight and dawn?"

"I was in the bell chamber. I told you. I was following a ghost."

"Which ghost?"

"I don't know his name. He was dressed like a scholar from the old dynasty. He knew about the veil, about the qi theft, about everything."

"And you expect me to believe this?"

"I expect you to believe evidence." Bai Su's voice hardened. "Check the bell tower. There's broken glass everywhere. Someone shattered the window before I arrived. And there was a footprint—my footprint—already in the dust."

The headmaster's eyes narrowed. "You're saying someone anticipated your arrival?"

"I'm saying someone has been one step ahead of us from the beginning." She picked up the scroll again, reading the poem a second time. "The ninth candle burns before the last. The ninth student dies before the ghost. This is a countdown. Whoever is doing this has a specific number in mind. Ten? Twelve? A hundred?"

"Why the poem?"

"I don't know. Maybe it's a message. Maybe it's a warning. Maybe it's a confession." She looked up. "But I know one thing for certain."

"What?"

"I didn't kill Lin Wei. And the real killer is still here, watching us, waiting for the tenth leaf to fall."

A knock interrupted them. The same servant girl from before entered, her face even paler than it had been in the bell tower.

"Headmaster, there's someone here to see you. He says it's urgent."

"Who?"

"He... he says he's from the Ministry of Rites. That he's been sent to investigate the disappearances."

The headmaster exchanged a glance with Bai Su. The Ministry of Rites had no jurisdiction over academy matters. Their purview was ceremonies, protocols, and imperial examinations—not death.

"Show him in," the headmaster said.

The man who entered was young, perhaps thirty, with a face that seemed carved from stone. He wore the dark robes of a minor official, but the way he moved spoke of something else entirely. Military training, perhaps. Or something older.

"Headmaster Zhou," he said, bowing slightly. "I am Inspector Yan. I've been assigned to look into the unfortunate incidents at your academy."

"Unfortunate incidents," the headmaster repeated. "We've had nine deaths, Inspector. That's more than unfortunate. That's a massacre."

"Indeed." The inspector's eyes swept the room, landing on Bai Su. "And you are?"

"Bai Su. New student."

"The one who discovered the first body."

It wasn't a question. Bai Su's heart rate quickened. "Yes."

"I've heard a great deal about you, Miss Bai. They say you can see ghosts."

"I can."

"How fortunate." The inspector smiled, but there was no warmth in it. "I've always been curious about such things. Perhaps you could assist me in my investigation."

"I'm already assisting."

"Good. Then you won't mind if I ask you a few questions." He produced a small notebook from his sleeve, flipping it open to reveal page after page of cramped handwriting. "Let's start with your whereabouts on the night of Chen Feng's death."

"I was in my dormitory. Asleep."

"Alone?"

"Yes."

"And the night of Lin Wei's death?"

"I was in the bell tower. Alone."

"Convenient." The inspector's pen scratched against the paper. "Do you have any witnesses?"

"The ghost I was speaking to."

"Ah, yes. The ghost." He looked up, his eyes冰冷的. "And where is this ghost now?"

Bai Su opened her mouth to answer, but the words died in her throat. The shadow in the corner of the room had begun to move, detaching itself from the wall with a slow, deliberate grace. It took shape—a young man in tattered robes, his face obscured by darkness.

The ghost scholar.

He raised a finger to his lips. *Silence.*

Bai Su's blood froze.

"Miss Bai?" The inspector's voice cut through her trance. "Is something wrong?"

"No." She tore her gaze away from the shadow. "I... I don't know where he is. He appears and disappears at will."

"Convenient," the inspector repeated, writing something down.

The ghost scholar moved closer, circling behind the inspector. His hand reached out, hovering just above the man's shoulder, and Bai Su saw something she hadn't noticed before.

A thread of silver light, thin as a spider's silk, connecting the ghost's fingers to the inspector's spine.

The ghost was feeding on him.

"Stop," Bai Su said, the word escaping before she could stop it.

The inspector looked up. "Excuse me?"

"Nothing. I... I need some air." She turned to leave, but the ghost scholar's voice caught her, a whisper that only she could hear.

*He's not who he says he is.*

Bai Su froze.

*The Ministry of Rites sent no one. I checked the records. He's a ghost wearing a dead man's skin.*

She forced herself to breathe. "Inspector Yan, when did you say you arrived at the academy?"

"I didn't." His eyes narrowed. "But I came this morning, shortly after the body was discovered."

"And you came alone?"

"My assistant is waiting in the courtyard. Why do you ask?"

"No reason." Bai Su's mind raced. If the ghost scholar was telling the truth—and she had no reason to doubt him—then the man standing before her was another piece of the puzzle. A false inspector, sent by someone to muddy the waters.

Or to silence her.

*He knows about the veil,* the ghost scholar's voice continued. *He knows about the qi theft. He knows about you.*

"Miss Bai." The inspector stood, his movements too fluid, too smooth. "I think it's time we continued this conversation somewhere more private. Headmaster, if you'll excuse us."

The headmaster looked uncertain, but he nodded. "The reading room is empty. You may use it."

"Thank you."

The inspector's hand closed around Bai Su's arm, his grip like iron. She felt cold—not the cold of the ghost, but something deeper, something that seeped into her bones and whispered of graves long forgotten.

"After you," he said, guiding her toward the door.

She looked back at the ghost scholar, who had retreated into the shadows once more. His eyes met hers, and she saw something she hadn't expected.

Fear.

The inspector was not a ghost. He was something worse.

He was a corpse that had learned to walk.

And he was taking her to a place where no one would hear her scream.

title: Chapter 16
content:
CHAPTER 16: The Song Before the Fall

The headmaster's study smelled of burning paper and old secrets.

Bai Su stood before the massive oak desk, her hands still damp from the frantic washing she had done in the courtyard well. The water had run clear, but she could still feel the blood beneath her nails, phantom and accusing.

"Miss Bai." Headmaster Wei did not look up from the scroll unrolled before him. His brush moved in precise, measured strokes, each character forming with the careful precision of a man who knew exactly what he was writing. "I trust your exploration of the bell tower was... enlightening."

The words hung in the air like a trap waiting to spring.

"I found a ghost," Bai Su said carefully. "A scholar. He claims he was murdered."

"Claims?" The headmaster finally raised his eyes. They were ancient, those eyes, carrying weights that no living man should bear. "You doubt him?"

"I doubt everything in this academy."

A thin smile crossed the headmaster's face. "Good. Doubt is the first lesson of scholarship. The second is knowing when to set doubt aside." He set down his brush and folded the scroll with deliberate care. "The scholar you met—did he give you his name?"

"No. He disappeared before I could ask."

"Disappeared." The headmaster's smile widened. "Or did you disappear? In the Wutong Academy, the distinction is often meaningless."

Bai Su felt a chill crawl up her spine. "What do you mean?"

"I mean that you have been walking between worlds since the moment you stepped through our gates." He rose from his desk, his robes rustling like dried leaves. "The living see the academy as it is. The dead see it as it was. But you, Miss Bai—you see both. Do you know how rare that is?"

"I see ghosts."

"You see *truth*." He walked to the window, his back to her. "The bell chamber. The footprint. The blood on your hands. Tell me—when you touched that window, did you feel the glass? Or did you feel the air on the other side?"

Bai Su's breath caught.

She remembered. Her hand reaching out. The glass parting like water. The wind from the courtyard blowing against her face, even though the window had been closed.

"I felt... both."

"Because you were both." The headmaster turned, and for just a moment, Bai Su saw something flicker in his eyes—not age, but something older. Something that had been waiting. "There is a reason you were admitted to this academy, Bai Su. It was not your scores. It was not your recommendations. It was the simple fact that you could see what others cannot."

"What do you want from me?"

"Nothing." He returned to his desk. "Everything. The truth of what happened to Chen Feng. The identity of the killer. The reason why the ghost students are growing bolder." He picked up his brush again. "Find these things, and you will have your answers."

"And if I don't?"

"Then you will become one of the ghosts you seek to understand." He dipped the brush in ink. "The academy keeps its secrets, Miss Bai. It always has. The only question is whether you will leave them, or become one of them."

---

Bai Su left the headmaster's study with more questions than answers.

The corridors of Wutong Academy stretched before her, empty and echoing. The living students were in their classrooms, the dead students in their shadows. She walked alone, her footsteps the only sound in the silence.

But she was not alone.

She felt them watching. The ghosts. Dozens of them, hundreds, pressed against the walls like wallpaper made of eyes. They did not move. They did not speak. But they saw her, and she saw them, and the weight of that mutual recognition was crushing.

"Miss Bai."

She turned. A boy stood at the end of the corridor, no older than fourteen, his face pale and his robes stained with what looked like ink. But it wasn't ink. It was too dark, too thick, too red.

"Who are you?"

"My name is Liu Wei." He bowed, formal and precise. "I was a student here. I died in the third year of my studies."

"How?"

"The same way Chen Feng died." He looked up, and Bai Su saw that his eyes were hollow, empty sockets filled with darkness. "I fell from the bell tower. They said it was an accident. They said I slipped."

"Did you?"

He smiled, and it was the saddest smile Bai Su had ever seen. "I was pushed."

The words hit her like a physical blow. "Who pushed you?"

"I don't remember." He touched his head, where the dark stain was thickest. "I hit the ground first. The memory came second. By then, it was too late."

"Too late for what?"

"To tell anyone." He stepped closer, and Bai Su forced herself not to retreat. "There are three of us, Miss Bai. Three students who fell from the bell tower in the past ten years. Chen Feng was the fourth. But we are not the only ones."

"Who else?"

"The drowned scholar in the library. The poison victim in the dining hall. The strangled girl in the east wing." He counted them off on his fingers, each name a nail in a coffin. "Seven ghosts, Miss Bai. Seven students who died before their examinations. Seven souls trapped in this academy, waiting for a justice that will never come."

"Why are you telling me this?"

"Because you are the first one who can hear us." His hollow eyes met hers. "The first one who can see. The first one who can *do* something."

"What can I do? I'm just a student."

"No." He shook his head. "You are more than that. You have always been more than that." He reached out, and his hand passed through her arm, leaving a trail of cold. "The blood on your hands, Miss Bai. It was not Chen Feng's."

"Then whose was it?"

He opened his mouth to answer—

The bell rang.

Once. Twice. Three times.

Liu Wei's face contorted in pain. His form flickered, wavered, began to dissolve like smoke in wind.

"The bell," he gasped. "It's calling me. It's calling all of us."

"What's happening?"

"The killer." His voice was barely a whisper now. "They're taking another one."

"Who? Where?"

But Liu Wei was already gone, dissolving into the shadows like he had never been there at all.

Bai Su ran.

---

The courtyard was empty when she arrived.

The bell tower loomed above her, silent and watchful. The moon hung in the sky like a dead eye, casting pale light across the stones. Nothing moved. Nothing breathed.

But something had changed.

The air was different. Thicker. Heavier. It pressed against her skin like wet cloth, making it hard to breathe. The shadows seemed longer than they should be, stretching and reaching like grasping hands.

"Show yourself," she called out. "I know you're here."

Silence.

Then, a voice. Sweet and familiar, singing that old folk song she had heard in the bell chamber. It came from everywhere and nowhere, wrapping around her like a lover's embrace.

*"The moon is high, the wind is cold,*  
*The scholar's tale is left untold.*  
*He climbs the tower, counts the bells,*  
*And in the dark, his spirit dwells."*

Bai Su's blood ran cold.

The song stopped.

A figure stepped out of the shadows, and Bai Su's heart stopped with it.

It was a girl. Young, no older than sixteen, with long black hair and a face that might have been beautiful once. But her skin was pale as paper, her eyes dark as ink, and her smile—

Her smile was the smile of someone who had already won.

"Hello, Bai Su." The girl's voice was the song, sweet and terrible. "I've been waiting for you."

"Who are you?"

"Don't you recognize me?" The girl tilted her head, and her hair fell away to reveal a scar that ran from her temple to her jaw. "We've met before. Many times. In the bell tower. In the library. In your dreams."

"Impossible."

"Is it?" The girl stepped closer, and Bai Su saw that she left no footprints in the dust. "You've been seeing me since the day you arrived. You just didn't know it."

"The shadow in the bell chamber. That was you."

"Very good." The girl clapped her hands, a mockery of applause. "I was starting to think you would never figure it out. But you did. You found the footprint. You felt the blood. You asked the right questions."

"What do you want?"

"Want?" The girl laughed, and it was the sound of breaking glass. "I want what every ghost wants, Bai Su. I want to live again. I want to feel the sun on my skin. I want to taste food, to breathe air, to *exist*."

"And you're killing people to do it."

"Borrowing." The girl's smile widened. "I'm borrowing their life qi. Just a little at first. Enough to feel alive for a few hours. But soon..." She looked at her hands, translucent and glowing. "Soon, I'll have enough to be human again."

"And then what? You'll just walk out of here?"

"No." The girl's eyes met hers, and they were ancient, hungry, desperate. "Then I'll find the person who killed me. And I'll make them pay."

"Who killed you?"

The girl's smile faltered. For just a moment, Bai Su saw something flicker in those dark eyes—pain, rage, sorrow, all mingled together like blood and water.

"I don't remember."

"Then how do you know you were killed?"

"Because I remember falling." The girl's voice dropped to a whisper. "I remember the wind. I remember the ground rushing up to meet me. I remember the sound of my own bones breaking." She touched her scar. "And I remember someone pushing me."

"Who?"

"I told you. I don't remember."

"Then why are you killing innocent people?"

"Innocent?" The girl laughed again, but this time it was bitter, hollow. "No one in this academy is innocent. Not the students. Not the teachers. Not even you, Bai Su."

"What do you mean?"

The girl stepped closer, close enough that Bai Su could see her own reflection in those dark, dead eyes.

"You've been here before, Bai Su. You just don't remember it."

"That's impossible."

"Is it?" The girl reached out and touched Bai Su's cheek. Her hand was cold, so cold it burned. "Then explain the blood on your hands. Explain the footprint that matched your shoe. Explain why you can see us, hear us, *touch* us, when no one else can."

Bai Su had no answer.

"You're one of us, Bai Su." The girl's voice was a whisper now, soft and sad. "You just haven't died yet."

The bell rang again.

Once. Twice. Three times.

The girl's form flickered. "I have to go. The bell calls me."

"Wait—"

"Find me, Bai Su." The girl began to fade, dissolving into the moonlight like morning frost. "Find me before I find you. Or I promise you—the next time we meet, you won't walk away."

And then she was gone.

Bai Su stood alone in the courtyard, her heart pounding, her mind racing.

The song echoed in her ears, sweet and familiar and utterly terrifying.

*"The moon is high, the wind is cold,*  
*The scholar's tale is left unt

title: Chapter 17
content:
CHAPTER 17: The Song Before Dying

The headmaster's study smelled of burning paper and old secrets.

Bai Su stood before the massive rosewood desk, her hands clasped behind her back so the headmaster wouldn't see them tremble. She could still feel the phantom blood on her palms, could still hear that sweet folk song echoing in her skull like a lullaby from a nightmare.

Headmaster Zhao was not alone.

Three other teachers occupied the room—Old Xu from the archives, whose milky eyes seemed to look through rather than at her; Madam Lin, the calligraphy instructor, whose brush strokes were said to bleed when she wrote the names of dead students; and a man Bai Su had never seen before, tall and gaunt, with a scar that ran from his temple to his jaw like a second mouth.

"The bell," Headmaster Zhao said without preamble. "You heard it ring nine times."

It wasn't a question.

"Yes, Headmaster."

"And you went to investigate."

"I did."

"Alone."

The last word hung in the air like an accusation. Bai Su felt her throat tighten.

"There was no one else nearby. The tower was locked. I thought—"

"You thought wrong." The scarred man's voice was gravel and broken glass. "The bell tower has been sealed for forty years. No student is permitted entry. No teacher is permitted entry. The only person authorized to enter that chamber is the headmaster himself."

Bai Su's heart stumbled. "I didn't know."

"Ignorance is not innocence." The scarred man stood, his height casting a shadow that seemed to swallow the room. "What did you see?"

She considered lying. The instinct was there, sharp and survival-born. But something in the man's eyes told her he would know. That he always knew.

"A broken window. A footprint. Blood on my hands that wasn't there before."

Madam Lin's brush paused mid-stroke. Old Xu's milky eyes widened, if that was possible.

"And the song?" the scarred man pressed. "Did you hear the song?"

Bai Su's blood turned to ice.

"How did you know about the song?"

No one answered. The four teachers exchanged glances that spoke entire conversations without words. Finally, Headmaster Zhao sighed—a sound like wind through a tomb.

"Sit down, Miss Bai. There are things you need to understand about this academy. Things we had hoped to keep hidden until the end of the term."

---

The story they told her was worse than any ghost tale.

Wutong Academy had not always been a school for the living and the dead. Once, it had been a prison. Not for criminals—for poets, scholars, and dreamers. The Emperor's personal collection of inconvenient geniuses, locked away so their ideas could not spread.

In the third year of the academy's existence, a young woman was brought in. Her name was Wei Ling. She was eighteen years old, the daughter of a disgraced minister, and she had written a poem that made the Emperor weep with rage and recognition.

She died in the bell tower.

Not by execution. Not by suicide. By something far worse. According to the records, Wei Ling simply... stopped being. Her body remained, but the thing that made her Wei Ling—her soul, her essence, her *qi*—vanished as if it had never existed.

"The official report called it a wasting disease," Headmaster Zhao said, his voice heavy with old grief. "But we who were there knew better. She was taken. Consumed. By something that had been waiting in the bell tower since before the academy was built."

"What was it?" Bai Su asked, though she wasn't sure she wanted to know.

"We don't have a name for it. But it feeds on life qi. And it learned from Wei Ling how to imitate humanity. How to walk among us. How to choose its victims."

The scarred man leaned forward. "When the academy became a school, we thought the seal would hold. We thought the living students' presence would keep it dormant. But something has changed in the past month. The ghost students have become restless. The bell has begun to ring on its own. And you, Miss Bai, have been having dreams."

It wasn't a question.

"How did you—"

"The shadow that follows you. The song you heard. The blood that appears and disappears on your hands." The scarred man's voice dropped to a whisper. "Wei Ling has chosen you."

Bai Su's vision Tunneled. She gripped the arms of her chair, her knuckles white.

"I don't understand. Chosen me for what?"

"To be her vessel," Madam Lin said softly. "To carry the last piece of her soul back into the world of the living. So she can finish what she started."

"And what did she start?"

The four teachers exchanged another glance. This one was different. This one held fear.

"She wrote a poem," Headmaster Zhao said. "The same poem that got her imprisoned. The same poem she was composing when she died. It is a poem of endings. The end of dynasties, the end of empires, the end of death itself. If she finishes it, if she speaks it aloud with a living voice, she will unravel the boundary between life and death. Not just for Wutong Academy. For the entire world."

---

Bai Su left the headmaster's study in a daze.

The corridors of Wutong Academy had never seemed darker. The lanterns flickered as if troubled by her presence. The portraits on the walls watched her with eyes that followed every step.

She needed to find the ghost scholar. The one who had warned her. The one who knew more than he was telling.

But when she reached the library, he wasn't there.

Instead, she found Li Wei.

The senior student was sitting at their usual table, surrounded by scrolls and inkstones, his face pale in the candlelight. He looked up when she entered, and his expression shifted from concentration to concern.

"Bai Su. You look terrible."

"I feel worse."

She sat down across from him, her legs threatening to give out. Li Wei pushed a cup of tea toward her, steam curling from its surface.

"Drink. It's the good stuff—stolen from the headmaster's private stores."

She managed a weak smile. "When did you become a thief?"

"When the academy started hiding information that could get us all killed." He pulled a scroll from his pile and spread it across the table. "I've been doing some digging. About the bell tower. About the girl who died there."

Bai Su's heart skipped. "Wei Ling."

Li Wei's eyes widened. "You know about her?"

"The headmaster just told me. She's been dead for forty years. She's trying to come back. She's chosen me as her vessel."

Li Wei's face went through a series of expressions—shock, fear, and then something that looked almost like relief.

"That matches what I found." He tapped the scroll. "According to this, Wei Ling wasn't the only one who died in that tower. There were others. Seven others. All poets. All killed by the same thing."

"Seven?"

"The records say six. But I counted the names. There are eight."

Li Wei unrolled the scroll fully. It was a registry of deaths, written in precise calligraphy, each entry marked with a date and a cause. Bai Su scanned the names—

And stopped.

Her blood turned to ice.

The eighth name was hers.

"Bai Su," she whispered. "Date of death: three months from today. Cause: completion of the unfinished poem."

She looked up at Li Wei. He looked as shaken as she felt.

"There's more," he said, his voice barely audible. "Look at the date of the first death. Wei Ling's death."

Bai Su looked.

The date was forty years ago. Exactly.

But it was the second number that made her stomach drop.

Wei Ling's year of birth was the same as Bai Su's.

Her season of birth was the same.

Her day of birth was the same.

"She's not choosing me as a vessel," Bai Su said slowly, the horror dawning like a terrible sunrise. "She's choosing me because we're the same. The same soul. The same fate. She's not possessing me—she's *becoming* me."

Li Wei grabbed her hand. His grip was warm, alive, grounding.

"Then we stop her. We find the ghost scholar. We figure out how to break the connection."

"The ghost scholar is gone. The teachers are hiding something. The bells are ringing on their own." Bai Su's voice cracked. "I don't know what to do."

Li Wei was quiet for a long moment. Then he stood, pulling her up with him.

"There's one person who might know more than the teachers. One person who was there when Wei Ling died."

"Who?"

"The ghost bell ringer. The one who was found hanging from the rope the morning after her death. He's been bound to the tower for forty years, ringing the bell every night to keep her soul from escaping."

"But he's a ghost. He can't speak. He can only ring the bell."

Li Wei's smile was grim. "Then we'll have to find another way to communicate."

He led her out of the library, through the moonlit courtyard, toward the tower that loomed against the starless sky. The door was still unlocked. The stairs still creaked. The air grew colder with every step.

At the top, the bell chamber was empty.

But the rope was moving.

Back and forth, back and forth, as if pulled by invisible hands. The bell didn't ring. The rope made no sound. It simply swayed, hypnotic and wrong.

"Show yourself," Bai Su said, her voice echoing in the stone chamber. "I know you're here. I know you were the first one she killed. I know you've been trapped here for forty years."

The rope stopped.

A figure materialized beside it. A young man, no older than Bai Su, with a rope burn around his neck and empty eyes that held forty years of silence.

He raised one hand.

In his palm, written in what looked like dried blood, was a single character:

*SONG*

"He wants us to sing?" Li Wei asked.

The ghost shook his head. He pointed at Bai Su. Then at the window. Then at the sky.

"He wants me to remember the song," Bai Su whispered. "The one I heard in my dream. The one I heard when I woke up with blood on my hands."

The ghost nodded.

Bai Su closed her eyes. The song came back to her in fragments—a melody that seemed older than the academy, older than the mountains, older than the concept of time itself. Words in a language she didn't know but somehow understood.

She opened her mouth to sing.

The ghost lunged.

His hands closed around her throat—cold, dead, desperate—and Li Wei screamed, trying to pull him off, but the ghost was too strong, his grip too tight—

And then Bai Su understood.

He wasn't trying to kill her.

He was trying to stop her from singing.

Because if she sang the song, Wei Ling would complete her poem.

And if Wei Ling completed her poem, the world would end.

Bai Su stopped struggling.
