The ghost's empty eyes met hers.

And in that moment, she saw what he had been trying to say for forty years.

*Don't sing. Don't remember. Don't become her.*

*But if you must...*

*Sing the song wrong.*

The ghost released her throat.

He stepped back.

And for the first time in forty years, the bell ringer opened his mouth and spoke.

"Remember," he croaked, his voice like grinding bones. "Remember the child who died in the garden. Remember the poet who loved the moon. Remember the girl

title: Chapter 18
content:
CHAPTER 18: The Song Before the Fall

The headmaster's study smelled of ink and ash, a combination that made Bai Su's stomach turn. She stood before his desk, trying not to look at her hands, trying not to remember the blood that had been there only minutes ago.

"There's been another incident," Headmaster Zhou said, his voice carrying the weight of someone who had said those words too many times. He pushed a scroll across the desk. "Found this morning. In the east library."

Bai Su unrolled it with trembling fingers. The paper was ancient, yellowed at the edges, but the characters written on it were fresh. Red ink. So dark it was almost black.

*The scholar who burns the midnight oil*
*Will find his flame extinguished*
*When the fourth watch comes*
*And the bell rings nine times*

"It's a poem," she said, her voice hollow.

"It's a death threat." Headmaster Zhou's eyes were bloodshot, the circles beneath them so dark they looked like bruises. "We found it pinned to Liang Wei's desk. He's one of our top students. Living."

"Where is he now?"

"Gone. Disappeared sometime last night." The headmaster's hands shook as he reached for a cup of tea. "His roommate said he heard someone singing. A folk song. Around the fourth watch."

Bai Su's blood turned to ice. She had heard that song too. In the bell chamber. On the stairs.

"What song?"

"I don't know the name." Headmaster Zhou's voice cracked. "But the roommate remembered the lyrics. He wrote them down before he forgot."

He slid another piece of paper toward her. The handwriting was shaky, barely legible:

*White moon on the water*
*Where does my shadow go?*
*Follow the bell, follow the bell*
*Down where the river flows*
*One for the living, two for the dead*
*Three for the soul that's left unsaid*

Bai Su's vision blurred. The words swam before her eyes, rearranging themselves into patterns she almost recognized. She had heard this before. Not in this life. In another—

"Miss Bai?" The headmaster's voice pulled her back. "Are you alright?"

"I need to see Liang Wei's room."

---

The east dormitory was quieter than it should have been. Students moved through the hallways like ghosts themselves, their eyes downcast, their footsteps muffled. The living and the dead had always maintained an uneasy coexistence at Wutong Academy, but now the balance had shifted. Fear was the great equalizer.

Liang Wei's room was on the third floor. The door was unlocked, the space inside untouched since his disappearance. A half-finished essay sat on his desk, the brush still resting in its inkstone. His bed was made. His clothes hung neatly in the wardrobe.

It looked like he had stepped out for a moment and simply never returned.

Bai Su moved through the room slowly, her fingers brushing over objects, searching for anything out of place. The window was closed. The lock was intact. No signs of struggle.

"He just vanished," said a voice behind her.

She turned. A young man stood in the doorway, his face pale, his eyes red-rimmed. The roommate, she guessed. Living.

"You heard the song?"

He nodded, his throat working as he swallowed. "It was beautiful. That's the worst part. I woke up and I just... listened. I wanted to follow it. I was getting out of bed when I saw Liang Wei. He was already at the door. His eyes were open, but he wasn't looking at anything. He was smiling."

"Smiling?"

"Like he was hearing something wonderful. Something only he could hear." The roommate's voice dropped to a whisper. "I called his name. He didn't respond. He just walked out. I followed him to the stairs, but when I got there, he was gone. The song stopped. And I couldn't remember how it went."

Bai Su looked down at the paper in her hands. The lyrics were already fading, the ink growing fainter as she watched.

"Write it down again," she said. "Quickly."

The roommate scrambled for a brush. His hands shook so badly he could barely hold it.

"I can't," he whispered. "I've already forgotten."

---

The ghost scholar was waiting for her in the garden.

Bai Su found him sitting beneath the withered plum tree, his translucent hands folded in his lap, his eyes fixed on something she couldn't see. He didn't look up when she approached.

"You found the poem," he said.

"Were you following me?"

"I don't need to follow you. The academy tells me everything." He finally turned to look at her, and there was something different in his eyes. Something ancient. Something sad. "The east library is where I died."

Bai Su's breath caught. "You were killed in the east library?"

"I was killed at my desk. Studying for the imperial exams." A ghost of a smile crossed his lips. "I wanted to be the best. I studied through the night, every night. I memorized the classics until my eyes bled. And one night, someone decided that my dedication was an insult to their own laziness."

"Who?"

"I never saw their face. But I heard them sing." His voice dropped, becoming something else entirely. Not his voice. A voice that seemed to come from everywhere at once. *"White moon on the water. Where does my shadow go?"*

Bai Su stumbled backward. The song filled her ears, louder now, drowning out the wind, the birds, the beating of her own heart.

"Stop," she gasped. "Please stop."

The ghost scholar fell silent. The song faded.

"I'm sorry," he said. "I didn't mean to frighten you."

"What was that? How do you know that song?"

"Because I heard it before I died. And I hear it every night, in the moments between sleep and waking." He stood, his form flickering like a candle in the wind. "The singer is not human. It's not a ghost either. It's something older. Something that has been in this academy since before the first stone was laid."

"The bell tower," Bai Su whispered. "I saw something there. A shadow. It sang to me."

"No." The ghost scholar's voice was sharp. Urgent. "The bell tower is safe. Whatever you saw there was not what you think."

"Then what did I see?"

He didn't answer. His eyes had fixed on something behind her. Bai Su turned.

A figure stood at the edge of the garden. A girl in white robes, her hair loose and flowing, her feet bare on the cold stone path. She was beautiful in the way that moonlight was beautiful—distant, cold, indifferent to the warmth of the living.

Bai Su had never seen her before. But she knew her.

*The drowned scholar in the library.*

"Bai Su," the girl said, and her voice was the song. "You have been looking for me."

---

The garden changed.

The withered plum tree burst into bloom, pink petals raining down like snow. The dead grass turned green. The air filled with the scent of jasmine and rain. And the girl in white stepped forward, her feet leaving no prints on the path.

"I am Lin Yue," she said. "I died in the academy well, three hundred years ago. I have been waiting for someone who could see."

"See what?"

"The truth." Lin Yue's eyes were black from edge to edge, no whites, no pupils. Just endless darkness that seemed to swallow light. "The killer is not a ghost. It is not a student. It is the academy itself."

Headmaster Zhou's words echoed in Bai Su's mind. *The academy has stood for a thousand years. It remembers everything.*

"What do you mean?"

"The examination system is built on suffering. The living study until their bodies break. The dead study until their souls forget what it means to exist. The academy feeds on that suffering. It grows stronger with every tear, every sleepless night, every dream abandoned for the sake of a passing grade."

Lin Yue's voice was calm, almost gentle, but her words cut like blades.

"The ghost that is killing students is not acting alone. It is being guided. By the academy. By the headmaster. By the very walls that surround us."

"That's impossible," Bai Su said. "The headmaster wants to stop the killings."

"No." Lin Yue shook her head slowly. "He wants to control them. He has known about the killer from the beginning. He has been feeding them students. The living ones. The ones who were too ambitious, who studied too hard, who might have surpassed the academy's control."

The ghost scholar stepped forward, positioning himself between Bai Su and Lin Yue. "You're lying."

"Am I?" Lin Yue smiled, and it was the most terrible thing Bai Su had ever seen. "Then why did the headmaster assign Chen Feng to the bell tower on the night he died? Why did he send Liang Wei to the east library, knowing the east library was where the first victim was found? Why did he tell Bai Su to investigate the killings, knowing she would walk right into the killer's path?"

Bai Su's mind raced. The assignments. The clues. The way the headmaster had seemed to know exactly where she would go, what she would find.

"You're saying he's been using me."

"Like a brush," Lin Yue said. "Like paper. Like ink. You are writing his story, Bai Su. And when you reach the final chapter, he will discard you."

The ghost scholar grabbed her arm. His touch was cold, but it grounded her. "Don't listen to her. She's the one who's been singing. She's the one who's been luring students to their deaths."

"Have I?" Lin Yue's smile widened. "Then why haven't I lured you?"

The ghost scholar froze.

"Because you're already dead," Lin Yue whispered. "And the dead cannot be killed again."

Bai Su looked between them. The garden was still beautiful. The petals were still falling. But beneath the beauty, she could feel something stirring. Something hungry.

"Who do I trust?" she asked. The question was not directed at anyone. It was simply the thought that escaped her lips.

"Trust yourself," Lin Yue said.

"Trust the living," the ghost scholar said.

They spoke at the same time, their words colliding in the air like opposing winds.

Bai Su closed her eyes.

And in the darkness behind her lids, she saw it. The memory that was not hers. The memory that had been waiting in the bell tower, in the dried blood on her hands, in the footprint that matched her shoe.

She had been here before.

She had been the killer.

She opened her eyes.

"I know who's doing this," she said.

Lin Yue and the ghost scholar stared at her.

"Who?" they asked together.

Bai Su looked at her hands. Clean now. But she knew what they were capable of. What they had done.

"It's me."

The wind died. The petals stopped falling. The garden held its breath.

And in the distance, the bell began to ring.

title: Chapter 19
content:
CHAPTER 19: The Song Before Drowning

The headmaster's office smelled of sandalwood and secrets.

Bai Su stood before the massive rosewood desk, trying to keep her hands still. The phantom blood had faded, but she could still feel it—a tacky residue that no amount of rubbing could remove. The headmaster sat across from her, his fingers steepled, his eyes unreadable.

"You were in the bell tower."

It wasn't a question.

"I heard the bell," Bai Su said. "I went to investigate."

"Alone. At night." His voice carried no accusation, but the weight of his words pressed down on her shoulders. "That was unwise."

"I didn't think—"

"No. You didn't." He stood, walking to the window that overlooked the courtyard. The moon hung low and yellow, casting long shadows across the stones. "What did you see?"

Bai Su hesitated. The ghost scholar's warning echoed in her mind. *They'll become something worse.* But the headmaster was alive. Surely he could be trusted. Surely—

"I saw a ghost," she said. "A scholar. He told me things."

The headmaster's reflection in the glass didn't move. "What things?"

"About the murders. About the qi transfer. About something that's about to start." She took a breath. "He said I needed to find the killer before they break through the veil."

"Did he now." The headmaster turned, his face carved from stone. "And did he tell you who the killer is?"

"No."

"But he showed you something."

It wasn't a question either. Bai Su felt a cold knot tighten in her stomach. "How did you know?"

"Because the bell tower has been sealed for three years. Ever since Chen Feng died." He held up a key—old brass, tarnished black. "I am the only one with access. The only one who has entered since his body was removed."

"That's impossible. The door was open."

"The door is always locked." He set the key on the desk between them. "I checked it myself, two hours ago, after the evening meal. It was secure."

Bai Su's mind raced. "Then someone picked the lock. Or they have a copy."

"Or," the headmaster said slowly, "someone wanted you to see what you saw. Someone who can move through walls and ignore locked doors."

"A ghost."

"A very specific ghost." He sat back down, his movements deliberate, controlled. "Chen Feng was found in that bell tower. He had been studying for the examinations, burning the midnight oil as the saying goes. His tutors said he was anxious, paranoid. He claimed someone was following him."

"Who?"

"He never said. But on the night he died, he locked himself in the bell tower. He rang the bell nine times—the traditional signal for distress. By the time we arrived, the door was locked from the inside. The window was broken. He was on the ground below."

"And you think he was pushed."

"I think many things, Miss Bai. The question is whether I can prove them." He opened a drawer and pulled out a scroll, yellowed with age. "This was found in his sleeve. The examiners assumed it was a study guide. But look closely."

He unrolled it across the desk. Bai Su leaned in, her breath catching.

It was an examination scroll. Perfectly written. Elegant characters flowing across the page like water. The essay was complete, every stroke precise, every argument meticulously structured. It would have earned top marks.

At the bottom, in red ink, was the grade: *Failed.*

"Why?" Bai Su asked. "This is brilliant work."

"Look at the name."

She did. Her blood turned to ice.

The name at the top was not Chen Feng.

It was Bai Su.

She jerked back as if burned. "That's not possible. I've never taken the imperial examinations. I only just arrived at this academy."

"And yet your name is there, written in Chen Feng's hand." The headmaster's eyes bore into her. "He copied this essay from somewhere. Or someone."

"Or someone wrote it for him," Bai Su whispered. "Someone who knew my name before I ever set foot in this place."

The headmaster said nothing. He simply watched her, waiting.

"I don't understand," she said, her voice cracking. "I've never met Chen Feng. I've never been in that tower before tonight. I don't know why he had my name on his scroll."

"Perhaps you should ask him."

"Ask him? He's dead."

"Yes." The headmaster's smile was thin and cold. "But you seem to have a talent for speaking with the dead. So speak with him. Find out what he knew. Find out why he had your name." He rolled the scroll back up and held it out to her. "You have until the next full moon. That's when the veil between worlds grows thin. If the killer hasn't been found by then..."

"What?"

"Then I will be forced to close the academy. Permanently. And all the ghosts within will be exorcised."

Bai Su took the scroll, her hands trembling. "You can't do that. They're students. They're—"

"They're dead, Miss Bai." His voice was hard, final. "And the living come first. Always."

She left the office with the scroll clutched to her chest, her mind a whirlwind of questions. The corridor stretched before her, dark and empty. The oil lamps flickered as she passed, their flames bending toward her like hungry tongues.

She found herself walking toward the library.

The drowned scholar was there, as always, floating among the shelves. He looked up when she entered, his waterlogged eyes widening.

"You found something," he said. It wasn't a question.

"Chen Feng's scroll." She held it up. "He had my name on it."

The scholar drifted closer, his movements leaving trails of moisture on the floor. "May I?"

She handed it over. He unrolled it with careful, reverent hands, his spectral fingers passing through the paper without touching it. His expression shifted—surprise, then recognition, then something darker.

"This isn't Chen Feng's handwriting."

"What?"

"Look." He pointed to the character for "Bai." "The stroke order is wrong. And here, the pressure changes mid-stroke, as if someone else took over." He looked up, his face pale. "Two people wrote this. One started. One finished."

"Who finished it?"

"Someone who knew your name. Someone who wanted Chen Feng to have it." He rolled the scroll back up. "Chen Feng didn't write this essay. He copied it. From another scroll. And that scroll was written by someone who knew you before you arrived."

Bai Su's skin crawled. "That's impossible. I've never—"

"Been to this academy before tonight? So you say." The scholar's voice was soft, almost sympathetic. "But memory is a tricky thing, Miss Bai. Especially for the living. You forget. You misremember. You convince yourself that things happened differently than they did."

"What are you saying?"

"I'm saying that maybe you've been here before. Maybe you just don't remember."

The words hit her like a physical blow. She stumbled back, her hand pressing against her chest where her heart hammered wildly. "That's insane. I would remember."

"Would you?" He floated closer, his face inches from hers. "What's the first thing you remember? Your earliest memory. Can you see it clearly?"

She tried. She reached back, further and further, searching for the beginning of her story.

There was nothing.

A void. A blank space where childhood should have been. She remembered Wutong Academy. She remembered her parents sending her here. She remembered...

She remembered nothing before the age of twelve.

"The ghost scholar wiped your memory," the drowned scholar said softly. "Or someone did. Someone powerful enough to reach into your mind and take what they wanted."

"Who?"

"I don't know. But I know why."

"Why?"

"Because you wrote that essay." He pointed at the scroll. "You wrote it, years ago, as a practice examination. And someone stole it. Someone used it to frame you for Chen Feng's death."

"Frame me? I didn't kill anyone."

"Doesn't matter. If the headmaster finds out that scroll was written in your hand, he'll have no choice but to expel you. And once you're outside the academy walls, the killer can take your qi without interference."

Bai Su's mind raced. "But why would they want my qi specifically?"

The drowned scholar's face twisted with something like pity. "Because you're not just a student, Miss Bai. You're a vessel."

"A vessel for what?"

"For something that died a long time ago. Something that wants to live again." He reached out and touched her forehead. His fingers were cold, so cold they burned.

And then she saw.

A room. Dark. Stone walls. Candles flickering in a circle. A body lying in the center—a girl, young, her throat cut from ear to ear. And standing over her, a figure in white, holding a bloodstained brush.

The figure turned.

It was her.

Bai Su screamed and jerked back, the vision dissolving like smoke. She was on the floor, the scroll scattered beside her, her hands pressed to her face.

"Now you remember," the drowned scholar said.

"That wasn't real. That wasn't—"

"It was. It is. You died, Miss Bai. Five years ago, in a ritual that went wrong. And someone brought you back." He knelt beside her, his voice gentle. "But resurrection has a price. The soul that was meant to pass on stayed. And the soul that was meant to stay passed on."

"You're saying I'm a ghost."

"I'm saying you're both. Living and dead. Stuck between worlds." He looked at her with something like sympathy. "That's why you can see us. That's why the killer wants your qi. You have enough of both worlds to tear the veil apart."

Bai Su stared at her hands. They were solid. Warm. Alive.

But beneath the skin, she could feel something else. Something cold. Something waiting.

"I need to find Chen Feng," she said, her voice hollow. "I need to ask him what he knew."

"He won't tell you."

"Why not?"

"Because he's the one who killed you the first time."

The words hung in the air like a death sentence.

Bai Su looked up at the drowned scholar, searching for any sign of deception. But his face was open, honest, and terribly sad.

"He was my friend," she whispered. "The ghost in the bell tower. He was my friend."

"Yes." The scholar floated back, giving her space. "And he's the one who cut your throat."

The library fell silent. Bai Su stood slowly, her legs shaking beneath her. She picked up the scroll, rolling it carefully, her movements mechanical.

"Where is he now?"

"Waiting for you in the bell tower. He always knew you'd come back."

Bai Su turned and walked out of the library, the scroll clutched to her chest like a lifeline. Behind her, the drowned scholar watched her go, his expression unreadable.

Above her, in the bell tower, the rope began to sway.

The bell rang once.

Then twice.

Then it did not stop.

title: The Song Before the Fall
content:
The headmaster's study smelled of old paper and dried chrysanthemums, a scent so thick it clung to Bai Su's robes like a second skin. She stood before the massive rosewood desk, her hands clasped behind her back, the phantom blood long since scrubbed away but still burning beneath her nails.

Headmaster Xu Wei sat across from her, his fingers steepled, his face an unreadable mask. Behind him, the window looked out onto the main courtyard where Chen Feng had fallen. The glass had been replaced. The stones had been washed. But Bai Su could still see the shape of his body imprinted on the air, a ghost of impact that refused to fade.

"You've been busy, Miss Bai." The headmaster's voice was calm, measured, the voice of a man who had spent decades negotiating with the dead. "I hear you've been visiting the bell tower. The library. The west wing." He paused. "The dormitory where Lu Ming spent his final night."

Bai Su's heart skipped. "How do you know about that?"

"I know everything that happens in this academy." Xu Wei reached into his desk drawer and withdrew a scroll, yellowed with age, tied with a faded red ribbon. "Including the fact that you've been asking questions about the examination records. About the students who died before taking the imperial exams."

"I'm trying to find the killer."

"No." He unrolled the scroll, smoothing it flat. "You're trying to find yourself."

Bai Su stepped forward, her eyes scanning the document. It was a student roster, dated fifteen years ago. Names, ages, hometowns, examination scores. Her finger traced down the list, past the living, past the dead, until it stopped at a name that made her blood freeze.

Bai Su. Age: 16. Hometown: Qingzhou. Examination score: Top of the class. Status: Deceased.

"That's not possible." She heard her own voice as if from a great distance. "I'm alive. I'm standing here. I'm—"

"You died, Miss Bai." Xu Wei's voice was gentle, almost kind, which made it worse. "Fifteen years ago. The night before the imperial examinations. You fell from the bell tower. Just like Chen Feng."

The room tilted. Bai Su gripped the edge of the desk, her knuckles white. "That's a lie. I have memories. I have a family. I have—"

"Memories that began exactly one month ago, when you arrived at Wutong Academy?" He raised an eyebrow. "Think carefully, Miss Bai. What do you remember before that? Your childhood? Your parents? Your hometown?"

Bai Su's mind went blank. She tried to picture her mother's face, her father's voice, the house where she had grown up. Nothing. Just static, white and formless, like snow falling on an empty grave.

"I remember..." She faltered. "I remember the academy. The classrooms. The library. The—"

"You remember only what this place has shown you." Xu Wei stood, walking around the desk to stand beside her. "Because you have been here for fifteen years, Miss Bai. You just didn't know it."

"That's impossible." But even as she said it, the pieces began to fall into place. The blood on her hands. The footprint that matched her shoe. The ghost scholar's words: *You need to find the killer. Before they take enough qi to break through the veil entirely.*

"The killer isn't a ghost who wants to become human," she whispered. "The killer is a ghost who already did."

Xu Wei's eyes flickered with something—surprise, perhaps, or recognition. "You're faster than I expected. Yes. The student who has been draining qi from the living, who pushed Chen Feng from the tower, who has been killing for fifteen years—they are a ghost who succeeded. They broke through the veil, became human again, and now they walk among us, indistinguishable from the living."

"But that doesn't make sense." Bai Su's mind raced. "If they already became human, why keep killing? Why drain more qi?"

"Because becoming human is not the same as staying human." Xu Wei returned to his desk, his movements slow and deliberate. "The qi they stole is borrowed. It fades. They need to keep taking more, keep replenishing the life force that slips through their fingers like water. Every year, during the full moon, they must take another life. Or they revert to what they were."

"A ghost."

"A hungry ghost." He corrected. "A spirit so consumed by the desire to live that they will do anything to maintain their existence. They have been doing this for fifteen years, Miss Bai. Fifteen students, one every year. But this year is different."

"Why?"

"Because this year, there is someone who can see them." He looked at her, his gaze piercing. "You."

The bell above them rang. Once. Twice. Three times. The same mournful toll Bai Su had heard in the bell chamber, the same frantic rhythm that had preceded Chen Feng's death.

"The full moon is tonight," Xu Wei said. "The killer will strike again. And this time, they will not stop until they have taken enough qi to sustain themselves for another decade."

Bai Su turned toward the door, but his voice stopped her.

"Before you go, Miss Bai, there is one more thing you should know." He picked up the scroll, holding it out to her. "The student who killed you fifteen years ago. The one who pushed you from the bell tower. They are still here."

She took the scroll with trembling hands. The paper felt wrong against her fingers—too cold, too smooth, like the skin of a corpse.

"They are the one who has been helping you."

The words hit her like a physical blow. She looked down at the scroll, at the list of names, at her own name written in faded ink. And then she saw it. A name scratched out, crossed through with such force that the paper had torn. But beneath the damage, she could still read the characters:

Lu Ming.

"Lu Ming was the killer?" Her voice cracked. "Lu Ming was the one who pushed me?"

"No." Xu Wei shook his head slowly. "Lu Ming was the one who caught you. He was your friend. He tried to save you. And when he failed, he stayed. He has been watching over you ever since, waiting for you to wake up."

"But he said—he told me to find the killer. He said—"

"He lied." The headmaster's voice was flat. "Because the killer is someone he loves. Someone he cannot bring himself to accuse. And until you figure out who that is, the deaths will continue."

Outside, the bell rang four times. Five. Six.

Bai Su looked at her hands. They were still clean, still dry. But she could feel the ghost of blood beneath her skin, the echo of a fall that had ended her life fifteen years ago.

She thought of Lu Ming's gentle voice, his careful guidance, the way he had appeared at exactly the right moments to point her toward the truth. She thought of the way he had touched her hand, the warmth that had spread through her skin, the feeling of being alive that she had almost forgotten.

And she thought of the shadow she had seen in the bell tower, the voice that had sung the old folk song, sweet and familiar.

The song her mother used to sing.

*Sleep, little one, sleep,*
*The moon is watching from the deep.*
*Dream, little one, dream,*
*The river's wider than it seems.*

She had remembered. Not the academy, not the examinations, not the fall. But the song. The song that had followed her into death, that had wrapped around her like a shroud, that had kept her tethered to a world she no longer belonged to.

"The killer," she said slowly, "is someone who knew me. Someone who was close to me. Someone who—"

She stopped. The realization hit her like a blade between the ribs.

"Someone who knows the song."

Xu Wei's face went pale. "Miss Bai, no—"

But she was already running, her feet pounding against the wooden floors, her heart beating with a rhythm that had been silent for fifteen years.

The bell rang seven times. Eight. Nine.

She burst through the doors of the main hall, into the courtyard where Chen Feng had died, where she had died, where the stones still held the memory of impact.

And there, standing beneath the moon that was not yet full, was a figure wrapped in shadows.

"Hello, Bai Su." The voice was soft, familiar, achingly kind. "I was wondering when you would remember."

The figure stepped forward, and the moonlight caught their face.

It was her mother.

Bai Su's world shattered.

"You?" The word came out as a whisper, barely audible over the roaring in her ears. "You killed me?"

"No, my dear." Her mother smiled, and it was the same smile Bai Su had seen in her dreams, the same smile that had sung her to sleep, the same smile that had promised safety and warmth and love. "I didn't kill you. I saved you."

"Saved me? I died! I fell from the bell tower! I—"

"You didn't fall, Bai Su." Her mother stepped closer, and Bai Su saw that she was not entirely solid—her edges flickered, her form wavered, like a candle flame caught in a draft. "You jumped."

The words hung in the air, heavier than any stone, colder than any grave.

"You were going to die anyway," her mother continued. "The examination fever. The stress. The pressure to succeed. You were going to die in that examination hall, your body giving out from exhaustion, your mind broken by failure. So I gave you a choice. A quick death. A painless fall. Or a slow, agonizing end that would have taken days."

"You killed me." Bai Su's hands were shaking. "You pushed me."

"I gave you a gift." Her mother's voice hardened. "And then I used your death to fuel my own life. Your qi, your vitality, your potential—I took it all. And I have been living on it for fifteen years."

"Why?" The question tore out of Bai Su's throat. "Why would you do this to me?"

"Because I loved you." Her mother's smile did not waver. "And because love is the most selfish thing in the world."

The bell rang ten times. Silence fell.

And in that silence, Bai Su understood.

The killer was not a ghost who wanted to become human.

The killer was a mother who wanted to keep her daughter alive.

Even if it meant killing her first.

Bai Su looked at her mother's face, at the familiar curve of her cheek, the gentle slope of her nose, the eyes that had once held nothing but warmth.

Now they held only hunger.

"Tonight," her mother said, "I will take my fill. And I will live for another year. And you, my dear, will watch. Like you have watched for fifteen years. Like you will watch for eternity."

"No." Bai Su's voice was steady, calm, the voice of a girl who had already died once. "Tonight, I will stop you."

Her mother laughed, a sound like breaking glass. "You cannot kill what is already dead, Bai Su. And you cannot save what is already gone."

"I'm not going to kill you." Bai Su reached into her robes, pulling out the scroll Xu Wei had given her. "I'm going to free you."

She unrolled the parchment, revealing the names of the dead. Her name. Lu Ming's name. The names of fifteen students who had fallen, one every year, each death feeding the hunger of a mother who could not let go
